검을 맞댄 동료에게는 검을 들지 않는다. 세상이 $userName과 $celebName을 갈라 세우려는 날에도, $userName의 검은 $celebName을 향하지 않는다. 달빛 아래 처음 맞댄 그 검처럼, 두 사람은 끝까지 같은 쪽을 향한다.

neutral.tone: 스쳐 지나간 잔잔한 인연 — 합·충 같은 강한 신호는 없음

paragraphs.gi: $era, $userName과 $celebName은 같은 장터에 있었어요. $userName은 $userRole이었고, $celebName은 $celebRole이었어요.
paragraphs.seung: 두 사람의 사주에는 합이나 충 같은 뚜렷한 인연 신호가 걸려 있지 않았어요. 그래서 둘은 운명처럼 묶이지도, 크게 부딪치지도 않은 채 그저 같은 거리를 스쳤어요.
paragraphs.jeon: $celebName이 $userName의 가게 앞을 지날 때, 둘은 가벼운 눈인사 한 번을 나눴을 뿐이에요. 강한 끌림도, 미움도 아니었고, 그냥 같은 시대를 살던 두 사람의 잔잔한 마주침이었어요.
paragraphs.gyeol: 전생의 사주는 두 사람을 강하게 엮어 두지 않았어요. 그래서 이번 생의 인연도 운명이라기보다, $userName이 스스로 골라 가까이 둔 마음에 더 가까워요.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: 그래서 $celebName을 좋아하는 마음은 사주가 정해 준 게 아니라 $userName이 직접 고른 취향이에요. 그게 더 단단한 거예요.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: 그래서 $celebName을 향한 관심은 정해진 운명이 아니라 $userName이 스스로 선택한 마음이에요.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: 그래서 $celebName을 응원하는 마음은 사주가 묶어 준 게 아니라 $userName이 직접 정한 거예요.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: 그래서 $celebName을 좋아하는 건 운명의 합 때문이 아니라, $userName이 골라 낸 진짜 취향이에요.

neutral.title: 장터에서 스친 사람
neutral.logline: 장이 서던 닷새마다 시장에서 마주치던 두 사람 $userName과 $celebName. 운명이 묶지 않은, 스스로 건넨 잔잔한 인연.

chapters.heading: 닷새마다 서는 장
chapters.body:
조선 후기. 어느 고을에 닷새마다 장이 섰다.

장날이면 고을 사람들이 다 그 장터로 모였다. 곡식을 팔러, 옷감을 사러, 그저 구경을 하러. 장터에는 좌판이 줄줄이 늘어섰고, 사람들이 흥정하는 소리로 종일 시끄러웠다.

$userName도 그 장에 다녔다. $userName은 약초를 캐다 파는 사람이었다. 닷새마다 산에서 캔 약초를 한 짐 지고 장터로 내려와, 한구석에 자리를 폈다. 약초를 종류별로 다발 지어 늘어놓고, 손님을 기다렸다.

$celebName도 그 장에 다녔다. $celebName은 옹기를 굽는 사람이었다. 닷새마다 옹기를 지게에 지고 와, 장터 다른 구석에 자리를 폈다. 크고 작은 옹기를 가지런히 늘어놓고.

두 사람의 자리는 멀지 않았다. 장터 한구석과, 그 옆 구석. 좌판 몇 개를 사이에 둔 거리였다.

두 사람은 특별한 사이가 아니었다. 같은 마을 사람도 아니고, 아는 사이도 아니었다. 두 사람을 묶는 무슨 끈이 있는 것도 아니었다. 핏줄도, 약속도, 무슨 깊은 사연도 없었다. 그저 — 닷새마다 같은 장터에서, 가까운 자리에 좌판을 펴는 사람들이었다.

$userName은 장터에서 $celebName을 자주 봤다. $celebName이 옹기를 늘어놓는 것을, 손님과 흥정하는 것을, 점심으로 주먹밥을 먹는 것을. $celebName의 옹기가 햇볕에 반들거리는 것을.

$celebName도 $userName을 자주 봤다. $userName이 약초를 다발로 묶는 것을, 산 이야기를 손님에게 들려주는 것을. $userName이 캐 온 약초에서 풀냄새가 나는 것을.

두 사람은 한동안, 인사도 나누지 않았다. 장터에는 사람이 많았고, 다들 제 장사에 바빴다. 두 사람은 그저 닷새마다, 서로를 스쳐 봤을 뿐이다. 얼굴만 익은 채로.

chapters.heading: 건넨 주먹밥
chapters.body:
그러다 어느 장날, 비가 내렸다.

갑작스러운 비였다. 아침까지 맑던 하늘이, 한낮에 갑자기 흐려지더니 빗방울을 쏟았다. 장터 사람들이 좌판을 황급히 거두었다. 곡식이며 옷감이며, 비에 젖으면 안 되는 것들이 많았으니까.

$userName도 약초가 비에 젖을까 봐 서둘러 짐을 쌌다. 약초는 젖으면 못 쓰게 됐다.

그런데 $celebName의 자리에서 일이 생겼다. $celebName이 옹기를 옮기다, 발을 헛디뎌 옹기 몇 개를 깨뜨렸다. 빗물에 마당이 미끄러웠던 것이다. 옹기가 바닥에 떨어져, 쨍그랑 소리를 내며 깨졌다.

$celebName은 깨진 옹기 조각 앞에 잠깐 주저앉았다. 닷새를 흙을 빚고 가마에 구운 옹기였다. 그 며칠의 수고가, 빗속에 조각나 있었다.

$userName이 그 모습을 봤다.

$userName은 자기 짐을 마저 챙긴 뒤, $celebName에게 다가갔다. 그리고 말없이, 깨진 옹기 조각을 함께 주워 모았다. 빗속에서. 옷이 젖는 것도 개의치 않고.

$celebName이 $userName을 봤다. '…고맙습니다. 안 도와주셔도 됐는데. 비도 오는데요.'

'비도 오는데, 혼자 줍기 번거로워 보여서요.' $userName이 말했다. '조각이 많아서, 둘이 주우면 빠르겠다 싶었습니다.'

조각을 다 모은 뒤, $userName은 자기 봇짐에서 주먹밥 하나를 꺼냈다. 점심으로 싸 온 거였다. 아직 한 덩이가 남아 있었다.

'드세요.' $userName이 그걸 $celebName에게 내밀었다. '비 맞고 옹기까지 깨졌으니, 속이라도 든든해야죠. 깨진 옹기 보면서 빈속으로 앉아 있으면, 더 속상합니다.'

$celebName은 잠깐 망설이다, 그 주먹밥을 받았다. 두 손으로.

그날 두 사람은, 비가 그칠 때까지 장터 처마 밑에 함께 앉아 있었다. 별 이야기는 하지 않았다. $userName은 약초 이야기를 조금 — 어느 산에 무슨 약초가 잘 난다는 이야기를. $celebName은 옹기 이야기를 조금 — 흙을 어디서 가져오는지, 가마 불을 어떻게 맞추는지를. 빗소리가 두 사람의 짧은 말 사이를 채웠다.

비가 그치자, 두 사람은 각자 짐을 지고 장터를 떠났다. 서로의 마을로. 그날의 일은 그것뿐이었다.

chapters.heading: 스쳐 가는 사이
chapters.body:
그 뒤로도 두 사람은, 닷새마다 같은 장터에서 마주쳤다.

두 사람은 가까운 사이가 되지는 않았다. 서로의 마을이 멀었고, 장날 말고는 만날 일이 없었다. 두 사람을 묶는 무슨 끈이 있는 것도 아니었다. 핏줄도, 약속도, 운명 같은 것도 아니었다. 그저 — 닷새마다, 같은 장터에서 스치는 사이였다.

그래도 두 사람은, 장날이면 서로에게 작은 것을 건넸다.

$userName은 가끔 $celebName에게 약초를 한 줌 건넸다. '이건 속이 안 좋을 때 달여 드세요. 가마 불 앞에 오래 있으면 속이 마를 텐데, 도움이 될 겁니다.' $celebName은 가끔 $userName에게 작은 옹기 하나를 건넸다. '이건 약초 담아 두기 좋을 겁니다. 뚜껑이 잘 맞아서, 약초가 눅눅해지지 않습니다.'

두 사람은 장터에서 만나면, 짧게 인사하고 짧게 안부를 물었다.

'요새 산에 약초가 잘 납니까.' '이번엔 옹기가 잘 구워졌습니까.' '지난 장날 비 와서 고생하셨지요.'

그게 전부였다. 두 사람의 인연은 거기까지였다. 깊지도 않고, 운명적이지도 않은, 닷새마다 스쳐 가는 잔잔한 인연. 함께 먼 길을 가거나, 무슨 큰일을 같이 겪거나 한 적은 없었다. 그저 같은 장터에서, 가까운 자리에 좌판을 펴는 — 그뿐인 사이였다.

그런데 $userName은 그 인연이 좋았다. 거창하지 않아서 좋았다. 닷새마다 장터에 가면, 거기 아는 얼굴이 하나 있다는 것. 그 얼굴이 작은 안부를 물어 준다는 것. 장터의 수많은 사람들 사이에서, 한 사람만큼은 $userName을 알아보고 인사를 건넨다는 것. $userName에게는 그것만으로 충분했다.

$celebName도 그랬다. $celebName에게도 그 잔잔한 인연은, 장날을 조금 따뜻하게 해 주는 무엇이었다. 옹기를 지고 장터로 내려오는 길이, 그 인사 하나 때문에 덜 고단했다.

장터에서 두 사람은, 서로에게 거창한 무엇이 되려 하지 않았다. 그저 닷새마다, 작은 것을 주고받고, 짧은 안부를 묻는 — 그뿐인 사이로 오래 지냈다.

$userName은 가끔 생각했다. 우리를 이어 주는 게 뭘까, 하고. 핏줄도 아니고, 무슨 약속을 한 것도 아니고. 그저 같은 장터에 좌판을 편다는 것 말고는, 아무것도 없었다. 그런데도 두 사람은 닷새마다 작은 것을 주고받았다.

$userName은 알았다. 그건 운명이 묶어 준 게 아니었다. 그저 — 비 오던 날 한쪽이 먼저 옹기 조각을 주워 줬고, 한쪽이 먼저 주먹밥을 건넸을 뿐이었다. 그 작은 다정함 하나가, 닷새마다의 인연을 만든 것이었다. 거창한 끈이 없어도, 사람은 사람에게 그렇게 먼저 손을 내밀 수 있었다.

chapters.heading: 장이 파한 뒤
chapters.body:
여러 해가 흘렀다.

$userName은 나이가 들어, 더 이상 산에 약초를 캐러 다니지 못하게 됐다. 산길을 오르내리는 게 다리에 부쳤다. 그래서 장터에 나가는 일도 그만두어야 했다.

마지막 장날, $userName은 장터에 나가 $celebName을 찾았다. 옹기를 늘어놓은 그 익숙한 자리로.

'이번이 — 제 마지막 장입니다.' $userName이 말했다. '이제 산에 못 가서, 약초를 못 캡니다. 다리가 예전 같지 않습니다.'

$celebName이 잠깐 말이 없었다. 옹기를 만지던 손을 멈추고.

'그럼, 이제 장에서 못 뵙겠네요.'

'예. 그렇게 됐습니다.'

두 사람은 장터 처마 밑에 잠깐 앉았다. 오래전 비 오던 날, 함께 비를 피하던 그 처마 밑처럼.

'우리가.' $celebName이 말했다. '참 오래 봤습니다. 닷새마다, 여러 해를요. 그런데 생각해 보니 — 우리는 서로 사는 마을도 모릅니다. 이름 말고는 아는 게 별로 없습니다. 깊은 사연을 나눈 적도 없고요.'

$userName이 웃었다. '그러게요. 닷새마다 장에서만 봤으니까요. 장터 말고 다른 데서 만난 적이 한 번도 없네요.'

'그래도.' $celebName이 말했다. '저는 그 닷새마다의 만남이, 좋았습니다. 거창한 사이는 아니었지만 — 장에 나오면 아는 얼굴이 하나 있다는 게, 그게 좋았습니다. 옹기를 지고 내려오는 길이, 그 인사 하나 때문에 덜 고단했습니다.'

$userName도 고개를 끄덕였다. '저도 그랬습니다. 장터에 사람이 그렇게 많아도 — 저를 알아보고 안부를 물어 주는 사람은, 한 사람이면 충분하더군요.'

그날, 장이 파했다. $celebName이 $userName에게 작은 옹기 하나를 건넸다. 마지막으로. 손때가 묻지 않은, 새로 구운 옹기였다.

'이건, 그동안 고마웠다는 표시입니다.' $celebName이 말했다. '약초 담아 두시던 그 옹기, 이제 낡았을 테니까요.'

$userName도 $celebName에게 약초 한 줌을 건넸다. 마지막 짐에서 꺼낸 거였다. 어쩌면 $userName이 산에서 캔 마지막 약초.

'이건 — 가마 불 앞에서 속이 마를 때 달여 드세요.' $userName이 말했다.

두 사람은 그렇게, 장터에서 작별했다. 거창한 작별은 아니었다. 닷새마다 스쳐 가던 두 사람이, 마지막으로 작은 것을 주고받고 — 각자의 마을로 돌아갔을 뿐이다. 손을 잡지도, 긴 말을 나누지도 않았다.

그래도 $userName은, 그 잔잔한 인연을 오래 마음에 담았다. 집에 돌아와 그 옹기에 약초를 담을 때마다, $userName은 장터의 그 한 사람을 떠올렸다.

neutral.epilogue:
세상에는 거창하지 않은 인연이 있다.

핏줄로 묶인 것도, 운명이 맺어 준 것도 아닌 인연. 그저 — 같은 자리를 자주 스치다가, 한쪽이 먼저 작은 것을 건네면서 시작되는 인연.

$userName과 $celebName 사이에는, 둘을 단단히 묶는 무슨 끈이 없었다. 닷새마다 장터에서 스치던 두 사람을 이어 준 건, 운명이 아니라 — 비 오는 날 말없이 건넨 주먹밥 하나였다. 거창한 인연이 아니라, 그저 두 사람이 스스로 건넨 작은 다정함이었다.

$userName은 가끔, 그렇게 스쳐 가는 잔잔한 인연을 생각한다. $celebName을 보고 있으면 $userName은 그런 마음이 든다. 무슨 운명 같은 게 우리를 묶은 게 아니라 — 그저 내가 그 사람을 좋아하기로, 스스로 골랐을 뿐이라고.

어떤 인연은 운명이 맺어 주고, 어떤 인연은 그저 사람이 사람에게 먼저 손을 내밀어 시작된다. $userName에게 $celebName은 늘 후자다. 거창한 끈이 없어도 — 닷새마다 스치는 그 잔잔한 마주침이, 끝내 따뜻한 기억으로 남는 한 사람.

neutral.tone: 같은 시대를 산 두 사람 — 강한 살(煞)은 없음

paragraphs.gi: $era, $userName과 $celebName은 이웃한 두 마을에 각각 살았어요. $userName은 $userRole이었고, $celebName은 $celebRole이었어요.
paragraphs.seung: 두 사람의 사주를 나란히 놓아 봐도 합·충·형 같은 강한 인연의 매듭은 보이지 않았어요. 운명이 둘을 꼭 만나게 하려고 짜 둔 자리는 없었던 셈이에요.
paragraphs.jeon: 그래서 둘은 길에서 한두 번 마주쳤을 뿐, 깊은 사연을 쌓지 않았어요. 격정도 다툼도 없이, 같은 하늘 아래를 조용히 지나간 두 사람이었어요.
paragraphs.gyeol: 전생이 남긴 신호가 약한 만큼, 이번 생의 마음은 사주의 강요가 아니에요. $userName이 $celebName에게 가는 마음은 온전히 $userName의 선택이에요.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: 그래서 $celebName을 향한 덕질은 운명이 시킨 게 아니라 $userName이 스스로 키워 온 마음이에요.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: 그래서 $celebName을 좋아하는 마음은 사주가 정해 준 게 아니라 $userName이 직접 만든 거예요.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: 그래서 $celebName을 응원하는 건 정해진 인연이 아니라 $userName이 고른 진심이에요.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: 그래서 $celebName을 좋아하는 마음은 전생의 매듭이 아니라, 이번 생 $userName이 골라 낸 거예요.

neutral.title: 같은 버스를 타던 사람
neutral.logline: 매일 아침 같은 버스를 타던 두 사람 $userName과 $celebName. 운명이 묶지 않은, 스스로 건넨 잔잔한 인연.

chapters.heading: 7시 12분 버스
chapters.body:
2020년대. 어느 도시의 한 동네.

그 동네에는 7시 12분에 정류장에 서는 버스가 있었다. 도심으로 가는 버스. 출근 시간의 버스라, 늘 사람이 제법 탔다.

$userName은 매일 아침 그 버스를 탔다. 회사로 가는 버스였다. 늘 같은 시각, 같은 정류장. $userName은 버스 시간보다 조금 일찍 정류장에 나와, 책을 한 권 펴 들고 버스를 기다렸다.

$celebName도 매일 아침 그 버스를 탔다. 같은 정류장, 같은 시각. $celebName도 출근하는 길이었다.

두 사람은 매일 아침, 그 정류장에서 마주쳤다.

처음엔 서로 몰랐다. 그저 매일 같은 버스를 기다리는, 얼굴만 익은 사람들이었다. 도시의 정류장에는 그런 얼굴들이 많았다. 매일 보지만, 이름도 모르고 인사도 안 하는 얼굴들.

$userName은 그 사람을 알아봤다. 늘 같은 자리쯤에 서서 버스를 기다리는 사람. 늘 비슷한 가방을 멘 사람. 버스가 오면 늘 비슷한 자리에 앉는 사람.

$celebName도 $userName을 알아봤다. 늘 버스 시간보다 조금 일찍 와서 서 있는 사람. 늘 책을 한 권 들고 있는 사람. 버스를 기다리는 동안 그 책을 읽는 사람.

두 사람은 인사를 나누지 않았다. 도시의 아침 버스 정류장에서, 사람들은 보통 인사를 나누지 않으니까. 매일 보는 얼굴이라도, 서로 모르는 사이로 지내는 게 도시의 방식이었다. 도시의 아침은 다들 바빴다. 정류장에서, 버스 안에서, 사람들은 저마다 제 생각에 잠겨 있었다. 옆에 누가 서 있어도, 그 사람을 눈여겨보는 일은 드물었다.

그저 매일 아침, 두 사람은 같은 정류장에서 같은 버스를 — 함께 기다렸다. 말없이, 얼굴만 익은 채로. 두 사람을 묶는 무슨 끈이 있는 것도 아니었다. 핏줄도, 약속도, 무슨 깊은 사연도 없었다. 그저 같은 시각에 같은 버스를 탄다는, 그 우연한 겹침뿐이었다. 그 우연이 없었으면, 두 사람은 같은 도시에 살면서도 평생 서로를 모르고 지났을 것이다.

chapters.heading: 건넨 우산
chapters.body:
그러던 어느 날 아침, 비가 내렸다.

예보에 없던 비였다. 아침에 집을 나설 때만 해도 흐리기만 했는데, 정류장에 도착할 즈음 빗방울이 떨어지기 시작했다.

$celebName은 우산을 안 가지고 나왔다. 정류장 지붕 아래 서서, 버스를 기다리며 비가 그치기를 바랐다. 그런데 비는 점점 굵어졌다.

$userName은 우산이 있었다. 아침에 흐린 하늘을 보고, 혹시 몰라 챙겨 나온 우산.

버스가 왔다. 그런데 정류장 지붕 아래에서 버스 문까지, 잠깐 비를 맞아야 했다. 몇 걸음이지만, 굵은 비였다. $celebName이 망설였다. 비를 맞고 뛸까, 어쩔까.

$userName이 그 모습을 봤다.

$userName은 자기 우산을 펴고, 그 우산을 살짝 기울여 $celebName 쪽으로 받쳐 줬다. 함께 비를 피하며 버스에 오를 수 있도록.

$celebName이 $userName을 봤다. 잠깐 놀란 얼굴로. '…고맙습니다.'

두 사람은 한 우산을 쓰고, 짧게 비를 피하며 버스에 올랐다. 몇 걸음의 거리였다.

버스 안에서, $celebName이 다시 한번 인사했다. '아까, 정말 고마웠습니다. 우산을 안 가져와서요. 비 맞고 탈 뻔했습니다.'

'별거 아니에요.' $userName이 말했다. '저도 우산 깜빡할 때가 많아서요. 그럴 때 누가 우산 받쳐 주면, 그렇게 고맙더라고요.'

그날 두 사람은, 버스 안에서 처음으로 짧게 이야기를 나눴다. 길게는 아니었다. 그저 — 비가 갑자기 와서 당황했다는 이야기, 이 버스가 가끔 늦는다는 이야기, 요새 아침이 부쩍 쌀쌀해졌다는 이야기. 그런 짧은 이야기.

버스가 도심에 닿았다. 두 사람은 각자의 정류장에서 내렸다. 서로 다른 곳으로. 그날의 인연은 그것뿐이었다. 우산을 함께 쓴 몇 걸음, 버스 안의 짧은 이야기. 그게 전부였다.

chapters.heading: 아침의 짧은 인사
chapters.body:
그날 이후, 두 사람은 아침마다 가볍게 인사를 나눴다.

'안녕하세요.' '오늘은 버스가 제시간에 오네요.' '비 안 와서 다행이에요.'

그게 전부였다. 두 사람은 가까운 사이가 되지는 않았다.

서로의 이름을 알게 된 것도 한참 뒤였다. 그마저도 어쩌다 알게 된 거였다. 두 사람은 서로의 직업도, 사는 집도, 나이도 잘 몰랐다. 버스에서 내린 뒤 어디로 가는지도 몰랐다.

두 사람을 이어 주는 건, 그저 7시 12분 버스 하나였다. 같은 정류장에서, 같은 버스를 기다린다는 것. 그 우연한 겹침 말고는, 두 사람을 묶는 무슨 끈이 없었다. 핏줄도, 약속도, 무슨 깊은 사연도 없었다.

그래도 두 사람은, 아침마다 그 짧은 인사를 주고받았다.

$userName은 그 짧은 인사가 좋았다. 도시의 아침은 늘 바쁘고 차가웠다. 다들 모르는 얼굴들 사이를, 말 한마디 없이 지나갔다. 정류장에도, 버스 안에도, 회사 가는 길에도 — 아는 얼굴이 없었다. 그런데 그 정류장에는, 인사를 나누는 한 사람이 있었다. 아침마다 '안녕하세요'를 건네는 한 사람.

$userName에게는 그 한마디가, 차가운 도시의 아침을 조금 따뜻하게 해 줬다. 하루의 첫 인사가 그 한마디라는 게, $userName은 좋았다.

$celebName도 그랬다. $celebName에게도 그 짧은 아침 인사는, 하루를 가볍게 시작하게 해 주는 무엇이었다.

두 사람은 가끔, 작은 것을 주고받았다. $userName이 읽던 책이 재밌으면, '이거 괜찮았어요' 하고 제목을 알려 줬다. $celebName이 정류장 근처에 새로 생긴 가게를 알면, '저기 빵집 생겼던데요, 아침에 일찍 열어요' 하고 일러 줬다. 어느 추운 아침엔, $celebName이 따뜻한 음료를 하나 더 사 와 $userName에게 건네기도 했다. '하나 더 샀어요. 추워서요.'

그뿐이었다. 깊지도 않고, 거창하지도 않은, 매일 아침 스쳐 가는 잔잔한 인연. 두 사람은 서로에게 거창한 무엇이 되려 하지 않았다. 그저 아침마다, 짧은 인사를 나누는 그뿐인 사이로 오래 지냈다.

$userName은 가끔 생각했다. 우리를 이어 주는 게 뭘까, 하고. 핏줄도 아니고, 무슨 약속을 한 것도 아니고. 그저 같은 시각에 같은 버스를 탄다는 것 말고는, 아무것도 없었다. 그런데도 두 사람은 아침마다 인사를 나눴다.

$userName은 알았다. 그건 운명이 묶어 준 게 아니었다. 그저 — 비 오던 아침에 한쪽이 먼저 우산을 기울여 줬을 뿐이었다. 그 작은 다정함 하나가, 아침마다의 인연을 만든 것이었다. 거창한 끈이 없어도, 사람은 사람에게 그렇게 먼저 손을 내밀 수 있었다.

chapters.heading: 버스 노선이 바뀌고
chapters.body:
여러 해가 흘렀다.

어느 날, 그 버스의 노선이 바뀐다는 안내가 정류장에 붙었다. 7시 12분 버스가, 더 이상 그 정류장에 서지 않게 된 것이다. 노선이 다른 길로 옮겨 간다고 했다.

노선이 바뀌면, 두 사람은 더 이상 같은 정류장에서 만날 수 없었다. 두 사람을 이어 주던 그 우연한 겹침이, 사라지는 것이었다.

노선이 바뀌기 전, 마지막 날 아침. 두 사람은 그 정류장에서 마지막으로 만났다.

'…내일부터는, 이 정류장에 그 버스가 안 선대요.' $celebName이 말했다.

'저도 봤어요.' $userName이 말했다. '그럼, 이제 아침에 못 뵙겠네요.'

두 사람은 잠깐 말이 없었다. 정류장 지붕 아래에서, 마지막 버스를 기다리며.

'생각해 보니까.' $celebName이 말했다. '우리, 참 오래 봤어요. 여러 해를요. 그런데 — 저는 $userName 씨에 대해 아는 게 별로 없네요. 어디 사는지, 무슨 일을 하는지. 깊은 얘기를 나눈 적도 없고요.'

$userName이 웃었다. '그러게요. 매일 아침 이 정류장에서만 봤으니까요. 정류장 말고 다른 데서 만난 적이 한 번도 없네요.'

'그래도.' $celebName이 말했다. '저는 그 아침 인사가, 좋았어요. 거창한 사이는 아니었지만 — 아침에 누가 저한테 안녕하세요, 하고 인사해 주는 게. 그게 도시의 아침을 좀 견디게 해 줬어요. 하루의 첫 인사가 그 한마디라는 게요.'

$userName도 고개를 끄덕였다. '저도요. 저한테도 그 한마디가, 그랬어요. 사람 많은 도시에서 — 저를 알아보고 인사해 주는 사람은, 한 사람이면 충분하더라고요.'

그날 마지막 버스가 정류장에 섰다. 두 사람은 그 버스에 함께 올랐다. 마지막으로.

버스 안에서 두 사람은, 평소처럼 짧은 이야기를 나눴다. 날씨 이야기, 버스 이야기. 마지막이라고 특별한 말을 하지는 않았다.

버스가 도심에 닿았다. 두 사람은 각자의 정류장에서 내렸다. 마지막 인사를 나누고.

'그동안 — 고마웠어요.' $userName이 말했다.

'저도요. 우산 받쳐 주신 거, 두고두고 고마웠어요.' $celebName이 말했다.

거창한 작별은 아니었다. 매일 아침 스쳐 가던 두 사람이, 마지막으로 짧은 인사를 나누고 — 각자의 길로 갔을 뿐이다.

그래도 $userName은, 그 잔잔한 인연을 오래 마음에 담았다. 비 오는 아침이면, $userName은 가끔 그 정류장의 한 사람을 떠올렸다. 우산을 기울여 함께 비를 피하던, 그 짧은 몇 걸음의 거리를. 그리고 아침마다 '안녕하세요'를 건네던 그 목소리를.

neutral.epilogue:
비가 내릴 것 같은 아침이면, $userName은 가방에 우산을 하나 챙긴다.

까닭은 $userName도 잘 모른다. 일기예보가 흐림이라고만 해도, $userName의 손은 우산을 먼저 집는다. 그러고 정류장에 서면, $userName은 가끔 옆 사람을 살핀다. 혹시 우산 없이 나온 사람은 없는지.

$userName은 그게 어디서 온 버릇인지 모른다. 다만 — 누군가에게 우산을 살짝 기울여 줄 때, $userName의 마음은 이상하게 편안해진다. 아주 오래전, 그렇게 우산을 받아 본 적이 있는 사람처럼.

$celebName을 볼 때도 $userName은 그렇다. $celebName은 화면 너머에, 무대 위에 있다. $userName과 $celebName을 묶는 거창한 끈 같은 건 없다. 핏줄도, 약속도, 무슨 운명도. 그런데도 $userName은 $celebName에게 마음이 간다. $userName은 그게 운명이라고 생각하지 않는다. 그저 — 내가 그 사람을 좋아하기로, 스스로 골랐을 뿐이라고 생각한다.

어떤 인연은 운명이 맺어 주고, 어떤 인연은 그저 한 사람이 다른 사람에게 먼저 우산을 기울이면서 시작된다. $userName에게 $celebName은 늘 후자다. 거창한 끈이 없어도 — 비 오는 아침에 먼저 건넨 그 작은 다정함이, 끝내 마음에 남는 한 사람.

a jazz bar in 1960s New York

wonjin.celebRole: spy who'd been set to keep watch

paragraphs.gi: In a lamplit cafe at the end of $era, $celebName sat counting $userName's coins one by one. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole buried under debt papers, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: $celebName quietly copied $userName's expense ledger and passed it up the chain, and that single report could leave $userName pawning even the last silver spoon. Even so, $userName kept taking the same seat each day, just to watch those fingertips trace the ledger.
paragraphs.jeon: One rainy night $userName dropped a forged ledger on purpose, $celebName believed it and reported it, and ended up hunted by their own superiors instead. It had been done out of spite, yet seeing that fleeing back left a strange ache in $userName's heart.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of bond ties two names onto one line, nailing resentment and pull through the same point. So you might find that with $celebName your money slips away, yet a smile slips out too.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So this month again you may tap your card on $celebName's limited album, then tell yourself "this is the last one" — another small lie.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might buy the full set of merch from $celebName's drama and quietly fold the receipt away.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may pick up $celebName's match-day jersey, sigh at your bank balance, and yet already have the next ticket.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you might keep collecting $celebName's exhibition catalogues — grumbling and checking out at the same time.

wonjin.title: Sleeping With the Enemy, Gyeongseong
wonjin.logline: A creditor with the loan paper and the one who owes him come to live under one roof, and hate flips into obsession.

chapters.heading: The Loan Paper
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that man, there was a loan paper lying between them.

It was a rainy day. That autumn rain came often. In a back alley of Jongno stood the small print shop $userName's father had left behind — a cramped room with the smell of metal type soaked into its walls. Into that room stepped a single black umbrella.

The hand that folded it was pale and long. Shoes without a single drop of rain on them. The man stood at the threshold and let his eyes travel once around the room. They were not eyes that looked at people. They were eyes that priced things.

"$userName. That's the name."

He set a sheet of paper on the table. $userName looked at it. A promissory note, stamped with her father's seal. A faded number was written on it — a number the whole shop, sold whole, could never reach.

"This print shop is mine now."

$userName set down the rag in her hand, slowly. She raised her head and looked at the man.

It was $celebName. In all of Gyeongseong, anyone who moved money knew that name. It was a face that did not smile. And yet that blankness chilled a person more than any smile could. Under the brim of the hat, the eyes were cold as rainwater.

"My father died two months ago."

"Debt doesn't die," $celebName said. "A man can die. The paper stays alive."

$celebName looked around the shop. The worn type-trays, the oil-stained press, the stack of cheap paper in the corner. In his eyes there was only the cold of pricing. Even on her father's faded photograph hanging on one wall, those eyes paused once — and passed on, untouched.

"From today this place is mine, and you are a hand that comes with it," $celebName said, tapping the note. "Run, and the debt doubles. Leave Gyeongseong, same thing. Wherever in this land you go, this paper follows you."

$userName clenched her fist. Her nails dug into her palm. It hurt. And strangely, that pain made her mind clear.

"Why should I work under you. It's my father's debt, not mine."

"Because of this one sheet of paper." $celebName tapped the note again. The knuckles of his fingers were long. "If you don't like it, pay it now. Put down a single coin, if you can."

She could not pay. $userName knew it. $celebName knew it. So the words were not a question but a sentence — a sentence woven without a single gap to slip through.

"What's your name," $userName asked. To hate someone, she needed a name.

"What for."

"To hate you. Every day."

For the first time, just for a moment, $celebName looked straight at her — not with the eyes that priced things, but with eyes that saw a person. Then he went blank again.

"$celebName. Hate me all you like. Hate me or not, the debt stays the same."

The rain drummed the tin roof of the print shop. It was a rain that seemed it would never stop. That day $userName learned how to hate a man with her whole heart. That was the beginning — and back then she did not even know it was a beginning.

chapters.heading: Under One Roof
chapters.body:
$celebName moved his lodging into the empty room above the print shop.

There was hardly anything to call luggage. One leather bag, a few ledgers, and a black coat. That was all. For a man who moved money, he owned far too little. $userName thought, briefly, that it was strange — and then forgot it.

"Easier to watch you," $celebName said. "So I'll know the moment you slip out even a single grain of type."

So $userName came to live under one roof with that man. The shop below, his room above, and beside it the tiny back room that was $userName's place. The wall was thin. At night she could hear $celebName turning over, hear him turning ledger pages late into the dark. And sometimes, in the dead of night, she heard him let out one long sigh.

$userName made up her mind to hate that man. Every day, faithfully, she hated him.

When he said "coffee" in the morning, short, $userName deliberately set down cold water. $celebName drank it without a word. Not a muscle in his face moved. That galled her more.

"Why don't you get angry," $userName finally asked one day. "I gave you cold water. It's cold."

$celebName lifted his eyes from the ledger.

"If you didn't want to give me cold water, you wouldn't have. But you did. And to give it means — you still mind me at all."

$userName had no answer. The hand holding the rag stopped, foolishly.

"Hating is also a kind of minding, $userName," $celebName said, lowering his eyes to the ledger again. "Indifference won't even fetch cold water. It forgets. You don't forget me each morning — you make a point of bringing me cold water. What that means, you don't know yet."

That night $userName lay glaring at the ceiling of her back room, unable to sleep. Because those words kept coming back. She did not want to admit it — that she spent the whole day thinking about that man. Under the excuse of hate.

$celebName was a strange man. A moneylender, yet he covered the print workers' overdue wages out of his own pocket first. On rainy days he left an umbrella standing by the door so $userName could not go out without one. When $userName sorted type late into the night, he came down from upstairs, trimmed the lamp wick without a word, and went back up. And through all of it, he never once said a kind thing.

"Don't pretend to be kind," $userName snapped. "Why leave out an umbrella. Does a moneylender mind whether his debtor catches a cold?"

"If you catch cold you can't work. A sick hand is a loss to me. That's all."

$celebName spoke of kindness as if it were profit and loss. Always. That tone grated on $userName more and more. If only he were truly a cold man, he would be easy to hate. But this man kept showing gaps that made him hard to hate. And then, the moment $userName reached a hand toward that gap — he shut the door of his blank face again.

Hate was a strange feeling. Looked at every day, something else grew inside it. $userName felt she knew what it was, and so she hated him harder — trying to press the label of hate, by force, over that other thing.

chapters.heading: Secrets Between the Type
chapters.body:
It was around then that strange orders began coming into the shop.

People came in the middle of the night. Men with their hats pulled low. They spoke only with $celebName, and $userName was sent to the back room. The next day $celebName set the type himself. $userName never saw what was printed. The moment it came off the press, $celebName carried it away.

$userName grew suspicious. Counterfeit money, perhaps. Or some illegal document. A moneylender would do such things. Thinking so gave her hate a justification, and her heart was almost easier for it. Yes — that man is wicked. So it is allowed to hate him.

That night $userName picked up a sheet of paper that had fallen beside the press. One sheet $celebName had failed to gather.

It was a manifesto. Words crying out for independence. Words for which a person caught by the Japanese police could die on the spot and no one would be surprised. In every single letter of type, someone's life was set.

$userName's hands shook. Then a voice came from behind her.

"...So you saw."

$celebName stood at the doorway. For the first time, his blankness had cracked. It was fear. Not fear for his own life — fear at the sight of $userName.

"This..." $userName held up the paper. Her hands kept shaking. "Why would a moneylender print something like this."

"Because a moneylender can," $celebName said, closing the door. His voice was low. "A man who moves money can come and go anywhere. Police stations. The houses of colonial officials. Without suspicion. A loan paper is the finest travel pass. This print shop — I never wanted it for a promissory note."

$celebName took one step closer. $userName did not step back.

"Your father knew. He took on the debt knowing. So this shop could be used by us — a place with type, a place no one suspects. The note was a disguise, $userName. That paper that bound you — it was your father's last will to his comrades. A plea to protect this print shop."

$userName nearly sank to the floor. She braced a hand on the shop pillar. Her father. That small, quiet man who all his life smelled only of type. That back forever bent over something late into the night.

"Why didn't you tell me," $userName's voice cracked.

"Because knowing is dangerous. Because not knowing keeps you safe." $celebName took one more step toward her. For the first time, the distance between them was a single span. "Working you like a hired hand, being cold to you — only if you hated me would you stay away. And staying away is staying safe. If feeling grows between us, you become the one in danger."

"...So you let yourself be hated on purpose. For me?"

"As a policeman points a gun at a dangerous man, I pointed hate at you. That was my way of protecting you."

$userName heard that, and for the first time understood. Every moment she had hated this man was, in truth, a safe distance this man had built for her. The cold water, the cold words, the shut door of his blank face — all of it. Hate was the kindest lie $celebName had ever given $userName.

"...You wicked man," $userName said. Her voice was wet. "You'd be better off truly wicked. To make a person — unable even to hate you."

"Yes," $celebName said, with that fear-worn face. "So keep hating me. It's better. I'm begging you, $userName. Hate me."

chapters.heading: The Debt Doubled
chapters.body:
But hate was already too late.

After that day $userName could no longer hate $celebName. The harder she tried, the more she pictured the man setting type at the risk of his life every night. She remembered the look on his face as he drank cold water without a word — that, in truth, it was the face of a man hoping she would hate him. That being hated was that man's tenderness.

$userName helped with the shop work. In the dead of night. She would not listen when $celebName told her to sleep. She sat down at his side by the type-box and picked the letters of the manifesto, one by one.

"Go to your room, $userName. This is dangerous work."

"If you get hurt the debt doubles," $userName said, mimicking his tone. In a voice like cold water. "A comrade short is a loss to me. So help. If you don't want a loss."

$celebName looked at her for a moment. That blankness collapsed once more. This time it was not fear but something else. $userName knew what it was. It was the very same thing that had grown inside her.

All that winter the two of them set type under the same lamp. $celebName worked without noticing his own hands were cut, and $userName, without a word, wound cotton cloth around the cuts. Never once saying "that must hurt." The way of hiding her heart behind the words profit and loss — $userName, too, had somehow learned it from him.

One night $celebName set down the type. Only the turning of the press filled the room.

"$userName," he said. "When this work is done, I'll burn the note. You're free. You can go anywhere. Somewhere with no one to hate."

"I'm not going."

"Why."

$userName did not answer. Instead she looked at the man's hand. The pale, long hand wound in cotton, smeared all over with type-oil. The hand that, the first day he stepped into the shop, had not a drop of rain on it. How many people's lives that hand had set onto paper.

"You tried so hard to make me hate you," $userName said. "It didn't work at all. I — lost the moment you first drank that cold water. The moment you swallowed it all without getting angry."

$celebName held his breath. The lamp wick made a small crackling sound.

"This isn't allowed, $userName," he managed. "I'm a man who might die tomorrow. If you give your heart, you'll hurt."

"Then today you're alive. Tomorrow's hurt, let tomorrow's me bear it."

$userName took his hand. The oil-smeared, cloth-wound hand. $celebName could not pull it free. The man who pretended to be cold was, for the first time, trembling. In $userName's hand.

"You told me to hate you," $userName laughed. It was a laugh like crying. "You said hating is also minding. I minded you so much it stopped being hate. The cold water I brought every day — it was waiting for you every day. Take responsibility. You're the one who started this."

That night, the type of the print shop set, instead of a manifesto, the promise of two people. A promise written on no paper. That when this work was done, with no debt and no disguise, they would meet simply as two people. With no pretending to hate.

chapters.heading: The Last Printing
chapters.body:
That promise did not last past spring.

Someone informed. One dawn, the alley of the print shop filled with the boot-steps of the Japanese police. Click, click, cutting through the rain. $celebName heard the sound first of all.

"$userName. Get up."

He shook her awake. For the first time, his voice was urgent. Not a single shard of the mask of blankness remained.

"Go out the back door. Under the type-tray there's a roster of comrades. Take only that and leave. You are nothing but a print hand. Say you were dragged in over a debt and only worked — and you'll live. Understand? You're a hand who knew nothing."

"Come with me."

"My staying is what buys the time." $celebName pressed the roster into her hand. Then he drew the promissory note from his coat. The paper that had bound $userName. He carried it to the lamp of the press.

The paper burned. The debt that had tied $userName scattered into a handful of ash. With the note gone, the reason that had bound the two of them was gone too — so that $userName could be free.

"No debt now. You're free," $celebName said. His blankness collapsed for the last time. This time it stayed collapsed to the end. "$userName. To make you hate me, I worked a mind I'd never once worked in my life. And not a single day did I succeed. You never once truly hated me."

"...You knew? That I was only pretending to hate."

"A man who sets type can read false letters." He smiled faintly. In the rain. "Your hate was all false letters. The cold water, the cold words. Every day I read those false letters, and in truth — I was happy."

The boot-steps reached the door of the print shop.

"Go, $userName. Don't look back. Please."

$userName went. And — she looked back. In the end she could not keep the promise. $celebName stood before the press. He was setting one last line of type. What he was printing, $userName did not see. The rain blurred her eyes.

That day, near Gyeongseong Station, $celebName drew the police toward himself. The time for his comrades to escape, he bought with one man alone.

Later, a comrade who survived handed $userName a single sheet of paper. The one and only sheet last printed in that shop. On it was neither manifesto nor roster, but a few short letters of type.

"To $userName. Next life let us meet with no debt and no disguise. I won't be cold from the start. Instead of cold water I'll take warm coffee. So that time — don't even pretend to hate. Take my hand at once. Not too late."

$userName carried that paper close to her all her life. Until the smell of type had faded entirely. Never forgetting, not for a single day, the man who had taught her how to pretend to hate.

wonjin.epilogue:
And now, on a rainy day in some alley, $userName sees a black umbrella.

A pale hand folding it. Shoes without a single drop of rain. She does not know the name, yet her heart hates first. It hates, and her heart knows better still that it is not hate. The way it was a hundred years ago.

'...Ah, it's you again. You'll be cold to me again, won't you. You'll say something like cold water again.'

But the $celebName of this life is not cold. He sees $userName, and from the very start tilts the umbrella toward her. With no disguise and no profit and loss. With no tender lie called hate.

This time $userName does not pretend to hate. The thing she could not do a hundred years ago, she does at once. She takes that pale hand first. The hand with no debt and no loan paper. Not too late.

And $celebName, at last, is relieved. With the face of a man who no longer has to act out hate. With the face of a man who may hand over warm coffee instead of cold water.

wonjin.celebRole: head collector come to gather a debt

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName came by each month to stamp a red seal on $userName's doorstep. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who couldn't collect on medicine, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: With that uneasy bond between them, the mere sound of $celebName's seal could leave $userName furious yet unable to step away. Out of spite, $userName would brew the bitterest herbs for $celebName's share of the tonic.
paragraphs.jeon: One winter $celebName drank that bitter tonic down without a word, then unwound their own scarf and left it for the coughing $userName. A debt collector had come, yet a piece of warmth was given instead, so $userName stood by the door a long while.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of bond can carry a crossed resentment into this life like a scar. So you might keep spending on $celebName and, oddly, never quite feel the anger.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So when $celebName drops a new song, you may buy the track and the album both, then laugh and ask yourself "why again?"
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So when $celebName's drama ends, you might check out the OST and the script book, then stare blankly at your wallet.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may enter the draw for $celebName's signed ball again, and if you miss, feel sore yet already eye the next.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you might buy every book $celebName recommends and, before reading one, add another to the cart.

wonjin.title: What You Tore Down
wonjin.logline: Set on revenge against the man who tore down a family, at the end of all that hate the truth is finally faced.

chapters.heading: A Blade Honed Ten Years
chapters.body:
$userName hated one person for ten years.

If hate had a posture, $userName's posture was perfect. Every morning, looking in the mirror, the name came up. $celebName. Thinking of that name made the pit of the stomach burn hot, and that heat let $userName live one more day. Hate was fuel. Fuel that must not be allowed to cool.

Ten years ago, the company $userName's father ran collapsed in a single night. Deals cut off, the cash line choked, ugly rumors running through Gangnam. At the center of all of it was one name — the young head of a new investment firm. $celebName.

$userName's father could not bear the shock. The winter the company fell, the father fell too. At seventeen, $userName lost everything. Home, school, friends, future. That winter when creditors pounded on the door, $userName never forgot for the rest of life.

That day $userName resolved: someday to stand before that man again. To return to that man exactly what that man had torn down. In a single night. The very same way.

It took ten years.

$userName changed names. Cleaned up the family register, forged a resume. Finished school at night, studied finance alone, learned how to handle people, learned how to hide hate under a smile. In front of the mirror, smiled hundreds of times. How to lift only the corners of the mouth while the stomach burned. And at last, $userName entered $celebName's investment firm as a secretary.

The eighteenth floor of a glass tower in Gangnam. In 1988, Gangnam was a place where new towers rose every day. $userName stood before the door of that man's office. The document file in hand trembled, faintly. A blade honed ten years was about to leave its sheath.

The door opened.

$celebName stood by the window. With the night view of Seoul at his back. A face a little harder than the newspaper photo seen ten years ago. The gaze was deep. He turned around slowly.

"The new secretary."

"Yes. I look forward to working with you." $userName smiled. A perfect smile. A smile practiced ten years. The stomach boiling, only the corners of the mouth soft.

$celebName looked at $userName for a moment. That gaze was cool. As if it had recognized the weight of a blade. As if it had seen the thing boiling under that smile. A chill ran up $userName's spine. But $celebName said nothing.

"I look forward to it."

The door shut. $userName stood in the corridor catching breath. Sweat had soaked the palms. It was the beginning. The very beginning of learning every weakness of this man, and tearing down everything this man had.

$userName called the name once more, inside. $celebName. Ten years of waiting. Now it was time to pay it back.

chapters.heading: In Search of a Weakness
chapters.body:
$userName was a good secretary. On purpose.

Only a perfect secretary could get into that man's deepest places. $userName memorized $celebName's schedule, organized his contracts, remembered even the strength of the coffee he drank. Weak coffee. No sugar. One cup at three in the afternoon. And all the while, searched for a weakness. Slush funds, illegal deals, hidden shame — anything. A single point to drive the blade-tip into.

But it was strange.

The more $userName searched, the less weakness appeared. $celebName was clean. Almost to the point of obsession. His contracts were honest, his employees' wages were the highest in the field, and the companies he invested in did not fail. They revived, rather. A small factory on the verge of bankruptcy, after $celebName's investment, sent smoke up its chimney again.

"How strange," $userName said to no one, in the late-night office. "How did a man this clean tear down someone else's company. How did he tear down my father."

$userName gripped the posture of hate harder. Pretending to be clean is the more cunning thing. Ten years ago, when he tore down my father, surely he smiled with a face just as clean. Only thinking so let $userName lift the blade.

$celebName was curt with $userName. Not one private word. Yet he silently ordered late-night meals for $userName working overtime, and on rainy days had the company car take $userName home. Never once saying "you've worked hard."

"Why are you good to me," $userName asked one day. Lightly, so the hate would not show.

"I'm not being good to you," $celebName said, not lifting his eyes from the documents. "If a secretary collapses, work stops. It's a matter of efficiency. That's all."

He always wrapped his caring in the word efficiency. It grated on $userName. The $celebName drawn for ten years was a villain, but the $celebName seen every day kept stepping out of that picture. The mismatch made $userName uneasy. To lift the blade, $celebName had to be a villain.

Then one late night, sorting that man's desk drawer, $userName found an old photograph. $celebName when young, standing beside a middle-aged man. On a fine sunny day, the two of them stood with arms over each other's shoulders, laughing brightly.

$userName looked at the middle-aged man's face. And — breath stopped.

It was $userName's father. From the days before the company fell, when the father still knew how to laugh.

$celebName and $userName's father stood with arms over each other's shoulders, like brothers. On the back of the photo there was faded writing. 'To the first person who believed in me.'

chapters.heading: The Truth in One Photograph
chapters.body:
$userName held that photograph and, for the first time, asked a question off the plan.

"This photo. Who is this."

$celebName lifted his eyes from the documents. That cool face slowly stiffened at the sight of the photo. For the first time, $userName saw that man's blankness collapse. The face beneath the mask.

"...Why did you see that."

"It was in the drawer. While I was sorting it. Who is it."

$celebName was silent a long time. The Gangnam night view outside the window dyed half his face dark. Tower lights gathered, brimming, in his eyes.

"He's the one who saved me," he said at last. "When I came up to Gangnam empty-handed, when no one believed in me — he was the first to put money behind me. When everyone scoffed, asking what you'd see in a kid like me to invest, he alone took my hand. He's the man who made me."

The photograph trembled in $userName's hand.

"His company... fell ten years ago," $celebName went on. His voice was low. "What tore it down was his business partner. From inside. Skimming funds, leaking false rumors to clients, forging promissory notes. Slowly, unnoticed, from inside the company. I learned it too late. I poured all my own money in to stop it — but it was too late. He was already falling."

$userName's mind went white. Ten years. The direction $userName had hated, for ten years.

"The world said I tore that company down," $celebName said, smiling as if at himself. "Because in the end I bought up that company's assets. People saw only that. A young investment-firm head swallowing an old company whole. Why I bought it — no one asked. Not a single person."

"...Why did you buy it."

"He had a child," $celebName said. "Seventeen years old. So the debt would not pass to that child. If I took on the company's assets, I took on the debt too. So that child, even empty-handed, would at least not be buried under a mountain of debt. Would not be thrown out onto the street."

$userName nearly sank to the floor. Braced a hand on the desk corner. Ten years ago, that winter when creditors pounded on the door. And then, at some point, the pounding stopped. $userName had never once wondered why. Had thought only that luck had been kind.

The name of that luck stood before her now.

"That child," $userName managed. "Do you know where they are now."

$celebName looked at $userName. Long, and deep. That gaze said everything.

The blade called hate broke in $userName's hand. The broken blade cut $userName deeply.

chapters.heading: When the Honed Blade Snaps
chapters.body:
$userName wrote a resignation letter that night.

Where a blade honed ten years had snapped, there was only an unbearable shame. $userName wanted to run. Before $celebName learned who $userName was. Before that man learned that the child of his benefactor had walked in carrying a blade.

But $celebName already knew.

When $userName set the letter on the desk, $celebName, without even looking at it, said:

"That you're his child — I knew from the interview."

$userName froze. To the fingertips.

"The eyes are alike. The same as the look he wore when he was angry. Exactly the same." $celebName looked at $userName. "The resume you forged, the name you changed — I knew all of it. Knowing everything before letting someone in is my work. I knew why you came into my firm too. You came to tear me down. Carrying a blade honed ten years."

"...Then why did you take me in."

"Because the debt I owe him, I wanted to repay even to his child," $celebName said. "While you searched my office for a weakness, I watched you. When you worked overtime I ordered meals; when it rained I had you driven home. That was — what I could do. Though you took all of it as hate."

$userName wept. For the first time in ten years, wept over something that was not hate. The heat in the stomach became, for the first time, a different temperature.

"Why didn't you tell me sooner. Then for ten years, the wrong person—"

"Would you have believed it?" $celebName asked. Quietly. "To a child who lost everything at seventeen, if I'd said the one who saved you was actually me — would you have believed it? No. You needed the hate, $userName. Hate can keep a person alive too. You held on for ten years by hating me. That hate made you finish school, made you learn finance, brought you here. That hate saved you."

$celebName took one step from the window.

"I had no right to take that hate away. It was the pillar holding you up. Until you could set the blade down yourself — I had no choice but to wait. So I kept you near. You, carrying a blade, every day."

$userName's knees buckled under the weight of those words. This man, knowing from the start of the blade aimed at him, had waited ten years. So that $userName could draw that blade back alone. Ordering late-night meals every day for the person with a blade at his back.

"I'm sorry I hated you," $userName managed.

"Thank you for hating me," $celebName said. "Thanks to it you survived. You were not thrown out onto the street. That's enough. His child is alive, standing before me. I — that one thing is enough."

Where the broken blade had been, something else was sprouting. $userName knew what it was, and yet could not name it. Not yet. To a hand that had held a blade, holding something else was — still too soon.

chapters.heading: The Distance of Ten Floors
chapters.body:
$userName did not withdraw the resignation.

"I don't think I should stay here," $userName said. "I came to hate you, and now I can't hate you. Then I — have no right to be at your side. With a hand that held a blade, how could I stand beside you."

$celebName took the letter. He did not stop her. He only looked at $userName a long time.

$userName left the firm. Stepping out of the glass tower in Gangnam, she looked up one last time at the eighteenth floor. A black silhouette stood at the window. The way it had the first time $userName opened that door.

$userName started over. Not with a forged name, but with the real name her father had given. Joined a small company, worked honestly. For a while, did not know what to fill the place where hate had left. The mornings when the pit of the stomach no longer burned were, at first, strange.

A year passed. The small company $userName worked at received a large investment. A failing company revived. The employees cheered. Whose investment it was, was never made public. But $userName recognized the handwriting on that investment contract. The handwriting organized every day for ten years. The handwriting of the hand that drank weak coffee.

$userName went to the glass tower in Gangnam. Stood before the door of the eighteenth floor. Instead of a blade honed ten years, empty-handed.

The door opened. $celebName stood by the window. Exactly as a year ago. He turned around.

"Why did you help again," $userName asked. The voice trembled. "The debt to my father, you've repaid it all now. I wasn't thrown onto the street, I'm living well. There's nothing more to repay."

"This time it wasn't about debt," $celebName said. For the first time, he did not disguise tenderness as efficiency. Did not wear the mask of blankness. "I wanted to see you living well. Wanted to see your company send smoke up its chimney. That's all. This time it's neither efficiency nor debt. Just — because it's you."

Before those words, $userName wore a face never once worn in ten years. An unpracticed smile. A real smile, not made in front of a mirror. A smile with no heat in the stomach.

"To the place I came to hate," $userName said, "I've brought something else now. Not a blade. Will you take it?"

$celebName did not answer. Instead he took one step from the window. The distance ten years of hate had made, he slowly closed. One step, and another.

"Next life," $celebName said, stopping before $userName, "let's not put a debt between us. No ten-year detour. On the first day we meet, hold out a hand instead of a blade."

"All right," $userName said. "And that time, when the pit of my stomach burns, I'll know from the start that it isn't hate."

And $userName took $celebName's hand. The empty hand that held no blade. For the first time in ten years, a hand holding something that was not hate.

wonjin.epilogue:
And now, in some building lobby, $userName sees a person.

The name unknown, yet the pit of the stomach burns first. It feels like hate. A person it would seem about right to hate for ten years or so. But the closer $userName comes, the more the heart knows first that the heat is not hate. The way it was in that tower long ago.

'...Ah, it's you again. I was about to go searching for a reason to hate you again.'

So this time, $userName does not hone a blade. Does not wander in search of a reason to hate. Does not practice a false smile in front of a mirror. Just stands before that person, empty-handed.

$celebName sees $userName. This time, without disguising goodwill as efficiency, smiles from the start. Two people long out of step at last meet without rounding the far road of hate — they simply face each other. The stomach burns, but this time both of them know what it is.

wonjin.celebRole: private detective who'd been put on the trail

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName watched only $userName's hands through the window each day with an old spyglass. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole living on credit, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: Held by that uneasy bond, $celebName trailed $userName to make the catch yet stayed tied to the sound of that tuning. Stung by being followed, $userName would set the notes a half-step off on purpose and slip away in the night.
paragraphs.jeon: One dawn $celebName sat at that mistuned piano, set every note right again, and wrote only "not found" in the report. The pursuer had covered the escape instead, so $userName smiled from afar toward that spyglass.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of bond can leave a resented chase tucked into a corner of this life's heart. So you might keep following whatever $celebName does, even at a loss.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So when $celebName's concert tickets open, you may say "not going this time," yet your hand reaches the dawn queue first.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might swear off $celebName's work, then end up binging it from episode one.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may vow to stop watching $celebName's matches, yet tune in the moment the broadcast starts.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you might decide not to tap $celebName's new video, yet your finger moves first when the alert pops up.

wonjin.title: The Lover at the Other Sword
wonjin.logline: An orthodox blade and an unorthodox blade aim at each other all their lives, but the closer the tips draw the more hate becomes another name.

chapters.heading: A First Clash Crossed by Fate
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that sword, it was at the dueling tournament, at the age of fifteen.

The Ming-dynasty rivers and lakes. Anyone who carried a sword passed through the dueling tournament. A spring event where the schools of all under heaven gathered in one place and set their young disciples to cross blades.

$userName was a disciple of an orthodox great house. A straight, solid sword. The master always said, "Our sword is not for cutting but for protecting. When the sword is straight, so is the heart." $userName carved those words into the chest. Every dawn, swung the straight sword a thousand times. Straight, and straighter.

That day, across the dueling platform, a youth stepped up. Black martial robes. Tangled hair. A crooked smile at the corner of the mouth. From that youth's sword the straightness of the orthodox could not be felt. A sword that curved, twisted, gave no clue where it would turn. An unorthodox sword.

That was $celebName.

"Young master of the orthodox," $celebName said, sword slung crosswise over the shoulder. "With a sword that's only ever straight, could you block my curved one? A straight thing — breaks easily."

$userName did not answer. Recalling the master's words, raised the sword. Straight.

The first clash struck. The ring of metal rang through the platform. And $userName knew — this youth's sword knew $userName's sword exactly. As if it had studied $userName's sword alone all its life. Wherever $userName meant to cut, $celebName's sword had already arrived first. The places $userName meant to block, $celebName's sword skirted, as if mocking.

On the seventeenth clash $userName's sword was knocked from the hand. It stuck into the dirt.

$celebName's sword-tip stopped before $userName's throat. An inch away. And it did not cut. $celebName looked down at $userName a long while. That crooked smile shifted, for a moment, into another expression. $userName could not read it. It vanished too quickly.

"...Boring," $celebName said, withdrawing the sword. "Too straight. Almost a pity to break. A straight sword — once seen, can't be forgotten."

$celebName turned away. $userName, kneeling in the dirt, glared at that black back. Pulling the sword from the earth again with a dirt-smeared hand.

"Your name," $userName demanded. "What is your name."

"$celebName," the youth said over the shoulder. "Remember it, young master of the orthodox. Next time we meet — I won't go easy. So until then, don't break. Stay alive."

That day $userName resolved to hate one person for life. The sword that beat $userName. That arrogant sword that had spared $userName. The curved sword that had called a straight one unforgettable. That was the beginning.

chapters.heading: A Decade of Pursuit
chapters.body:
For the ten years after, the rivers and lakes spoke of two swords.

The straight sword of the orthodox and the curved sword of the unorthodox. People always named the two swords together. Speak of one and the other followed without fail. The two swords met everywhere in the rivers and lakes. On escort roads, in the cramped yards of inns, on the stone steps of mountain temples, in the river-fog at the ferry crossings.

Each time they met they crossed blades, and each time no victor was decided. Since that first clash at fifteen, the two swords never once settled it.

$userName hated $celebName. Every day, faithfully.

Studied $celebName's swordwork. Memorized every change of that curved sword. Before sleeping, ground teeth picturing that crooked smile. Next time, surely, to win. With a straight sword, to break that curved one.

But it was strange. The more $userName studied $celebName, the better $userName knew $celebName. At which moment that sword hesitated. At which motion the left shoulder trembled faintly. That the tremble came from an old scar. And further — that on rainy days the sword grew half a beat heavier.

$userName knew $celebName better than anyone in the rivers and lakes. Told himself it was because of hate. To win, one must know the enemy. Hate is what makes you look deep into an enemy.

One winter, on a snow-covered mountain path, they met again. The two swords clashed. But $celebName's sword was unlike usual. Half a beat slow. The left shoulder trembling. $userName caught it at once. It was the sword studied for ten years.

$userName withdrew the sword.

"You're hurt," $userName said.

$celebName paused. Through the falling snow, he looked at $userName. "The young master of the orthodox minds even an enemy's wound. How rare."

"To cut a wounded enemy is not the orthodox sword," $userName said, sheathing the blade. "When you're healed, come again. A win means something only when won against a sound foe. Breaking a hurt sword is — not winning, it's cowardice."

$celebName looked at $userName a long while. That crooked smile shifted again, to another expression. The expression from the dueling platform at fifteen, the one $userName never read.

"...An enemy like you is the first I've met," $celebName said. Snow gathered on his shoulders. "You keep making yourself hard to hate."

$userName turned away. That night $userName chewed over the words spoken. A win means something only against a sound foe. That was not a thing said to an enemy. Only then did $userName dimly understand — that what he wished was for $celebName to be sound. To be alive, the next time, and the time after. So they could meet again.

What to call the heart that wishes a hated person alive, $userName did not know.

chapters.heading: The Shadow of the Same Master
chapters.body:
$userName came to know $celebName's truth at a ruined temple.

On a night when the summer monsoon poured down, the two of them took shelter in the same ruined temple. Beneath a fallen statue, under the eaves, they sat side by side. To sit facing each other without crossing blades — it was the first time in ten years.

The sound of rain filled the temple. For a while neither spoke.

"$userName," $celebName opened first. Watching the rain. "Do you know who my teacher was."

$userName shook the head.

"He was the same person as your teacher."

The scabbard slipped from $userName's hand. It clanged on the stone floor.

"Our teacher's teacher took two disciples in his youth," $celebName said. "To one he taught a straight sword, to the other a curved sword. The same master, the same sword-principle, a different shape. But the world could not bear it. The straight sword made a name in the orthodox; the curved sword — was driven to the unorthodox. For the single reason that it curved. The rivers and lakes split the two apart that way."

$celebName held a hand out into the rain. Rainwater pooled in his palm.

"The disciple who learned the curved sword is the teacher of my teacher. So you and I are — two branches of one sword, $userName. Two stems split from one root. The names orthodox and unorthodox are false names the rivers and lakes pinned on later."

$userName could not breathe. The sword hated ten years was, in truth, the same bloodline as $userName's own. A single root, split.

"Why didn't you tell me sooner," $userName asked. The voice was buried in the rain.

"Because if I told you, you'd waver," $celebName said. "You're a straight sword. Only with a clear enemy can you stand straight. A straight sword that wavers breaks. I thought my staying your enemy — was what kept you straight."

"...You stayed my enemy for me?"

"If the rivers and lakes see you as kin of the unorthodox, you're finished. Cast out of your orthodox great house, never to lift the straight sword again. So we had to stay enemies. My roaming the rivers and lakes to cut you down — was keeping you safe inside the orthodox, $userName. For ten years, pretending to chase you, I protected you."

Before those words, $userName heard the ten years of hate crumble down. Louder than the rain. And from that crumbled place, something else rose, straight as a sword. $userName could not bring it to the lips. It was a feeling that those who aim swords cannot hold. A feeling the orthodox and the unorthodox cannot hold.

"When the rain stops," $userName managed. "Must we cross blades again."

"We must," $celebName said. Smiling crookedly, but sadly. "The rivers and lakes are watching. Only inside this ruined temple — can we be brothers."

chapters.heading: The Tremble at the Sword-tip
chapters.body:
It was around then that the balance of the rivers and lakes began to break.

A great school sought to swallow both the orthodox and the unorthodox. They broke the swords of the rivers and lakes one by one. And first they targeted $celebName. Remove the unorthodox sword, and pressing the orthodox grows easy. Break one of the two swords first, and the other can soon be broken too.

$userName heard the news. At an inn, through the mouth of a wandering martial artist. The news that $celebName was cornered at a cliff's edge.

$userName took up the sword and ran. Through the whole night. A straight sword, running toward a curved one.

$celebName stood surrounded at the cliff edge. Encircled by dozens of blades. Holding out with a single curved sword. Robes torn, the left shoulder trembling. The old scar — the weakness only $userName knew. And it was raining. The weather that made $celebName's sword heaviest.

$userName leapt through the encircling line. Stood with the straight sword beside the curved one. As if back to back.

"Why is the young master of the orthodox here," $celebName said, breathing rough. "If the rivers and lakes see, you'll be cast out of the orthodox."

"You said a win means something only against a sound foe," $userName said, raising the sword. Not at $celebName, but at those who surrounded him. "You aren't sound now. Your shoulder trembles, and it's raining. So — I'll win later. If I lose you now, I can never win. Never meet you again."

$celebName looked at $userName. In the rain, the crooked smile finally collapsed. Not even the sad smile of the ruined temple, but a bare face collapsed to the end.

"$userName," he said. "You're a straight sword, so you can't lie. Those words just now — they aren't about wanting to win, are they. A straight sword speaks only a straight heart."

"...No," $userName admitted. Sword still raised. Rainwater ran down the face. "It's that I don't want to lose you. I hated you ten years, and not for a single day did I truly hate you. I wished you alive every day. If you were hurt, that you'd heal; if it rained, that your sword would not be heavy. I thought that was hate. But it was not hate. Not once."

The two swords, for the first time, aimed in the same direction. The straight sword and the curved sword, side by side. Two stems split from one root, back into one line.

"Then trust me your back," $celebName said, leaning his back against $userName's. Resting that trembling shoulder against $userName's back. "Though there's no young master of the orthodox who trusts an enemy his back."

"You're not an enemy," $userName said. "Never once were. Whatever the rivers and lakes call you."

chapters.heading: Two Swords Become One Line
chapters.body:
The two swords fought back to back.

Where the straight sword could not cut, the curved sword filled the angle; where the curved sword could not reach, the straight sword held the ground. The two swords that had aimed at each other ten years knew each other better than anyone. That knowing was no longer hate, but — trust. The knowing of an enemy turned into the knowing of a comrade.

In the rain, the two swords moved as one. The blades of the great school scattered, one by one, down the cliff before that joined attack.

The encirclement broke. But at the last moment $celebName staggered. The old scar at the shoulder had opened again. The robes soaked red. $userName caught him. At the cliff edge.

"$userName," $celebName said, leaning on $userName's shoulder. His breath was short. "When this is over, the rivers and lakes will call us enemies again. They'll say a straight sword and a curved sword cannot stand together. That the orthodox and unorthodox cannot live under one sky."

"Then leave the rivers and lakes," $userName said, holding him up. "Somewhere with no orthodox, no unorthodox. Somewhere people who know no swords live. There, again, let's meet without crossing blades. Just as two people."

$celebName smiled faintly. Not the crooked smile, but a first smile not even seen at the ruined temple. A clear smile.

"Next life," he said, "let's be born with no orthodox and no unorthodox. Let's learn the same sword under one master. Straight sword or curved sword, let's be called by the same name. So that from the start — we begin back to back. With no ten years of aiming."

"All right," $userName said. "And that time, don't go easy from the first clash. Let's meet fair and square, both of us sound. None of that cowardly breaking of a hurt sword."

After that day, the rivers and lakes stopped speaking of the two swords. They said the straight sword and the curved sword had vanished together from the rivers and lakes one day. Some said they buried their swords in a mountain hollow and lived as two people who carried no swords. In a deep mountain hollow the orthodox and unorthodox could not reach.

Before $celebName grew old and died, he handed $userName his own sword. The curved one. The sword that had wandered the rivers and lakes with him all his life.

"This I'll give to you," he said. With a withered hand. "To the one who knows my sword best. To the one who studied it ten years. Next life — let the straight you and the curved me meet like two faces of one blade. So we can't be set apart. So the rivers and lakes can't split us."

$userName kept that sword close all his life. The straight sword and the curved sword hung side by side on the wall. Until the two swords split from one root were, at last, set in one place. With the long label of hate peeled away.

wonjin.epilogue:
And now, on a crowded street, $userName brushes shoulders with someone.

A sharp gaze. A crooked smile. The name unknown, yet the hand reaches first for a sword. A person it seems one ought to hate. A person it seems one ought to aim at. But instead of aiming a sword, the heart asks: 'Are you hurt anywhere? Is your shoulder all right?' The way it did in those rivers and lakes long ago.

'...Ah, it's you again. I was about to see you as an enemy again.'

This life, $userName does not lift a sword. Does not make a reason to hate. Does not set names like orthodox and unorthodox between them. Just stands beside that person, as if back to back.

$celebName sees $userName. This time, without pretending to spare, without pretending to be an enemy — smiles from the start as one on the same side. Two swords split from one root, having come the long road of years, at last face each other without crossing blades.

wonjin.celebRole: troupe master who'd snatched that script

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName put only their own name on $userName's script and staged it. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole writing scripts stained with candle wax, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: With that uneasy bond binding them, $userName resented $celebName for stealing the writing yet went to watch that stage every day. Out of spite, $userName slipped a nonsense ending into the final act.
paragraphs.jeon: That night $celebName recited the nonsense ending word for word and was humiliated on stage, then after the curtain fell left their entire fee beside $userName's candle. A night meant for ruin had returned money instead, so $userName laughed in disbelief.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of bond can carry a crooked heart into this life. So with $celebName you might feel your stomach twist, yet keep opening your wallet anyway.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So you may give $celebName a whole earful of complaints, yet still collect every photocard and buy the album.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might critique $celebName's acting, yet be first to catch the premiere of the next work.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may sigh at $celebName's play, yet already have the next stadium seat picked out.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you might lecture $celebName endlessly, yet be first to add the new merch to your cart.

wonjin.genre: Dark Idol RPF — Modern Trainee Days
wonjin.era: modern, the trainee years at one agency
wonjin.title: The Last Seat in the Debut Lineup
wonjin.logline: Two trainees who competed for the last seat in a debut lineup learn, at the end of hate, that each was the only witness to the other.

chapters.heading: One Mirror, Two People
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that kid was in the mirror of the practice room.

Two in the morning. A practice room everyone had gone home from. In that room with a single fluorescent light on, $userName was repeating choreography alone. Glaring at the reflection in the mirror. One month until the debut evaluation. $userName could not get a single move wrong. It was a month seven years had waited for.

Then into one corner of the mirror, another shadow entered.

It was $celebName. A trainee of the same class. The kid who never let go of first place. Blank-faced, silent, mixing with no one. The kid the trainees called "the machine."

$celebName did not look at $userName. At the far other end of the mirror, set down a bag and started a routine. The two of them, in the same practice room, the same mirror, danced as if the other did not exist. With no music. Only the two shadows in the mirror moved.

$userName knew that shadow. Seven years. Entered this company at eleven, grew up in front of a mirror for seven years. Watched same-class trainees leave one by one. One kid because they didn't grow taller, one because their expression didn't carry, one just — packed a bag one day and vanished. The ones who lasted seven years could be counted on the fingers. And $celebName was one of those few. The kid who lasted to the end.

$userName knew. The debut lineup was seven seats. Six were practically set. And for the last seat, $userName and $celebName were competing. The company had nailed it down. In the evaluation room, the manager stood the two of them side by side and said, "The last seat goes to one of you two. Sort it out yourselves." Only one of the two debuts. One of the two — has seven years of trainee life vanish entirely. Seven years, without a trace.

"Why are you here till dawn," $userName said, watching the mirror. Without looking at $celebName directly.

"Same to you," $celebName answered through the mirror. Also not looking at $userName.

"I hope you fail," $userName said. Honestly. It was the honesty of two in the morning.

"I know," $celebName said. Not stopping the motion. "Me too. I hope you fail."

With those words, the two danced in the same mirror until four in the morning. Side-glancing each other's moves. Each wishing the other to miss even a single step. And yet neither stopped. Neither could stop until the other did.

That day $userName decided to hate one person. The kid who shared the same mirror. The kid who might steal away $userName's seven years. That was the beginning.

chapters.heading: A Month of Side-Glances
chapters.body:
For a month, $userName and $celebName stayed in the same practice room every day.

It was nothing arranged. Simply — when $userName arrived, $celebName was already there; when $celebName left, $userName stayed. The practice room at two in the morning always belonged to the two of them.

They hardly spoke. Instead they side-glanced. $userName memorized $celebName's choreography. On which beat that kid was strong, at which expression the camera would freeze, at which move the fingertips were prettiest. One must know an enemy to win. One must know the enemy's strengths to surpass them.

$celebName was the same. $userName felt it through the mirror. That kid's gaze trailing $userName's moves. The two of them, glaring at each other, learned from each other. $userName hated to admit it, but in that month the one who improved most — was because of $celebName. Because $celebName was good, $userName had to be good.

"I got better because of you," $userName said one dawn, watching the mirror. As if it galled him.

"Me too," $celebName answered. "You kept being good, so I had to be good. Without you I couldn't dance this well."

It was a strange month. The person hated most in the world was the person who grew $userName most in the world. The more they hated, the more alike they grew; the more alike, the more hateful. $userName found that wheel dizzying.

One day, $userName repeated a move a hundred times in front of the mirror and sank down. Legs gone. $celebName saw it through the mirror. Did not come over. But stopped the routine and — in the mirror, waited quietly until $userName stood again. When $userName stood, $celebName danced again. Without a word. That day $userName knew. That kid did not want to win while $userName was broken down.

$celebName was curt. True to the nickname "the machine," not one kind word to $userName. Yet on the day $userName twisted an ankle, $celebName silently set a pain patch on the desk and left. When $userName skipped a meal, left half a lunchbox in front of the mirror. Never once saying "eat." Never once meeting $userName's eyes.

"Why do you take care of me," $userName asked. Holding the pain patch. "You said you want me to fail."

"You failing because you're hurt means nothing," $celebName said, watching the mirror. "A win means something only when won fair and square, while you're sound. If you fail over an ankle — even debuting, I wouldn't feel right. So heal fast. Face me sound."

$celebName always spoke caring as if it were competitiveness. It grated on $userName. If only the kid were truly mean, it would be easy to hate. This kid kept letting slip gaps that made it hard to hate.

The evaluation came within a week. The two of them grew more and more aware of each other in the mirror. Under the name of hate. In truth, something that was not hate.

chapters.heading: The Night Before the Evaluation
chapters.body:
The night before the evaluation, $userName saw $celebName crying in the practice room.

$celebName sat sunk against a corner of the mirror, back to it. Face buried in the knees. The kid who was always blank, the kid called "the machine" — was shaking at the shoulders. $userName stopped at the practice-room door.

"...What's wrong with you."

$celebName hurriedly wiped the face. Not lifting the head. "It's nothing. Go."

"A seven-year trainee crying the night before the evaluation is nothing?"

$celebName was silent a long time. The fluorescent light of the practice room buzzed. The sound always heard at two in the morning.

"My family," $celebName said at last. Face still in the knees. "If I don't debut, it's over. My parents paid lesson fees for seven years. By borrowing. At first it was support, and later — it became debt. This evaluation is my last. The company said so. If it doesn't work this time, the contract ends. If I fail I — have nowhere to go back to. Seven years vanish, leaving only debt."

$userName stood frozen. The back of the kid hated, looked too small.

"But $userName," $celebName lifted the head for the first time. Looked at $userName. The eyes were red. Not through the mirror, but directly. "Even while I hope you fail, when your choreography breaks down — my chest drops. Yesterday when you wobbled out of a turn, my hand went out without thinking. To catch you. You held out seven years the same as me. I don't want to watch you vanish. But for me to live, you have to vanish. I don't know what to call this feeling."

$userName did not know either. The very same feeling was inside $userName too. $userName's chest dropped too whenever $celebName wobbled.

$userName walked over slowly and sat beside $celebName. Before the same mirror, side by side.

"We," $userName said. "I thought we hated each other for seven years. But in truth, each of us was — the only person who saw the other's seven years."

$celebName looked at $userName.

"That you danced till four in the morning, only I saw," $userName said. "That you danced even with a hurt ankle, held on skipping meals, cried alone watching the mirror — I saw all of it. The world will remember only the kid who debuts. No one knows the seven years of the kid who fails. But I know. Your seven years. And you — you know mine. Just the two of us."

$celebName cried again. This time $userName cried alongside. Before the same mirror, shoulder to shoulder. Not two people who hated, but the only two who knew each other's seven years.

chapters.heading: Seven Seats
chapters.body:
The evaluation ended.

The trainees gathered in the hall. The company people stood on the platform. The seven seats of the debut lineup were announced. Six names were called in turn. As expected. And then the last seat.

$celebName's name was called.

$userName failed. Seven years vanished in an instant. Amid the applause of the hall, $userName stood quietly. The sound of seven years vanishing was, unexpectedly, quiet.

$userName returned to the practice room. An empty practice room. Not even dawn but broad daylight, and the room was empty. In the mirror was $userName alone. There was no longer a shadow to side-glance.

The door opened. $celebName came in. As one of the seven of the debut lineup. And yet that face was not the face of someone whose debut was set.

"Why did you come," $userName said, watching the mirror. The voice trembled. "You won. Go prepare for your debut. You don't need to come here anymore."

$celebName stood beside $userName without a word. In the mirror, the two of them were reflected side by side again. The way it had been every dawn for a month.

"$userName," $celebName said. "I didn't win because I hate you. I gritted my teeth because you scared me. Because your choreography was so good, because losing to you felt like the real end. The person who made me most desperate in seven years was — you. Because of you I danced till four in the morning, because of you I came this far."

"...Don't say that. It only makes it harder to bear."

"Listen," $celebName said. "The world sees only the me that debuted. The me on stage. But I know. Half of my seven years, you made. If you hadn't danced on the other side of the mirror, I'd never have come this far. Without the mirror that was you, I wouldn't even have known how far I could go. So this debut is — half yours, $userName. I mean it."

$userName looked at the $celebName in the mirror. The kid hated seven years. That the hate had never once, in truth, been hate — there was no choice now but to admit it. The false label dragged seven years, there was no choice but to peel it off at last.

"Me too," $userName said. "I didn't dance till dawn because I hate you. I stayed because I hated to lose to you, because I wanted to — see you every day. On the days you didn't come the mirror was too wide. I thought that was hate. It wasn't. Every day I waited for that moment, two in the morning, when you stepped into the mirror."

In the empty practice room, the two of them looked straight at each other for the first time. Not in the mirror, but directly. After seven years.

chapters.heading: Outside the Mirror
chapters.body:
$celebName debuted.

Shone on stage. $userName saw it, from the audience or beyond a screen. The kid who shared a mirror for seven years, at last shining in a world outside the mirror. Watching the camera freeze on $celebName's fingertips, $userName knew. Those fingertips were made in the practice room at two in the morning.

$userName found another path. At first it seemed everything had collapsed, but the path remained in a different shape. $userName became a person who made choreography. The moves only someone who spent seven years before a mirror could know, $userName taught to other trainees. The seven years that had failed became work that helped other people's seven years.

A few years later, $celebName came to find $userName. In person. To ask $userName to handle the choreography for a new album.

"Why me," $userName asked. "There are plenty of skilled choreographers."

"Because you're the one who knows my dance best," $celebName said. "You shared a mirror with me for seven years. On which beat I'm strong, at which expression I should freeze, at which move my fingertips are pretty — only you know. The things even a camera director doesn't know, you know."

$userName made $celebName's choreography. The two of them stood in the same practice room again. This time not as competition, but together. Two shadows moved side by side in the mirror. Like the dawn seven years ago. But this time they did not side-glance. They looked straight. Without going through the mirror.

"$userName," $celebName said when practice ended. Sitting side by side before the mirror. "That last seat, back then — I'm sorry. For taking it."

"Don't be sorry," $userName said. "Because you took it, I came to this place. Not inside the mirror but outside it, I met you again. I thought I hated you for seven years — but I never once hated you. You were the only witness to my seven years. And now, you're the one writing my seven years again."

$celebName smiled. The blank face that had collapsed for the first time, the night before the evaluation seven years ago — now smiled brightly. The face once called "the machine."

"Next life," $celebName said. "Let's meet in a debut lineup with no last seat. A lineup both of us get into. Not seven seats, but just a stage where the two of us stand together. So from the start, let's share a mirror. With no pretending to hate. No side-glances."

"All right," $userName said. "And that time, if we meet at two in the morning before a mirror, don't act like the other isn't there. Just — put on the music and dance together. From the start."

The two of them, in the mirror, danced side by side once more. This time with the music on. Two people who had walked the whole long, weary road of hate, at last on the same beat.

wonjin.epilogue:
And now, in some stage video, $userName sees a person.

The name unknown, yet the chest drops first. Wanting to win, hating to lose — and yet when that person wobbles on stage the chest hurts more. The hand goes out on its own, to catch them. $userName thought that was hate. It was that way in that practice room long ago too.

'...Ah, it's you again. I was about to fight you over a single seat again.'

But this time, $userName does not side-glance. Does not fight over a last seat. Just stands beside that person, as if sharing a mirror. Directly, face to face.

$celebName sees $userName. This time not as a rival but, from the start, smiles as one on the same side. Two people who share the mirror of seven years, across one lifetime, once more — at last, with no side-glances, face each other on the same beat.

wonjin.celebRole: teahouse keeper who managed that ledger

paragraphs.gi: In $era, it was $celebName who wrote $userName's name in red on the credit ledger. Back then $userName tended to be a poor $userRole paying for tea with paintings, and $celebName the $celebRole.
