paragraphs.seung: Caught in that uneasy bond, $celebName pressed $userName over the debt yet secretly hid each painting behind the ledger. Out of spite, $userName drew $celebName's face unflattering on purpose and hung it right on the shop wall.
paragraphs.jeon: One night $celebName quietly took down that unflattering portrait, held it close, and paid off $userName's whole debt with their own money. A painting meant to mock had cleared the debt, so $userName felt sore yet laughed.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of bond can drift an odd tie into this life. So in front of $celebName you might not mind the loss at all.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So you may grumble at every $celebName teaser, yet buy a second lightstick and feel quietly pleased.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might rage at how $celebName's work ended, then pre-order the director's-cut Blu-ray anyway.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may be hurt by $celebName's transfer rumors, yet be first to order the new-season jersey.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you might have a word about $celebName's latest, yet check out the new merch the second the alert hits.

wonjin.title: For the Sake of One King
wonjin.logline: Two concubines who quarreled all their lives over one king learn, at the end of hate, that only the other ever truly saw them.

chapters.heading: Two Who Entered the Same Day
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that woman was crossing the palace gate on the same day.

Two palanquins entered the great palace gate side by side. In one rode $userName, in the other $celebName. Two concubines chosen on the same day. Two people come to serve the same king. The high palace walls cast long shadows over the palanquins.

Stepping out of the palanquins, the eyes of the two women met.

$celebName was beautiful. Dangerously so. A face that, once seen, could not be forgotten. And yet that face held no smile. Only a cold, hard blankness, like a blade. Neither the thrill nor the fear of a first day in the palace was on that face.

"Since we entered the same day," $celebName said. The first words spoken. A low, cool voice. "We shall see each other often from now."

"So we shall," $userName answered.

$celebName, passing close by $userName, said very low. Quiet enough that the court ladies could not hear.

"Remember this. In this palace, the one who shows mercy vanishes first. Tenderness is — the most dangerous thing here."

$userName glared at that back. A woman who bared hostility from the very first day in the palace. A woman who threw a warning before any greeting, any smile. Though they had ridden in on the same day, from the first moment she met $userName that woman had drawn $userName as an enemy.

$userName set the heart too. If that woman is an enemy, treat her as one. The palace walls were high, and inside them, they said, the soft-hearted did not survive. Before being chosen and brought in, the last thing $userName's mother said was also that. "In the palace, trust no one. Especially the woman who enters the same day." $userName carved those words into the chest. And the name $celebName, with them.

The palace was deep. Pavilions ran on endlessly, the corridors were a maze, every word held a meaning two and three layers deep. The king's favor was only one, and over that one the concubines quarreled all their lives. To become a concubine meant, all one's life, to vie with other women over one person's heart.

$userName and $celebName became the two who quarreled most often, most sharply. Over who sat in the higher seat at tea, over the order of receiving tribute goods, over the turn of serving the king. Whenever the two met, the court ladies held their breath. A cold air ran through the corridors.

Hate began that way. Between two people who crossed the same gate on the same day. That day $userName resolved to be wary of one person for life. The woman cold as fog.

chapters.heading: Shadows of the Corridor
chapters.body:
Ten years passed. The two still quarreled.

$userName hated $celebName. Every day, without a gap. Watched every movement of that woman. Which pavilion she entered, which color of robe she favored, what manner of speech she used before the king, which senior court lady she kept near. To survive in the palace, one must know the most dangerous enemy first.

But it was strange. The more $userName watched $celebName, the better $userName knew $celebName than anyone. That the woman could not sleep on rainy nights. That when thunder rolled she watched out the window as if recalling her childhood. That on nights the king entered another pavilion she kept a lamp burning till dawn. And further — that beneath that cold blankness, a deep loneliness always pooled.

One winter night, $userName met $celebName in the corridor. Both sleepless. Cold moonlight seeped between the corridor pillars.

"Sleepless again," $userName said. As if mocking.

"The same of you, surely," $celebName answered. "To be out in the corridor at this hour."

The two stood side by side a while in the corridor. The cold moonlight stretched the two shadows long. On one corridor, two shadows almost touching.

"Strange, isn't it," $celebName said suddenly. Watching the moon. "Hundreds of people in this palace, and the one I meet in the corridor in the dead of night is always you. We seem to lose sleep at the same hours. That the one awake on the same night is, of all people, each other."

$userName did not answer. But those words did not leave for days. That they lost sleep at the same hours.

$celebName was said to be a fearsome woman. The court ladies shrank at the mere mention of her name. But $userName had seen things. That woman secretly sending medicine to a sick young court lady. Granting her own share of food to a starving low servant. And never once pretending to be merciful. Trying hard not to be caught at the mercy she gave.

"You said the one who shows mercy vanishes first," $userName asked one day. "Do you not show mercy yourself? I saw you sending medicine to a young court lady."

$celebName paused a moment. Across that blade-like face, a faint crack passed.

"If you are not caught, you do not vanish," $celebName said. "Mercy given in secret survives too. And — you caught me. In this palace, the one who has seen my mercy is the first."

Only then $userName knew. That under the excuse of hating $celebName, she had been looking only at that woman, every day. Of the palace's hundreds, only that woman.

chapters.heading: The King's Favor Was a Lie
chapters.body:
$userName came to know the truth the night the king lay ill.

The palace stirred. The king's illness was deep. The concubines each reckoned the futures of their own children, and conspiracies grew in every pavilion. Over whose child would become crown prince, every lamp in the palace burned through the night. $userName and $celebName were in the very center of it.

That night, in a sealed pavilion, $userName met $celebName. $celebName was burning a pile of old letters in a brazier. The firelight made that cold face flicker.

"What are you burning," $userName asked.

$celebName looked at $userName a long while. Then, for the first time, set down that blade-like blankness. Before the brazier's fire.

"The record of our ten years of quarreling."

$userName paused. "...What do you mean."

"It was a thing the king commanded," $celebName said, feeding a letter into the fire. "The king feared the concubines being on good terms. When concubines join hands, the houses behind them join hands too. The relatives grow. And then the king's seat grows precarious. So — he set us to quarrel. On purpose."

$celebName looked at $userName. Across the firelight.

"That we entered the palace on the same day. It was no chance. The two most alike women, chosen from the most similar houses, brought in on the same day. So we would be aware of each other like a mirror. The same favor cast as bait, and us quarreling over that bait."

$userName's hands shook.

"The first day in the palace, when I showed you hostility," $celebName went on. "That was a thing done by command. Only if we were enemies from the start was the king at ease. The words that the merciful vanish first — those too were the king's words carried straight to you. To stop you from growing tender toward me."

"...Then our ten years."

"It was a stage the king built," $celebName said, feeding the last letter to the fire. "We did not quarrel over the same king, $userName. We were two people used, the same way, by the same king. The one who put the king between us was the king himself."

$userName nearly sank before the fire. Braced a hand on a pavilion pillar. Ten years of hate had been bait thrown by one person. A hate the two women had not made, a hate someone made for them.

"Why tell me only now," $userName asked. "After ten years of holding your tongue."

"The king will soon be gone," $celebName said, watching the brazier's fire. "And then the stage ends. Before the stage ends, I — wanted to tell you the truth. That for ten years, not for a single day did I truly hate you. Because you are the one and only person in this palace who has seen my mercy."

chapters.heading: What Only They Knew
chapters.body:
The king passed away.

The palace was thrown into uproar. The king's relatives sharpened knives, and conspiracies seethed around the young crown prince. The concubines split into their factions. They were nights when a wrong step meant a cup of poison. Through every corridor of the palace, the shadow of death lay.

$userName and $celebName crossed those nights together. The two who quarreled ten years, this time stood back to back.

$celebName knew every path of the palace. Which corridor led to a secret passage, in which pavilion a person could hide. $userName knew every person of the palace. Which senior court lady could be trusted, which eunuch was on whose side. One opened the way, one sorted and blocked the people — and the two of them protected the young crown prince. From the hands of the king's relatives.

One night, the relatives' men flooded toward the crown prince's quarters. $celebName opened the door of a secret passage, and $userName, holding the young crown prince, went into that darkness. $celebName blocked the passage entrance. With her own body. In the darkness $userName saw that back. The back of the woman hated ten years, standing before the door for $userName and the crown prince. That night, the two had not discussed a single word — yet who would open the way and who would block, they already knew. Two people who had watched only each other for ten years moved hand in step without a word.

"$userName," $celebName said one night. The two stood again in that corridor. The corridor where they first met in the dead of night. The same cold moonlight came down.

"While we quarreled for ten years, the one who knew us best was — each other. Even the king did not know us that well. The king saw us only as bait, never as people. That you cannot sleep on rainy nights, I know. That you watch out the window when it thunders, too. That I keep a lamp burning till dawn, you know too. That I do it only on the nights the king enters another pavilion."

"...I know," $userName said. "I know all of it."

"Hate makes a person look deep," $celebName said. "Because to beat an enemy one must know the enemy. Under the excuse of hating each other, we watched only each other for ten years. The one who knows me best in this palace is — you, $userName. Not the king. Not the king, but you."

$userName took the hand of that blade-like woman. The hand thought to be cold was warm. Though it was a hand hated ten years.

"Me too," $userName said. "In this palace the one who saw me is only you. The king never saw me. He saw me only as bait. But you — you watched me for ten years. Under the name of hate. To beat me, you looked at me every day. That was — the deepest gaze I received in this palace."

The two stood side by side in the corridor. Two shadows no longer enemies overlapped as one under the moonlight. In that corridor where, ten years before, they first met. This time without glaring at each other.

chapters.heading: Two Who Step Out of the Palace
chapters.body:
The crown prince safely took the throne.

The young crown prince the two had protected together became the new king. The relatives withdrew, the conspiracies settled. Spring light entered the palace again.

$userName and $celebName decided to leave the palace together. To walk out of the stage the king had built, on their own feet.

"For a concubine to leave the palace has no precedent," an old senior court lady tried to stop them. "Such a thing has never been heard of."

"We were never concubines," $celebName said. "We were bait. Bait the king threw. Bait is gathered up when the stage ends. We are going to gather ourselves up."

The two crossed the palace gate on the same day. The gate they had entered ten years before in two palanquins, this time they walked out side by side. With no palanquin, no concubine's robes.

Crossing the gate, $celebName said:

"$userName. We thought we quarreled over the same king. For our whole lives. But what we faced each other over for our whole lives was not the king — it was each other. The king was not even between us. All along, we were looking at each other."

"I know," $userName said. "For ten years the person I thought of most was not the king but you. The first person I recalled waking, the last before sleep. I thought it was hate, but that meant — I never forgot you for a single moment."

$celebName stopped walking. Before the palace gate. That blade-like blankness finally undid itself to the end. It was a face never seen before. A face with a smile, not there even on the first day in the palace.

"Next life," $celebName said. "Let us not put a king between us. With no bait, no stage. With no one to set us quarreling. Let us meet simply as two friends crossing the same gate on the same day. Holding hands from the start. Offering a greeting instead of a warning."

"All right," $userName said. "And that time, on the first day in the palace, instead of hostility — I'll just smile for you. 'Since we entered the same day, we'll see each other often,' I'll say it warmly."

The two crossed the palace gate. Out of the stage the king had built. Two people who at last admitted that ten years of hate had, in truth, been ten years of longing.

The two lived together into old age in a small village far from the palace. Not concubines, not enemies, not bait, but simply the two who knew each other best. Staying awake together on rainy nights, watching out the window together when it thundered.

wonjin.epilogue:
And now, at some gathering, $userName sees a person.

A graceful, cold face. The name unknown, yet the heart grows wary first. A person it seems one ought to quarrel with. A person it seems one ought to vie with over something. But the closer $userName comes, the more the heart knows that the wariness is, in truth — something that lets one never forget that person for a moment. The way it was in that palace long ago.

'...Ah, it's you again. I was about to see you as an enemy again.'

This life, $userName puts nothing between the two of them. No bait, no stage, no king to quarrel over. Nothing that sets them vying. Just stands before that person.

$celebName sees $userName. This time, instead of a hostile warning, offers a greeting with a smile from the start. Two people who crossed the same gate on the same day, having come the long road of years, at last take each other's hands.

wonjin.celebRole: office clerk who collected the tax

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName came each month to stick a seizure notice on $userName's shop door. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole with no silver coin, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: Because of that uneasy bond, $userName resented $celebName for posting those notices, yet guarded the door each time, afraid that hand might stop. Out of spite, $userName picked the ugliest silk and pushed it off as tax.
paragraphs.jeon: $celebName took that ugly silk and made it into a handkerchief embroidered with $userName's name, then set it adrift on the canal. The tax collector's own heart had been read aloud, so $userName stared at the canal a long while.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of bond can follow an odd resentment into this life. So with $celebName your money may leak, yet your heart stays strangely warm.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So you might write "this is really the last one" on $celebName's merch, then write the same thing again next comeback.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you may grumble at $celebName's premiere ticket prices, yet book the stage greeting of the next work too.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you might sigh at the cost of $celebName's away game, yet have the lodging booked first.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you may call $celebName's exhibition fee steep, yet walk out having bought the catalogue too.

wonjin.title: The Third Deletion
wonjin.logline: Two people who hate again even after their memories are erased; before the third deletion, they learn what the hate ever was.

chapters.heading: A First Meeting With No Memory
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that person was in the gray corridor of the Bureau of Memory Management.

The 22nd-century city managed memory. Citizens' minds were inspected on a schedule, and unstable memories, dangerous emotions, recollections harmful to the city — were deleted. Cleanly. With no trace. People called it "maintenance." After being maintenanced, they said, you came out lighter.

$userName was a maintenance technician of the Bureau. The work was tending people's memories. Deciding which memory to keep and which to erase, handling that border. That day too was an ordinary shift. Until one person walked toward $userName from the far end of the corridor.

It was $celebName. An analyst of the same Bureau. A blank, cold person, the gaze like a blade. The person the gray uniform suited best.

The two passed in the corridor. In that moment, something throbbed inside $userName's head. Something like pain, something like a signal. A vibration ringing in a deep place. $userName stopped.

"...Have we," $userName asked. "met before."

$celebName had stopped too. Across that blank face, a crack passed for a moment. That person too seemed to feel something.

"No," $celebName said. "Today is the first time. There's no record."

But there was no certainty in that tone. The same for $userName. The two had clearly met for the first time, and yet somewhere in the head — already said they knew each other. Memory said "first," and something beneath it said "no."

From that day, $celebName grated on $userName. Each time they passed, the head throbbed. When that person's gray uniform appeared in the gray corridor, the temples tightened.

$userName thought the cause of that pain was $celebName. Seeing only that person makes my head ache, so that person is the problem. So $userName began to hate $celebName. Without even knowing why. Without even knowing it was a beginning. Forgetting, too, that hate is supposed to have a reason.

That night $userName came home and looked in the mirror. The 22nd-century mirror was not only a mirror. It was also a device that read a citizen's emotional state and reported it to the city. The mirror read $userName's face and floated small letters. 'Emotion stable. Faint hostility detected — within normal range.' $userName saw those letters. Hostility was normal, it said. The city called hate "normal." $userName was reassured by that. Reassured, $userName once more called the throb in the gray corridor — hate.

chapters.heading: A Trace Not Erased
chapters.body:
$userName hated $celebName. But that hate had no reason.

$celebName had never wronged $userName. Not once. If anything, in a curt way, $celebName took care of $userName. When an error appeared in $userName's maintenance record, fixed it without a word; on nights $userName worked overtime, kept the analysis-room light on too. When $userName skipped lunch, left a nutrition capsule on the analysis-room desk. Never once saying a kind thing.

"Why do you help me," $userName asked.

"If an error remains in a technician's record, the whole city shakes," $celebName said. "It's a matter of efficiency. That's all."

$celebName always rendered goodwill into the city's language, calling it efficiency. It grated on $userName. $userName had decided to hate, yet kept seeing gaps hard to hate. A hate with no reason, and $celebName kept making that hate pointless.

One late night, only the two were left in the analysis room. While sorting maintenance records, $userName suddenly saw $celebName watching out the window. The night view of the 22nd-century city. An orderly night view where every window was lit the same gray light. $celebName's profile was sunk in that light.

"What are you looking at," $userName asked.

"Sometimes," $celebName said. Still watching the window. "In this city, I get the feeling there's something I've forgotten. Clearly something important, but I don't know what. Like an empty space. Have you never had that."

$userName could not answer. Because $userName had. Had it every day. Each time $userName passed $celebName in the gray corridor, just such an empty space throbbed in the head. That day $userName, for the first time, thought that $userName's pain and $celebName's empty space might be — the same shape.

Then one day, $userName happened to look into $userName's own memory file. A thing possible only for a technician. They could view citizens' memories, but a technician who looked into their own was rare. It was a regulation violation. But that day, $userName was curious about the nature of that throb.

And found it. In $userName's memory there were — two traces of deletion.

Deletion was supposed to leave no trace. That was the principle of "maintenance." Cleanly, with no trace. Yet in $userName's memory, in the same place, there were two empty holes. The trace of something erased twice, in the same place.

With trembling hands $userName analyzed those empty holes. The faint resonance of the deleted memory remained. A very faint resonance visible only to a technician's eye. $userName carefully restored that resonance.

A form rose up.

It was $celebName's face.

$userName stood frozen. In the gray analysis room, the memory file open. At the very center of what had been deleted twice, there was $celebName. Both times, that person's face.

chapters.heading: A Person Hated Twice
chapters.body:
$userName went to find $celebName. Carrying the restored resonance.

"What is this," $userName asked. Closing the analysis-room door. "Why are you inside my erased memory. And twice, at that."

$celebName looked at that resonance a long while. The face restored from $userName's memory — $celebName's own. Then that blade-like blankness collapsed. To the end.

"...So you finally found it," $celebName said. The voice trembled. "The third time. And this time too, you found it in the end. You always — find it in the end."

"The third time. What do you mean."

$celebName opened the analyst's own memory file. The memory of $celebName's own self. There too, in the same place, were two empty holes. In the exact position as $userName's.

"$userName," $celebName said. "We did not meet for the first time. This is the third. We met each other twice, and — twice, had our memories deleted together. On the same day, in the same place."

$userName's head throbbed. The two empty holes.

"Do you know what emotion the 22nd-century city fears most," $celebName asked. "It is not hate. Hate pulls people apart — so the city even encourages hate. Citizens who hate each other do not band together. They are easy to control. What the city fears is two people becoming too deeply connected. Then a place the city's hand cannot reach is born."

$celebName looked at $userName. Under the light of the gray analysis room.

"Twice, we touched each other too deeply. Deeper than the city could control. Each time, the city deleted our memories. Cleanly. And yet — each time we met again. At the same Bureau of Memory Management. And each time, we began by hating each other. Do you know why."

$userName shook the head.

"Deletion can erase memory," $celebName said. "But there is one thing it cannot erase. The bond that once forms between two people — carved into the deepest place of the heart. It lies deeper than memory, so the deleter cannot reach it. When memory is gone, that bond loses its place. And — it appears in the form of hate. Each time we met and the head throbbed, it was the erased bond recognizing the other. Love lost its place and became pain."

Before those words $userName lost breath. The two hates had, in truth, been two erased loves. The reason of the reasonless hate was, at last, there.

chapters.heading: Before the Third Deletion
chapters.body:
The Bureau of Memory Management summoned the two.

It was a notice of a third deletion. The city had caught it again. That $userName and $celebName were connecting again. That the hate it had encouraged was turning back into the thing it feared. That the pain was returning to a bond.

The two stood in the gray corridor before the deletion room. The corridor where they first passed. The corridor where everything began, and everything was erased twice.

"$userName," $celebName said. "If we're deleted this time, we start over again. Meet again in the gray corridor, the head throbs again, and we hate each other again. Forever. A third time, a fourth, a hundredth. Each time we meet we hate, hate and barely learn the truth, and then are erased again."

"...Is there no way. To break this wheel."

"There's one," $celebName said. "Just before deletion, into the deepest place of our memory — the place where the bond lives — we carve a small message. At a depth the deleter cannot reach. Into the bond, not the memory. Then, the next life when we meet, instead of hate that message will rise first. Sooner than the pain."

"What message."

$celebName took $userName's hand. For the first time. In the gray corridor, before the deletion-room door.

"The thing we realized latest in this life," $celebName said. "We met three times, and spent all our time to love hating, three times. Each time we learned the truth too late. We began with hate, and by the time we reached the truth a deletion notice always came. Not once have we ever properly been together. So the message for the next life is this. 'Don't hate. That pain is erased love. Take the hand from the start. There's no time.'"

$userName wept. In the 22nd-century gray city, holding a third deletion notice.

"We met three times," $userName said. "And not once have we properly loved. Only met, only hated."

"The fourth time," $celebName said, gripping $userName's hand tight. "The fourth time let's not do that. Trust the message, skip the stage called hate, and go straight — to each other. Don't mistake the pain for hostility."

Before going into the deletion room, the two carved that message into the deepest place of each other's memory. A thing possible only for a technician and an analyst. Into the place the deleter could never reach. The very bottom of the heart, where the two people's bond lived.

chapters.heading: The Place the Deleter Could Not Reach
chapters.body:
The third deletion was carried out.

$userName lost the memory again. $celebName the same. Inside both their heads, every memory of each other was erased once more. Cleanly, with no trace.

Some time later, the two passed again, as if for the first time, in the gray corridor of the Bureau. The head throbbed again. At the sight of the gray uniform, the temples tightened.

But this time, it was different.

$userName stopped. In the head, instead of pain — one sentence rose. A sentence that felt carved by someone, that felt not $userName's own, and yet rang from the deepest place. From a place deeper than pain.

'Don't hate. That pain is erased love. Take the hand from the start. There's no time.'

$userName looked at $celebName. That person too had stopped in the middle of the corridor. Across that blank face passed an expression as if the same sentence had risen. The expression of one hearing something beyond the throb.

"...Listen," $userName said. This time $userName did not ask "have we met before." "This will sound strange. When I see you, my head aches. It seems I should hate you — and yet it seems I shouldn't. From the deepest place in my head, someone tells me not to hate. That the pain is something else."

$celebName came slowly closer. In the gray corridor. In that corridor where they had begun three times by hating, this time skipping hate from the start.

"It's the same for me," $celebName said. "Looking at your face, instead of hate — relief comes. That at last we've met. That this time it isn't too late. In my head it says, 'There's no time. Take the hand.'"

The two took each other's hands in the middle of the gray corridor. Memory was erased three times, but the bond in that deep place the deleter could not reach — survived across three deletions. And this time, a single sentence carved into that bond arrived before the hate.

The city would catch it again. Would try to delete again. But now the two knew. Whatever is erased, the bond remains. The very bottom of the heart, the city's hand cannot reach. And carve one sentence into that bottom, and the two of the next life — without passing through the futile stage called hate, meet straightaway.

"If we're deleted again next time," $userName said. "Let's leave another message. Shorter. Deeper. Where the deleter can never, ever reach."

"Yes," $celebName said. "However many times we're erased, in the end we — come to each other. Because that is carved into our bond."

The two walked out of the gray corridor together. With no memory, but without letting go of the hand. This life, there was time left to love.

wonjin.epilogue:
And now, on a crowded street, $userName sees a person.

The name unknown, yet a deep place in the head throbs. A person it seems one ought to hate. But beneath that throb, a sentence rises as if carved by someone. 'Don't hate. That pain is erased love.' Like the sentence carved several lives ago.

'...Ah, it's you again. I was about to begin by hating you again.'

So this time, $userName skips the hate. Does not mistake the pain for hostility. Just stands before that person and follows the sentence carved in the deepest place. Because there is no time.

$celebName sees $userName. This time, instead of hate, holds out a hand with relief from the start. Two people who, however many times erased, in the end come to each other — at last, not too late, meet.

wonjin.celebRole: creditor who'd come with the debt papers

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName fingered $userName's debt papers from the very back row of the seats. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole even borrowing for pointe shoes, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: Held by that uneasy bond, $celebName had to collect the debt yet folded those papers away again at every one of $userName's turns. Out of spite, $userName kept a last performance ticket but withheld it from $celebName's seat on purpose.
paragraphs.jeon: That night $celebName stood ticketless under the theatre eaves, watched the last dance through the window, then tore the debt papers and scattered them over the snow. A night meant to shut them out had cleared the debt, so $userName's heart raced even while sore.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of bond can reach a crossed heart into this life. So even at a loss, you might always watch $celebName with a smile.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So you may say you're "sulking" at $celebName, then stream the comeback at five in the morning.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might pretend to be cross with $celebName, yet replay the teaser ten times over.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may say you're angry at $celebName, yet be first to put on the cheer jersey on match day.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you might say $celebName let you down, yet not even manage to turn off the new-content alerts.

wonjin.era: 1967 Hong Kong, a rain-soaked back alley
wonjin.title: The Muzzle That Was a Brother
wonjin.logline: Two who grew up as brothers in one gang aim muzzles at each other, and at the end of hate learn the true name of betrayal.

chapters.heading: Two Children Under One Eave
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that kid was in the rain of a Hong Kong back alley.

1960s Hong Kong. A time when neon bled into the rain and the shadow of the gangs lay over every alley. Parentless children were common in the streets. $userName was one of them. Ducking out of the rain under some eave, there was already a child crouched there.

It was $celebName. Skinny, the clothes all soaked, only the eyes fierce.

"Tight here," $celebName said. As if growling.

"Just till the rain stops," $userName said.

The two children sheltered from the rain under one eave. An eave narrow enough that their shoulders touched. The rain ran in to their feet. For a long while, without a word, the two watched the same rain.

"You got nowhere to go either," $celebName asked after a while. The growling voice had softened a little.

"Yeah," $userName said.

$celebName asked no more. Instead shifted a hand's width aside. So $userName would catch less rain. $userName moved into that hand's width. That was the first tenderness $celebName ever gave $userName. Not in words, but in a hand's width of space.

That was the start. That night, the adults of the street took the two children in. It was a gang that gathered and raised the children of the streets. The boss stood before the two children. Through cigarette smoke.

"From today you two are brothers," the boss said. "Brothers don't aim guns at each other. No matter what. That's the one and only rule of this trade. Break everything else, but never that."

$userName and $celebName became brothers. Shared one room, ate the same food, grew up on the same streets. Catching the same rain. $celebName was curt. Never one kind word. Yet whenever $userName was in danger, he always stepped in front first. When a knife flew, blocked it with his own body.

"Why do you keep stepping in front," $userName asked.

"If you die my share of the work goes up," $celebName said. With a shrug.

$celebName always acted as if caring were a bother. $userName knew the act. The lie of "not very hungry" every time $celebName gave up a share of food, the way he slipped his own blanket over $userName on cold nights. $userName knew all those lies and pretended not to. And so held $celebName as a true brother. A brother thicker than blood.

The two lived as brothers that way for ten years. That the day those ten years would end would ever come — back then, they did not know. The day that rule, that brothers do not aim guns at each other — would break.

chapters.heading: A Muzzle Aimed
chapters.body:
Ten years later, $userName stood before the muzzle of $celebName's gun.

It was a rainy pier. 1967 Hong Kong, neon melting into the rain. The pier lamps swayed dimly. $celebName had a gun aimed at $userName. Breaking the one and only rule — that brothers do not aim guns at each other.

"$celebName," $userName said. "What is this. We're brothers."

"It's the boss's order," $celebName said. The face was blank. So blank it was, instead, frightening. "You betrayed the gang. Passed our information to the police. Deal sites, the boss's hideout. A traitor is dealt with. That's the rule."

"I didn't betray anyone! Not once!"

"There's evidence," $celebName said. "It came out of your room. The radio a police informant uses. It was hidden under your bed."

$userName had never seen that radio. Someone had planted it. Certainly. And yet $celebName — believed it. Or seemed to believe it.

"You know me," $userName said. Rainwater ran down the face. "We lived ten years as brothers. Under the same eave. Eating the same food. Do I look like a person who'd betray? To you, do I look like that?"

$celebName's muzzle trembled, faintly. Just for a moment. Faint enough that only $userName could catch it. Faint enough that only a brother of ten years could catch it.

"...You do," $celebName said. "Brothers? There's no such thing as brothers in this trade. That's a lie the boss drove into our heads. To use us. I deal with you, and become the boss's right hand. That's what I've wanted for ten years. From the start."

A gunshot rang. Tearing through the sound of rain.

$userName felt something hot lodge in the shoulder. The body staggered. Over the pier railing, into the black sea, fell. Sinking between rainwater and seawater, $userName looked one last time up at the pier.

$celebName stood there. That blank face was — collapsing, up on the pier. Gun still in hand. $userName saw it. Sinking, saw it clearly. That face looked as if it were crying.

That day, $userName decided to hate the person who had been a brother. That hate kept $userName alive. Made $userName crawl out of the black sea. Without the hate, $userName would simply have sunk in that sea.

chapters.heading: The Reason the Muzzle Trembled
chapters.body:
$userName did not die.

Near dawn, caught in a fisherman's net, $userName survived. Carrying the gunshot wound in the shoulder, slipped into another city. The back alleys of Macau. And for three years, lived hating one person. $celebName. The person who had lived ten years together under the lie of brotherhood and shot $userName on a rainy pier.

$userName prepared revenge. With hate as fuel. Investigated everything about $celebName. Which alley that person frequented, with what sorts he kept company, where he slept. One must know an enemy to bring him down. To kill a brother, one must know that brother better than anyone.

But the more $userName investigated, the stranger the things that came up.

$celebName had not become the boss's right hand. On the contrary, after that day he had been pushed to the gang's fringe. Dealing with $userName was supposed to make him the right hand — but it was the exact opposite. That person guarded only a run-down billiard hall, fallen to the gang's lowest place.

$userName dug deeper. And found a person who knew the truth of that pier. An old gang member who had been there. $userName bought the old man drinks and asked.

"$celebName shot me," $userName said. "That day on the pier."

"He did," the old gang member emptied a cup. "And tell me — where were you hit."

"...The shoulder." $userName touched the shoulder. The place still scarred.

"$celebName was the finest marksman in this trade," the old member said. "A man who never missed a heart within ten paces. Not once. And you were hit in the shoulder, fell into the sea, and lived. A marksman shot a brother from ten paces, and it's the shoulder. Do you think that's chance."

$userName's hand shook. The cup wavered.

"$celebName shot you in the shoulder on purpose," the old member said. "So you'd fall into the sea. So you could live. Pretending to deal with you in front of the boss — he saved you. And for that price, $celebName lost the boss's trust. For not carrying out the order properly. Pushed to the fringe. The price of saving you, he gave up his whole place."

$userName was silent a while. The sound of rain came from outside the bar window.

"But why," $userName managed. "Why go that far. If he'd killed me as ordered, $celebName would've been the right hand. Lived easy."

"Who knows," the old member said, refilling the cup. "I asked him the very same once. The night he was pushed to the fringe. And you know what he said. — He said, 'You can't shoot someone you sheltered from the rain under the same eave with.' He said only that one line and shut his mouth. The same eave. What that means I still don't know. Do you."

$userName knew. Knew too well. A certain rain of 1960, that eave narrow enough that shoulders touched.

$userName recalled the collapsing face seen on the pier. That one instant the muzzle trembled. That face, looking as if it cried, seen while sinking.

Three years of hate broke in an instant. In the hand gripping the cup.

chapters.heading: Betrayal Was a Lie
chapters.body:
$userName went to find $celebName.

$celebName was at the gang's fringe, in a run-down billiard hall. A face much thinner over three years. Wiping a billiard table. When $userName opened the door, $celebName lifted the head. And — froze, as if seeing a ghost. The dry cloth dropped from his hand.

"...You were alive," $celebName said. The voice trembled. "Truly."

"Why the shoulder," $userName asked. Asked only that.

$celebName was silent a long time. The fluorescent light of the billiard hall flickered. The sound of rain came from outside the window. Like that day on the pier.

"That radio that day," $celebName said at last. "The boss planted it. The boss wanted you removed. You were gaining too much trust. The brothers all followed you. The boss hated his seat being threatened. So he framed you as a traitor and — told me to shoot you. He was a man who believed that making brothers shoot brothers makes the other brothers afraid and more obedient."

"...So you hit the shoulder on purpose."

"Refuse the order and we both die," $celebName said. "Refuse in front of the boss, in front of the men — and it's over for both, on the spot. So I pretended to shoot you, and saved you. Hit the shoulder, dropped you over the pier railing. The sea is deep, so you could live. That was — the last thing I could do for a brother."

"Why did you say that day there's no such thing as brothers. A lie the boss drove in."

"So you'd run," $celebName said. "If you remembered me as a brother, you wouldn't take revenge — you'd come back to save me. Because you're that kind of fool. And then the boss would kill you again. Truly kill you. So you had to hate me. Hating me, you had to go far, forever far. Hate would make you run. That was saving you, $userName."

$userName collapsed on the spot. To the billiard-hall floor. The three years of hate had, in truth, been time to run that $celebName had bought by throwing away his whole place. A brother's last gift, sending $userName far with the tool of hate.

"And for that price you were pushed to the fringe," $userName said. "Ten years of standing, all of it."

"A cheap price, for saving a brother," $celebName smiled faintly. The first smile in three years. "The boss drove into our heads that brothers are a lie. To use us. But $userName, I knew that day on the pier. Brothers are not a lie. Because a brother — pretends to shoot, yet saves. Because a brother even lies, telling you to hate, to make you run."

chapters.heading: A Muzzle in the Same Direction
chapters.body:
$userName and $celebName, this time, aimed the muzzles at the boss.

The two were brothers. The brothers the boss had called a lie. One knew the ways, one knew the people. One the alleys, one the gang's weak points. The two stood back to back and walked into the deepest place of the gang. Two children who had once been under the same eave, this time inside the same fight.

It was a rainy pier. The very pier where, three years before, $celebName had shot $userName. The lamps swayed the same.

The boss stood there. Seeing the two, the boss sneered. Through cigarette smoke.

"Brothers banded together again. Pathetic. There's no such thing as brothers. In the end you two will shoot each other too. Everyone sells out a brother to save their own skin. I taught you that."

"Brothers don't aim guns at each other," $userName said. "The one and only rule you taught. We didn't break it. Not three years ago, not now. $celebName didn't shoot me. He saved me. Hit the shoulder, dropped me into the sea. Fooling your eyes."

The two muzzles aimed in the same direction. The two muzzles that had been brothers, for the first time, side by side. Not at each other, but at the boss.

That night, the boss's age ended. On a rainy pier.

$userName and $celebName left the gang. Walked, on their own feet, out of the neon-bled back alleys of Hong Kong. Two street children who had once sheltered from the rain under one eave, now become two people growing old.

"$userName," $celebName said. The two were sheltering again from rain under some eave. Like ten years ago. "Next life, let's not meet in a place like a gang. In a place with no one who teaches that brotherhood is a lie. With no one who plants hate to use us. Let's just — meet under one eave, and start as brothers from the start."

"All right," $userName said. "And that time, don't aim guns at each other. Don't pretend to hate. When it rains, just sit together under the eave. And don't growl that the spot's tight, either."

$celebName laughed. Soaked in rain. That fierce, growling child now laughed brightly.

The two left the pier. Two people who had proven, with that one instant the muzzle trembled, that brotherhood is not a lie. And into old age, they sheltered from rain together under one eave.

wonjin.epilogue:
And now, on a rainy street, $userName sees a person.

A fierce gaze. A curt face. The name unknown, yet the heart recalls betrayal first. A person it seems one ought to hate. A person one shouldn't show one's back to. But the closer $userName comes, something else rises beneath that wariness. 'That person is one who would save me even while pretending to shoot.' The way it was on that pier long ago.

'...Ah, it's you again. I was about to suspect you as a traitor again.'

But this time, $userName does not aim a muzzle. Does not run with hate. Just stands beside that person, as if sitting under one eave. Even if the spot is tight.

$celebName sees $userName. This time, with no need to pretend to shoot, smiles like a brother from the start. Two people who have come the long road of years, at last stand looking the same direction. Catching the same rain.

wonjin.celebRole: mask seller who'd bought up that debt

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName gathered $userName's IOUs one by one, smiling behind a mask. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who'd staked even a last ring, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: Because of that uneasy bond, $userName resented $celebName for waving those IOUs, yet kept seeking the eyes behind that mask. Out of spite, $userName picked a fake mask — only painted to look jeweled — and pushed it off as payment.
paragraphs.jeon: $celebName took that fake mask, pulled $userName's hand for one dance in the middle of the ball, then burned every IOU in a candle flame. The fake mask meant to mock had burned the debt, so $userName was absurdly happy.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of bond can carry an odd pull into this life. So even looking at an empty wallet because of $celebName, you might find a smile.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So you may complain plenty about $celebName, yet end up pressing the checkout button on the limited album.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might grumble at $celebName's casting news, then guard the live broadcast of episode one.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may nitpick $celebName's form, yet find yourself buying the season pass again.

wonjin.title: Two People and an Inheritance
wonjin.logline: Two heirs quarreling over an old man’s inheritance face, at the end of a fog-shrouded manor’s secret, what their hate truly was.

chapters.heading: The Manor in the Fog
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that person was at the entrance of a fog-shrouded manor.

London, 1884. On the outskirts of that city, where gaslight blurred in the fog, stood the great manor of the Blackwood family. A manor with black ivy covering every wall, darkness pooled in every window. $userName had been summoned there by a solicitor's letter. An old man said to be a distant relative had passed away — and $userName, the letter said, was one of the heirs.

Stepping down from the carriage, the manor was half-sunk in fog. The front door creaked open. Out of the fog, the silhouette of another person showed. Already arrived first.

It was $celebName. A pale, beautiful person in black mourning clothes. On that face there was neither grief nor smile. Only a cold, hard blankness, like marble. The fog touched those shoulders, then seemed to skirt them, untouched.

"Another heir, it seems," $celebName said. A low, cool voice.

"And you," $userName answered.

An old solicitor led the two into a drawing room. A fire burned in the hearth, but the room would not warm. With trembling hands the solicitor unfolded the will.

"The deceased named the two of you as heirs. There is, however, a condition." The solicitor cleared his throat. "The inheritance shall not be divided. Only one of the two of you shall have — everything of the Blackwood family. The manor, the land, the whole fortune. The other shall — leave empty-handed."

The eyes of $userName and $celebName met. In that moment, an invisible line was drawn between the two. In the cold air of the drawing room.

"The deceased also wished the two of you to dwell in this manor together for one month," the solicitor went on. "After one month, the true heir shall be revealed. What that means — I do not know. The deceased left only those words."

One month. One manor. And an inheritance to take from the other.

The solicitor left, and only the two remained in the drawing room. The hearth-fire crackled.

"For one month," $celebName opened first. "let us not interfere with each other. Fairly. Whatever trial the deceased prepared, let each pass it by their own strength."

"Agreed," $userName answered.

That was the first promise the two exchanged. A cold promise, an enemy offering an enemy. Hearing it, $userName thought: that person too, like me, is desperate for this inheritance. Behind that pale face, there is something desperate too. That thought made the hate harder still. The more desperate the enemy, the more dangerous.

That day $userName resolved to be wary of one person. The cold heir met in the fog. That blankness colder than the dark of the manor. That was the beginning.

chapters.heading: A Month Under One Roof
chapters.body:
$userName and $celebName dwelt in that manor together for one month.

The manor was vast and grim. Corridors that ran on endlessly, dust-laid portraits, creaking stairs, and at night, an unknown sound from somewhere. The two used separate rooms, but took meals at the same table. Seated facing each other at the two ends of a long table. The space between them was filled by a few wavering candles.

"In one month, the inheritance will be mine," $celebName said during a meal. Setting down a fork.

"That remains to be seen," $userName answered.

The two sharpened their edges that way every day. At the two ends of the table, trading cold words. $userName watched $celebName. Which room of the manor that person frequented, what documents they searched, in which corridor they lingered long. One must know an enemy to seize an inheritance. To win within a month, one must first know the rival.

But that watching made $userName know things unintended too. $celebName, each time the manor's great clock struck midnight, stopped and listened to the sound to the end. Stood long at a window at the corridor's end, watching the fog that would not lift. When a young maid of the manor was cold, silently left her own shawl over a chair and went. That marble-like face held no expression, but $userName came to know that the blankness was, in truth — the face of a person long alone.

$celebName was curt. Never one kind word. Yet on the night $userName lost the way in the fog-bound garden, $celebName came out in person with a lantern to search. Parting the fog.

"Why did you come to find me," $userName asked. Before the lantern. "If I vanished in the fog the inheritance would naturally be yours. With no need to wait a month."

"They say the inheritance of an heir dead in the fog is cursed," $celebName said, tilting the lantern toward $userName. "I have no wish for a cursed inheritance. That's all."

$celebName always wrapped goodwill in the excuse of superstition. That excuse grated on $userName. If only the person were truly cold it would be easy to hate. This person kept letting slip, like fog, gaps hard to hate.

One day, the two met in the manor's library. Both stood before the same bookshelf. Over an old book on which the Blackwood family tree was drawn. When $userName reached out a hand, $celebName's hand went toward the same book. The two hands touched a moment on the book's spine. Both withdrew their hands. As if scalded.

"You first," $celebName said. Curtly.

"...No. You first."

The two ended up looking at that book together. Standing side by side before one shelf. Tracing the family tree with a finger, at a certain point the two fingers pointed almost to the same place. $userName saw it. The point where, on one branch of the family tree, two stems split. At the time $userName did not know what it meant. Only remembered that, before that book, $celebName's profile was — closer than expected.

In the manor there was one locked room. At the far east end, an oak door laid with dust. The solicitor said even he did not know the key. $userName was curious about that room. In that room, perhaps, lay the secret of the inheritance. When the month was about half gone, $userName began to search for the key to that locked room. Rifling every drawer of the manor.

chapters.heading: The Locked Room
chapters.body:
When $userName at last opened the locked room, $celebName was already inside.

$celebName seemed to have found the key first. That person stood at the room's center, looking at one wall. Holding a lantern. When $userName opened the oak door and stepped in, $celebName turned slowly. Across that cold face, a deep crack had formed for the first time.

"...It would have been better not to see," $celebName said. Quietly.

$userName looked at the wall $celebName was watching. And — stopped breathing.

On the wall hung two portraits. Portraits of two children. One child resembled $userName, the other resembled $celebName. In faded frames, the two children were painted side by side.

"That old man," $celebName said. "The deceased who summoned us to this manor — was our grandfather, $userName."

The lantern trembled in $userName's hand. Shadows wavered on the wall.

"Our parents were siblings," $celebName said. "But when they were young, they quarreled over this very inheritance. The fortune of the Blackwoods. The siblings became enemies at the end of that quarrel. Never speaking, living their whole lives hating each other. And — they handed that hate down even to us. We are cousins, yet we never once met. Because our parents kept us apart. So we wouldn't even know the other existed."

$userName looked at the portraits of the two children. Cousins never met. Two children torn apart by an inheritance called hate.

"Grandfather," $celebName said. "all his life regretted it. That his two sons became enemies over an inheritance. That the hate carried even to the grandchildren. That two grandchildren who had never met became each other's enemies before they were even born. So — before he died, he summoned us to this manor."

"Why. To set us quarreling over the inheritance again?"

"To end the hate." $celebName pointed to another wall of the locked room. Raising the lantern. There, framed and hung, was the old man's last letter. $userName read it.

"To my two grandchildren. This inheritance is a trap. If you quarrel over it, you become enemies like your fathers. The hate descends one more generation. But if together you renounce it — then at last you become family. The family I could not make. Dwell one month together. And then choose. The inheritance, or each other."

chapters.heading: An Inheritance Called Hate
chapters.body:
$userName and $celebName sat a long while in the locked room.

Beneath the portraits of the two children. Facing the faces of cousins never met. A single lantern wavered between them. Fog seeped through the crack of the oak door.

"We," $userName said. "were set, even before we were born, to hate each other. With nothing to do with our own wills."

"A hate handed down by our parents," $celebName said. "A hate we did not make. We never once wronged each other, yet on being born — we were told to hate. Over an inheritance. To see each other as an enemy. That was the first inheritance given us. Received before the fortune itself."

$userName recalled the past fortnight. The fortnight of looking, under the excuse of watching $celebName, only at that person every day. The night $celebName came with a lantern to find $userName lost in the fog. The fortnight when, though trading cold words at the two ends of a table, it felt empty when that person was not at the table.

"I," $userName said. "thought through this fortnight that I was being wary of you. Because you were an enemy. But in truth — I was curious about you every day. When you weren't seated at the end of the table, the manor felt too vast. I thought it was hate. It was not hate. It seems it was never once hate."

$celebName lifted the head. That marble-like blankness at last collapsed. To the end. In the lantern's light.

"It was the same for me," $celebName said. "For the first time in my life I met family — and it was you. I was taught you were a person to hate, but on meeting you I could not hate you. You are the only person who noticed that I listen to the midnight clock to the end. That I stand long at the window at the corridor's end, only you saw. In this world the one who has seen me is — only you, $userName. The me that neither my parents nor my grandfather ever saw."

The two looked again at the old man's letter. The inheritance, or each other. In the lantern's light, those words wavered.

"I will renounce the inheritance," $userName said.

"So will I," $celebName said. "The inheritance called hate, and the inheritance called fortune — both, I will renounce. The hate our parents handed us, let us end it in our generation. So it descends no further."

The two took each other's hands in the locked room. Two cousins never met, at last becoming family. Setting down together, with their own hands, the inheritance called hate. The portraits of the two children watched that, from within their faded frames.

chapters.heading: Where the Fog Lifted
chapters.body:
On the day the month ended, the solicitor came again to the manor.

"Has the true heir been revealed," the solicitor asked in a trembling voice.

"We both renounce the inheritance," $userName said.

"The manor, the land, the fortune. All of it," $celebName added.

The solicitor wore a startled face. Then drew from his coat another envelope. An old envelope, sealed with wax.

"The deceased... left one more will for this case," the solicitor said, unfolding it with trembling hands. "He said to open it only if the two of you renounced the inheritance."

The solicitor read.

"If you chose not the inheritance but each other — then you have already received the true inheritance. The thing I never had in all my life. The reconciliation of siblings. The thing called family. So now, take the manor and the fortune. They are no longer a trap. In the hands of two who have ended hate, an inheritance becomes, at last, a blessing. Please, in that house, become the family I could not make."

$userName and $celebName looked at each other. In the fog-bound drawing room.

The two lived in that manor together. In the place where they set down the inheritance called hate, they raised the true inheritance called family. The fog-bound manor was no longer grim. The oak door of the locked room was thrown wide, and the portraits of the two children — grew old beside two cousins who smiled. In every window of the manor, instead of darkness, a lamp was lit.

"$userName," $celebName said one night. The two stood side by side in the garden where the fog had lifted. Stars showed. "Next life, let's meet in a place with no trap like an inheritance. In a place where parents don't hand down hate. Let's just — be born as family from the start. Cousins, or strangers, a relationship where we can take hands from the start."

"Agreed," $userName said. "And that time, even if we first meet at a fog-bound entrance, let's not be wary. Let's not draw any invisible line. Let's just — smile as if we'd met family long awaited. From the start."

$celebName smiled. In the garden where the fog had lifted, under starlight. That marble-like blankness was now bright as spring sun.

The two lived in that manor together into old age. In the place where the inheritance called hate ended, at last two people who had become true family. Listening together to the midnight clock, walking together the fog-bound garden.

wonjin.epilogue:
And now, on some fog-bound street, $userName sees a person.

A pale, beautiful face. The name unknown, yet the heart draws a line of wariness first. A person it seems one ought to hate. A person it seems one ought to quarrel with over something. But the closer $userName comes, beneath that wariness something else rises. 'That person was my family from long ago.' The way it was in that fog-bound manor long ago.

'...Ah, it's you again. It felt as if someone had told me to hate you.'

So this time, $userName draws no line of wariness. Does not follow an inherited hate. Just stands before that person, as if meeting family long awaited.

$celebName sees $userName. This time, instead of cold blankness, smiles like spring sun from the start. Two people who long ago set down together the inheritance called hate — at last face each other as family.

dohwa.celebRole: silk-shop merchant who passed by each day

paragraphs.gi: At a corner of the $era market, $celebName's steps kept stopping before the painting $userName had hung. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole hanging pictures on the street, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: $userName's painting carried a charm that could make anyone glance back once more. $celebName, a bolt of silk under one arm, found their feet halting on their own — watching the painting hand longer than the painting.
paragraphs.jeon: One market day $celebName left a bolt of silk as payment, and inside it $userName's profile had been secretly embroidered. Meaning to buy a painting, the heart had been given away, so $celebName turned red and vanished into the crowd.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of pull can carry a street of gathered eyes into this life. So wherever $celebName is, they might catch your eye first.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So even in a group photo, you may find $celebName's face within a tenth of a second.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So even when $celebName passes by in a brief role, you might recognize that scene at once.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So even $celebName sitting far off on the bench, you may pick out at a glance.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So even in a crowd within a short clip, you might spot $celebName's back first.

dohwa.title: Gyeongseong 1929 — The Modern Boy of That Winter
dohwa.logline: At a sleet-blown Gyeongseong Station, a modern boy who draws every eye is met again at a cafe window.

chapters.heading: Sleet
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that man was in front of Gyeongseong Station, with sleet drifting down.

That year, winter came unusually early. It was only November, and already something — snow or rain, no one could tell — was falling from the sky. People turned up their coat collars and hurried along with quick steps. The line for the streetcar stretched long, and everyone stamped their feet against the cold.

In the middle of that line, one man alone stood perfectly still.

A black Inverness coat. A fedora pulled down at a slant. Around the man who waited for the streetcar with a cigarette in his mouth, the crowd drew back a hand's width — only there. As if an invisible line had been drawn around that spot. The line was packed, but the air beside him stood empty.

$userName stood outside that line, a bundle of books held to the chest. They were books borrowed from the night school. Books that must not get wet. So $userName pulled them in tighter, deeper into the coat.

I shouldn't look, $userName thought. And yet kept looking.

The way the cigarette smoke scattered in the cold air. The way the ungloved fingers slid slowly into the coat pocket. And the absurd illusion that the sleet seemed to skirt around that one pair of shoulders, never touching them.

$userName had never in life seen a person like that. A person who simply stood still, and yet the center of the street tilted toward him. Without realizing it, $userName had leaned a step in that direction.

"...What are you looking at."

Caught.

$userName turned away in a hurry, but it was too late. The man let the cigarette drop, ground it out slowly with the heel of a shoe. Then he stepped out of the line and walked straight this way.

Click. Click.

The sound of the shoes cut clear through the hiss of sleet. That sound outran $userName's heartbeat by a step each time. $userName held the book bundle tighter. Wanted to run, but the feet were stuck to the ground.

"Student. You were just looking at me."

The man stopped before $userName.

"Ah, no."

"You were."

There was no anger in the voice. If anything, a trace of something amused. That flustered $userName more.

He was right there. The man's shadow covered $userName's whole face. Into that shadow the sleet could not enter. For a moment, $userName felt the inside of that shadow was warmer than outside it.

The man bent down slowly. To $userName's eye level. Beneath the hat brim, his eyes showed. Eyes that, though they did not smile, looked as if they were smiling. The instant $userName met those eyes head-on, $userName forgot how to take a breath. Truly — one beat of it.

"Remember this," the man said. A low voice.

"Next time we meet — that time, I won't pretend not to know you."

Before $userName could even reckon what those words meant, the streetcar came. It stopped with a clang of metal. People surged aboard.

The man boarded too, leaving only those words. On the last step, it seemed he glanced back this way for a moment. Through the window, just for a moment. Or perhaps not. Perhaps it only looked that way because $userName wished it.

The streetcar departed. The street became ordinary again. The sleet, the people, the cold — all of it the same.

And yet $userName could not move from that spot.

Not noticing the book bundle in hand soaking through with sleet. Until the calves went numb and the nose-tip flushed red, $userName stood there a long while. Because that one line — that he wouldn't pretend not to know — could not, in the end, be sorted into a threat or a promise.

Later $userName came to know that the man was $celebName. That in all of Gyeongseong, there was no one who did not know him. And that, that night, $userName did not sleep a wink — that, no one knew. Only $userName knew it.

chapters.heading: The Window Guest
chapters.body:
The rumors were many.

Some said he was the youngest son of a rich house. Some said he was a man of unknown origin drifted in from Manchuria. There was talk that he was a modern youth back from study in Japan, and talk that he was the hidden heir of some magnate. Every cafe in Jongno was a place he frequented, and there was even a joke that any schoolgirl he smiled at once would lie sick three days.

$userName scoffed at that joke. To lie sick three days over one man. Was that a thing a person did. Childish, $userName thought.

$userName scoffed that way — until $celebName was sitting in that cafe.

Of all places, it was the cafe $userName worked at.

By day $userName attended the night school's lessons, and in the evening worked at a small cafe in Jongno. Brewing coffee, wiping cups, taking guests. That day too was an ordinary evening. Until the bell on the door rang.

With a cold gust, a black coat stepped in.

$userName froze, rag in hand. It was that man. The man who, in front of Gyeongseong Station, had made $userName forget a breath for one beat.

$celebName looked slowly once around the cafe. Then he went to the innermost seat, the window seat with a clear view down the street. He sat, took off his hat. And then he crooked a finger toward $userName.

"Coffee here. And — you."

$userName doubted both ears.

"...Me?"

"Yes. You."

$userName brewed the coffee. The hands shook, and two drops spilled on the saucer. Wiped with the rag, spilled again. Barely managed to carry the cup to the window. As $userName set the cup down, the man asked.

"What's your name."

"...Why does a guest ask the server's name."

"You won't tell me?"

"I won't tell you."

$userName said it curtly on purpose and turned away. Behind, $celebName smiled for the first time. Soundless, only the corner of the mouth. $userName did not see it and yet felt it. Because that smile warmed the air of the cafe by a degree.

From that day on.

Every day the same hour, the same window, one cup of coffee. And one line each time.

"You've tied your hair up today."

"The other day you looked angry. Who did that?"

"Your hands are chapped. Put salve on them. Leave them and they'll crack."

$userName never smiled, to the end. Answered only short. Set the coffee down and turned away at once. Curtly, coldly. That this drove $celebName to distraction — back then, $userName truly did not know. Nor how long, behind, he watched $userName's turned back.

Another server whispered to $userName.

"$userName. You know that guest only looks at you? He only comes when you're on shift. Other hours, not even a glimpse of him."

$userName pretended not to hear. But that night the words would not leave the head.

$userName did not want to admit it. That when that hour came, the heart reacted first to the door's bell. That on the days he did not come, the window seat looked strangely empty, and all evening the mind sat in that empty place.

What that meant, $userName tried hard not to think. Thinking it seemed it would mean trouble.

A few days later, $celebName sat at the window again. To $userName, setting the coffee down, he said.

"$userName."

$userName's hand stopped.

"...How do you know my name."

"You wouldn't tell me, so I looked it up," $celebName said. That smile hung at the corner of his mouth again. "What you won't give me — it only makes me want to know it more."

That day, for the first time, $userName found it hard to hold a curt face.

chapters.heading: The Black Coat
chapters.body:
Winter deepened.

Snow began to pile on the streets of Jongno. Frost gathered on the cafe windows, and the guests' steps grew rare. Still $celebName came every day. The same hour, the same window.

That day the snow came heavy. When $userName closed the cafe and stepped out onto the street, fat flakes had covered the road white.

And beneath the streetlamp, a black coat stood.

Snow had piled thick on the shoulders. They were the shoulders of someone who had waited a long time.

"...What are you doing here."

$userName asked. The voice trembled. Whether from the cold, or from some other reason, $userName could not tell.

"I'll walk you home," $celebName said.

"$userName. These past few days Jongno is thick with police. Checkpoints are frequent. Don't walk the night roads alone."

It was a voice with no teasing in it. The smile that always hung at his mouth was not there that day. And so $userName could say nothing at all. When he was serious, $userName did not know what to do.

$celebName took off his Inverness coat.

"Oh, don't. It's cold."

"It's fine."

And he draped that coat over $userName's shoulders. The coat was warm with his body heat. It smelled of him. Of cigarettes and cold wind and something that could not be named. Inside that coat, $userName felt small. It was not a bad feeling.

"Just once," $celebName said. "Today only, pretend you'll accept it."

The two walked side by side. The snow fell soundless, and footprints stayed behind in two rows. $celebName walked half a step toward the street side. As if to block the wind from the road himself.

For a long while they walked without a word. Only the sound of snow underfoot.

"$userName," $celebName opened first. "Why do you never once smile for me."

"...Because there's nothing to smile about."

"I come every day."

"Is that something to smile about?"

$celebName stopped walking. $userName stopped too. It was beneath a streetlamp. Into that light, sleet drifted down again. As on the first day they met.

$celebName looked at $userName. Beneath the hat brim, those eyes did not smile that day. They were only serious.

"To me," he said. "that face of yours that won't smile is the thing I want to see every day. Everyone else smiles at me. Even people who've just met me. Only you don't. So I keep wanting to see your face."

$userName did not know what to answer. The heart beat so loud, it was frightening that $celebName might hear the sound.

"...Childish."

That was all $userName managed. $celebName laughed at that. This time with sound.

"Yes. Childish," he said. "That something childish could feel this good — I didn't know it either."

They walked again. Until they reached the front of $userName's home.

There, $userName tried to take off the coat and return it.

"Keep it," $celebName said. "We'll meet again. Give it back then."

$userName carried that coat into the house. That night, $userName could not take it off for a long time. With it draped over the shoulders, paced the room.

This thing $userName had scoffed at as childish — $userName was doing it too. Losing sleep over one man. Unable to take off a coat, using its smell as an excuse. $userName could no longer scoff at those schoolgirls.

Because $userName was just the same.

chapters.heading: The Man's True Self
chapters.body:
$userName came to know $celebName's true self just before spring.

It was a night in February. The cafe had emptied, and $userName was wiping the last cup. $celebName sat at the window seat. That day, in particular, he watched the street a long time. Like a person waiting for something.

$userName set the coffee down beside him.

"Mr. $celebName," $userName asked. "May I ask you something? ...What kind of person are you. There are so many rumors, but I don't know a single true thing."

$celebName was silent a long while. The eyes that watched the street turned slowly back to $userName.

"...I can tell you," he said at last. "To you, I think I can tell."

He lowered his voice.

"I'm not a rich man's son, nor a drifter from Manchuria. I'm a courier who carries funds for the independence movement. Looking like an idler among people is the safest. It draws no suspicion."

$userName set down the cup being wiped. The hand stopped.

"Sitting at this window every day, too," $celebName went on. "From this seat the street is best seen. Who's tailing, where the police are posted. I saw all of it from here."

$userName's chest dropped. That time, sitting in that seat and tossing idle jokes, had in truth been time staked on a life.

"Then," $userName barely asked. "The first time you spoke to me. ...Was that a disguise too?"

$celebName looked $userName straight in the eyes.

"The first three days, yes," he said honestly. "An idler trading silly jokes with a server. Looking that way was safe. So I spoke to you. At first."

At those words, one side of $userName's chest went cold.

"But after that it wasn't," $celebName said. "After three days, I forgot all about disguise. Because you wouldn't smile for me. Because I wanted to see that curt face soften just once. Knowing all the danger, I came every day. To see you."

$userName wept. Meant to hold it back, but could not.

That this man crossed the street every day at the risk of his life. That sitting in that dangerous seat, he idly noted whether $userName's hands were chapped or hair tied up. None of it could be borne. $userName was angry. Not at $celebName, but at this age. An age where even one person caring for another had to be staked on a life.

$celebName rose from his seat. And slowly drew $userName into an embrace.

It was the first time. His arms were as warm as the coat. No — warmer.

"$userName," he said, resting his chin on $userName's head. "When this work is done. When it's all done — I'll live like a real idler. Not a disguise, a real one. Rise late in the morning, loaf all day, sit at the window of the cafe you work in and watch only you all day. I'll live like that. I promise."

Held in those arms, $userName raised a finger.

"...Promise it."

"I promise."

They hooked their fingers. When spring came, when it was all done. That those words could be such warm words, $userName learned for the first time that day.

Outside the window, snow still remained. Even so, $userName believed spring would come soon.

chapters.heading: A Single Note
chapters.body:
That promise was not kept.

Spring came. But $celebName was not there.

The following spring, there was a mass arrest at Gyeongseong Station. One independence organization collapsed whole. $userName heard the news from a street extra-edition. The hand holding that extra trembled. Surely not, surely not, thinking.

For days $celebName did not come to the cafe. The window seat sat empty. $userName wiped that empty seat over and over. As if wiping it would bring him.

He did not come.

Instead, one person came.

A stranger. He opened the cafe door, came in, and without a word set a black bundle on the counter. Then he bowed deeply toward $userName. A long while. And turned and left.

$userName unwrapped that bundle.

There was the black Inverness coat. The coat he had draped over $userName's shoulders. The coat $userName could not bring herself to take off that night.

And in the coat's inner pocket, a single folded note.

$userName unfolded that note. The hands shook so much the writing seemed to waver.

"To $userName.

I'm sorry. I couldn't make it to spring. I'm sorry I couldn't keep the promise.

I had to hand the last document to a comrade. And draw the police's eyes elsewhere. So I stood in that place. The place I first met you, in front of Gyeongseong Station. That place where the sleet drifted. Since I first saw you there, ending it there — strangely, it set my heart at ease.

$userName. Next life let's meet with no disguise. Not pretending to be an idler, let's meet as a real idler from the start. I'll prepare more silly jokes too. I'll make you laugh until you're sick of it. So that time — smile for me at once. Not too late.

Don't wait. Just live well. Eat well, put salve on your chapped hands. And then some winter, if on a sleet-blown day someone in a black coat stands on the street — that's me. Don't pretend not to know me then. I'll recognize you first.

That coat, keep it. Give it back when we next meet."

$userName held that note to the chest and sank down on the cafe floor. Spring sun came through the window and touched $userName's shoulder. It was warm. That was the more unbearable thing.

$userName kept that coat all life long.

Every winter that came, $userName took out that coat. Draped it over the shoulders. The smell of cigarettes faded each year, but the warmth, strangely, remained. On sleet-blown days, $userName searched the streets for a black coat. All life long.

Without finding it, one life of $userName ended.

And a hundred years passed.

dohwa.epilogue:
And now, on a sleet-blown road, $userName sees a man in a black coat.

The name unknown, yet the heart knows first. $userName forgets again how to take a breath for one beat. The feet tilt on their own that way. The way it was in front of Gyeongseong Station, a hundred years ago that day.
