'...Ah, it's you again.'

This life, $userName sees $celebName and smiles from the start. At once. Not too late. As if keeping, this time, the promise unkept a hundred years ago.

And $celebName sees that smile. Amid the sleet, the face that smiles toward him from the very start.

With the face of one who has walked a hundred years, $celebName is at last relieved. That $userName did not pretend not to know him. That $userName smiled at once. That $userName came, not too late.

dohwa.celebRole: regular who always reserved the front row

paragraphs.gi: In $era, the front row always held the same rose perfume — and $celebName. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who quieted the room with song, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: $userName's stage carried a charm, so people laughed and wept along with that expression. $celebName drained their glass until closing, just to catch one of those looks.
paragraphs.jeon: One night, beside the rose $celebName left behind, a single line read: "let tonight's smile be one only I saw." Sitting there as a fan, they'd written a heart and gone, so $userName stared at that seat a long while.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of pull can reach those gathering eyes into this life. So one expression of $celebName's might keep stirring your heart.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So at one cut of $celebName's eye-smile in a stage fancam, your heart may drop.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So even when $celebName only lifts the corner of a mouth on screen, you might hold your breath too.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So one face $celebName makes after a goal could set the mood of your whole day.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So at the moment $celebName laughs absently in a video, your heart might just melt.

dohwa.title: My Bias Recognized Me
dohwa.logline: From the stage he picks out the me in the front row, and a pull that crosses the fan-and-star line begins.

chapters.heading: Front Row
chapters.body:
That $userName got that ticket was a miracle.

Standing, front row. Right at the barricade. After three years of frantic clicking through ticket sales, it was the first such seat ever caught. At the exact second sales opened, $userName's hands shook so badly the payment button was pressed wrong twice. And on the third try, the seat-confirmed screen appeared. $userName screamed alone in the room.

On the day of the show, $userName lined up five hours early. It was winter. The feet went numb and breath fogged. Still it was good. $userName held a light-stick, fidgeting with it, waiting for the line to shrink. Today, $userName would see $celebName from the very closest distance. That one fact made the cold bearable.

$userName had liked $celebName for three years now. A single fancam, seen by chance, was the start. The instant $userName saw that person on stage, a strange feeling came. A first-seen face, and yet — as if seen somewhere before. The true nature of that feeling, $userName never came to know. $userName simply, came to like them. Deeply.

$userName went inside the venue. Found the spot in front of the barricade. The stage was right there. A distance that, with a reaching hand, seemed it could be touched. $userName's heart pounded madly.

The lights went out. The whole hall sank into darkness. And a roar broke out.

Light surged up from the stage floor. At the center of that light, $celebName stood.

$userName forgot to breathe.

It was a face seen a hundred times and more on video. But in person it was different. Completely different. The light seemed to fall differently on that person alone. Dozens stood on the stage together, and yet $userName's eyes kept going to one person only. No one else could be seen.

$celebName sang. Turned. Smiled. At one of those smiles, the whole hall collapsed together.

$userName collapsed too. Waving the light-stick. Calling the name until the throat tore. And, a little, weeping. $userName did not know why the tears came either. They simply came. Like a person who had finally met something long awaited.

It was then.

$celebName's gaze on the stage, sweeping slowly over the audience — stopped.

The front row. Before the barricade. At the very spot where $userName stood.

An illusion, $userName thought. A person on stage could not pick out one of the audience. There were thousands. $userName shook the head. It was the tears, making me see wrong.

But $celebName looked again. Once more. This time, clearly, was looking at $userName. Even while singing, the gaze fixed on $userName alone.

And smiled slightly.

It was not a stage smile. Not a smile aimed at the whole audience. A smaller, truer smile. With the face of someone who had — recognized one person.

$userName, at that spot, lost the strength in the legs.

Even on the way home after the show, $userName kept recalling that smile.

In the subway window, $userName saw the reflected face. An ordinary face. A face that was only one of thousands. And yet $celebName, out of those thousands, had of all faces seen this one. Twice.

$userName shook the head. Don't read meaning into it. A fan reading meaning into a star — the most common, the saddest thing. And yet — shaking it, the smile would not fall away.

chapters.heading: 12:47 a.m.
chapters.body:
$userName did not sleep a wink that night.

Lying in bed, watching the ceiling, $userName replayed that scene a hundred times. $celebName had clearly looked. Had clearly smiled. But what did that mean? Did he do that for any front-row fan? He might. He's a pro. Yes, that must be it.

$userName sorted it that way. Sorted it, and sorted it again. The head could sort it out, but the heart could not. That smile kept coming back.

The next morning, $userName's social media notifications exploded.

In a fancam of that show, $userName was captured. A fan crying in the front row. The video spread fast, and one comment below it had risen to the top.

"Did you see $celebName's expression change when this person cries? Look at 12:47 a.m. Genuinely chilling."

$userName tapped that 12:47 segment of the video. The hands shook.

On stage $celebName, mid-song, looked at the audience. Exactly the spot where $userName had stood. And the expression — changed. From the stage face, for just an instant, into another face. The face of someone who had run into a long-known person on the street by chance. Startled, glad, and somehow wistful.

'It's an illusion.' $userName set the phone face-down. 'It only looks that way because of the fancam quality. People reading meaning into it.'

But that week, $celebName had a live broadcast.

$userName watched it. It was a thing always watched. But that day, one fan asked in the chat.

"How does it feel to see a fan crying at a show?"

$celebName read that question through the screen. Then was silent a moment. Usually he answered right away, glibly. That day he stayed still a long while.

Then he smiled. With eyes that looked at some far place beyond the screen.

"Some people," $celebName said. "feel like someone I've known a very long time. Even though it's clearly the first time. Looking out at the audience from the stage, there are times one person looks like that. Strange, isn't it. I don't know why myself."

$userName, watching that broadcast, dropped the phone.

In the dropped phone's screen, $celebName was still looking at a far place.

chapters.heading: The Fan Signing
chapters.body:
$userName won a spot at a fan signing.

Bought forty copies of the album. The bank account went empty. $userName worried a moment about how to manage next month's living costs. And then stopped. It didn't matter. To see $celebName right before the eyes, with one table between them. For those thirty seconds, a bank account was nothing.

Getting the winning message, $userName practiced all week what to say.

'I got through a hard time listening to your songs.' 'I'll always support you.' 'Please keep performing for a long time.' Ordinary words. Safe words. Words that could be said within thirty seconds. $userName practiced them in front of a mirror. But strangely, it felt like the real thing $userName wanted to say was something else. What that was, $userName did not know either.

On the day of the signing, $userName lined up. The people ahead sat at the table one by one and rose. $userName's turn drew near. The heart climbed to the throat.

The turn came.

$userName sat in the chair before the table. Raised the head.

$celebName saw $userName and — stopped.

Pen in hand. The hand reaching to pull over the next album, halted there. $celebName's eyes widened slightly. Then slowly, very slowly, those eyes softened into a smile.

"...Ah."

$celebName said it small. Small enough no one else would hear. Small enough the staff would not hear.

"The front row."

$userName's heart dropped. Truly, a falling feeling.

"You remember?" $userName barely asked. All the practiced words had flown away.

"I remember," $celebName said, signing the album. But that signing took longer than usual. It looked less like writing than like dragging out the time. The pen kept hesitating on the paper. "From the audience that day, only one person was clearly visible. Out of thousands. I don't know why myself. While I was singing, my eyes kept going that way."

$celebName slid the signed album slowly toward $userName. And for a moment, just a moment, stopped the hand near $userName's. At a distance almost, but not quite, touching.

"This will sound strange," $celebName said. Lowering the voice more. "But it doesn't feel like the first time seeing you. $userName."

$userName stopped breathing.

$userName had not said the name. It had only been written on a post-it on the album cover. But the way $celebName called that name — it was not the pronunciation of someone reading it for the first time. It was the pronunciation of someone who had called it long, very long.

Behind, the staff hurried the next turn. $userName had to rise. The thirty seconds were over.

Behind $userName rising to leave, $celebName's voice followed, small.

"See you again. $userName."

That sounded not like a goodbye, but like a promise.

Leaving the signing hall, $userName held the album tight.

Opened the page $celebName had signed. Beside the writing, $userName's name was there. Clearly the writing $celebName had done. $userName touched that name with a fingertip.

See you again. That one line stayed in the ear. $userName knew it was a courtesy, and yet it kept sounding like a promise. They had only sat across a table for thirty seconds, but those thirty seconds weighed like thirty years.

chapters.heading: The Backstage Corridor
chapters.body:
That night, $celebName made a strange request of the manager.

"That fan... can we invite them to the next show?"

The manager was bewildered. "Suddenly? Who? Why?"

$celebName could not explain. Just — that they had only sat across a table for thirty seconds, and when those thirty seconds ended and $userName rose, it had strangely felt like a loss. Like he had to see them again. Like otherwise he'd miss something else. Such things, he could not bring himself to say. The manager would think it strange.

$celebName himself found it strange. Since debut he had met tens of thousands of fans. Not once had it been like this. But that fan — from the start, did not feel like a first time.

Word came to $userName a week later. From the agency's official account. A message inviting $userName to a backstage tour at the next show.

$userName read that message twenty times. Thought it was a scam. Screenshotted it and sent it to a friend. The friend said it looked like a scam too. But it was real.

On the day of the show, after the show ended. $userName stood in the backstage corridor. A corridor where the heat of the venue still lingered on the walls. Staff hurried back and forth.

And $celebName, just off the stage, walked from down the corridor.

There was no stage light. Hair damp with sweat, a tired face. Just a person. A tired person who had poured out two hours on stage and come down.

But the instant he saw $userName, that tired face — softened again. As at the fan signing.

"You came," $celebName said.

"...Thank you for inviting me," $userName said.

"I wanted to invite you," $celebName said honestly. It was not a stage line. "Calling a fan out separately, I don't do that. Not once since debut. But $userName — it felt like I had to. Like I'd regret it for a long time if I didn't. Strange, isn't it."

At the end of the corridor was a large window. Beyond it the city's night lights spread out. The two stood naturally side by side before that window. For a long while, without a word, they watched the lights.

"Can I ask something like this?" $celebName opened first. "$userName, why did you cry. At that show. At 12:47 a.m."

$userName thought a long while. And decided to be honest.

"I don't know," $userName said. "It's just... watching you stand on the stage, it felt like I'd finally met someone I'd waited for very long. Even though it was clearly the first time. Since I first saw a fancam three years ago, it kept being that way. The feeling of, where have I seen this person."

At those words, $celebName looked at the $userName reflected in the window.

"It's the same for me," $celebName said. The voice trembled a little. "When I saw $userName in that audience. Exactly the same. Where have I seen that. Clearly a person I know. That thought never left me the whole time I was singing."

The city's lights lit the two of them, from beyond the window.

chapters.heading: A Pull That Crosses the Line
chapters.body:
After that show, $userName began to dream the same dream.

It was an unfamiliar place. A place with no stage and no audience. The era, too, unknowable. In the dream $userName held someone's hand, and strained with all strength not to lose that hand. And in the end lost it. The held hand drew away. Each time $userName woke from the dream, the palm felt cold. Like someone who had truly lost something.

The face of that person in the dream was not visible. But $userName knew. Who it was.

It was $celebName.

Through the agency, $userName and $celebName came to exchange occasional greetings. Through official channels, carefully. They were not long greetings. 'I watched the show, it was wonderful.' '$userName, how was your day.' That was all.

And yet at those short lines, $userName's day brightened. The same for $celebName. Even receiving the roar of tens of thousands on stage, the one line $userName sent that day stayed longer in the heart.

Both knew. That between fan and star there was a line. One was a person on stage, one a person in the audience. So the two did not cross that line. Only, standing side by side upon it — they looked at each other a long time.

One day, $celebName asked.

"$userName. Can I ask something strange? Lately I keep dreaming the same dream. In a place with no stage and no audience, a dream of losing someone's hand."

$userName's hand, gripping the phone, went stiff.

"...Me too," $userName said. "I dream that dream too. The dream of losing a hand. When I wake, my palm feels cold."

For a long while, there was no reply from $celebName. Then a message came.

"$userName. We — couldn't we have met in that dream. In that place with no stage and no audience. A very long time ago. We lost each other once then — and that's why, this life, I picked $userName out of the audience so instantly, isn't it. So as not to lose you again."

$userName, reading that message, looked out the window.

Somewhere in the city's lights was $celebName. Not on a stage, but just a person. Dreaming the same dream as $userName, gripping a cold palm like $userName.

This life was not the first time they met. Both now knew it. That at 12:47 a.m., $celebName on stage picking out $userName in the audience — was not discovering someone seen for the first time, but recognizing a hand lost long ago.

$userName held the phone to the chest. Afraid, and at the same time strangely reassured. This life, it felt that hand would not be lost.

dohwa.epilogue:
The two had truly met before.

Very long ago, somewhere with a different name and a different age. In a place with no stage and no audience. There, the two once lost a hand, and so chose to meet again.

That promise carried $userName and $celebName into this life. That is why $userName picked out $celebName instantly among thousands, and why $celebName saw $userName, one person alone, clearly from the audience. At 12:47 a.m., in that brief instant.

Now $userName still watches $celebName's stage. Waves a light-stick, sometimes weeps. Before that face that is first-seen and yet not the first.

And $celebName on stage, sweeping the audience, always stops at one place. There $userName is. The hand lost long ago.

They have met again. This time with a stage between them. Even so, they have, in the end, met again. This life — so as not to lose each other.

dohwa.celebRole: wandering painter who came each day

paragraphs.gi: In $era, a long line formed before the teahouse at opening time, and $celebName was always at its end. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole with a graceful way of pouring tea, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: $userName's teahouse held a charm, so a single cup could make anyone want to return. $celebName filled a sheet of paper each day — drawing the hand that lifted the cup, not the taste of the tea.
paragraphs.jeon: One rainy day, in the teahouse of $celebName's left-behind drawing, there were no guests at all — only $userName, drawn bright. Meaning to draw guests, only the owner had been captured, so $userName quietly held that drawing close.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of pull can carry that drawing light into this life. So in front of $celebName you might smile brighter than usual.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So in front of $celebName a silly grin you don't usually wear might slip out.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So at $celebName's signing event you may be so nervous you smile twice as much as usual.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So seeing $celebName at the stadium, you might stop cheering and just grin to yourself.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So watching $celebName's live, you may sit there beaming all alone at the screen.

dohwa.era: a magic kingdom of spirits and mage-towers
dohwa.title: The One Who Calls the Moon
dohwa.logline: A genius of the mage-tower is drawn to the me whose body gathers spirits, and the two perform a forbidden rite together.

chapters.heading: The Child Spirits Gather To
chapters.body:
$userName was a strange child from a young age.

When $userName walked, wind spirits followed the hem of the clothes. When $userName wept, water spirits gathered and drank in the tears. When night came, moonlight lingered especially long, especially late, at $userName's window alone. As if it meant to look in upon $userName.

The village people feared $userName. The child the spirits gather to. Some called it a good omen, some an ill one. Either way, $userName was lonely. Good omen or ill, no one came near. The children would not play with $userName, and the adults watched $userName from afar and murmured.

Beside $userName were only the spirits. But spirits were not warm. Spirits could not comfort $userName. They only gathered.

That day too $userName sat alone in a forest clearing. The place farthest from the village. The place no one came to. Light spirits circled slowly around $userName. Like a small cluster of stars, soundless.

"...Astonishing."

A voice came. $userName raised the head.

A man stood there. A person in the robe of the mage-tower. A mage. And not an ordinary mage. The robe's emblem was that of an archmage. For an archmage of the tower to come to a frontier forest like this was — unheard of.

"I've studied spirit-lore all my life," the man said, drawing slowly closer. His eyes followed the spirits circling $userName. As if entranced. "This many spirits gathering to one person, I've never seen. Never even in a book. What — are you?"

$userName shrank. It was that question again. The question of what. The question heard all of life. People always asked $userName as a 'what,' never as a 'who.'

"...I'm just $userName," $userName said small.

The man stopped. Then, smiled.

The one called the cold genius of the tower, in that clearing, smiled for the first time. A smile that seemed to slip out without his knowing.

"Right. Sorry. I asked wrong," the man said. "Let me ask again. I am $celebName. A mage of the tower. Who are you, $userName?"

At that question, $userName's heart leapt once, large.

All of life, no one had asked $userName that way. They asked only what, never who. There had been no one to ask who. $userName did not even know how to answer it. Had never had to answer it.

$celebName came one step closer toward $userName. The spirits did not guard against him; they quietly made way. The spirits, too, seemed to judge this person all right.

"$userName," $celebName said. "I want to study you. No —"

$celebName stopped his words. His face showed he was startled himself at what he had been about to say.

"No. Not study. I want to know you more. Not what you are, but who."

At those words, for the first time in life, $userName was not afraid of someone coming near. This man was the first person who, even seeing the spirits around $userName, did not run.

chapters.heading: The Tower Genius
chapters.body:
$celebName was a famous person in the mage-tower.

A genius who became archmage at the youngest age. And the coldest person. He mixed with no one, kept no one near. He cared for nothing but magic, they said. People looked up to him, but could not come close.

That $celebName began to stay in the frontier village. Because of $userName.

At first he called it research. Every day $celebName came to the forest clearing and asked $userName about the spirits. How $userName felt the spirits, what the spirits said, which spirit came first. $userName answered, stumbling. They were things no one had ever asked all of life. Answering, $userName realized — that $userName knew this much about spirits.

"The spirits are lonely," $userName said one day. "Like me."

$celebName stopped his pen. The hand writing something halted there. He looked at $userName. Not with eyes that saw a research subject, but with eyes that saw a person.

"Are you lonely?"

Instead of answering, $userName lowered the head. The word lonely, $userName had never spoken aloud. Speaking it, it felt $userName would truly become lonely.

$celebName was silent a long while. Then said, small.

"I was lonely too. In the tower. No one came near. A genius, they said, cold. They all feared me. Looked up to me, and feared me. That those two could come together, I learned in the tower."

"...Just like me."

"It seems so. Just like."

From that day, $celebName's questions changed. Questions about spirits grew fewer, questions about $userName grew more. What color $userName liked. What $userName's childhood dream was. Which star in the night sky $userName liked best. What $userName feared most. What $userName most wanted to have.

Each time $userName was startled. Because no one had ever asked such things. And $userName came to love the time of answering those questions. Of the whole day, that time was best.

When night came the two sat side by side in the clearing. Light spirits gathered around $userName, and in that light $celebName's profile looked soft. Not the cold genius of the tower, but just the face of one person long lonely.

One night, $celebName, watching the light spirits, said.

"$userName. Being near you is strange. You make me more curious than magic. All my life I knew only magic. More than any book of the tower, I want to know you."

The spirits around $userName, at those words, glowed bright. As if the spirits had known $userName's heart first. While $userName did not yet know the name of that heart.

One night, $celebName told $userName his own story.

When he first entered the tower, he was seven, he said. His talent showed too early, so he grew up in the tower, apart from family. There were no peers, and the masters saw him only as 'a talent to be studied.'

"I have no memory of anyone calling my name with tenderness," $celebName said. "They all called me 'Archmage $celebName.' Even at seven years old."

At those words, $userName quietly took $celebName's hand. The spirits gathered over the two hands, wrapping them in small light.

chapters.heading: A Withering Body
chapters.body:
The problem was $userName's body.

That the spirits gathered to $userName was not a blessing.

What $celebName found at the end of his study was cruel. The spirits, gathering to $userName, were taking $userName's life-force, little by little. The spirits did it with no malice. They simply gathered to the light that was $userName. As moths gather to a flame. And that flame was, slowly, going out.

$userName was fated to wither faster than an ordinary person. There had been a reason the village called it an ill omen.

The day $celebName found that out, for the first time he lost his composure. That ever-calm face collapsed.

"$userName," he said. The voice trembled. "Listen to me. There's a way."

$userName looked at him quietly.

"There's a rite that binds you and the spirits. Instead of the spirits taking your life, another mage shares the burden. The weight of the spirits, divided between two," $celebName said. "But — it's a forbidden rite. The tower will never allow it. Once bound, it cannot be undone."

"...Then no," $userName said.

"I'll do it."

$userName raised the head. "$celebName."

"I'll carry half the burden of your life," $celebName said. "Do that rite, and you and I are bound for life. If one hurts, the other hurts. If one's lifespan shortens, so does the other's. If one dies — so does the other."

"That's why it can't be done!"

$userName shouted for the first time. $userName, who all of life had lived with only a small voice, that day for the first time raised a cry.

"Why would you do this for me. We've barely met. You're the archmage of the tower. A person with a long road of life ahead. Why would you — for an ill omen like me."

$celebName came before $userName. And looked $userName straight in the eyes. The cold genius's eyes were hotter than ever.

"You're not an ill omen," $celebName said. "Those words, never speak them from your mouth again."

$userName closed the mouth.

"We've barely met and yet it's like this," $celebName went on. "I can't imagine a world without you. Clearly we've barely met, and yet it feels I've known you all my life. It feels I've found again a person I had lost. So this is — not reason, $userName. I can't explain it. It's just, you and I have to be bound. As if it was decided that way from the start."

That night, the two went to the clearing. Light spirits gathered more than ever before. As if they had waited for this moment.

$celebName began to chant the spell of the forbidden rite.

Moonlight came down over the two, long and slow.

chapters.heading: The Exiled Genius
chapters.body:
The tower's envoy came to the frontier village a few days after the rite ended.

A forbidden rite caught at once on the tower's detection web. Who, where, what rite was performed — the tower did not fail to know.

The envoy unfolded the tower's order before $celebName. Stripped of the rank of archmage. Permanent exile from the tower. Forbidden access to the spellbooks. Everything built up over a lifetime, written on a single sheet of paper.

"$celebName," the envoy said. "I will give you a last chance. The tower will study a way to reverse the rite. Sever the knot with that child and return to the tower, and your rank will be restored. You are the youngest archmage in the tower's history. Not a person to wither in this frontier with an ill omen."

$userName was listening. Hidden behind the corner of the house. The chest sank. $celebName had to go back. To the tower. To his place. $userName could not let him lose all of that because of $userName.

As $userName moved to step out from the corner, $celebName spoke first.

"I have no need of a way to reverse it," $celebName said, pushing the order away with a hand. "No need of the rank. Nor of the tower."

"$celebName. Think well. Over a single ill omen—"

"Not an ill omen," $celebName cut the envoy off. The voice was low and firm. "That child is not an ill omen but $userName. And I, before being the tower's archmage, am $userName's person. That order — was decided the day I performed the rite. No, before that. The instant I first saw that one in that forest clearing."

The envoy glared at $celebName a long while, then gathered the order and left.

$userName came out from the corner. Tears had gathered.

"Why did you do that," $userName said. "You could have gone back. To the tower. Had your rank again."

$celebName walked toward $userName. And wiped $userName's wet eyes.

"$userName," he said. "The tower gave me a place. Gave me honor. But not once did it ask who I was. All my life it called me only 'the genius $celebName.' What the tower saw was my talent, not me."

$celebName took $userName's hand.

"You were different. You asked me. What color I liked, what star I liked. What the tower never asked all my life, you asked in a forest. So — between the tower and you, if I had to choose, there's nothing to ponder. I'll be beside the one who recognized who I am."

At those words, $userName decided not to feel sorry anymore. Feeling sorry was treating $celebName's choice as a light thing.

From that day the two lived not as an exiled genius and a child called an ill omen — but simply as $celebName and $userName.

chapters.heading: Two Lives Bound
chapters.body:
The exiled genius, and the child called an ill omen.

Those were the names the world had pinned on the two. But the two shed those names and built a small house near the frontier forest. Sharing the fate that, if one hurt, the other hurt.

The burden of the spirits that had been gnawing at $userName's life, $celebName now carried half. $userName no longer withered fast. That the two lifespans were bound to one line meant — that they could grow old together.

The spirits that had gathered to $userName alone now circled the two of them together. Light spirits wrapped the house round like stars.

The village people still kept the two at a distance. Still, $userName was no longer lonely. $celebName was beside $userName.

"$celebName," $userName asked one night. "Don't you regret it? You threw away the tower, the honor, the archmage's seat. All over one person, me."

$celebName took $userName's hand. It was the very hand bound by light at the rite.

"I was lonely all my life in the tower," he said. "People looked up to me, but no one came near. Honor never once eased the loneliness. The archmage's seat was never once warm."

$celebName turned the head toward $userName.

"But here I'm not lonely. Because there's you, and the spirits. You did that in one stroke. What the tower could not do all my life, you did at once in a forest clearing. So I didn't lose — I gained. I lost the tower and gained you."

That day $userName knew.

That two people long lonely, having met each other, had become not lonely. The spirits had not bound the two. Nor had the rite joined them. The two had called to each other. The spirits had only heard that call before people did. And the rite had only given the call a form.

On bright-moon nights, $userName called the moonlight by old habit. Raise a hand, and moonlight came down long, wrapping the two together.

"You truly are one who calls the moon," $celebName said, smiling in that moonlight.

"No," $userName answered. $userName smiled too. $userName, who all of life had nothing to smile about, now smiled often. "I called $celebName. All my life. Long before we met, I was calling. The moon only heard the call in my place."

At those words, $celebName drew $userName into an embrace. The light of the fate that bound the two, in that moment, glowed warm.

dohwa.epilogue:
Two lives bound by a forbidden rite — if one vanishes, so does the other.

So the two closed their eyes on the same day, at the same hour. Holding hands. The hand bound by light at the rite, never letting go to the end.

And that light did not vanish. A knot of fate does not come undone, even crossing the ages.

Now $userName, before a first-seen $celebName, feels the chest bound for no reason. The hand goes that way on its own. As if the place once bound by light, long ago, still remembers.

$celebName is the same. Seeing $userName, he feels he has found again a lost half.

There are no spirits. No tower. Even so the two call to each other again. As they always have, long before meeting.

dohwa.celebRole: poor painter who drew only that face

paragraphs.gi: In $era, painters jostled to take $userName as their portrait model, and only $celebName quietly lifted a brush. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole always chosen for portraits, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: $userName's face carried a charm, so once the painting began it was hard to set the brush down. Short on money for paint, $celebName drew $userName alone over a hundred times, and each time felt the flutter of a first sight.
paragraphs.jeon: One day a fire swept the studio and the paintings burned, and $celebName was first to run out clutching one portrait of $userName. With everything ablaze, only one face had been saved, so $userName understood that heart at last.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of pull can carry those gathering eyes into this life. So you might be the first thing held in $celebName's gaze.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So when $celebName sweeps the camera on stage, the look might seem to land your way.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So watching $celebName's work, that gaze can feel as if it sees you beyond the screen.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So when $celebName scans the stands, the eyes may seem to stop at your seat.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So when $celebName looks at the camera in a video, it might feel like your eyes met.

dohwa.title: The Qin of Jiangnan
dohwa.logline: A wandering swordsman's feet are bound by the music of a player called the fairest in Jiangnan.

chapters.heading: The Tune of the Water-Town
chapters.body:
Qing-dynasty Jiangnan, a water-town where canals cut through the village.

Water flowed between house and house, and people came and went by boat. Lanterns swayed, reflected on the water. In the finest courtesan house of that water-town was $celebName.

Called the fairest in all Jiangnan. But what truly entranced people was not the face. It was the qin. When $celebName played the qin, even the water of the canals paused its flow for a moment — so the talk ran through the water-town. They called it exaggeration, but anyone who heard that music once called it exaggeration no longer.

$userName was a wandering swordsman. One sword and one bundle. Drifting the rivers and lakes with no fixed place. Not even knowing what was sought, simply walking because the legs would not stop. That day too, walking so, $userName stepped into that water-town. Searching for a place to lodge a night.

Walking along the canal, a tune flowed from somewhere.

$userName stopped walking.

All of life, drifting the rivers and lakes, $userName had heard every kind of sound. The sound of sword-wind, people's screams, the noise of taverns, the last breath of the dying. But that tune was unlike any of those sounds. It bound $userName's feet. From the hand gripping the sword, for no cause, the strength drained away.

$userName followed that tune. At the end of the canal, a courtesan house bright with lanterns. At its pavilion window sat $celebName, playing the qin.

$userName stood frozen on the spot. With the bundle slung, with the sword belted.

The tune ended. $celebName slowly raised the head. Below the pavilion, a wandering one with a sword belted was looking up.

The two pairs of eyes met.

$celebName was used to countless men looking. To see the fairest of Jiangnan, men came from far places. But that wanderer's eyes were — different. Not eyes that coveted a face. They were the eyes of someone who had heard the tune. The eyes of someone who had truly heard $celebName's qin.

"...You there, wanderer," $celebName said from the pavilion. "How did my tune strike you."

"More than any sound heard in all my life," $userName answered. Without knowing it, the words in the heart came straight out. "It bound my feet."

$celebName, holding the qin, looked down a long while at the wanderer first seen. Even after the tune had stopped, a long while.

The innkeeper told $userName the story of that courtesan house. The fairest of Jiangnan, $celebName, was by birth of the line of a renowned musician, but the household had fallen and at a young age $celebName had been sold to the courtesan house. The qin had been learned from the dead father. So $celebName's tunes carried one thread of sorrow laid through them, the innkeeper said. What bound the listener's feet was that sorrow.

$userName emptied a wine cup hearing this. All of life, drifting, $userName had seen sorrow until sick of it. But the sorrow of that tune was — strangely, impossible to turn from. As if someone were playing $userName's own sorrow in $userName's place.

chapters.heading: The Swordsman With Bound Feet
chapters.body:
$userName could not leave that water-town.

The plan had been to lodge a single night. But the next day, and the day after, $userName could not leave. Each night, $userName's feet turned toward the courtesan house at the end of the canal. Standing below the pavilion, $userName heard $celebName's qin. Until the tune ended, without a stir.

$celebName recognized the wanderer. Every day the same spot, below the pavilion. With a sword belted. One who did not try, like the other men, to come up to the pavilion, but simply heard the tune and quietly vanished.

"Wanderer," $celebName asked one night from the pavilion. "How is one who drifts the rivers and lakes bound so long to a single village."

"The tune holds me," $userName answered.

"The tune, or the person."

$userName could not answer. $celebName laughed small. At that laugh the pavilion lanterns swayed. The lights on the water swayed with them.

A few days later, $celebName called $userName up to the pavilion. It was the first time. $celebName did not show the qin up close to just anyone. Even when a rich man of Jiangnan paid great coin, $celebName did not seat him beside the qin.

$userName climbed to the pavilion. $celebName, tuning the qin strings, said.

"Wanderer. What is it you seek in the rivers and lakes."

"I do not know," $userName answered honestly. "I have drifted long, and forgotten what I seek. At first it seems I set out seeking something, but I walked too long and forgot what it was. I simply walked. Because the legs would not stop."

"Yet here you stopped."

"So I did. Here I stopped."

$celebName tuned the strings. Then began to play a tune. That tune was softer than any tune called the finest of Jiangnan. It was a tune $celebName played for one person alone.

"This tune," $celebName said between the notes. "is for you. For the place where the legs that drifted the rivers and lakes stopped. A tune offered to that place."

That night, $userName knew for the first time. That though the way of gripping a sword had been learned all of life, the way of gripping a heart had never once been learned. And that that heart — was now, up in the pavilion, swaying along the qin strings.

On the way down from the pavilion, $celebName called $userName to a stop.

"Wanderer," $celebName said. "The smell of blood is steeped into your sword. You have cut many people."

$userName stopped. Could not deny it.

"In the rivers and lakes, if one does not cut, one is cut," $userName said. "I survived that way. But surviving, to do what — that thought always came. I cut and cut, walking, not even knowing where I went."

$celebName plucked a qin string once. A clear sound spread through the pavilion.

"It would be well if it were decided now, what that sword will protect," $celebName said. "A sword that cuts and a sword that protects — though the same sword, they carry a different weight, I have heard."

$userName chewed over those words all the way back to the inn. A sword that protects. It was a thing $userName had never once thought of in all of life.

chapters.heading: A Heart Within Silk
chapters.body:
$celebName's heart was all set into the qin.

In words, $celebName said not one thing. For the fairest of Jiangnan to give a heart to a nameless wandering swordsman was, in itself, a dangerous thing. The courtesan house master, the rich men of the water-town, would not stand for it. So $celebName spoke only through the tune.

$userName heard that tune every day, and every day understood a little more. What was set within the tune.

One day $celebName handed $userName a bolt of silk.

"The wanderer's bundle is all worn through," $celebName said. "Make a new bundle with this silk."

$userName took that silk and returned to the inn. Spread the silk under the lamp. And — stopped.

At the inner edge of the silk, an embroidery had been worked. Very small. In a place no one would find unless they spread it open on purpose.

It was $userName's profile. The figure below the pavilion, sword belted, listening to the tune.

$celebName had embroidered, stitch by stitch, in the daytime hours when the qin was not played, the $userName he looked down upon from the pavilion every night. It must have taken several days.

$userName looked at that silk a long while. Drifting the rivers and lakes, $userName's hand had not trembled though it cut people with the sword — but the hand that touched that embroidery trembled.

The next night, $userName went below the pavilion. $celebName stopped the tune and looked down.

"I saw the silk," $userName said.

$celebName's face flushed red, visible even in the lamplight. $celebName clutched the qin and, for the first time, looked away.

"...I said make a bundle," $celebName said. "I never said look at the inside."

"I looked at the inside," $userName said. "And now, I can never leave this water-town."

$celebName looked at $userName over the qin. The one called the fairest of Jiangnan, before a single wanderer, with a face that had all its heart found out. That face was brighter than the pavilion lanterns.

$userName did not sleep that night.

Unfolded the embroidery inside the silk again and again. Stitch by stitch, $userName reckoned the time $celebName had spent thinking of $userName, lifting the needle. With what heart someone who only played the qin up in the pavilion had worked that embroidery in the daytime hours when the qin was set down.

$userName had never had, in all of life, a person who made something for $userName. A wanderer had no such person. One bundle, one sword. That was all $userName owned.

But now, one bolt of silk had been added. On that silk was $userName's profile. Proof that someone had watched $userName so long, so closely.

$userName held that silk to the chest and, for the first time, admitted that a wanderer's life had been lonely.

chapters.heading: The Hired Blades
chapters.body:
The hearts of the two soon became talk in the water-town.

The rumor spread fast along the canals. That the fairest of Jiangnan had given a heart to a single nameless wandering swordsman.

The courtesan house master pressed $celebName. A rich man of the water-town threatened $userName. That a mere wanderer dared look upon the fairest of Jiangnan. That night, several hired blades came to the inn $userName lodged at.

$userName drew the sword.

It was a sword drawn countless times, drifting long. But the sword of that day was — different. It was the sword of one who had something to protect. In the drifting days, $userName's sword had no weight. Because $userName did not know what it was swung for. That day, weight was set into $userName's sword for the first time.

$userName drove off the hired blades. Wounded them, but did not kill them. The sword of one who has something to protect does not cut people carelessly.

After driving off the blades, $userName stood a long while in the inn yard.

A drop of blood fell from the sword-tip. But that day's drop of blood was unlike before. Before, after cutting, the heart was left empty. That day — the heart was full. What a sword of one who has something to protect is, $userName came to know for the first time in that yard.

$celebName heard of the commotion from the pavilion. That hired blades had gone to strike the wanderer. $celebName clutched the pavilion rail all night and gazed at the end of the canal. That $userName would be safe. The one who had never prayed for anyone's safety, prayed all night that night.

When at dawn $userName appeared below the pavilion, $celebName nearly forgot the qin and ran down the stairs. Barely held back. The water-town must not see the fairest of Jiangnan running down to a wanderer. Still, that heart alone reached $userName before the tune did.

That night, $userName went to the pavilion at the end of the canal.

"$celebName," $userName called that name for the first time. "Leave this water-town with me."

$celebName, holding the qin, was silent a long while.

"It is a wanderer's life," $celebName said at last. "A life with no fixed place. I have lived all my life only in this pavilion. I know nothing but the qin. Whether I could bear a drifting life, I do not know."

"Let us leave with the qin in your arms," $userName said. "I will open the road with the sword. You fill that road with the tune. Anywhere in the rivers and lakes, wherever your tune reaches is our home. It is not that there is no fixed place — wherever you are is the fixed place."

$celebName, holding the qin, came slowly down from the pavilion. The one who all of life had lived only up in the courtesan house pavilion, for the first time set foot on those stairs and came down. One step, one step.

On the canal water a small boat floated. The two boarded that boat together.

chapters.heading: The Tune at the Canal’s End
chapters.body:
The boat slid slowly along the canal.

The lanterns of the water-town drew away behind. $celebName turned the back on the courtesan house lived in all of life, the pavilion, all those familiar things. Yet on the boat there was no fear in $celebName's face. $userName was beside.

"Wanderer," $celebName tuned the qin strings on the boat. "Give this tune a name. It is the first tune of leaving with you, so it must have a name."

$userName thought a moment. Watching the lantern light scatter over the water.

"The Qin of Jiangnan," $userName said. "Let us call it so."

"The Qin of Jiangnan."

"It is the tune that bound my feet, bound my heart, and at last brought me to your side. A tune begun in Jiangnan — so let us call it so."

$celebName rolled the name once more in the mouth, then smiled. And began to play.

That tune — the one said to make even the water of the canals pause its flow — this time flowed for one person alone. On the boat, along the canal, into the dark.

On that boat, $userName at last knew what had been sought all of life.

A place for the feet to stop. It was not a place. It was a person. All of life $userName had drifted seeking a place, but the thing that should have been sought was a person. And that person was now, on the boat, playing the qin.

"$celebName," $userName said. "Now even if the legs do not stop, I am not afraid. Wherever I drift, you are beside me. It is not drifting — it is going together."

$celebName, playing the tune, looked at $userName.

"Then let us drift together all our lives," $celebName said. "You opening the road with the sword, I filling it with the tune. So that wherever the Qin of Jiangnan reaches, one line of our story is left behind."

The boat slid into the dark. The qin's tune followed behind it. The last lantern of the water-town lit the two people's boat a long while.

When the boat had wholly left the water-town, dawn began to break.

Morning sunlight broke over the water. $celebName stopped the qin and looked back once at the receding water-town. It was the place lived in all of life. The sorrow was there too, and the qin was learned there.

"$celebName," $userName said. "Do you regret it?"

$celebName shook the head.

"The water-town was a place that caged me," $celebName said. "In that pavilion I was caged all my life and only played the qin. People came to hear my tune, not to see me. Only you were different. You heard the tune — and saw me."

$celebName held the qin again.

"Now this qin is not a caged tune, but a tune that departs. A tune that goes with you. So in my departing heart, there is not one heavy thing."

Sunlight lit the two people's boat. The Qin of Jiangnan flowed over the morning canal.

dohwa.epilogue:
The wandering swordsman and the musician of Jiangnan drifted the rivers and lakes together ever after.

$userName opened the road with the sword, and $celebName filled that road with the tune. Wherever the two stayed, one strand of the Qin of Jiangnan was left behind.

And that tune, crossing the ages, flows still.

Now $userName, if $celebName's voice or song is heard from somewhere, the feet stop for no reason. Stopping whatever was being done, $userName follows that sound. The legs that drifted the rivers and lakes still remember how to stop before that tune alone.

And $celebName, too, knows that footfall. On a stage or anywhere, the one person stopped before his tune — $celebName always recognizes them. The way he recognized, from the pavilion, that wanderer below it. This life too the two found each other again, with a tune between them.

The tune bound $userName again, and $celebName came again to stand beside. Because the Qin of Jiangnan has never once stopped playing.

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $userName's recital days always sold out, and the front row always held $celebName. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who filled the hall, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: $userName's playing carried a charm, so every listener went home humming that melody. $celebName wrote down the tremor of the bow-holding wrist more eagerly than the notes.
paragraphs.jeon: One night, on the last page of $celebName's copied score, instead of notes it read: "the one beat where you rest is the part I love most." Meaning to copy a piece, a person had been written, so $userName looked at that line a long while.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of pull can reach that drawing melody into this life. So you might keep leaning an ear toward a single word of $celebName's.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So one offhand line $celebName drops in a live, you may chew over all day.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So a short answer $celebName gives in an interview, you might listen to again and again.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So a one-line remark $celebName leaves after a match, you may read until you know it by heart.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So a word $celebName lets slip at a video's end, you might capture and save.

dohwa.title: You Beyond the Screen
dohwa.logline: A rookie and a great star hide their true hearts amid scandal and the flash of cameras.

chapters.heading: The Audition Hall Corridor
chapters.body:
1955, Los Angeles. The film studio was a factory that sold dreams.

Hundreds knocked on that factory's door every day. To get a face onto the screen once. To receive a single line of dialogue. And most of them left the factory without earning a name.

$userName was at the lowest place in that factory. A rookie actor. Still a nameless bit player. A person competing with a hundred others for a single line.

That day $userName sat in the audition hall corridor, waiting for a turn. The script in hand was sweat-soaked, the letters smudged. Compared to the splendid dress of the actors seated nearby, $userName's clothes were shabby. $userName kept opening and closing the hands on the knees. It was the hundredth rehearsal of the lines.

A stir rose at the end of the corridor. People all turned their heads one way.

$celebName walked through.

$userName knew him too. Could not not know him. A face hung on the marquee of every theater in the country. A great star on every magazine cover. Just $celebName passing through the corridor changed the air. People held their breath. Light seemed to move along with that person.

$celebName walked, meeting no one's eyes. For a star that was a familiar thing. To only receive gazes, not return them. To, while receiving hundreds of gazes, set one's own gaze nowhere.

But passing in front of $userName, $celebName — stopped.

$userName raised the head. $celebName was looking down at $userName. The great star's gaze, clearly resting on one nameless bit player.

"...The script," $celebName said.

"Yes?"

"Grip the script like that and the letters all smudge," $celebName said. Then, smiled slightly. It was not the radiant smile of the magazine covers. A smaller, truer smile. "Ease the tension. You look — like someone who'll make it."

$celebName walked on again. Bringing hundreds of gazes along again.

$userName sat there, looking down at the smudged script.

Someone who'll make it. That one line brightened, for a moment, that shabby, long corridor. $userName chewed those words a long while. It could be the empty courtesy a star always tosses to a bit player. But — that gaze had not been the gaze of empty courtesy.

Waiting for the turn, $userName chewed over that one line.

An actor seated nearby said, as if mocking. "Don't get excited because $celebName spoke to you. People like that toss a line to anyone passing in the corridor. Pretending to be kind is part of the job."

$userName did not answer. Maybe those words were right. But — the gaze when $celebName had stopped was not a gaze tossed to anyone. $userName knew the difference. A person who has lived all of life being ignored comes to tell a true gaze from an empty one.

Inside the audition hall, $userName recited the lines without trembling. Someone who'll make it — that one line propped up the back.

chapters.heading: The Same Film
chapters.body:
$userName was cast in that film.

It was not a large role. Still there were three whole lines of dialogue. For $userName it was a first. Receiving the casting call, $userName stood a long while in the small apartment, holding the receiver.

Arriving at the set on the first day, $userName learned who the lead of that film was.

It was $celebName.

$userName's heart dropped. To stand in the same film as the great star who had tossed a line in the audition corridor. For a chance — it was a strange chance.

On set, $celebName was far. The lead actor's trailer was separate, staff surrounded him, the director hung on his every word. $userName waited for a turn in a corner of the set. For three lines of dialogue.

But $celebName came over toward $userName every break.

At first $userName thought it chance. The second time too. By the third or so, $userName knew. $celebName was coming on purpose. The lead actor, going out of the way, to the corner where a bit player sat.

"$userName," $celebName came holding two coffees. Handed one to $userName. "Set coffee is terrible, but at least it's warm."

"...A great star brings a bit player coffee?"

"Great star." $celebName repeated the words, then laughed. "I hate that. Not that — just call me $celebName."

$userName took the coffee. It was, truly, warm.

"$userName," $celebName said. Where there were no cameras, $celebName's voice was lower, softer. Not a stage voice. "When I first saw you in the audition hall, it was strange. Clearly a first-seen face — but it felt like I'd seen you somewhere. Very long ago. So my feet stopped without my knowing. In that corridor."

$userName did not know what to answer.

Because $userName had felt the very same. From the instant $celebName passed through the corridor. That feeling of first-seen, and yet not the first.

$userName came to wait for that coffee every day.

When the break bell rang, $userName's eyes turned, without knowing it, toward the lead's trailer. Whether $celebName was walking over with two coffees. When he came, $userName's day brightened, and on days the shoot ended early and he did not come — the day was strangely long.

"$userName, why did you become an actor?" $celebName asked one day.

$userName thought a moment. "Because the people inside the screen don't look lonely. Always with someone, always loved by someone. I envied that."

$celebName was silent a long while at those words. Then laughed small. "The loneliest one inside the screen is me, $userName. When the applause ends, there's no one."

That day $userName learned for the first time that there is such a thing as a great star's loneliness.

chapters.heading: The Flash
chapters.body:
As the shoot went on, the two grew close.

Every break they talked in a corner of the set. $celebName taught $userName acting. How not to tremble before a camera. How to set true feeling into three lines. How to speak with the expression.

$userName improved fast. With $celebName beside, the camera was not frightening. The day the director nodded for the first time at $userName's acting, $celebName smiled brighter than anyone.

$celebName changed too. The staff murmured. That cold great star, lately, laughed often. Because of whom, the set people all knew.

The problem was the cameras. Not the cameras inside the film, but the cameras outside the film.

In Hollywood there were always reporters. People who lived on scandal. The great star $celebName's every move was their prey.

One day, $celebName drove $userName off the set in his car. To eat dinner at a small restaurant. It was an ordinary dinner. The first time the two sat across each other with no cameras and no staff.

The instant they stepped out of the restaurant, flashes burst.

It was reporters. Someone had tipped them. The sound of camera shutters poured down like rain. $celebName reflexively hid $userName behind his back.

"$celebName! What is your relationship with this rookie actor?"

"A new lover? Give us a word!"

$celebName said nothing. He gripped $userName's hand tight and moved them quickly through the crowd. Flashes kept bursting behind.

In the car, $celebName was silent a long while, gripping the wheel.

"$userName," he opened at last. "I'm sorry. I dragged you into this because of me."

"$celebName," $userName said. "It's all right."

"It's not all right," $celebName said. "By tomorrow your face will be in every magazine. They'll write that an unproven rookie is using a great star. Hollywood — is that kind of place."

$userName looked at the city lights outside the window. And said quietly.

"$celebName. Then we'll have to hide our true hearts. The cameras won't leave us be."

$celebName lifted a hand from the wheel and took $userName's hand.

"Even hidden," he said. "that doesn't make the heart vanish. Hiding it and not having it are different."

In the silence of the car, $userName looked at $celebName's profile.

The city lights flowed over that face and vanished. A face seen a hundred times on magazine covers, and yet $celebName in that moment resembled none of those covers. Just the face of a person afraid he might hurt one person.

"$celebName," $userName said. "For me — it's not the castings drying up that scares me. It's you being put in a hard place because of me."

$celebName put strength into the hand on the wheel.

"It's the same for me," he said. "I don't worry about myself. I'm afraid $userName will get hurt."

The two were silent a long while. Two people with the same fear, sitting in the same silence.

chapters.heading: A Cruel Headline
chapters.body:
The next day, that photograph was in every magazine.

The article headlines were cruel. 'An Unknown Actor Using a Great Star.' 'A Calculated Meeting.' 'Who Is $celebName's New Lover — A Suspicious Rookie.' Overnight $userName became Hollywood's target.

On the street people pointed. In restaurants they murmured. The casting calls cut off all at once. The three lines of dialogue $userName had received went to another actor. $userName became a nameless person again. No — became a person worse than nameless, a person with a bad rumor stuck to them.

$celebName came to find $userName. To $userName's small apartment. Hat pulled low, shaking off the reporters.

"$userName," $celebName said. "I'll hold a press conference. Say it's all untrue. That we're only colleagues on the same film. That we just had one meal. Then $userName can work again. The rumors will settle."

$userName looked at $celebName a long while. And asked.

"Is that your true heart?"

$celebName closed the mouth.

"$celebName," $userName said. "Hiding our hearts in front of the cameras, I can do that. That's acting. I'm an actor. But — lying about my heart even when it's just us, I can't do. The moment I say at a press conference 'we're nothing to each other,' I'm afraid you'll come to believe that lie too."

$celebName took one step toward $userName.

"The true heart," he said. "is from the instant I first saw you in the audition hall corridor. When I saw that hand gripping the smudged script. I knew then already. That I couldn't simply pass this person by. Clearly a first time, and yet it felt like a person I'd known too long. So my feet stopped. When in all my life they'd never stopped before anyone."

"Then don't hold the press conference," $userName said.

"Your work cuts off. Your acting life could end."

"Let it cut off," $userName said. "If I can be beside you, living as a nobody is nothing. Even if I never get on a screen — I've gotten you, the person beyond the screen."

After $celebName left, $userName opened the magazine again.

On the glossy cover, $userName's face was set. 'A Suspicious Rookie.' $userName looked at those letters a long while. Once $userName would have collapsed. But strangely, $userName did not collapse.

It was because of what $celebName had said. That from the audition hall corridor it had not felt like a first time. $userName had felt it too. From the instant $userName first saw $celebName, it felt $userName had found someone lost.

Before that feeling, the letters of the magazine looked small. The rumor of one age looked small before a bond that had crossed the ages.

$userName closed the magazine. And resolved. This time — not to lose.

chapters.heading: Leaving the Screen
chapters.body:
$celebName looked at $userName a long while.

Then, smiled. It was not the radiant smile of the magazine covers. The smile of someone whose heart has been wholly found out. A smaller, truer smile.

"Then let's become nobodies together," $celebName said.

$userName looked at him. "...What?"

"I'll quit being a star too," $celebName said. "Living an ordinary life with $userName is better than being on a cover. It's been so for a long while. Even if my face is on a hundred covers, there was no one to look at that cover with me. I'm used to applause, but when the applause ended and I went back to an empty trailer — it was always cold."

$celebName took $userName's hand.

"After meeting $userName, for the first time, the time after the applause wasn't cold. So — the screen, that's enough now."

At those words tears came to $userName. Sorry that $celebName threw everything away because of $userName, and at the same time — warm, because that heart was so clear.

In the end the two left Hollywood.

$celebName refused his next picture. The agency held on, but he would not listen. The magazines clamored a while. '$celebName, sudden retirement.' 'An Unknown Actor Ruined a Great Star.' And — were soon forgotten. Hollywood was a place that forgot the departed fast.

The two went to a small town with no cameras and no flashes. A place where the sea was visible.

There $userName and $celebName lived no longer hiding their true hearts. In the morning they brewed coffee together, by day walked the shore, in the evening watched the same film — not the film the two had been in, but a film made by others. Ordinarily.

"$celebName," $userName asked one evening. "Don't you miss the screen? The applause."

$celebName smiled.

"With $userName beyond the screen," he said. "what is there to miss inside it."

The first winter in the small town, the two sat before a fireplace.

$celebName put on an old film. It was the very work the two had been in together. The scene where $userName spoke three lines came on. The $userName on screen was young, clumsy, and shining.

"$userName," $celebName said, watching the screen. "Those three lines, back then — I still have them memorized."

$userName laughed. "Were those lines worth memorizing?"

"They're lines $userName spoke," $celebName said. "If they came from $userName's mouth, three lines or one, I memorize them all. I do."

At those words, $userName did not regret leaving the screen one bit. More than the applause inside the screen, this one line before the fireplace was warmer. $userName rested the head on $celebName's shoulder. On screen, two young people were still shining.

dohwa.epilogue:
The screen tried to set the two apart, but the two had each other beyond the screen.

The flashes went dark, the magazines yellowed. Even so the true hearts the two shared in the small town stayed, crossing the ages.

Now $userName, when $celebName comes onto a screen — passing through as a bit player, glimpsed briefly in a crowd — recognizes him at once. Among countless faces, only that face is clearly seen.

The gaze met once in the audition hall corridor is still alive inside $userName.

Whatever screen $celebName is beyond, $userName's eyes find that person first. And always find them.

As it has been from the start.

dohwa.celebRole: wandering musician who couldn't leave that shopfront

paragraphs.gi: In $era, one wave of $userName's hand gathered a crowd on the street, and $celebName stood closest to it. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who raised a street with silk, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: $userName's shop held a charm, so people came to watch the one who unrolled the silk more than the silk. $celebName played, all day, only a tune for $userName instead of one for the customers.
paragraphs.jeon: One evening $celebName bought a length of silk and hid a small bell inside it, so the sound rang through the shop with $userName's every move. Meaning to buy silk, a sound had been set always near, so $userName held the bell tight.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of pull can carry those gathering eyes into this life. So when $celebName draws near, you might notice the footsteps first.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So even when $celebName's voice blends faintly into a group song, you may find that part at once.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So even when $celebName's way of speaking sounds among other actors, you might catch it in a moment.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So even as $celebName's number flits across the screen, you may recognize it in half a second.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So even when $celebName's laugh slips briefly into a video, your ear might react first.

dohwa.era: Hanyang in the reign of King Yeongjo
dohwa.title: That Face in the Portrait
dohwa.logline: A court painter cannot escape the face of the man who is named, every time, as the model.

chapters.heading: The Painter of the Royal Bureau
chapters.body:
Joseon, the reign of King Yeongjo, the Royal Bureau of Painting in Hanyang.

$userName was a painter there. One who painted royal portraits, and the portraits of meritorious subjects. The work of moving a person's face onto silk with a single brush, $userName had done all of life.

$userName's brush was exact. Whoever's face it was, it was moved without an inch of error. The angle of the eyes, the height of the nose-bridge, the faint tilt at the corner of the mouth. No one in the Bureau painted as exactly as $userName.

But the old painters of the Bureau said one thing was missing from $userName's paintings.

"$userName. Your painting is exact. But it has no heart," an old painter said. "The face is the same, but the person isn't there. You moved the shell, you couldn't move the person."

$userName did not understand those words. Paint the face the same and that was enough — what was this thing called heart. The heart is not visible to the eye, so how was it to be moved onto silk. $userName took those words for the old painter's pointless fault-finding.

That day, a person came into the Bureau as a model. The subject of a new portrait of a meritorious subject.

It was $celebName.

$userName, in the middle of laying out the painting tools, looked at that person.

And the hand stopped. Without $userName's knowing.

All of life $userName had painted hundreds of faces. The face of a king, the face of a minister, even the wrinkles of an old subject. But $celebName's face was — different. What was different, $userName did not know either. Simply — the gaze would not fall away. The brush had to be lifted. But the eyes first, bound themselves to that face.

"Painter," $celebName said. "How is it you do not lift the brush, but only look at the person."

"...I am sorry."

$userName hurriedly lifted the brush. But that day, $userName's brush did not move as usual. Trying to move $celebName's face onto silk, the hand kept trembling. That brush that had moved without an inch of error kept wavering before that face.

$userName finished that day's portrait with a trembling hand.

Leaving the Bureau, $userName looked down at the hand.

It was a hand that had never trembled in all of life. A hand that did not tremble even painting a king's royal portrait, even before living power. And that hand had trembled before $celebName's face. $userName, unable to know the nature of that tremble, looked down at the palm a long while.

It was a thing that could not be named. So it was the more frightening. $userName resolved to master that tremble the next time. But the next time, and the next, that hand trembled the same before that same person.

chapters.heading: A Painting With Heart
chapters.body:
Seeing the portrait painted that day, the Bureau's painters were startled.

"$userName. This painting is — different," an old painter said, standing a long while before the portrait. "For the first time it has heart. The person in this painting is there. Not a shell — the person is there."

$userName looked at the portrait $userName had painted.

$celebName's face was on the silk. Truly, it seemed to be there. The gaze was alive, and at the corner of the mouth was a trace of a word about to be spoken. $userName, painting that portrait with a trembling hand, had moved something more. Not the face — something else.

That day, $userName understood the old painters' words for the first time. A painting with heart.

And grew afraid. Very much so.

A painting with heart. That meant, then, that $userName's heart had been reflected onto the silk. That what $userName felt looking at $celebName's face had been laid, entirely, into that portrait.

$userName, presenting that portrait to the Bureau, fretted that someone might notice that heart.

After that, strange things followed.

Whenever there was a portrait to be painted at the Bureau, $celebName was named as the model. At first $userName thought it chance. The second time too. By the third or so $userName wondered. How was it always the same person who came as the model.

Each time $userName painted $celebName, $userName's brush trembled, and that tremble carved heart onto the silk. The Bureau, looking at those portraits, praised that $userName's skill deepened by the day. Only $userName knew. That it was not skill but — heart.

Every night $userName took out those portraits and looked at them.

The paintings said to have heart. The old painter's praise was frightening. Praise meant, then, that someone had read that heart. $userName touched the gaze of $celebName on the silk with a fingertip. In the gaze $userName had moved, $userName's own heart was reflected.

It must not be found out. The heart a painter held toward a young man of a noble house could be moved onto silk, but could not be set out into the world. $userName put that portrait into the deepest place of the painting-box. As if hiding the heart.

chapters.heading: The Model Named Again
chapters.body:
$userName came to know the answer a long while later.

$celebName had been putting in requests to the Bureau himself. That if there was a portrait to be painted, he be used as the model. And — only at the seat where $userName held the brush.

The day $userName learned of that, $celebName again entered the Bureau as a model.

"...How is it you keep entering this Bureau," $userName asked. Laying out the painting tools, the gaze set on the silk. $userName could not bring the eyes to look at $celebName's face head-on.

$celebName was silent a moment.

"Because the painter's brush," $celebName said at last. "how it moves a person so — I wished to see it."

"...Is that all."

"That is not all," $celebName said. "It is because I wish to see those eyes, when the painter paints me."

From $userName's hand the brush nearly fell.

"Painter," $celebName went on. "when you paint me, your eyes are unlike when you paint another. I saw it. Seated in the model's place, I watched you. What is not in your eyes when another painter paints me is, when you paint me, there. Wishing to know what it is, I keep entering this Bureau."

$userName stopped the hand on the silk.

All of life $userName had done only the work of painting faces. Had never looked into their own heart. But that day for the first time $userName knew — that their own heart had been reflecting onto the silk, and that the model $celebName had noticed it first.

"When I paint you," $userName said small. "the brush keeps trembling. The brush that all my life never once trembled, before you it trembles. Though I strive to still that tremble, it will not be stilled."

$celebName loosed the model's pose and turned the head slowly toward $userName.

"That tremble," $celebName said. "is what puts heart into the painting, painter. The old painters said your paintings had no heart, I hear. Now that heart has entered. And it is — because of me."

chapters.heading: A Heart Upon the Silk
chapters.body:
$userName painted $celebName's portrait, and painted it again.

Painted it as the Bureau's work. Painted it on days with no work. At night, alone beneath a lamp, $userName spread the silk and moved the $celebName remembered. The face seen by day, the laughing face, even the face that grew briefly bored in the model's seat.

Portraits painted so piled up. In the deep of the painting-box, in a place no one would see.

The problem was rank. $userName was a painter of the Royal Bureau, and $celebName a son of a meritorious subject. Between the two was a line that could not be crossed. For a painter to set a heart on a young man of a noble house was a thing dangerous even to speak of.

So $userName moved the heart only onto silk. In words, said not one thing. Silk was safe. Silk told no one.

But one day, $celebName saw $userName's painting-box.

$celebName, entered the Bureau as a model, opened the painting-box while $userName had stepped away a moment. Inside it were dozens of his own portraits.

When $userName returned, $celebName held those portraits in hand.

$userName's face went white.

"Painter," $celebName said, looking at the portraits. The voice trembled. "Is all of this — me?"

"...I am sorry. That is merely—"

"The me in these paintings," $celebName cut off $userName's excuse. "is more like me than the me I saw in a mirror. The me reflected in the painter's eyes is clearer than the true me. A mirror reflects only my shell, but this painting — reflects even a me I did not know."

$celebName lifted one portrait. It was the one $userName had painted longest beneath a lamp.

"Painter," $celebName said. "with what heart did you paint this. Tell me honestly. The silk has already said it all. Now I wish to hear it from your mouth."

$userName could hide it no longer.

The heart moved all of life only onto silk, that day for the first time $userName brought out into words.

"From your face," $userName said. "I cannot escape. Though I close my eyes, that face is seen. So I painted it. If I did not paint it, my heart felt it would burst. If I did not move it even onto silk, I could not bear it."

chapters.heading: Beyond the Royal Portrait
chapters.body:
That day, $celebName received $userName's heart.

The line of rank still stood there. A painter and a son of a meritorious subject. That line was not erased by one person's heart. Still, $celebName chose to cross it.

"Painter," $celebName said. "Listen. Living as the son of a meritorious subject was always stifling to me. Walking the road my house decided, meeting the person my house decided. Nowhere in that life was there a me. Like a king in a royal portrait, I too was a single fixed picture."

$celebName pointed to the portraits $userName had painted.

"But the me inside your silk is alive. The me reflected in your eyes is not the me my house decided, but — simply me. I wish to become the me inside that painting. So, painter, leave Hanyang with me. Casting off both the house and the seat of a meritorious subject's son."

The two left Hanyang. Casting off both the Royal Bureau painter and the meritorious subject's son. Far off, they settled in a small county.

There $userName painted. No longer royal portraits, no longer portraits of meritorious subjects. It was $celebName's face. The morning face, the laughing face, the sleeping face.

$celebName became $userName's model all his life.

"Painter," $celebName asked one day. "Painting the same face all your life — is it not tedious?"

$userName stopped the brush and smiled.

"This face," $userName said. "is different each time it is painted. Yesterday's you and today's you differ, the laughing you and the sleeping you differ. Painted all of life, it is a face that cannot be wholly painted. So there is no room for tedium."

At those words, $celebName drew near to $userName.

"Then paint it all your life," $celebName said. "As long as my face is not wholly painted, you cannot leave me. I find that — good."

That day too $userName painted $celebName's face. With a trembling brush. With that tremble full of heart. The $celebName upon the silk was always there. All of life, there.

dohwa.epilogue:
The painter painted one person's face all of life, and that person became the painter's model all of life.

The silk rotted, the portraits scattered. Even so the gaze toward that one face stayed, crossing the ages.

Now $userName, seeing $celebName's face once — a single photograph, a single scene on a screen — cannot lift the eyes from it. Among countless faces, only that face enters clearly. Like a person who has painted only that face all of life.

$userName's gaze can never escape that face. As it has been since the day, in the Royal Bureau, the brush first trembled.

$celebName's face is, to $userName, a painting never wholly finished in a lifetime. So $userName again, looks at that face. Long, and long.

dohwa.celebRole: peddler who set down their load at that sound

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $userName's single song gathered the whole market to sit, and $celebName had set their wares down at their feet. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who drew a street together, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: $userName's voice carried a charm, so every listener stopped whatever they were doing. Even when business was poor, the loss never stung $celebName at all if only that sound could be heard.
