paragraphs.jeon: One market day $celebName laid an unsold load entirely at $userName's feet, smiling, "today's fee for the song." At that happy face, ruined business and all, $userName smiled along.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of pull can reach that drawing song into this life. So in front of $celebName you might be glad even at a loss.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So coming back from $celebName's concert, you may go hungry for days yet feel full at heart.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So spending all your money on $celebName's merch, you might not mind the empty account.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So pouring travel costs into $celebName's match, you may still find the way home pure joy.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So paying $celebName's exhibition fee twice over, you might not feel a hint of regret.

dohwa.era: a sword-and-magic world a modern person has fallen into
dohwa.title: The First Meeting of a Second World
dohwa.logline: Fallen into another world, the me who meets that world’s hero wavers between the old world’s memory and a new feeling.

chapters.heading: The World I Fell Into
chapters.body:
$userName was an ordinary person.

In an ordinary city, $userName lived an ordinary day. Woke in the morning, went to work, came home in the evening, on weekends slept late. A life with nothing special. $userName sometimes thought that life dull. Wished something different would happen.

That day too. The way to work, a crosswalk, a traffic light. Waiting for the signal to change, $userName looked at the phone. And — when $userName came to, $userName was somewhere else.

An unfamiliar forest.

Two moons hung in the sky. The trees were a shape never seen, and the air smelled of something like magic. A scent both rank and sweet, a scent not of this world.

It was another world. $userName had fallen into a world of swords and magic.

$userName wandered a long while. Afraid. The wish that something different would happen — now regretted. The ordinary way to work, those days thought dull — once gone, they could not have been more precious.

Before $userName, wandering the forest, a monster sprang out.

Black scales, red eyes. A beast never seen. $userName screamed. There was nowhere to run. The feet froze. The monster's claws flew toward $userName.

Then, light flashed.

The monster was cut in two. Before $userName, a person stood. A person with a sword. From the sword-tip, light was scattering.

It was $celebName.

$userName saw that person for the first time. Clearly, the first time. And yet the instant $userName saw that face — $userName's chest, strangely, leapt. An unfamiliar world, an unfamiliar person. And yet, not unfamiliar. The person standing beneath the two moons seemed somehow a person known.

"Are you all right?" $celebName asked, sheathing the sword.

$userName could not answer. From looking at $celebName's face.

"...Is something on my face?" $celebName asked, awkwardly.

"No," $userName barely said. "It's just... it's the first time seeing you, but it doesn't feel like the first time."

$celebName paused at those words. The hand gripping the sword went briefly stiff. Then he said, small.

"...Me too. Just now. From the instant I first saw you, I thought that."

chapters.heading: The World's Hero
chapters.body:
$celebName was that world's hero.

A swordsman who protected people from monsters. Every village knew his name, and people looked up to him. That $celebName took in $userName, who had nowhere to go. Brought $userName to his village, arranged a place to stay.

"People from another world come sometimes," $celebName said. "One or two a century. Some search for a way back to their old world, some settle here."

"...Is there a way back?"

"There is. It takes long, and is very hard, but. You must find an ancient magic circle."

$userName was relieved at those words. And at the same time, strangely, one corner of the heart grew heavy. There was a way back, and yet the heart was heavy — $userName could not tell why.

$userName lived with $celebName. The side of a hero was dangerous. Monsters appeared endlessly, and $celebName always drew the sword. Each time, $celebName hid $userName behind his own back. Never once, not once, forgetting.

"$celebName," $userName asked one day. "Why do you protect me so? I'm not even a person of this world. I'd only be a burden."

$celebName stopped cleaning the sword and looked at $userName.

"It's strange," $celebName said. "It's been only a short while since I met you, but protecting you feels — too natural. As if I've done it from long ago. When I draw the sword, my body hides you behind me first. Before I even think."

$userName was the same. Behind $celebName's back felt, from the start, like $userName's place. A place never once stood in, and yet standing there the heart was at ease.

At night the two sat before a campfire. Two moons hung in the sky, and the flames crackled.

"$celebName," $userName asked. "Have we, by any chance, met somewhere?"

"No," $celebName answered. "I've lived all my life in this world, and you came from another. There's no way we could have met."

"Then why is this so familiar."

$celebName watched the campfire a long while. The light wavered over his face.

"I don't know," he said. "From that forest where I first saw you, I keep thinking it. We've never met, and yet — it feels like I lost you once and found you again."

chapters.heading: Two Hearts
chapters.body:
$userName began to search for a way back to the old world.

$celebName helped. They turned over ancient spellbooks, sought out sages of far places. The two set out on a long journey together. Through that journey, the two grew closer and closer.

$celebName showed $userName this world. The hill where the two moons rose most beautifully. The lake where magic light drifted. The desert night where stars poured as if they could be touched by hand. In those scenes, $userName slowly forgot the old world.

No — it was not forgetting. Two hearts had grown inside $userName.

One was the heart that wished to go back to the old world. There $userName's life was. Family, friends, familiar streets, the ordinary way to work. All those things once thought dull were now longed for.

And the other was the heart that wished to stay beside $celebName. In this unfamiliar world, only $celebName was not unfamiliar. Behind $celebName's back, that place was, to $userName, more at ease than anywhere.

One night of the journey, the two sat on the hill where the two moons rose.

"$celebName," $userName said. "It's strange. I miss the old world, and at the same time — I don't want to leave here. Two hearts keep fighting."

$celebName was silent a long while. The two moons hung above that silence.

"$userName," he said at last. "May I speak honestly?"

"Yes."

"I hope you don't find a way back. I think such a thing. I know I shouldn't. You have an old world. And yet — I think it every day. That the magic circle is never, ever found."

At those words $userName's chest hurt. $celebName's true heart was too clear. And — that true heart made one of the two hearts inside $userName heavier.

chapters.heading: Before the Magic Circle
chapters.body:
At last, the two found the magic circle for going back.

It was in the deepest place of an ancient ruin. In dust and dark, the magic circle glowed faintly. If $userName stood upon it, $userName could go back to the old world. To that ordinary day.

$celebName was silent a long while before the magic circle.

"$userName," he opened at last. "Now you can go back. It's what you were searching for. The magic circle is here."

$userName looked at the magic circle. The light turned slowly, in a ring. One step forward — and back to the world where family, friends, familiar streets were.

But the feet would not move.

"$celebName," $userName said. "Just now, when we found the magic circle, do you know what expression you wore?"

$celebName closed the mouth.

"You did not look glad," $userName said. "It was the face of someone who'd found a thing they did not want to find."

$celebName looked at $userName. It was not a hero's face. It was the face of one person, afraid of losing something again.

"$userName," $celebName said. "I'll speak honestly. For the last time. I hope you don't go. From that forest where I first met you, I was afraid I'd lose you again. Again. As if — like a person who had lost you once."

Again.

That word lodged in $userName's chest.

"$celebName," $userName said. "You just said 'again.' That you're afraid to lose me 'again.' Why 'again'? We met for the first time. This life, we met for the first time."

$celebName closed the mouth. He too did not know why that word had come from his own lips.

"I don't know," he said. "It's just... the word came out of my mouth. When I see you, it feels like meeting again someone I'd lost once. Beneath the two moons, it feels I sent you away once. So this time — I don't want to lose you. I don't want to send you away 'again.'"

At those words, $userName felt one of the two hearts quietly settle.

chapters.heading: The One Who Stayed
chapters.body:
$userName stood a long while before the magic circle.

The memory of the old world rose up. The faces of family, the voices of friends, familiar streets, the ordinary way to work once thought dull. That was clearly $userName's own. A precious thing. $userName did not take that memory lightly.

But $userName looked at $celebName.

Beyond the magic circle's light, that face afraid $userName would leave. The face that said it was afraid to lose $userName 'again.'

$userName stepped one step back from the magic circle.

The light slowly, slowly began to go out.

"$celebName," $userName said. "It's the same for me. This world is unfamiliar, but only you are not unfamiliar. From that forest where I first saw you. We have, clearly — met somewhere. This is not the first time. That you said 'again' is not a lie."

$celebName came toward $userName. With a face that could not believe it.

"You're not going back?" he asked.

"I'm not going back," $userName said. "The memory of the old world is precious. I'll keep it all my life. Still — more than that memory, the heart that wants to be beside you is larger. Strange, isn't it. It's been only a short while since we met."

$celebName drew $userName into an embrace. The light of the two moons came down over the two.

"It isn't strange," $celebName said. "We didn't meet for the first time in a second world. We met somewhere once, parted once, and so met again. This time — we don't part. Not the magic circle, not the worlds, can set us apart."

In that embrace, $userName felt the two hearts become, at last, one.

Behind, the magic circle's light went wholly out. $userName did not look back. The road back had vanished, and yet strangely — the heart was lighter than ever. Like a person already come home. Not to a place to leave, but to a place to stay.

dohwa.epilogue:
$userName stayed in the second world. Beside $celebName.

In that world where two moons rose, the two lived long together. That it was not a first meeting, both in the end knew.

And that bond crossed a world once more.

Now $userName, before a first-seen $celebName, feels the chest leap for no reason. Clearly the first time, and yet it does not feel like the first. It feels like finding again something lost.

$celebName is the same. Seeing $userName, he feels they have met 'again.'

Never met, and yet familiar. That familiarity is not a lie. The two have truly, time and again, crossed worlds and met each other anew. And this time too, they have met, in the end.

dohwa.celebRole: person who minded the gramophone by the stage

paragraphs.gi: In $era, when the lights rose and the room's eyes turned to $userName, $celebName watched only that one person from the side of the stage. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who halted a room with one step, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: $userName's dance carried a charm, so every watcher traced those moves with their eyes. $celebName, lowering the needle to the beat, kept missing a count, chasing those fingertips each time.
paragraphs.jeon: One night, for that one beat where $userName would still their feet, $celebName even chose the silence between songs and rebound a record, leaving it before the dressing-room mirror. Called work, the record was in truth only for that one person, so $userName looked at it a long while.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of pull can carry that pouring light into this life. So your heart might race at the single timing of $celebName's applause.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So at the moment the lights swing $celebName's way on stage, you may hold your breath in your seat.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might wait for the moment the cinema goes dark, just to catch $celebName's first cut.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So at the single scene of $celebName stepping onto the court, your palms may sweat.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So for that half-second $celebName appears on screen, you might start the video from the top.

dohwa.title: The Record Shop by the School
dohwa.logline: A record-shop part-timer and the customer who only ever bought the same tape grow close by trading mixtapes.

chapters.heading: The Same Tape
chapters.body:
1995, the university district of Seoul.

At the end of an alley was a small record shop. The sign was faded, and opening the door rang a bell. Inside, cassette tapes and CDs filled the walls. From an old speaker, music always flowed.

$userName was a part-timer there. By day attended school lessons, in the evening watched the counter at the record shop. It was not a shop with many customers. Still $userName loved that small shop. Loved the time surrounded by music.

Because there were few customers, $userName quickly came to remember one customer.

It was $celebName.

$celebName came to the record shop two or three times a week. And always went to the same corner. The inner part of the shop, the corner where one singer's tapes were gathered. Chose only that singer's tapes.

At first $userName just thought, so it goes. By the second time, $userName found it curious. By the third or so, $userName was — waiting for that customer.

Because that singer was $userName's most loved singer. The only customer who bought tapes from that corner, in this shop, was $celebName alone.

That day too $celebName chose one tape from that corner and set it on the counter. That singer again.

"This singer," $userName spoke up without knowing it. "Do you like them?"

$celebName raised the head. The eyes met $userName's.

"...Yes," $celebName answered, awkwardly. The face of someone flustered to be spoken to by a stranger. "I like them a lot."

"Me too," $userName said. "Almost no one buys this singer's tapes except you. At our shop. So I was a little... glad."

At those words, $celebName smiled slightly. It was a first-seen smile. At that smile, the small, old record shop brightened a little.

"Then," $celebName said. "there are two people in this neighborhood who like this singer."

That day, even after that customer left the shop, $userName stood a long while at the counter. Two people in this neighborhood who like this singer. Those words stayed, strangely long, in the heart.

chapters.heading: The Mixtape
chapters.body:
After that, whenever $celebName came, the two talked of music.

With the record-shop counter between them. What songs $celebName liked, what lyrics $userName underlined. At first it was a word or two. Then it slowly grew long. On days the shop had no customers, the two talked a long while. Until one song ended on the speaker and the next began.

$userName came to learn. That $celebName was a student at the same school. A different department, but somewhere on the campus they must surely have passed.

"It's strange," $userName said. "Same school, but I never once saw you on campus. And of all places we first meet at the record shop."

"Right," $celebName said. "If we'd met on campus, we might have just passed by. So many people. But meeting here — we don't pass by. It feels like this shop made us meet."

One day, $celebName set a small bundle on the counter.

"This," $celebName said. The ears slightly red. "I made it myself."

It was a mixtape. A tape $celebName had recorded himself of the songs he liked. On the case, a song list was written densely in his own hand.

"I picked only songs I thought $userName would like," $celebName said. "From all the talking, it seemed you'd like songs like these."

$userName took that tape. Looked at the handwriting on the case. The heart that had chosen each song, one by one, thinking of $userName. The time spent pondering how to join one song to the next.

A few days later, $userName made a mixtape too. All night, pressing and releasing the record button. Choosing only songs $celebName seemed likely to like.

When $celebName next came, $userName set that tape on the counter.

"Me too," $userName said. This time $userName's ears were red. "I made one."

The two traded mixtapes. In one cassette tape, a heart that words could not say was set full.

chapters.heading: Side A and Side B
chapters.body:
Trading mixtapes became the language between the two.

When $celebName put some song on side A of a tape, $userName answered with side B. Asked with songs, answered with songs. The word that they liked each other, neither could say with the mouth. In the university district of 1995, that was too large a word. So the two chose songs. Hiding the heart in the lyrics.

$userName listened to the tape $celebName gave until it wore out. Put it in a Walkman, on the way to school, before sleeping. Until the tape stretched. Wound the stretched tape with a pencil and listened again, and again.

Listening to those songs, $userName felt $userName knew what $celebName meant to say. Every line of lyric sounded in $celebName's voice.

One day, $celebName did not come to the record shop. A week passed and he did not come.

$userName kept glancing at the door from the counter. Waiting for the bell to ring. That singer's tape corner looked strangely empty. $userName, pretending to tidy that corner, kept standing before it.

Only after two weeks did $celebName come. The face was haggard.

"...You didn't come," $userName said. Striving not to show the worry. And yet the voice trembled.

"Something happened at home," $celebName said. "I missed you, $userName. And this record shop. Lying down, I kept thinking of this shop."

$userName's heart leapt once, large.

"$userName," $celebName said. Fidgeting at the counter with a finger. "What is this thing we're doing. Trading mixtapes. Talking with songs. An ordinary customer and a part-timer — don't do it this way."

$userName could not answer.

"For me," $celebName said. "the time of choosing songs for $userName is the best of the day. Opening this shop's door is the most thrilling. When the bell rings, that instant $userName looks up. What heart this is, I think $userName knows too."

$userName gripped the hands tight beneath the counter.

"I know," $userName said. "Because it's the same for me. When the bell rang, even knowing it was a customer — I hoped it would be $celebName."

From the record shop's old speaker, that day in particular, a good song was flowing.

chapters.heading: The Stretched Tape
chapters.body:
The season changed.

Summer went and autumn came. $userName and $celebName had traded dozens of mixtapes by then. A heart begun with songs came, at some point, to pass between them even without songs.

The two walked the campus together. Ate tteokbokki in the alley by the school. Waited together for the record shop's closing time. When $userName pulled down the shutter, $celebName was waiting in front of it. The two walked side by side beneath the streetlamps, to the bus stop.

Words were still few. Still, between the two, even silence was comfortable. The comfort known only to people who like the same songs.

One autumn night, as $userName, having finished closing, pulled down the shutter, $celebName said.

"$userName. Do you remember the first day I came to this record shop?"

"Of course," $userName said. "You chose that singer's tape and set it on the counter."

"Actually," $celebName said. "that day, I didn't really need that tape. I already had it at home."

$userName stopped the hand pulling down the shutter.

"Then why did you buy it?"

"Because $userName was at the counter," $celebName said. Beneath the streetlamp light, $celebName's ears were red again. "When I first stepped into this shop and saw $userName at the counter — strangely, I just couldn't leave. I felt I had to buy something. So I went to the most familiar corner and bought, again, a tape I already had. I needed an excuse to speak to $userName."

$userName looked at $celebName a long while at those words.

"That singer," $userName said. "Is that why no one but you ever bought their tapes? Because you kept buying them as an excuse, that corner became your regular corner?"

$celebName laughed. It was the laugh of someone found out.

"...Yes. Something like that."

$userName laughed too. That the meeting once thought a chance had, in truth, been an excuse $celebName made. And yet strangely, $userName felt that was more like fate than a chance.

chapters.heading: Graduation, and Then
chapters.body:
The season changed again, and graduation drew near.

On graduation day, $celebName gave $userName a last mixtape.

"This is the last tape," $celebName said.

"...The last?" $userName's expression went stiff.

"Because I won't talk with mixtapes anymore," $celebName said. "Hiding a heart in songs — I mean to stop that now."

$celebName stood straight before $userName. Amid the bustle of the graduation, only the two were quiet.

"$userName," he said. "Not with songs, just with my own words I'll say it. From the day I first saw $userName at the record shop by the school, I kept liking $userName. That chance of choosing the same tape — though it was, in truth, an excuse I made — still, it seems we were meant to meet. In that small shop. Two people who like the same music could not, in the end, fail to meet."

$userName took the last mixtape in hand. And put it in the Walkman. Played the first track of side A.

What flowed out was not a song. It was $celebName's voice.

"$userName. On this tape I put no songs. I only recorded the words I couldn't say all this time. I like you. From the start, until now, and from now on. Not hiding behind a song, now I'll just say it. I like you, $userName."

$userName wept hearing that voice. Amid the bustle of the graduation, with the Walkman held to the ear.

And $userName too answered, not with a song but with $userName's own words.

"I like you too. From the day in that record shop, when $celebName first set that tape on the counter. Even knowing you were a customer, I kept waiting. Every time the bell rang."

$celebName took $userName's hand. The two, in graduation gowns, stood facing each other among the people.

The two began at the record shop by the school, and walked that alley together a long, long time.

dohwa.epilogue:
The small record shop by the school closed down long ago. The cassette tapes vanished too.

Even so the hearts of the two people who liked the same songs stayed, crossing the ages.

Now $userName, when a song $celebName liked flows from somewhere — the heart knows first. Even seeing only a back among people that resembles $celebName's, the feet turn that way.

The bond of choosing the same tape still works inside $userName.

Whatever $celebName listens to, whatever $celebName likes, $userName strangely comes to like the same thing. Like the two people who, in that record shop of 1995, stood side by side before the same corner.

The mixtapes vanished, but the two hearts that interlocked like side A and side B — are still playing.

yeokma.celebRole: dock worker who unloaded cargo at every port

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName loaded $userName's bag onto every departing ship by hand. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who couldn't stay three days in one port, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A restless streak kept pushing $userName's feet toward the next port, always leaving a dear one behind. $celebName tied the same handkerchief to the railing of every departing ship.
paragraphs.jeon: One dawn $userName missed the ship and stayed on the dock, and instead of waving that handkerchief $celebName came and sat beside them for the first time. Having stayed in one place with the one always seen off, $userName forgot, for a while, the thought of leaving.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of restless streak can carry those departing steps into this life. So your feet might still only near $celebName.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So for a $celebName concert, you may book a flight first — another city, another country, no matter.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So for a $celebName stage greeting, you might take a train all the way to a regional cinema.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So for a $celebName away game, you may catch the first dawn bus just to be there.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So for a $celebName exhibition, even at the far edge of a city, you might buy a ticket and go.

yeokma.title: At Every Harbor, It Was You
yeokma.logline: A navigator $userName who drifts the seas changing ships meets dock-hand $celebName again at every harbor he puts in to.

chapters.heading: Lisbon, the First Ship
chapters.body:
Once you set foot on the sea, the old navigators said, you can never leave it for the rest of your life.

$userName heard that at seventeen, and at twenty knew it was true. The sea was cruel. It took comrades with storms, made teeth fall out with scurvy, kept land out of sight for months at a time. And yet a person who had once known the sea could not stay fixed to land. When the ground beneath the feet did not sway, it was, instead, unsettling.

The docks of Lisbon were always thick with the smell of fish and tar. That day too $userName was looking for a new ship. The ship he had been sailing had finished its last voyage and been scrapped. For a navigator, having no ship was the same as having no legs. $userName walked along the docks, searching for a ship that wanted crew.

Then a dock-hand carrying cargo blocked $userName's path. A great cask of rum slung on the shoulder.

"Move. Can't you see?"

$userName stepped aside. The hand set down the cask and, with a sweat-soaked face, looked at $userName.

"A navigator?"

"...How did you know."

"The hands. The hands of a rope-puller show it. The calluses sit differently. A dock-hand's hand is thick all across the palm, but a navigator's hand is hard only at the finger-joints. From gripping and loosing a rope."

The hand grinned. That was $celebName. A man whose name $userName did not even know, who hauled cargo on the Lisbon docks.

"Looking for a ship, aren't you. That face has been circling the docks for days."

$userName was surprised. That $userName had circled the same docks for days, this dock-hand knew.

"The Daughter of the Wind, at the far end. Looking for a navigator. The captain's temper is foul, but the ship is sound, and above all the route is long. For someone meant to drift far, that's a good thing, isn't it."

$userName could not even properly say thanks. The hand had already slung the next cask onto the shoulder. $userName sought out that ship, and that day wrote a name on the contract. The Daughter of the Wind was leaving for West Africa in three days.

The night before departure, $userName saw that dock-hand again, in a tavern at the end of the docks. The hand sat alone with a single glass of rum before him.

"Thanks to you, I got a ship," $userName said.

$celebName raised the head.

"Good. With that ship's route... you won't be back for a long while."

"The sea is all like that."

"Yes. The sea is all like that."

$celebName said it twice. As if speaking to himself. That night, $userName shared rum with that dock-hand met for the first time. Neither spoke much. So it was comfortable. People who had lived long on the sea spoke little. Living where the sound of waves was so loud that words could hardly be heard made a person that way.

"Is $celebName from Lisbon?" $userName asked.

"No. Lisbon people are something else. I'm just... a person who came along with the cargo. I was loaded onto a ship at some harbor and carried this far. That's all."

A person who came along with the cargo. Those words, strangely, caught at $userName's heart.

At dawn the next day, the Daughter of the Wind weighed anchor. From the deck $userName watched Lisbon draw away. Somewhere on the docks, someone burdened with cargo raised a hand. $userName raised a hand too. A very short parting between two people who did not even know each other's names. Bonds on the sea were all like that. Short, light, and soon forgotten.

chapters.heading: Accra, the Second Time
chapters.body:
A year passed on the sea.

The Daughter of the Wind moved harbor to harbor along West Africa's Gold Coast. $userName read the stars and read the currents and turned the sea into a road. That was a navigator's work. To draw a road where there was none. The sea had no signposts and no mile-markers. Only the stars, the wind, and the color of the water.

In a year $userName saw many harbors. And brushed past many people. Each harbor had new faces, and once departed, those faces were never seen again. That was the life of one who drifts. Every bond lasted only the length of one harbor.

When the ship reached the harbor of Accra, $userName idly watched the hands unloading cargo from the deck. Then the eyes stopped on one person.

A man with a rum cask slung on the shoulder. Hands set with calluses. A sweat-soaked face.

It was $celebName.

$userName doubted both eyes. In Accra, thousands of nautical miles from Lisbon. How.

When $userName went down to the dock and approached, $celebName, in the middle of setting down a cask, looked at $userName. And paused a moment.

"...The Lisbon navigator."

"You remember?"

"The hands. They're the same hands as then."

$celebName smiled. The very smile of a year ago.

"How did you get here."

"The Lisbon docks went quiet. The work dried up. A dock-hand goes where the work is. As a navigator goes where the ship goes."

So $celebName said. A navigator who goes where the ship goes. A person like $userName, $celebName saw as a person like himself.

That night too the two met in the harbor tavern. This time it was no chance — $userName sought him out. Asking dock-hand after dock-hand, $userName found the tavern $celebName was in.

"$celebName."

$userName called the name for the first time. The name they had traded a year ago in the tavern, in their cups.

"You remember the name."

"A navigator memorizes the names of every star. A person's name is nothing."

$celebName laughed aloud. $userName liked that laugh. After spending a year on the sea, one learns how precious the sound of a person's laugh is.

"$celebName can't stay in one place long."

"$userName's the same. We're both people of the road."

People of the road. $userName carved those words into the heart. No one in all of life had called $userName that. A drifter, a person with no fixed place — such things $userName had been called. People of the road. That seemed a phrase that did not make drifting a shame, but turned it into a road of its own.

"$celebName. There's something I want to ask. A year ago, you said you're a person who came along with the cargo. What does that mean?"

$celebName was silent a moment. Then took a sip of rum and said.

"That... I'll tell you when we next meet. Because it feels like we'll meet again."

$userName thought it a joke. But $celebName's eyes were not joking.

The next day, the Daughter of the Wind left for the Indian Ocean. $celebName came out to the dock to see it off, and raised a hand. $userName raised a hand too. This parting was a little heavier than a year ago.

chapters.heading: Goa, the Third Time
chapters.body:
Next was Goa, in India.

$userName now had a habit, each time the ship reached a harbor, of idly scanning the dock-hands. Even knowing it was foolish. The odds of a navigator and a dock-hand meeting again at every harbor were far harder than the work of reading stars and drawing a road.

And yet in Goa, $userName saw $celebName again.

This time $celebName recognized $userName first. Before $userName had even stepped off the ship, he was waving from the dock.

"$userName! Here!"

$userName laughed, at a loss. $celebName laughed too. The two stood facing each other in the middle of the dock, like old friends long parted.

"It's the third time. You know that?" $celebName said. "Lisbon, Accra, and now Goa."

"You were counting?"

"How could I not, by now."

That evening the two walked an alley of a Goa market. The smell of spices filled the street. For the first time $userName asked $celebName a long question. Why he did not settle in one place. If it was dock-hand work, it could be done at any harbor — so why move harbor to harbor like a ship.

$celebName walked a long while, then stopped. And looked toward the sea.

"A year ago I said I'm a person who came along with the cargo. Now I'll tell you."

$celebName went on slowly.

"As a child I grew up in a harbor village. A small village. The people there all lived rooted in one place. People for whom dying where they were born was a matter of course. But I — I couldn't do it. The same sea every day, the same docks, the same people. Everyone had decided what kind of person I was. Once decided, it didn't change. I couldn't breathe."

"So you left?"

"One day, I sent my belongings along with a departing ship. I boarded too, pretending to be part of that cargo. That was the first time. After that — when the cargo ran out at one harbor I went to the next, when the work dried up I went to the next again. I lived that way."

"Are you not stifled now?"

"Not stifled. But... lonely instead. For a person of the road, being lonely is the job. At no harbor is there anyone who knows me. Once I leave, I'm all forgotten."

$userName understood those words. Understood them so well it hurt the chest. Because $userName was just the same. Drifting all of life as a navigator, at no harbor was there anyone waiting for $userName.

That night, the two sat long in a Goa tavern. As the time to part drew near, $userName found it hard to open the mouth.

"$celebName. Where's the next harbor?"

"I don't know. Where there's work. And you?"

"Macao. After that, I don't know either."

The two looked at each other and laughed. Two people who both did not know what came next. So two people who both could not say 'let's meet again.'

"$userName," $celebName said, setting down the cup. "This is the third time we've met at a harbor. By now it might not be chance."

"Then what is it?"

"...Something like a promise. A promise no one made."

Those words stayed long in $userName's chest. A promise no one made. If such a thing truly existed, $userName wanted to keep it. For the first time in life, the wish rose to meet someone again.

chapters.heading: Not There in Macao
chapters.body:
$celebName was not in Macao.

The moment the ship docked, $userName searched the harbor. Among the hands, among the cargo-stacks, in the taverns. $celebName was not there. Only then did $userName realize. That $userName was searching for $celebName. That at every harbor, before reading the stars, $userName searched for a person first.

That was a dangerous thing. A person of the road must not search for anyone. Once the searching heart is born, leaving becomes hard. For a navigator to set a heart on one harbor was the same as dropping anchor. An anchored ship could not go far.

$userName stayed a month in Macao. The ship had gone in for repairs. For a month $userName went out to the docks every day. Each time a new ship docked, scanned among the hands. All month long $celebName did not come.

On the night the month was up, $userName drank rum alone in a tavern. Recalled, the way it had been when $celebName first spoke in Lisbon, that face setting down a cask and wiping sweat.

'This is the third time we've met at a harbor.'

Three times. That was all. Perhaps that was the end. The sea was wide, the harbors many, and both people of the road did not know what came next.

That night $userName resolved: when leaving Macao, to search for $celebName no longer. To fold up the searching heart. Only that way could $userName live as a navigator. With an anchor dropped in the heart, the sea could not be crossed.

But resolve was a strange thing. The instant $userName resolved to fold it up, it grew, instead, clearer. $celebName's laugh, the voice that said 'people of the road,' the words 'a promise no one made.' And that confession — that he was a person who came along with the cargo, that if he did not leave he could not breathe.

$userName knew. That it was not that $userName wanted to see $celebName. It was that, for the first time, $userName had met a person just like $userName. Drifting all of life, it was the first time $userName met a person who drifted the same way. What that meant, $userName slowly came to know through that month.

The ship finished its repairs. The Daughter of the Wind was set to leave for Nagasaki in Japan.

The day before departure, $userName went out to the docks one last time. Telling himself it was not to search for $celebName, just to look at the sea. In truth, $userName wanted to search once more, one last time.

At the end of the dock, one person stood.

Burdened with cargo on the shoulder, looking at the sea.

$userName's feet stopped. The heart beat one beat late.

chapters.heading: A Storm of a Month
chapters.body:
$celebName turned around slowly.

"I'm late. I'm sorry."

$celebName spoke first. Like a person late to an appointment.

"...A whole month."

$userName's voice trembled. Only then $userName knew. That $userName was angry. At being made to wait a month on the docks. At being made to resolve to search no longer.

"The ship I was on met a storm. Near Ceylon. The sails were all torn, the rudder broke. We nearly all died. The ship drifted and reached an uninhabited island, and we were trapped there a fortnight. It took another fortnight for another ship to find us and carry us to Macao. So — a month."

$userName lost the words. The anger cooled in an instant. In its place something else welled up. The fact that $celebName had nearly died choked $userName's breath.

"$userName."

$celebName set down the cargo and took one step toward $userName.

"When the ship was breaking apart off Ceylon, I thought of only one thing. That I had to reach Macao. That $userName would be in Macao. I lived on that one thought. Even trapped on the uninhabited island, I watched the sea every day and thought. That past the edge of that sea was Macao, and there was $userName."

Something welled up in $userName's eyes. Something not shed even in a year on the sea.

"Why did you come all the way to Macao. There's no work here. Even after nearly dying."

"Because I didn't come for the work."

$celebName said. For the first time, words that were not words of parting.

"$userName. I'm a person who can't stay in one place all my life. I said I'm a person who came along with the cargo. That doesn't change. But — even a person who can't stay in one place can return to one person. Not to a harbor, but to a person. I learned that on the island. I don't want to return to any harbor, but I wanted to return to $userName. $userName is my harbor. Whatever sea I'm on, I go where $userName is."

Before those words, $userName felt every resolve built up over a year crumble. The resolve that one must not drop an anchor in the heart.

And only then $userName knew $userName had been wrong. $userName had thought that dropping anchor meant stopping — but $celebName said he dropped anchor not in a harbor but in a person. An anchor dropped in a person moved when that person moved. That was not stopping. That was leaving together.

chapters.heading: The Ship to Nagasaki
chapters.body:
$userName looked at $celebName a long while. And opened the mouth.

"$celebName. All my life I lived without ever thinking of wanting to meet someone again. For a drifter to wish to meet someone again is to torment yourself. Because never meeting again is a drifter's fate."

$userName stepped one step closer.

"But $celebName — you met me again at every harbor. In Lisbon, in Accra, in Goa. That went against fate. The fate that a drifter never meets again. So now I want to live a different fate."

"A different fate?"

"A fate of drifting together. Not searching every harbor for you — but, from the same deck, watching the next harbor together."

$userName said it in a trembling voice.

"Tomorrow we leave for Nagasaki. The Daughter of the Wind. $celebName, board that ship with me. The navigator reads the stars, the hand loads the cargo. The two of us, and one ship sails."

$celebName looked at $userName. Over the Macao dock, a sea-wind passed between the two.

"I'm a person who can't stay in one place. Maybe I can't even stay on a ship."

"A ship is not one place. A ship is in a different sea every day. On a ship, we leave every day. We're not in one place. The drifting is the same. But — there's no need to search every harbor for you. Because at every harbor, you'll be at my side."

$celebName looked a long while at the Macao sea. And slowly, slung the cargo back onto the shoulder. This time not cargo to set down on a dock, but his own belongings to load onto a ship.

"...All my life I was a person who came along with the cargo. I said I boarded ships pretending to be part of the cargo. I never once chose a ship with my own feet."

$celebName looked at $userName.

"But this time — I'm following a person, not the cargo. I didn't choose the Daughter of the Wind, I chose $userName. For the first time, with my own feet."

$celebName smiled. The smile first seen in Lisbon. The smile seen in Accra, in Goa.

At dawn the next day, the Daughter of the Wind weighed anchor. On the deck stood a navigator and a dock-hand, side by side. Both were people of the road. Both did not know the next harbor.

But this time it was different. Wherever the next harbor was, the two would be on the same deck.

"$celebName," $userName said, watching Macao draw away. "Now there's no need to search every harbor for you."

"Why?"

"Because at every harbor, you'll be at my side."

$celebName leaned a shoulder against $userName's shoulder. The sun rose over the sea. The road still had no end, but this time it was a road the two walked together. The anchor of the heart was an anchor dropped in a person. So the two had not stopped — they had left together.

yeokma.epilogue:
That Lisbon dock has crumbled. The Daughter of the Wind sank to the sea-floor long ago, and the art of reading the stars to draw a road was pushed aside by charts and compasses.

Still, a trace remains in $userName's hands. The habit, when shaking someone's hand, of idly gauging whether the calluses sit at the finger-joints or across the palm. A causeless feeling-out, trying to recognize the hand of a person of the road.

What wells in $userName's heart when supporting $celebName is the sense of that unbelievable chance — meeting that person again, harbor after harbor. That stubborn bond that reached the same dock again, even with thousands of nautical miles between.

The anchor of the heart, it was said, is dropped not in a harbor but in a person. An anchor dropped in a person is not a stopping but a journeying-together. $celebName is, to $userName, the person who — to whatever sea $userName drifts — returns to the same deck.

So $userName does not fear a new harbor. Wherever the anchor is dropped, $userName knows that hand will be recognized there again. At every harbor, it was you — $userName, even across one lifetime, turns the bow toward the same person.

yeokma.celebRole: groom who tended the horses at each station

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName re-shod $userName's horse anew every time of departure. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole circling the relay stations with documents, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A restless streak drove $userName endlessly to the next station, never letting the heart settle in one place. $celebName always hung a bag of dumplings from the saddle of the departing $userName.
paragraphs.jeon: One snowy night $userName's horse hurt a foot and was held at the station, and $celebName hammered horseshoes through the night while they talked, at length, for the first time. Having spent one night with the one always passing by, $userName remembered that night long after.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of restless streak can carry that wandering road into this life. So if $celebName has prepared it, you might take it anywhere.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So if $celebName recommends a cafe, you may open a map and seek it out for a while.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So if it's a filming site $celebName visited, you might fit it into your travel route on purpose.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may go out of your way to stop near the training ground $celebName attended.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So if it's a street $celebName said they liked, you might walk it once, however far.

yeokma.title: A Road-Companion in the Sandstorm
yeokma.logline: A caravan guide $userName and an oasis-town trader $celebName, parted at a fork in the desert, meet again by each one’s own resolve.

chapters.heading: The Well of Dunhuang
chapters.body:
At the oasis of Dunhuang there was one well. Every caravan setting out into the desert passed that well.

$userName was watering the camels at that well. A caravan guide. One who read the desert's hidden roads from the stars and the shapes of the dunes and led the caravan westward. At dawn the next day, $userName had to lead a caravan laden with silk out into the desert.

Across the well, a trader was spreading out sacks of spices. The trader looked $userName's way, then came over with a handful of dried jujubes.

"A guide going west."

"...How did you know."

"You can tell from the order a guide waters the camels. A guide waters the weakest first. They tend to the one that'll fall first in the desert. A trader waters the most heavily-laden one first."

The trader pressed the jujubes into $userName's hand. That was $celebName.

"Take these. When your mind grows hazy in the desert, hold one in your mouth and you'll come back to yourself. Sweetness wakes a person."

When $userName moved to pay, $celebName waved a hand.

"I don't take from one who's leaving. The desert often carries people away. To burden a departing one with a debt makes that debt weigh them down in the desert. Leave light, and you come back alive."

Those words sounded strangely sad to $userName. Leave light. It was a tender other way of saying — we may never see each other again.

That night, the two sat in the oasis town's teahouse. $celebName told the stories heard from guides who crossed the desert. One caravan read the stars wrong and was buried in the sand; one chased a mirage and lost the way. The desert often carried people away that way. $userName listened, and looked at the calluses on their own palm. Hands stiffened into one posture all of life from gauging the constellations. A guide's hands aged that way.

At dawn the next day, the caravan left into the desert. $userName looked back once at the well. $celebName was raising a hand. $userName raised a hand too.

On the first day in the desert, when the mouth went dry, $userName held a jujube in the mouth. Truly, the mind came back. $userName recalled that trader's face. If $userName returned alive, $userName wished to stop by that well once again.

chapters.heading: Two Summers, Two Wells
chapters.body:
Crossing the desert once took forty days. $userName returned alive, and the next year again led a caravan out of Dunhuang. A guide's life was crossing the same desert over and over.

And at that well of Dunhuang, $userName saw $celebName again.

$celebName had not left Dunhuang either. Because Dunhuang was still a crossroads of the Silk Road. Each time the two met at the well they shared jujubes, sat in the teahouse and told the stories of the time between. $userName spoke of the mirages seen in the desert, and $celebName of the people of every kind of nation who passed through Dunhuang.

Two summers passed that way. The two became friends. And yet not once did they promise each other anything. Not promising was the courtesy of the desert. $celebName had said it — one must not burden a departing person with a debt. That $userName was a person who would leave, both of them knew.

The third summer, when $userName reached Dunhuang, $celebName said it. With a face unlike usual.

"$userName. Dunhuang has gone quiet. The river is dwindling. The crossroads is shifting eastward. I'll have to leave soon too. A trader goes where the goods sell."

$userName looked down at the jujube in hand. The wrinkles of the dried jujube resembled the dunes of the desert.

"$celebName. If you leave Dunhuang, where will you go next?"

"To the new crossroads in the east. They say a new oasis has formed where the river shifted. Traders are gathering there, I hear."

"...Then we."

"$userName crosses the desert westward, and I move to the eastern crossroads. Opposite directions. There'll be no chance our wells overlap."

$celebName drew a gourd of water from the well and held it out to $userName.

"This may be the last well. So — I'll draw you water. The most precious thing in the desert. Leave light, $userName. With no debt."

$userName took that water and drank. The throat closed up, and it did not go down well.

chapters.heading: The Fork
chapters.body:
At dawn the next day, the two parted at the well of Dunhuang.

$userName's caravan went west, toward the desert. $celebName's pack-camels went east, toward the new crossroads. The two drew away in opposite directions. They raised hands, and that was all.

$userName led the caravan into the desert. Read the stars, read the dunes. But this crossing was strangely long. Forty days did not feel like forty days. Each night $userName, watching the stars, looked without knowing it once toward the eastern sky. Somewhere over there would be $celebName.

On a night the stars were unusually bright, $userName lay among the caravan's camels and watched the eastern sky. A guide was a person who read direction from the stars. But what $userName read that night was not direction — it was their own heart.

In the middle of the desert $userName realized. That for the first time, $userName held in the heart a road other than the caravan's road. A guide did not choose his own road. All of life he only read the caravan's road. But now $userName's heart kept pointing to a road the caravan did not take.

After finishing that crossing, $userName heard a rumor in a city of Persia. A rumor among Silk Road traders. That at the eastern new crossroads a trader good with spices had come in, who always pressed jujubes free into the hands of guides setting out from the desert. Telling them to go with no debt.

Hearing that rumor, $userName stood still a long while. That was $celebName. The two had parted, and yet the rumor crossed the desert and reached even $userName.

That night, in the Persian inn, $userName resolved. To become not a guide who leaves leading a caravan, but a person who goes the road he himself wishes to go. That road was the east. The crossroads where $celebName was.

But $userName had no way to send word of that resolve to $celebName. The desert had no way to carry word. $userName could only go on his own feet. To go and meet, or not meet.

chapters.heading: A Guide Going East
chapters.body:
$userName left the caravan.

After handing over all the silk in Persia, $userName bade farewell to the caravan's master. It was a caravan that had been together all of life. The master asked, where are you going.

"East. Past Dunhuang, to the new crossroads."

"That's not a caravan road. A guide means to cross the desert with no caravan?"

"Because I'm a guide, I can cross. The stars are up the same whether a caravan is there or not."

$userName loaded only a bundle onto one camel and turned alone toward the east. For the first time, it was a road $userName chose himself, not one decided by someone else.

The night before leaving Persia, $userName could not sleep in the inn. A person who all of life had slept with a caravan, for the first time made ready to leave alone. It was frightening. But beyond that fear was a thrill never once felt before. $userName took a jujube from the bundle and held it in the mouth. The sweetness woke the mind. And that sweetness seemed to say the road $userName meant to take was the right one.

Crossing the desert alone was lonely. But $userName did not dislike that loneliness. Because this time $userName knew who would be at the end of it.

Then in the middle of the desert, $userName met a sandstorm. The sky turned yellow, and not an inch ahead could be seen. Neither stars nor dunes could be read. The guide had lost the way.

$userName set the camel down in a circle and crouched inside it. Took the last jujube from the bundle and held it in the mouth. To hold the mind. That sweetness brought $celebName's voice. 'Sweetness wakes a person.' The words heard at the first well of Dunhuang.

For two days $userName was trapped in the sand. And on the third day, the storm stilled. $userName read the stars. And led the camel east again. The lips all cracked and the feet blistered — but $userName did not stop. Because $userName knew who would be at the end of the road.

A few days later, $userName reached the small oasis newly formed where the river had dwindled. At a well there, someone had spread out sacks of spices.

chapters.heading: The Same Resolve
chapters.body:
$celebName raised the head. And looked at $userName. A long while, said nothing.

$userName came down from the camel. With lips cracked by the sandstorm, barely said.

"...I came to find you."

$celebName dropped the jujube in hand.

"$userName. The caravan. How can a guide be here with no caravan."

"I left the caravan. I quit being a guide. All my life I read only the caravan's road — and for the first time, I chose my own road. That road was here."

$celebName looked at $userName a long while. Then pointed to their own bundle set beside the well. The bundle was already tightly packed. A bundle made ready to depart.

"$userName. The truth is — I was about to leave too."

"...To where."

"To the west. Past Dunhuang, to the caravan crossroads. I heard a guide crosses the desert there every year. The guide I met at the well of Dunhuang. I thought, if I go to that crossroads and sell spices, someday I'll meet that guide again. So I packed my bundle."

$userName stopped still. At the very moment $celebName was about to leave westward, $userName had come east.

"$celebName. We were each about to leave toward the other. In opposite directions."

"...So we were. We very nearly missed each other again."

$celebName smiled. The smile seen at the well of Dunhuang. And over that smile, tears flowed.

"$userName. We're both people who leave. People who can't stay in one place. That doesn't change. But — when two leaving people leave toward each other, that's not a missing, it's a meeting."

$userName drew a gourd of water from the well. And held it out to $celebName. As the desert's greeting given to one who is leaving.

"$celebName. Let's not leave in different directions anymore. Let's leave in the same direction. Then we'll have no chance of missing each other."

$celebName took that water and drank. Then dipped the empty gourd into the well again. This time $celebName drew water and held it out to $userName. Two leaving people had to give each other water.

chapters.heading: One Bundle, Two People
chapters.body:
From that day the two packed one bundle together.

The bundle $celebName had packed tight to leave westward, the two unpacked and packed again. This time it was two people's belongings. A sack of spices and a guide's constellation-tools went into one bundle.

The two drifted the desert together. When the river dried they packed up together, when the crossroads shifted they led the camels together. $userName read the stars to find the road, and $celebName read which oasis the spices would sell best at. The two skills joined upon one road.

The day they reached the first oasis, $celebName spread out sacks of spices and pressed a handful of jujubes free into the hand of a caravan guide setting out. Telling him to go with no debt. $userName watched and smiled. It was the very greeting $celebName had given $userName at the first well of Dunhuang. $celebName still sent a departing person off light. Only now, there was one to send them off alongside.

$userName sometimes recalled the fork at Dunhuang. That dawn drawing away in opposite directions. The two could, then, have missed each other forever. If $userName had not heard the rumor in Persia. If $celebName had left westward first.

But the two had not missed each other. Because with the same heart, they had left toward each other. Because two leavings had met at a single point.

One night, when stars poured over a dune, $celebName said. Lying with the bundle for a pillow.

"$userName. At Dunhuang I said it. That one must not burden a departing person with a debt. That a debt weighs a person down in the desert."

"I remember."

"But now I know. That was — talk for a person leaving alone. When two leave together, it's not a debt but two who share the burden. Then both grow lighter. Leaving together is a thing that makes you lighter."

$userName chewed those words long. A person leaving alone fears a debt; two people leaving share the burden. That difference was the difference between loneliness and journeying-together.

At dawn the next day, the two crossed a dune with one bundle shared between them. The sun rose. Two shadows lay long, side by side, over the desert. Two shadows no longer drawing away in opposite directions, but in the same direction.

yeokma.epilogue:
That well of Dunhuang was buried in sand long ago. The procession of the caravan, the spot where spices were spread out, the guide's art of crossing the desert by stars — all sank beneath the dunes of time.

And yet $userName, before a fork, always looks long at one side. Standing where a road splits in two, the premonition rises that someone will be at one end. With no cause known.

What spreads through $userName's heart in recalling $celebName is the realization that the two who parted at Dunhuang in opposite directions had, it turned out, been leaving toward each other. That what looked like a missing was, in truth, another name for a longer meeting.

A person leaving alone fears a debt; two people leaving share the burden. The heart $userName holds toward $celebName is not a debt. It is a burden shared, that grows lighter for both when carried together.

So $userName does not hesitate at a fork. Whatever road is chosen, $userName knows the same person will be met at its end. As they were parted in the sandstorm and yet met again in the end — the two are, even across one lifetime, on a road leaving toward each other.

yeokma.celebRole: helmsman who moored the boats at each landing

paragraphs.gi: In $era, at every landing $celebName moored $userName's boat safely. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole moving along the canal, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A restless streak kept pushing $userName to the next landing, always turning a back to a dear street. $celebName hung a small lantern at the departing prow each time, lighting the receding night road.
paragraphs.jeon: One night $userName lost the boat to a storm and was left empty-handed, and $celebName offered their own boat and asked, for the first time, "may I come along?" The one always seen off had followed, so $userName felt steady even empty-handed.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of restless streak can reach that departing waterway into this life. So with $celebName, anywhere you go might feel good.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So thinking you're at the same concert hall as $celebName, a far city might feel near.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So if $celebName comes to a stage greeting, the way to that theatre may feel not far at all.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So if it's a stadium $celebName plays at, even the away road might thrill like an outing.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So if $celebName's exhibition opens, the trip to that city itself can become a glad journey.

yeokma.title: See You in the Next Town
yeokma.logline: A band man $userName who follows the gigs and $celebName, met at every motel, are tied together on Route 66 by a single postcard.

chapters.heading: Chicago, the Starting Point
chapters.body:
The 1960s. Route 66 cut across America at a slant, from Chicago to Santa Monica.

$userName was a band man on that road. With a guitar slung on the back, following the band, playing on small stages town to town. When a gig ended in one town, on to the next. That was the tour. Not staying, but flowing along the road.

$userName did not dislike that life. Leaving, you could leave yesterday's mistake behind in that town. On the road, every time, you could start over fresh.

That day it was a small club on the outskirts of Chicago. When $userName's band finished the first song, one person beneath the stage clapped. A beat later than the other guests, but more sincerely than anyone.

After the show that person came to the side of the stage.

"That guitar solo — you didn't play it from the sheet music, did you."

$userName, in the middle of putting the guitar in its case, looked at that person.

"...How did you know."

"A solo played from the sheet doesn't tremble. That one trembled just now. The fingers hesitated, just slightly, finding the next note. I liked that hesitation."

That was $celebName. A guest of the Chicago club whose name $userName did not even know.

"What does $celebName do?"

"A drifting laborer. I clean motels, wash diner dishes. When the money runs out in one town I go to the next. Following Route 66."

"Our band follows 66 too. From Chicago to Santa Monica."

That night, the two talked until the club closed.

"$celebName doesn't stay in one place?"

"I can't. Stay long in one town, and the people there decide what kind of person I am. Once decided, it doesn't change. That's stifling, so I leave. On the road, every time, you can become a new person."

$userName understood those words. It was the same for a band man. Stay long in one town, and yesterday's playing became that town's reputation. You had to leave to start fresh on the next stage. $celebName said he rode 66 too.

"$userName. Then see you in the next town. Maybe."

$userName thought it just a greeting. The kind people met on the road always made, a greeting not meant to be kept. When the van left the next morning, $userName had half forgotten that person.

chapters.heading: A Single Postcard
chapters.body:
A month later, at a motel front desk in Tulsa, $userName received a postcard.

Because the band announced its tour schedule in advance, which town and which motel they'd lodge at was set. Someone had seen that schedule and mailed a postcard to the Tulsa motel.

On the front of the postcard was a photo of Route 66. On the back was a short note.

"The trembling solo at the Chicago club. I'm making my way down 66 too. Now in Amarillo. One town ahead of $userName's band. See you in the next town. Maybe. — $celebName"

$userName looked at that postcard a long while. A month ago, a person talked with for one night at a club — had found out the band's schedule and sent a postcard.

$userName wanted to write back. But there was no way to know which motel $celebName lodged at. A drifting laborer had no fixed address. With nowhere to send a reply, $userName simply tucked that postcard inside the guitar case.

After that, town by town, a postcard came at every motel front desk.

"Now in Gallup. The diner owner I worked for yesterday was humming a tune — it sounded like the one $userName sings."

"Now in Winslow. The desert sunset is like a stage light. $userName would have liked it."

Each time $userName received a postcard, $userName knew that $celebName was running one town westward ahead of $userName. The same Route 66, toward Santa Monica, and $celebName always one step ahead. So the two never met. Only the postcards came. No — the postcards only came. Because $userName could not write back.

$userName gathered the postcards inside the guitar case. One, two, five. Those postcards were the trace of a companion never met.

chapters.heading: The One a Step Ahead
chapters.body:
Each time $userName received a postcard, $userName stepped, one step late, into a town $celebName had stayed in and left.

In that Amarillo diner, $userName sat at the counter and drank coffee. The waitress said: there'd been someone working here about a month ago. Did the work so neatly. $userName wanted to ask the waitress more, but a waitress could not know the whereabouts of a drifting laborer.

In that Gallup gas station, $userName put in gas and asked the owner. Did he by chance know someone who'd worked here about a month ago. The owner shook his head. There were more than one or two such people. On 66, drifting people were as many as grains of sand.

Only then $userName knew. That $userName was feeling out $celebName's trace town by town. That $userName was stepping, one town late, into the footprints of a person never once met.

In Winslow, $userName finished a stage and looked at the desert sunset. It was the sunset $celebName had written of on a postcard. "Like a stage light. $userName would have liked it." $userName truly did like that sunset. And in that moment, that $celebName was not at $userName's side — hurt, for the first time, achingly.

The postcards always came from one town ahead. So the two went the same road, but were never in the same town at the same time. When $userName arrived $celebName had already left, and when $celebName arrived $userName had not yet reached that town.

$userName lay on the Winslow motel bed and took out the postcards gathered inside the guitar case, turning them one by one. Amarillo, Gallup, Winslow. City names written, one after another, westward. It was the trace of one person flowing down Route 66. And that trace was always one town ahead of $userName.

$userName took out the first postcard, received in Tulsa, and looked at it again. "See you in the next town. Maybe." That "maybe" had not, even after a month, once become real.

$userName knew. At this rate, the two would never meet. Until 66 ended, they would keep missing each other by one town. Unless one of them stopped and waited.

chapters.heading: The Last Postcard
chapters.body:
At a motel front desk in Kingman, $userName received one more postcard. But this postcard was different.

"$userName. I think this is the last postcard. I'm almost at Santa Monica. Where 66 ends. When the road ends, there's no next town anymore. With no next town, I can't write 'see you in the next town' anymore. So I want to decide one last thing. Beneath the sign where 66 ends, I'll wait three days. The END OF THE TRAIL sign. I know $userName's band's last show is in Santa Monica. We'll meet there, or we won't. I, who only ran one town ahead, will for the first time stop and wait. — $celebName"

$userName read that postcard twice, three times.

$celebName said he would, for the first time, stop. A person who only ran one town ahead said he would wait beneath the sign. For the two to meet, one had to stop. And $celebName had stopped first.

That night, $userName lay on the Kingman motel bed and opened the guitar case. Inside were the gathered postcards. From Tulsa to Kingman, postcards received one town late. Postcards never once answered.

$userName laid out those postcards in order. That was the Route 66 the two had walked together. A road missed by one town and yet, in the end, never broken.

$userName resolved. Reaching Santa Monica, to give $celebName an answer for the first time. Not by postcard, but in person. And that answer had to be a word other than 'see you in the next town.' A wholly different word, to be said at the end of a road with no next town.

The next day, the band van set out for Santa Monica. It was the last stretch of 66. $userName watched the desert flow past the window. At the place this road ended, there was a person who had, for the first time, stopped.

chapters.heading: Santa Monica, the End of the Road
chapters.body:
The band van entered Santa Monica. The sign marking the end of Route 66 came into view. END OF THE TRAIL. Beyond it was the sea.

$userName saw someone standing beneath the sign. A person with a worn duffel bag slung on the back.

$userName jumped out before the van had even stopped. And at the end of the road, before that sign, stopped.

$celebName looked at $userName. A beat late, but bright — smiled. The smile seen at the Chicago club.

"...You really came. It was the third day. If you hadn't come today, we wouldn't have met."

"$celebName. The postcards, I got them all. From Tulsa to Kingman. I didn't throw away a single one."

$userName opened the guitar case and showed the bundle of postcards gathered inside. $celebName's eyes wavered.

"I couldn't write back," $userName said.

"Because I had no way to know which motel $celebName lodged at. Because a drifter has no address. So for a month, I only received postcards, with no way to answer. That made me so sorry."

$celebName shook his head.

"There's nothing to be sorry for. I was the one who sent postcards to someone with no address to answer to. I just — from one town ahead, wanted to see whether $userName was coming along well. Even if we couldn't walk together, I wanted you to know we were walking the same road."

"$celebName."

$userName looked at the sign. END OF THE TRAIL. The end of the road.

"66 ends here. There's no next town. So I can't say 'see you in the next town' anymore. Instead I'll give a different answer. The reply I couldn't give for a month."

$userName stepped one step closer.

"$celebName. Don't run one town ahead. Don't fall one town behind either. Be in the same town, on the same day, beneath the same stage. With me. The seat beside our van is empty. If $celebName rides there, we don't have to meet by postcard anymore. There'll be no missing each other by one step."

$celebName settled the duffel bag and drew something from inside it. A small bundle of postcards.

"...I gathered $userName's postcards too. Postcards $userName never sent. At every town, I stopped by the motel $userName had left, and wrote down the trace left there onto a postcard. Postcards I couldn't even mail. We were both writing each other postcards we could never answer."

$userName took that bundle of postcards. A month they had never met in person met, at last, in the two people's hands.

"Then now," $celebName said, smiling. "let's say it in person, not by postcard. In the same van."

chapters.heading: The First Town of a New Road
chapters.body:
That day, the band van set out again from Santa Monica.

Route 66 ended there, but the van entered a new road. Another road stretching north along the California coast. In the passenger seat sat a person with a duffel bag.

$userName, driving, looked sometimes at the passenger seat. $celebName was there. A person met for a month only by postcard, this time in a seat within reach of a hand.

The two took out the postcards each had gathered and looked through them together. The $celebName postcards $userName had gathered, the $userName postcards $celebName had gathered. Joining the two bundles, the month never met filled in like one diary.

"$celebName. We walked the same road for a month and never once met. That's strange, isn't it."

"It's not strange. Because we both only left. Two leaving people don't meet, even on the same road. One has to stop to meet. That's why I stopped beneath the sign."

"This time I'll stop," $userName said.

"As $celebName stopped beneath the sign. Next time, me. Stopping in turns that way, we can meet every time."

$celebName laughed. Then opened the duffel bag and took out a small notebook carried since Chicago. In it, $userName's band's tour schedule was written densely. Which town, which motel they lodged at, line by line.

"I sent the postcards with this. I always knew where $userName would be. But $userName didn't know where I was. That's why you couldn't get a reply. I'm sorry. For a month it was one-way."

"Now there's no need. Riding the same van, there's no need to write down the schedule."

$celebName closed the notebook. And turned on the radio. A guitar solo happened to be flowing out. $celebName listened a moment, then said.

"This one's played from the sheet. It doesn't tremble."

"How do you know?"

"$userName's solo trembles, but this one is too smooth."

$userName laughed. The words first heard at the Chicago club. Those words, crossing one lifetime, came again from the passenger seat.

The sunset tilted over the sea. $userName held the wheel with one hand, and held $celebName's hand with the other.

"$celebName. Now there's no need to write postcards."

"Why?"

"Postcards are written to someone far away. $celebName isn't far now. He's in the seat beside me."

The sign of a new road's first town brushed past the window. Two drifting people were, for the first time, arriving at the same town together.

yeokma.epilogue:
Route 66 is almost erased from the map. New highways pushed the towns off the road, and the neon of the diners and motels went out one by one. A month's worth of postcards faded too, in some drawer.

Still, $userName, you keep that habit. The wish to write a short note to someone far away. The thought, before an unfamiliar view, of 'I wish someone could see this.'

What wells in your chest when supporting $celebName is the sense of that month — walking the same Route 66 and yet always missing each other by one step. That precarious bond that, in the end, held by only a single postcard.

Two leaving people don't meet, even on the same road, it was said. One has to stop to meet. $celebName stopped beneath the sign at the end of the road for you, and this time it is your turn to stop. Two people who stop in turns that way meet again every time, however long the road.

So you do not fear the next town. Wherever you arrive, you know that face will be met there again. As the unmailed postcards touched, in the end, in two hands — you, even across one lifetime, arrive at the same town as the same person.

yeokma.celebRole: person who served tea at a teahouse

paragraphs.gi: In $era, before $userName left, $celebName always poured one last cup of tea. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole moving city to city with the shows, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A restless streak carried $userName endlessly to new cities, leaving a dear teahouse each time. $celebName always slipped a candy into the coat pocket of the departing $userName.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $userName booked a train ticket one car later on purpose and stayed at the teahouse a day longer. Having stilled those wandering feet by choice for the first time, $celebName held the cup and smiled a long while.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of restless streak can carry that wandering heart into this life. So near $celebName you might keep putting off plans to leave.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So when $celebName's short live starts, you may quietly push back the plans you were about to leave for.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So with one episode of $celebName's drama left, you might delay even your bedtime and watch to the end.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So if $celebName's match goes to extra time, you may push your whole next schedule back.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So even if $celebName's new video runs long, you might be unable to stop it, putting off your tasks.

yeokma.title: At Station 7
yeokma.logline: A cargo-ship navigator $userName records, in the ship's log, every chance brush past mechanic $celebName, station after station.

chapters.heading: Ship's Log — Station 7
chapters.body:
The navigator of the cargo ship Dust Road kept a ship's log on every voyage.

$userName was that navigator. The log usually recorded the route, the cargo, the fuel. But from one day on, something else began to be written in $userName's log.

"Station 7. Thruster bearing failure. A mechanic fixed it in an hour. Watching how he worked his hands at docking, he guessed I was a navigator. Name is $celebName."

That was the first line.

Station 7 was a small transfer base floating at a crossing of routes. A place where ships docked briefly and scattered. $userName's ship had a failure there, and $celebName fixed that failure.

"A navigator gives the thruster a little tap at the last moment," $celebName said, changing a part.

"Handles the ship like it's alive. A mechanic sees the ship as a machine, a navigator sees the ship as a comrade. You can see it in the fingertips."

When the repair finished and $userName paid, $userName asked. Had he been at Station 7 long.

"Not long. A mechanic moves station to station. Stay too long at one station and the hands stiffen. Repeat the same ship, the same failure, and the hands grow lazy."

Those words felt like $userName's own work. Stay too long at one station and the navigating sense went dull. The sense of gauging the distance between stars with the fingertips.

That night, $userName hesitated whether to add one more line to the log. Meeting one mechanic — was that a thing worth writing in a log. A cargo-ship navigator's log recorded route and cargo, not a person brushed past. But in the end $userName wrote that first line. The story of a person who, watching the hands at docking, guessed he was a navigator. Because in space, for someone to recognize $userName that way, was the first time.

The next day, when the Dust Road left Station 7, $userName looked at the maintenance-bay window. Someone in a work-suit raised a hand. $userName raised a hand too. And that first line of the log, $userName read once more that night.

chapters.heading: Names Growing in the Log
chapters.body:
Six months later, a second line appeared in $userName's ship's log.

"Station 19. Saw $celebName again in the maintenance bay. It is hundreds of light-hours from Station 7. $celebName recognized me too. Said, those are the hands. To meet the same person again in space is two grains of sand colliding again, $celebName said."

After leaving Station 19, $userName had a strange habit. When the ship docked at a station, before unloading the cargo, $userName looked once around the maintenance bay first. Among the work-suited people, $userName searched for one sun-darkened hand. To meet the same person again in space was two grains of sand colliding, they said — and $userName kept searching for that grain of sand.

After that, lines kept growing in the log.

"Station 33. Met again. The third time."

"Station 51. The fourth time. $celebName inspected my ship. Took no fee. Said it was a greeting, to make it safely to the next star."

Each time $userName docked at a station, $userName went to the maintenance bay with the log spread open. And when $userName met $celebName, that night $userName added one line to the log. The number of the station they met at again, and a short record.

On the observation deck of Station 51, when stars flowed across the window, $celebName looked at that log.
