"What is this?"

"A ship's log. Usually I record route and cargo, but lately I record the stations I met $celebName at. The log has become a record of the two of us meeting."

$celebName looked at that log a long while. Station 7, 19, 33, 51. The numbers written one after another.

"To meet someone again in space is a miracle, it's said. But looking at what's written in the log, the miracle has happened four times."

"When a miracle repeats four times, is it still a miracle."

$userName asked, and $celebName looked at the stars. Then answered slowly.

"...When a miracle repeats, it's a route. Someone is drawing that route."

$userName wrote that into the log. When a miracle repeats it's a route — $celebName. That night, $userName looked long at that line.

chapters.heading: A Broken Line
chapters.body:
One day, $userName saw a piece of news in the route data.

Station 7 had been closed.

As the routes changed completely, Station 7 was no longer a crossing. A station no ships passed was abandoned. The small transfer base where the two had first met was emptied whole, in space.

$userName opened the ship's log. Looked at the first line. Station 7. Thruster bearing failure. A mechanic... The station where the record of the two had begun was now gone.

$userName wrote one line in the log.

"Station 7 closed. The place we first met is gone."

And after that, no new line appeared in the log.

At Station 60, at 68, $celebName was not there. As the routes changed, $userName passed new stations, and at those new stations $celebName was not. The log's last line stopped at Station 51. And beneath it, the one line: Station 7 closed.

The news that Station 7 was gone followed $userName, strangely long. That small transfer base was only one of countless stations in space. And yet its being gone felt like the first page of the log had been torn out. A log with no first line felt as if every line after it floated in midair.

$userName sat alone on the observation deck of Station 68, turning the log. From Station 7 to 51, records of meetings. And at the end, the broken line.

$userName knew. The station where the two first met was gone, so perhaps the record of the two had ended there. A route with no starting point did not know where to return.

But $userName did not close the log. Beneath the broken line, an empty space still remained. $userName looked at that empty space and thought. If one more line could be written in this log, it should be not a station number but something else. Not 'met again,' but something that would no longer part.

chapters.heading: Station 7, Again
chapters.body:
The Dust Road's next destination was a newly built station.

A transfer base newly built as the routes changed. Its name was still only a number. Station 7. The number of the vanished station, inherited by a new station.

$userName looked at that number and stood still a long while. Station 7. The number written in the log's first line.

$userName's ship docked at the new Station 7. $userName, with the ship's log in hand, headed for the maintenance bay. Holding the log was a habit. To add a line if $userName met $celebName, $userName always went to the maintenance bay with the log. Even though it had been long since they met, that habit remained.

In the maintenance bay, someone in a work-suit was examining a thruster.

$userName's feet stopped.

$celebName turned around slowly. And looked at the log in $userName's hand.

"...That log. You still carry it."

$celebName spoke first.

$userName held the log tight.

"The last line stops at Station 51. Below it I wrote Station 7 closed. After that I couldn't write a single line. Because I couldn't meet $celebName."

$celebName lifted his hand from the thruster.

"I knew Station 7 was being closed too. That the station where we first met was vanishing from space was strangely hard to bear. So when I heard a new Station 7 would be built, I moved here. Even at the risk of my hands stiffening. A mechanic mustn't stay long in one place, but I decided to wait for $userName at this new Station 7."

$celebName took one step closer.

"$userName. A navigator doesn't forget a number. The number Station 7. So if I was at a station of this number, someday $userName would dock here, I thought. We can't return to the vanished station, but by its name we can meet again."

chapters.heading: The Same Route
chapters.body:
$userName opened the ship's log in hand. Beneath the broken line, there was an empty space.

"$celebName. I always searched for someone in space. Each time I docked at a station, I looked at the maintenance bay first. And when I met someone, I added a line to the log. Met again. But after Station 7 vanished, that line broke."

$userName held up the log.

"I don't want to write a station number in this empty space again. I don't want to write a 'met again' line again. To have met means soon to part. We'd part again at the next station."

$celebName looked at $userName. Stars flowed across the observation window.

"Then what do you want to write."

"The same route. There's an empty mechanic's seat on the Dust Road. The seat beside the navigator. If $celebName takes that seat, I can write in the log, instead of a station number, this: from today, the same ship as $celebName. Then there's no need to count the stations we met at. Because there's no station where we part."

$celebName's eyes wavered.

"...I'm a person who can't stay at one station. My hands stiffen."

"It's not staying at one station. A ship always moves. Even if $celebName boards the Dust Road, we leave every day. A mechanic's hands won't stiffen. There'll be a new failure every day — even a failure I make."

$celebName laughed a moment. Then suddenly his face grew serious.

"$userName. The truth is — I was about to say that first."

"...What."

"When I moved to the new Station 7, I resolved. That when $userName docked, I would be the first to say, let's take the same route. I wanted to stop meeting and parting at every station. So even at the risk of my hands stiffening, I waited for $userName at one station. A mechanic so afraid of staying in one place — for $userName, stopped in one place."

Before those words, $userName held the log tight. $celebName had stopped first, waited first. The words 'let's take the same route' — both held them in the heart at once.

$celebName took off the work-suit and set it on the maintenance bench.

"That empty space — write it. From today, the same ship. I'll fill that space."

chapters.heading: The Log's Last Line
chapters.body:
That day, the Dust Road set out again from the new Station 7.

Beside the navigator's seat, the mechanic's seat was filled. $celebName sat there examining the console. A sun-darkened hand moved slowly over the thruster-output graph.

$userName opened the ship's log. Beneath the line that had broken, in the empty space, $userName wrote one line.

"From today, the same ship as $celebName. No longer counting station numbers."

And beneath it, wrote one more line.

"This log is no longer a record of meetings. It is a record of journeying-together."

Both were people who could not stay at one point in space. $userName because the navigating sense would go dull, $celebName because the hands would stiffen. That did not change.

But now the two drifted inside the same ship. They drifted in the same direction. Watched the same stars, flowed along the same route.

One day, when stars flowed across the observation window, $celebName looked at the log and said.

"$userName. Station 7 existed twice. Once it vanished, once it was newly born. And the routes between changed countless times."

"I know."

"But we didn't vanish. Even with a station closed, even with routes changed. What vanished was the place, not the two people. The place vanishes, but two people on the same ship don't."

$userName wrote that into the log. Come to think of it, the log had first been a record of route and cargo, then a record of meetings, and now a record of journeying-together. One same notebook had become, three times, a different thing.

$userName sometimes recalled the vanished Station 7. That small transfer base where the two first met. The maintenance bay where the thruster bearing was replaced, the window where a hand was waved — all emptied into the vacuum. And yet a new station inherited that number, and at that new station the two met again. The place vanishes, but the name remains, carrying on to the next place.

$userName held the navigation device with one hand and closed the log with the other. $celebName's sun-darkened hand came quietly to rest on that log. Space still had no end. The stars were still far apart. But this time, every day of that long road would carry on to the log's next line.

yeokma.epilogue:
Station 7 once vanished, and once was reborn. The routes between were redrawn countless times. One ship's log has, by now, faded long ago in some corner of space.

Still, $userName keeps that habit of the hand. The fingertip that wishes, on arriving somewhere, to set the day down in a single line. The heart that wishes, on meeting someone, to leave that name written somewhere. The habit of being unable to let a meeting simply pass.

What is written in $userName's heart when supporting $celebName is those station numbers that grew, one line at a time, in the log. 7, 19, 33, 51 — numbers met and parted. And the one line at last written beneath the broken line: 'from today, the same ship.'

When a miracle repeats, it's a route, $celebName had said. Meeting again and again is not chance, but a road two people draw toward each other. The place vanishes, not the two people.

So $userName's log does not end. There is always one empty line left. Wherever the next station is, $userName knows that face will be met there and a new line written. Like that record begun at Station 7 — $userName, even across one lifetime, keeps writing the same log with the same person.

yeokma.celebRole: trader who sold water at each oasis

paragraphs.gi: Each time the dunes of $era were crossed, $celebName filled $userName's water flask at the oasis. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole cutting across the desert, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A restless streak kept pushing $userName endlessly toward the next dune, never letting them linger long at one well. $celebName always pressed a handful of dried dates and a small waterskin into the departing $userName's hand.
paragraphs.jeon: One year $userName lost the way to a sandstorm, and $celebName shared their own shade and remaining water to carry them through two nights. The one always passing by had given even their own share, so $userName lost the will to leave.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of restless streak can carry that departing sand-road into this life. So wherever $celebName is, however far, you might still go.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So for a $celebName concert, you may change flights just to reach that city.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So for a $celebName premiere, you might make the trip that day even if it's hours by car.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So for a $celebName away game, you may book lodging in an unfamiliar city ahead of time.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So for a $celebName exhibition, however far, you might buy a ticket for a day trip and go.

yeokma.title: The Companion of the Local Train
yeokma.logline: A musician follows the gigs city to city, and on the local train keeps meeting the same person in the seat across.

chapters.heading: The Carving on the Seat-Back
chapters.body:
The 1970s. The local train stopped at every station. It stopped even at the smallest, nameless flag-stops. So it was slow.

$userName was a drifting musician. Played the guitar at wedding halls, at tearooms, on small stages. When the work dried up in one city, went to the next. $userName's luggage was always plain. One clothes-bag, one guitar-bag.

When $userName rode the local train, $userName always chose the same seat. The window seat facing backward against the direction of travel. A person who leaves should watch the view going, not the view coming, $userName thought so.

That day too $userName sat in that seat and idly looked at the seat-back of the seat in front. On the worn wooden seat-back, someone had carved small words. Letters scratched in with something like a nail.

"A person who leaves watches the view going."

$userName looked at those letters a long while. It was the very thing $userName always said only in their own heart. Someone had carved those words into this seat-back.

Then a person sat in the seat across. Holding a cloth bundle.

Without knowing it, $userName pointed to the carved words on the seat-back.

"...These words, by any chance."

That person looked at the seat-back, then smiled slightly.

"I wrote them. A couple of months ago. I ride this local often. Always in this seat. Because a person who leaves watches the view going."

That was $celebName. A person in the seat across on the local train, whose name $userName did not know.

"$userName chose the view-going seat too."

"...I like it too."

The two looked at each other and laughed. Two people who liked the same seat, met for the first time on a local train. One line of carving on a seat-back tied them together.

The two rode together in that car for a couple of hours, to Daejeon Station. They did not say much. Between the rattling of the local train, they traded a word now and then. $celebName took a steamed sweet potato from the bundle and broke off half. The meal of a drifter. $userName ate that sweet potato and watched the autumn fields flowing past the window. It was the view going. Like the carving on the seat-back.

At Daejeon Station $userName got off. Through the window of the departing train, $celebName raised a hand. $userName raised a hand too.

chapters.heading: Same Seat, Different Stories
chapters.body:
After that, the two met again and again on the local train.

Both liked the window seat facing backward against the direction of travel, so they kept ending up in the facing seats of the same car. When $userName went to the next city, $celebName was in that car. Meeting, $celebName took a boiled egg or a steamed sweet potato from the bundle and shared it. The meal of a drifter.

One day, on a local train passing Gimcheon, $userName asked.

"$celebName. Why did you carve those words on that seat-back? 'A person who leaves watches the view going.'"

$celebName looked a long while out the window. Rice fields passed, mountains passed.

"...I'm a person who left a hometown. I lived a long time in one village. I thought I could never leave. But something arose that made me have to leave, and — the day I left, I rode the local. That day, for the first time, I sat in this seat. The window facing backward against the direction of travel."

"Why that seat?"

"Watch the view coming, and you see your hometown drawing farther and farther away. That hurt too much. So I sat in the view-going seat. To look only at where I'd go. That day, I carved those words into this seat-back. A person who leaves watches the view going. It was like a resolve, said to myself."

$userName, hearing that, looked again at the worn letters on the seat-back. It was not a simple scrawl. It was the resolve of one person who had had to leave.

"And $userName? Why does $userName like that seat?"

$userName thought a moment.

"I... I'm used to drifting. Stay long in one place and I'm uneasy. Watching the view going sets my heart at ease. But hearing $celebName's story — it seems leaving has never hurt me that much. Drifting was just a habit."

$celebName nodded.

"A person who drifts from habit, and a person who drifts from hurt. Still, both watch the view going. From the same seat."

The train reached Daegu Station. $userName rose. $celebName, holding the bundle, raised a hand.

chapters.heading: A Single Phrase
chapters.body:
After that, the two often met on the local train.

A small thing arose between them. As the terminal station drew near, $userName would quietly take out the guitar and play a single phrase. A spoken goodbye was awkward, but a single phrase of music was not. $celebName listened to that single phrase and gathered the bundle.

$celebName never once asked what song it was. He simply listened. Then one day, on a train passing Iksan, $celebName hummed along to that single phrase. The pitch was exact.

Surprised, $userName asked.

"You know this song?"

"$userName played it at every terminal station. I heard it so many times. A person who leaves, they say, should have one song of farewell. $userName's single phrase — became that song for me."

Those words stayed long in $userName's chest. The single phrase $userName played in place of a goodbye, $celebName had memorized. The song of farewell had become a thing the two both knew.

That night, on the local heading to the next city, $userName thought. That all of life, drifting, $userName had played only the song of farewell. At every terminal, at every parting person, that single phrase. And not once — had $userName played a song of meeting.

$userName sat a long while on the local heading to the next city, the guitar-bag held on the knees. To play a single phrase of farewell at every terminal now hurt one corner of the heart. A song of farewell was played to part. But $userName, more and more, did not want to part.

$userName looked at the seat-back of the local train. The words $celebName had carved. 'A person who leaves watches the view going.' $userName suddenly wished to carve other words beside them. The words that a person who leaves, too, sometimes wishes someone would sit in the same seat.

But $userName could not carve them. Not for lack of a nail. It felt those words should not be carved into a seat-back before being said to $celebName in person.

chapters.heading: The Empty Seat
chapters.body:
One day, when $userName boarded the local train — the seat across was empty.

$userName looked at that seat. The window seat facing backward against the direction of travel. The seat $celebName always sat in. That day it was empty.

At first $userName thought nothing of it. He must have ridden a different train this once. But on the next local that seat was empty too. And the one after.

Only then $userName knew. That each time $userName rode the local, $userName looked first at the seat across. Whether there was a person holding a cloth bundle. Whether, beside the carved words on the seat-back, that person was sitting.

$celebName did not come.

$userName looked at the carved words on the seat-back. 'A person who leaves watches the view going.' The words $celebName had carved the day he left his hometown. A frightening thought suddenly rose in $userName. $celebName had said he was a person who left a hometown. That something had arisen that made him have to leave. Perhaps — that drifting reason had carried $celebName off to some far place. To a place $userName could not reach.

That day, on the local heading to the next city, $userName resolved. If $userName met $celebName again — instead of playing a single phrase of farewell at the terminal, $userName would do something else. Not getting off at the station where $userName's work was, but getting off at the station $celebName got off at. To turn, for the first time, the feet that all of life had only followed the work — toward a person.

A few days later, $userName boarded the local train. The car always ridden. The window seat facing backward against the direction of travel. By habit, $userName looked at the seat across.

The seat was filled.

A person holding a cloth bundle was sitting there.

But that person was feeling out the carved words on the seat-back with a fingertip. The very words he had carved. With the face of someone deep in thought.

chapters.heading: Changing the Station to Get Off
chapters.body:
$celebName raised the head. And looked at $userName. For a moment, neither spoke.

"...You weren't around for a while," $userName said first. The voice trembled.

"I went back to my hometown," $celebName said, settling the bundle.

"The hometown I'd left. I thought I'd never go again, but something arose that made me have to go. I stayed there a long while. But — the whole time at home, this local train kept coming to mind. To be exact, the person who sat in the seat across from this one. The one who played a single phrase at every terminal."

$celebName pointed to the carved words on the seat-back.

"On the way back from my hometown, I sat in this seat again. And looked at these words. 'A person who leaves watches the view going.' The words I carved the day I left. But — this time those words looked a little lonely. That a person who leaves watches the view going, but watches that view alone."

$userName recalled a resolve made on the local train. The resolve hardened over several days.

"$celebName. I played a single phrase of farewell at every terminal. Because that was a thing between us. But — this time I won't play it."

$celebName looked at $userName.

"Instead — I'll get off at the station $celebName gets off at. Even if my work isn't at that station. All my life I drifted following the work. Not once did I get off following a person. But this time — I want to get off at the station $celebName gets off at. I want to watch the view going, together. Not from the seat across, but from the seat beside."

$celebName's eyes wavered.

"I'm a person who can't stay at one station. A person who has to drift."

"It's not staying at one station. It's drifting together. Riding the same train. I get off where $celebName gets off, and when $celebName takes the next train I take it too. The drifting is the same. But — you don't have to watch the view going alone."

$celebName looked a long while at the carved words on the seat-back. Then took a small nail from the bundle. The very nail he had carved the words with the day he left his hometown.

"$userName. With this nail, carve one more line beside the words. If there's something $userName wished to carve."

$userName took that nail. And beside the worn letters on the seat-back, slowly carved one line.

"The view going is less lonely watched by two."

chapters.heading: The Seat Beside
chapters.body:
That day, $userName passed the station where $userName's work was.

When the train stopped at that station, $userName did not rise. $userName simply stayed in the seat. Having moved this time, not to the seat across, but to the seat beside $celebName.

And at the station $celebName got off at, $userName got off too. For the first time, a station gotten off at following a person, not the work.

From that day the two rode the local train together. Always sitting side by side in the window seat of the same car, facing backward against the direction of travel. Side by side, not across. $userName went to the city where the work was, and this time $celebName got off at the same station too. There $celebName did needlework for hire, and $userName played the guitar. When the work dried up, the two rode the next local together.

They were still people who could not stay long in one city. When they stayed, the heart grew uneasy. That did not change.

But this time it was different. The view going, the two watched together. Two people who had parted at every terminal now got off together at every terminal.

$userName sometimes recalled the seat-back of the local. Two lines of words were carved there. The line $celebName carved the day he left, and the line $userName carved beside it.

That car would have vanished with the scrapped local. But $userName was not sad about it. The two lines of words were no longer only on that seat-back. Those two lines were now carved, unseen, over the seat the two sat side by side in every day.

One day, on a train running through a sunset, $celebName spoke of that seat-back. It was not the car the two first met in. It was a different local. Still $celebName said.

"$userName. The seat-back of the car we first met in, two lines are carved there. If someone rides that train and sees the words — they'll know, 'ah, a person who leaves is less lonely with two.'"

"In the place we left, those words remained."

"Yes. Even a drifting person can leave one line somewhere."

$userName took out the guitar. The terminal station was drawing near. The single phrase $userName had played in place of a goodbye, $userName played again. But this time it was not a farewell. It was the song of meeting of two people getting off together at the same station. $celebName hummed that single phrase along. The pitch was exact.

yeokma.epilogue:
That local train line was scrapped. The window seat facing backward against the direction of travel, the names of the small flag-stops, the days of carving words into a seat-back with a nail — all vanished. That worn seat-back where two lines of scrawl were carved would have rotted in some scrapyard.

Still, $userName, when leaving for somewhere, looks for a window seat facing backward against the direction of travel. To watch the view going. And when someone sits in the seat beside, for no cause the heart is set at ease a hand's width.

The heart $userName holds for $celebName resembles the second line carved into that seat-back — 'the view going is less lonely watched by two.' The warmth, when one person sits beside the view a leaving person watched alone.

There is a person who drifts from habit and a person who drifts from hurt, it was said. Still, both watch the view going, from the same seat. $userName knows. When two drifting people watch that view together, it is not drifting but going-together.

So $userName does not fear the next train. Wherever it reaches a terminal, $userName knows that face will get off alongside. Like the two lines carved side by side into a seat-back — $userName, even across one lifetime, sits with shoulders touching the same person, in the same seat.

yeokma.celebRole: carpenter who built the stage in every village

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName built $userName a new stage in every village. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole moving city to city, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A restless streak kept carrying $userName to the next village, always leaving a dear square. On every torn-down stage plank, $celebName carved a small star and fitted it again into the next village's stage.
paragraphs.jeon: One spring $userName halted the departing cart and said, for the first time, "come with me this time." Having taken along the one who always only built the stage and stayed, $userName found the road not lonely.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of restless streak can reach that wandering stage into this life. So with $celebName, any city might thrill you.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So when news of $celebName's tour drops, you may search the next city's dates first.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might thrill at looking up where $celebName's next work will film, ahead of time.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So when $celebName's next away venue is announced, you may look up that city first.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you might wait, anticipating ahead, which city $celebName's next exhibition will be in.

yeokma.title: The Night the Tent Comes Down
yeokma.logline: An actor of a traveling theater $userName and a drifting carpenter $celebName who builds the stage are tied together by a single star-mark.

chapters.heading: A Tent in the Market
chapters.body:
The 1930s. The traveling theater drifted the eight provinces.

Reaching a village, it pitched a tent in a corner of the market. Played a few days, and when the people had all seen it, struck the tent and went to the next village. The day of pitching the tent, and the day of striking it, repeated. That was the life of the traveling theater.

$userName was an actor of that theater. Wept and laughed on stage, sang and danced. People watched $userName and applauded. But the applause was left behind in that village. Strike the tent, and the faces of that village's people were all left behind too.

That day, the theater reached a market village. A tent had to be pitched. But the theater's carpenter had quit, and there was no one to build the stage.

A drifting carpenter who worked the market heard of the trouble and came. A sun-darkened person with a tool-bag in hand.

"You're looking for someone to build the stage."

"...A carpenter?"

"A drifting carpenter. I go market to market and take the work. I mend stables, build benches. I've built stages too."

That was $celebName.

$celebName built the stage. Fast and solid. $userName watched those hands from beside. The hands that drove the nails were exact. So the stage would not shake, he matched every corner carefully.

And after the stage was fully built, $celebName carved something into one corner of the stage with the hammer's tip. A small star shape.

"What is that?" $userName asked.

$celebName answered.

"On a stage I build, I carve a star. It's a drifting carpenter's mark. A stage with this star means a stage I built on my responsibility. A stage where an actor may forget their own body and act."

At the show that night, $userName truly forgot their own body and acted. On that star-carved stage $celebName had built.

A few days later the theater struck the tent. Before leaving, $userName looked around the market. The drifting carpenter was building a bench. $userName raised a hand. $celebName, hammer in hand, raised a hand too.

chapters.heading: One Who Follows the Star
chapters.body:
A month later, the theater reached another market village.

Again a tent had to be pitched. The theater still had no new carpenter. $userName worried about building the stage.

But in that market, there was a familiar face. A sun-darkened person with a tool-bag.

It was $celebName.

"...The traveling theater actor. Met again."

$celebName built the stage. Solid this time too. And in one corner, carved a small star.

After the show that night, the two sat behind the tent. The market lanterns blinked in the distance.

"$celebName. Is a drifting carpenter not lonely?"

$celebName watched the lanterns a long while, then said.

"Lonely. A drifting carpenter builds, all his life, only other people's things. I build benches, mend stables, raise stages — and all of it is someone else's. Not one thing is mine. So I carve a star. To leave at least a mark that I built it. Without that — in the place I leave, nothing remains."

Those words stayed in $userName's heart. One small star a drifting carpenter leaves. It was one person's way of enduring loneliness.

$userName wondered whether there was such a thing for $userName too. A thing a drifting actor leaves at every village. Applause? Applause vanished once left behind. The faces of the villagers? Those were forgotten too. All of life, drifting, $userName had left nothing in the place he left. I should have carved even one star, like $celebName. For the first time, $userName had such a thought.

"$userName is the same. An actor acts, all his life, only other people's lives. On stage I weep and laugh, but it's all the life of the character in the play, not my life. Strike the tent — and my own life stands there, just as empty."

The two were silent a moment. A drifting carpenter and a drifting actor. One who builds only others' things, one who acts only others' lives.

A few days later, the theater struck the tent. But this time $userName did one thing. Before leaving, $userName felt out with a fingertip the star carved into the stage plank. The mark $celebName had carved.

When a tent was pitched again at the next village, $userName looked for the plank with that star carved in it. It was the same plank. The theater's stage planks were reused village to village. Even with $celebName not there, that star was there. Looking at that star, $userName had a premonition that someone to build the stage would soon appear again.

chapters.heading: A Stage Where Stars Grow
chapters.body:
After that the two met again and again in the markets of the eight provinces.

At some market in Chungcheong, at some market in Jeolla, at a small market in the mountain villages of Gangwon. When the theater pitched a tent, $celebName was somewhere in that market. Each time they met, $celebName built the theater's stage. And in one corner, carved a star.

On the theater's stage planks, stars kept growing. One at a village, another at the next. $userName could count those stars. They were the number of markets the two had shared.

Before going on stage, $userName always felt out those stars with a fingertip. Then the heart was set at ease. Because a stage $celebName built did not shake. When the ground beneath was solid, an actor could forget their own body and act.

One night, after a show ended at a market in Gyeongsang, $celebName said. Behind the stage, beneath a sky pouring stars.

"$userName. The stars carved into the stage are already over ten. You know that?"

"You were counting?"

"How could I not, when I carved them. But — $userName touches those stars too. Before going on stage. I saw."

$userName paused a moment.

"...Because it sets me at ease. With that star there, the stage doesn't shake."

$celebName smiled. The smile seen at the first market.

"A drifting carpenter carves a star out of loneliness, and a drifting actor touches it to set the heart at ease. We use the same single star so differently."

$userName liked those words. What one person carved out of loneliness, another received as comfort. It was a small something the two had made together.

But that day too they parted. The theater had to strike the tent, and $celebName had to go to yet another market.

chapters.heading: The Last Tent
chapters.body:
Then one year, $userName's theater decided to disband.

The newly arrived moving pictures took all the people away. Pitch a tent at a market, and people no longer gathered as before. The theater could hold on no longer. The members left one by one. Some to the cinemas, some to their hometowns.

The theater decided to give one last show. To pitch a tent one last time at one market, build a last stage, give a last show — and then strike the tent forever.

$userName reached the last market for that last show. A tent had to be pitched. But there was no one to build the stage.

$userName began to build the stage alone. It was clumsy. The nails kept going crooked. The stage shook. Only then $userName knew how solid the stages $celebName built had been. That because the ground beneath did not shake, $userName had been able to forget their own body.

$userName, building the stage alone, kept recalling $celebName's hands. The exact hands driving the nails. The hands matching the corners carefully. With those hands not there, $userName's stage tilted, in the end, to one side.

$userName sank down before the shaking stage. And felt out the stage plank with a fingertip. To find a star. The stars $celebName had carved all this time.

The stars were there. More than ten stars, carved still into the worn planks.

Looking at those stars, $userName realized. That as $userName had left applause behind all his life, $celebName too had left stars behind all his life. But those stars, even left behind, did not vanish. They followed onto the stage of the next village just as they were. Applause vanished, but the stars did not.

$userName resolved before the shaking stage. If $userName met $celebName again, to say for the first time these words. The words never once said in all the years of pitching tents — 'let's drift together.' Because even if the theater disbanded, even if the tent vanished, with a carpenter and an actor, a small stage could be built anywhere.

On the morning of the last show, $userName looked at the stage.

The stage clumsily built the night before — had been, through the night, taken solidly in hand again by someone. The shaking corners were matched carefully. And in the very center of the stage, one star much larger than usual was carved.

$userName ran out of the tent.

chapters.heading: One Large Star
chapters.body:
$celebName stood outside the tent, in a corner of the market. With a tool-bag in hand.

"...The stage was shaking."

$celebName spoke first.

"I saw what $userName built. The nails had gone crooked. An actor on a stage like that can't forget their own body. So through the night I took it in hand again."

$userName stopped still.

"...You knew this was the theater's last show?"

"I knew. The moving pictures took all the guests away. So — I asked around for where the last market was. The last stage, at least, I wanted to build myself. A star-carved, unshaking stage."

$celebName pointed to the stage. In the center, a star much larger than usual was carved.

"Did you see the star in the center? Usually I carve it small in a corner — but this time, in the center, large. Because this is the last stage we share. Even if the stage vanishes, this star at least I wanted to leave large."

$userName recalled a resolve made before the shaking stage.

"$celebName. In the traveling theater I always struck the tent and went to the next village. Leaving the applause in that village. Because that was our life. But — now that the tent is vanishing, for the first time I want to say other words. Words never once tried in all the years of pitching and striking tents."

$userName stepped one step closer.

"$celebName. Drift with me. The theater disbanded. The tent is gone. But $celebName is a carpenter. With $celebName, a small stage can be built anywhere. And I'm an actor. With a stage, I can act anywhere. The two of us, even with the tent gone, can make a stage of our own. A star-carved, unshaking stage."

$celebName's eyes wavered.

"I'm a person who can't stay in one place. A drifting carpenter."

"It's not staying in one place. It's drifting together. The drifting is the same. But — $celebName builds the stage, and I stand on it. Every night the tent comes down, we strike it together and go to the next village together. $celebName said he builds, all his life, only others' things. But this stage we make — it's not someone else's. It's ours. The first thing $celebName has, that is his own."

$celebName looked a long while at the stage with the large star carved in it. And slowly, settled the tool-bag.

"...Ours. I hear such a word for the first time in my life."

"From now on you'll hear it often. At every stage we build, that word will follow."

chapters.heading: A Stage of Our Own
chapters.body:
That day, the theater's last show ended. The members struck the tent and scattered one by one.

$userName helped strike the tent too. But this night the tent came down without loneliness. $celebName was beside. When the tent was all struck and only the two remained in the emptied market.

And $celebName slung one stage plank with the large star carved in it onto the shoulder. From the theater's last stage, he had pried off that one plank.

"Take this plank. It's the first plank of our stage."

From that day the two drifted the eight provinces together. There was no theater. No tent. Still the two made a stage. $celebName built a small stage in a corner of a market, at the mouth of an alley. At its center always went the large-star plank brought first. And $userName climbed up and acted.

It was all right if not many people gathered. The two were not drifting for applause.

They were still people who could not stay in one place. Spend a few days in a village and they set out again. That they were used to drifting and uneasy at staying did not change.

But this time it was different. Every night the tent came down, the two struck the stage together and set out together on the next road.

One night, striking a small stage, $celebName said.

"$userName. I said a drifting carpenter builds, all his life, only others' things. That carving a star is out of loneliness."

"I remember."

"But now, on this stage, I carve the star not out of loneliness. I carve it because it's ours. The same star, but the heart of carving it has wholly changed."

$userName chewed those words long. A mark left out of loneliness had become a mark left out of being-together. Even two drifting people could call one stage they made 'ours.'

$celebName lifted the hammer and carved one star into that small stage plank. $userName, beside, felt out that star with a fingertip. The two people's mark was carved together onto one stage. The moon lit the emptied market. Two drifting shadows lay long, in the same direction.

yeokma.epilogue:
That traveling theater disbanded, and the tent vanished. The small stage of the market, the star a drifting carpenter carved with a hammer's tip, the bustle of the night the tent came down — all passed beyond the market of the age.

Still, $userName, when standing on a new stage, stamps the foot once. The gesture of checking whether the stage is solid. And the fingertip wishes to feel out somewhere on the stage, to find one small mark.

What $userName recalls in supporting $celebName is those stars that grew, one at a time, at every market. The small marks a drifting carpenter carved in a corner of a stage. The applause was left in the village, but those stars alone followed onto the next stage — that wondrous trace.

A star carved out of loneliness became a star carved out of being-together, it was said. The same mark, but the heart of carving it wholly changed. $userName knows. If two drifting people are together, even with the tent gone, a stage that is 'ours' can be built anywhere.

So $userName does not fear the next market. Wherever it reaches, $userName knows the stage will be built there together with that face. As they were together every night the tent came down — $userName, even across one lifetime, climbs the same stage with the same person.

yeokma.celebRole: peddler who packed the loads at the market mouth

paragraphs.gi: In $era, before $userName left for the frontier, $celebName always bound that load tight. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole carrying news to the frontier, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A restless streak sent $userName's feet endlessly to the frontier, never letting the heart rest on one street. $celebName always pressed a warm rice ball into the departing $userName's hand.
paragraphs.jeon: One winter $userName hurt a foot on the road and turned back, and $celebName set aside the market work to wrap that foot at their side for three days. The one who only ever saw them off had stayed, so $userName felt those three days stretch long as a road.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of restless streak can carry those departing steps into this life. So if it's a load $celebName has bound, you might take it anywhere.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So if it's a song $celebName recommends, you may listen to only that all the way to and from work.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So if it's a work $celebName says they like, you might seek it out and watch to the end.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may look up the sneakers $celebName is said to wear and wear them too.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So if it's something $celebName is said to use, you might track it down even from afar.

yeokma.era: late Goryeo, from Gaegyeong to the frontier
yeokma.title: The One Who Carries Word
yeokma.logline: A courier carries word to the frontier, and the one who packs his bundle at every post-station builds an affection upon the road.

chapters.heading: The First Post-Station
chapters.body:
Late Goryeo. From Gaegyeong to the frontier, word went on a person's feet.

A courier did that work. Took an official dispatch in Gaegyeong, rode a horse and rode again, carried it to the frontier garrison. Along the road were post-stations everywhere. The courier changed horses at a post-station, caught a breath, and set out on the road again.

$userName was that courier. All of life on the road. Did not know how to stay in one place. Stay, and the word was late, and when the word was late, a person died.

That day, $userName reached the first post-station. It was half a day since leaving Gaegyeong. The horse was spent.

At the post-station, a person met $userName. A person whose work was packing bundles.

"A courier?"

"...Yes."

"A long road ahead of you. That horse won't reach the frontier. Change it at the next post-station. And take this."

That person held out a small bundle to $userName. Inside were dried grain-flour and jerky. And the bundle's cord was tied with a knot of a special shape.

"This knot comes loose with one hand. When you must take a meal on horseback, you can't use both hands. It's a knot tied to suit a courier."

That was $celebName. The one who packed bundles at the first post-station.

When $userName moved to pay, $celebName waved a hand.

"I don't take from one who's leaving. Return safely, and repay me once then."

$userName took that bundle and mounted the horse. Before leaving, $userName looked back once at the post-station. $celebName stood there, watching the departing courier. With the face of one seeing someone off.

On the road $userName tried loosing that bundle with one hand. It truly came loose with one hand. $userName recalled the face of the one who had tied that knot.

chapters.heading: The One Who Sees Off
chapters.body:
$userName carried the word safely to the frontier. And received a reply, and returned to Gaegyeong. A courier's road was always a round trip.

After that, $userName went back and forth between Gaegyeong and the frontier again and again. And at the post-stations, met $celebName again and again. $celebName moved among several post-stations doing the work of packing bundles, and strangely, was often at the post-station $userName reached.

Each time they met, $celebName packed $userName's bundle. With that special knot that came loose with one hand. $userName repaid the debt incurred at the first post-station, and after that always paid for the bundle.

One night, $userName lodged at a post-station in the mountains. A courier could not ride the night roads. $celebName set out a supper. The two sat facing each other beneath a lamp.

"$celebName. Is the work of packing bundles pleasant?"

$celebName watched the lamp a long while, then said.

"Pleasant, and... lonely too. I pack only the bundles of people who leave. I put in grain-flour, tie the knot, say go safely. But the bundles all leave. The couriers all take them away. And I — go nowhere. From one place, I only see off the people who leave."

$celebName paused a moment.

"A leaving person has the loneliness of the road, and a person who sees off has — the loneliness of the place. The loneliness of all leaving and being left alone. That always weighs on me."

$userName, beneath the lamp, looked at $celebName's face. The face that had all of life packed the bundles of leaving people. The face that, staying in one place, only waved a hand.

$userName understood those words. $userName was, all of life, one who leaves. The loneliness of the road, $userName knew. But the loneliness of one who sees off — $userName heard for the first time. It was not only the leaving one who was lonely. The one who stayed and saw off was just as lonely.

At dawn the next day, $userName took to the road again. $celebName came to the post-station gate to see him off. Again, a seeing-off. $celebName was always the one who sees off.

"$userName. Return safely."

$userName rode the horse, receiving that seeing-off. But that day in particular, $userName was sorry that only he left.

chapters.heading: The Knot
chapters.body:
After that the two often met at the post-stations.

A small thing arose between them. Each time $celebName packed $userName's bundle, he tucked into the inside of the bundle one small separate knot. A palm-sized knot, the cord tied in a special shape. Not a meal-knot, just something like a keepsake.

"What is this?"

When $userName first asked, $celebName answered.

"To look at when you're lonely on the road. Look at this knot, and you'll recall who packed $userName's bundle. To recall that there was someone who saw you off."

$userName carried that knot tied inside the bundle. When lonely on the road, $userName felt out that knot with a fingertip. Then the lamp of the first post-station, $celebName's face seeing him off, rose to mind.

So the knots grew, one and another. Each time $userName met $celebName, one more knot was tied inside the bundle. The inside of $userName's bundle was, at some point, full of small knots. They were the number of post-stations the two had met at.

$userName, looking at those knots, thought. With what heart had $celebName tied these knots. The heart of pressing a knot into a leaving person's hand. The heart of, while staying in one place oneself, giving a leaving person something to recall oneself by.

$userName, riding the road, sometimes counted the knots inside the bundle with a fingertip. One, two, three. Each time a knot grew, it meant $userName had met $celebName once more. Each time $userName counted those knots, $userName recalled the lamp of the first post-station.

$userName knew. It was love. The love a person who sees off sends to a person who leaves. But that love was always one-way. $celebName always saw off, and $userName always left. Not once had $celebName set out following $userName.

$userName suddenly felt that was not fair. That every time one person endured the loneliness of the road, and one person the loneliness of the place.

chapters.heading: The Changed Courier Road
chapters.body:
War rose at the frontier. The garrison was hastily moved. With it, the whole courier road changed.

$userName no longer passed the post-stations he had always passed. He had to ride a wholly new road, past new post-stations, to the new frontier garrison.

At the post-stations of the new road, $celebName was not there.

$userName went back and forth on the new road, receiving a bundle at every post-station. But inside those bundles there was no knot. There was grain-flour and jerky, but not the knot $celebName had tied. Not the special knot that loosed with one hand either. The bundle-cords of the new post-stations had to be loosed with two hands.

One day $userName carried word from the frontier garrison. And on the way back to Gaegyeong, lodged a night at a post-station. That night $userName unpacked the bundle and looked at the old knots tied inside. The knots $celebName had tucked in all this time. Knots now all worn and coming loose.

$userName held those knots in hand and thought a long while.

$celebName had all of life only seen off. Packed the bundles of leaving people, tied the knots, stayed in place. Endured the loneliness of the place all his life. And $userName — not once had thought to take $celebName onto the road. Had only taken for granted that $celebName always saw him off.

$userName resolved. If $userName met $celebName again, to say for the first time these words. Not the seeing-off greeting 'be well' — but the request of journeying-together, 'let us go together.' To make the one who sees off, for the first time, a one who leaves. So that the two might share the same loneliness, upon the same road.

A few days later, $userName received another dispatch for the frontier. A deeper frontier, a newer road. $userName reached the first post-station of that road.

In the post-station yard, a person was packing a bundle. A knot of a special shape, just being tied to the cord.

$userName's horse stopped.

chapters.heading: The One Who Sees Off, Leaves
chapters.body:
$celebName, in the middle of packing a bundle, raised the head. And looked at $userName.

"...You're late."

$celebName spoke first.

"I knew the courier road had changed. Since the frontier garrison had moved, I thought the post-stations $userName passed would all have changed too. So I asked around for the post-stations of the new courier road. I deliberately moved to a post-station that needed a bundle-packing hand. To a post-station on a road $userName might pass."

$userName came down from the horse.

"...You moved post-stations to find me?"

"Didn't $userName always say it. That a courier carries to the end what he says he'll carry. I too — once I packed a person's bundle, wanted to pack it to the end."

$userName drew out the worn knots tied inside the bundle. The knots $celebName had tucked in all this time. $celebName saw them and paused.

"$celebName. I'm one who carries word to the frontier. All my life I only left. And — you all your life only saw off. Packed the bundles of leaving people, tied the knots, stayed in place. The loneliness of the road I always carried, and the loneliness of the place you always carried."

$userName stepped one step closer.

"I'm sorry for that now. You moved even post-stations to pack my bundle — and I never once thought to take you onto the road. So today, for the first time, I'll say these words. Not the seeing-off word, be well — but, let us go together."

$celebName's eyes wavered.

"I'm... one who sees off. One who, from one place, sends off the people who leave. I've never left."

"Then this time you leave. With me. All your life you packed only the bundles of leaving people, so now pack your own bundle, and — sling it on your shoulder and take to the road. Don't endure the loneliness of the place alone anymore. The loneliness of the road, and the loneliness of the place — let the two of us share both, upon the road."

$celebName looked a long while at the bundles piled in the yard. The bundles packed all his life for leaving people. Then slowly, picked up one of them and slung it on his own shoulder. A bundle he had packed with his own hands, for the first time he carried himself.

"...This bundle is, for the first time in my life, mine. Not a bundle to give a leaving person, but a bundle for me to sling and depart."

$celebName took the worn knots from $userName's hand and tied them fresh inside his own bundle.

"Then now, we are both people who leave. $userName carries the nation's word — and I carry $userName's meals at his side. No longer does only one person see off."

chapters.heading: The Road Between Two Points
chapters.body:
That day, the two took to the road for the frontier together. One courier and one person with a bundle slung, rode horses side by side.

From that day the two traveled the courier road together. $userName carried the word, and $celebName at his side packed the bundle. The two changed horses at every post-station, caught a breath at every post-station, and set out on the road again.

Because of the war, the courier road kept changing. The post-stations kept moving. But this time, even when the road changed, it was not frightening. $celebName was beside.

They were still people who could not stay at one post-station. $userName had to carry the word, and when he stopped a person died. That did not change.

But this time it was different. Upon that long road going back and forth between two points, now two people were together. Not a leaving person and a person who sees off — but two people who leave together and arrive together.

$userName sometimes recalled the loneliness of the place $celebName had endured all his life. The loneliness of all leaving and being left alone. $celebName no longer had to endure that loneliness. Because he had become a one who leaves.

But $userName knew. The countless couriers $celebName had packed bundles for and sent off all his life would too, somewhere, have felt out $celebName's knots with a fingertip and recalled that seeing-off. The heart that stayed in one place and saw off was lonely, but not in vain. Those knots had made someone on the road less lonely, every time.

One night, beneath the lamp of a mountain post-station, $celebName said. Tying a fresh knot into $userName's bundle.

"$userName. All my life I only pressed a knot into a leaving person's hand. To look at when lonely on the road. To recall there was someone who saw them off."

"I remember."

"But now I'm on the road too, so that knot is no longer needed. $userName doesn't have to feel out a knot when lonely. Just turn the head, and I'm at your side."

$userName chewed those words long. A knot was a thing to recall a person far away. To a person beside you, a knot was not needed.

Still $celebName did not stop the work of tying knots. Only now, he tied them not to see off a leaving person. He tied them to make the bundle-cords come loose easily with one hand. A knot for two people going the road together.

At dawn the next day, the two rode horses together. The long road between two points, this time the two drew together.

yeokma.epilogue:
That courier road of Goryeo was severed. The mountain post-stations, the hands that packed bundles, the age of carrying word on a person's feet — all vanished beyond the road. The special knot that loosed with one hand — there is no one now who knows how to tie it.

Still, $userName, when seeing someone off, checks that person's luggage once more. Whether the cords are well tied, whether the meals are packed. And seeing a leaving person, $userName wishes to set out following.

What $userName recalls in supporting $celebName is those knots that grew, one at a time, inside the bundle. The small keepsake a one who sees off pressed into a one who leaves. The knots that, felt out when lonely on the road, made you recall who had sent you off.

A leaving person has the loneliness of the road, and one who sees off the loneliness of the place, it was said. $userName knows. Those two lonelinesses are — no longer separate, when two people take to the same road. For two who leave together, there remains, with no seeing-off and no farewell, only the road they go side by side.

So $userName does not fear the next post-station. Wherever it reaches, $userName knows the horses will be changed there together with that face. Like that knot that loosed with one hand — $userName, even across one lifetime, rides the same road with the same person.

yeokma.celebRole: person who sold tickets at the dock club

paragraphs.gi: In $era, whenever $userName left, $celebName prepared a map of the next port. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole moving along the sailings, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A restless streak kept carrying $userName to a new port, always leaving a dear stage empty. $celebName slipped a small compass into the departing $userName's instrument case.
paragraphs.jeon: One dawn $userName let the just-leaving ship go on purpose and stayed on the dock, sitting beside $celebName. The one who only ever left had stayed for the first time, so $celebName folded the map and watched the sea together.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of restless streak can carry that wandering sea-route into this life. So your heart might turn only toward the port where $celebName is.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So whatever city you go to, the place a $celebName concert was held might come to mind first.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So even traveling, you may find yourself seeking the places $celebName filmed first.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So arriving in an unfamiliar city, you might first want to see the stadium $celebName played at.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So wherever you go, your heart might turn first to where a $celebName exhibition was held.

yeokma.title: The End of the Eurail Pass
yeokma.logline: Backpacking across Europe, $userName keeps running into $celebName at every city, and the two travel together until the pass expires.

chapters.heading: Paris, the First City
chapters.body:
A Eurail pass has an expiry date.

Buy a one-month pass, and for a month you can ride any train in Europe. And after a month, the pass becomes just a scrap of paper. $userName held that one-month pass in hand and left for Europe. $userName was twenty-four.

$userName had no thought of staying long in one place. Two days, three days in one city. And on to the next. For a month, $userName wanted to see as many cities as possible. That was backpacking. Not staying, but passing through.

The first city was Paris. $userName unpacked at a hostel. A dormitory room, rows of bunk beds. A place where people who lived out of one backpack gathered.

In the evening, in the hostel's shared kitchen, one person spoke to $userName. While cooking a cup of instant noodles.

"Are you from Korea?"

$userName raised the head.

"...How did you know."

"That small doll hanging on your backpack. Korean backpackers hang a lot of them. And turning your head at the smell of instant noodles. That's a Korean."

That was $celebName. A person met in a Paris hostel kitchen, whose name $userName did not know.

"Is $celebName backpacking too?"

"Yes. A one-month Eurail pass. Starting from Paris, I haven't decided how far I'll go. I'll drift until the pass ends."

"I have a one-month pass too. I'm starting from Paris too."

The two ate instant noodles together that night in the hostel kitchen. Talked about travel. Neither had a plan to stay in one place. Both were people who would drift until the pass expired.

The next morning, $userName boarded a train to the next city. As $userName left the hostel, $celebName waved from the kitchen.

"$userName. Have a good trip."

$userName waved too. Meetings while backpacking were all like that. One night at a hostel, and then each one's own road.

chapters.heading: What Hangs on the Backpack
chapters.body:
Five days later, in the kitchen of a hostel in Prague — one person was cooking a cup of instant noodles.

That person turned around. It was $celebName.

"...The Paris instant noodles."

$userName laughed, at a loss. $celebName laughed too.

"We meet here again?"

"Backpacking routes must be similar. Come east from Paris, and everyone stops in Prague."

That night, the two walked the bridge of Prague. The city lights wavered on the river. At parting, $celebName hung something small on $userName's backpack. A small keyring shaped like the Prague bridge.

"This is a Prague souvenir. I'll hang it on $userName's backpack. The mark that we met in Prague."

$userName did not stay still either. $userName took something small from their own backpack and hung it on $celebName's backpack.

"Then me too. The mark that we met, both of us should hang it."

That was the start. After that the two ran into each other again and again in the cities of Europe. In Vienna, in Budapest, in the square of some small city. Each time they met they ate instant noodles together, and at parting hung something small on each other's backpacks.

On $userName's backpack the things $celebName hung grew. On $celebName's backpack the things $userName hung grew. Each time they moved cities, the backpacks grew a little heavier. But $userName liked that weight. It was the number of cities the two had passed through together.

On the rooftop of a Budapest hostel, $celebName looked at their own backpack. Counting the things hung, one by one.

"$userName. Look at this. Paris, Prague, Vienna, Budapest. The cities we met in are all hanging on the backpack."

"The backpack has become our travel diary."

"So it has. But — this diary has a last page too. The day the pass expires."

$celebName said that, and looked at the night view. $userName looked at the night view too. It was beautiful, but even that beauty had an expiry date.

chapters.heading: The Days Running Out
chapters.body:
The one-month pass dwindled, day by day.

$userName could count the stamps on the pass. One stamp was one city, and between those cities the meetings with $celebName were mixed in. As the pass's empty spaces ran out, the days the two could spend together ran out too.

$userName, each time moving cities, had a habit of looking at the hostel's shared kitchen first. Whether there was a person cooking instant noodles. Even knowing it was foolish. To keep meeting the same person while backpacking was rarer than a pass full of stamps.

And yet the two kept meeting. With thousands of travelers going the same route, of all things the two were in the same city on the same day.

One night, in a Vienna hostel, $celebName, tidying their own backpack, said. Fidgeting with the hanging souvenirs.

"$userName. I'm a person who doesn't grow attached to a city. Two days, three days. I leave before the attachment grows. Because that doesn't hurt. For a person who has to leave, not growing attached is the way to survive."

"Is $userName the same?"

$userName thought a moment.

"Similar. I'm used to drifting. Staying makes me uneasy instead."

$celebName nodded. Then looked at the souvenirs hanging on their own backpack. The things $userName had hung, city by city.

"But $userName. I can't tidy this backpack. I'm good at not growing attached to cities — but the things hanging on this backpack, I can't take them off. Even though it's heavy. What does that mean."

$userName could not answer that. Because $userName felt $userName knew the meaning of those words. Not growing attached to cities, but to a person — that didn't work. $userName's backpack too had never once been tidied, grown heavy as it was.

The pass's empty spaces had only a few days left. That day, the two parted without speaking further of those few days.

chapters.heading: The Expiry Date
chapters.body:
A month had nearly passed. $userName's pass had only a single day left. And that last day was the same for $celebName's pass. The two had started traveling on the same day, so the expiry date was the same.

$userName reached the last city. It was Rome. The last city the pass could reach.

$userName unpacked at a Rome hostel and looked at the shared kitchen. Whether there was a person cooking instant noodles. $celebName was not there.

$userName lay on the hostel bed and looked at the pass, now nearly expired. A pass stamped full for a month. And looked at the backpack set at the foot. With the things $celebName had hung city by city, the backpack had grown heavy.

$userName looked at that heavy backpack and thought. The words $celebName had said in Vienna. 'The things hanging on this backpack, I can't take them off. Even though it's heavy.' $userName's backpack was the same. Never once tidied, only grown heavy.

$userName knew. The pass ended tomorrow. When the pass ended the two could no longer ride the same train. The 'next city' would vanish. But the things hanging on $userName's backpack — would not vanish when the pass ended. The weight would remain as it was.

That night $userName resolved. If $userName met $celebName tomorrow, to say, for the first time, other words. Not the goodbye 'have a good trip' — but wholly different words, words that could be said even after the pass ended. To say in person that a city can be left behind, but a person cannot.

On the morning of the expiry date, $userName decided to take the last train. The last train the pass could ride. $userName slung the heavy backpack and went to the Rome train station.

But $userName had no way of knowing. Which city $celebName was spending the last day in. Even on the same expiry date, there was no guarantee the two would be at the same station.

$userName stood on the Rome station platform. Before the last train. And looked around among the people.

At the far end of the platform, a person with a heavy backpack — turned the head toward $userName.

chapters.heading: The End of the Eurail Pass
chapters.body:
$celebName cut through the people and walked toward $userName. With the heavy backpack slung.

The two looked a long while at each other's backpacks. The things hanging on $userName's, the things hanging on $celebName's. The trace of the same cities, grown the same on both backpacks.

"$userName. I thought you'd be in Rome."

$celebName spoke first.

"...How."

"We both started traveling on the same day. So the expiry date is the same, and the last city the pass can reach is the same too. Rome. I thought $userName would spend the last day in Rome. So — I came to Rome too. To take the last train."

$celebName pointed to their own backpack.

"$userName. The pass ends today. This train is the last. Once I ride it, there's no next city anymore. But — this backpack. Even when the pass ends, this weight doesn't go away. I couldn't tidy this backpack the whole month."

$userName recalled a resolve made in the Rome hostel.

"$celebName. The whole backpacking trip I said 'have a good trip' at every city. Because that's a backpacker's greeting. But — now that the pass is ending, I want to say other words."

$userName set down their own backpack before $celebName. The hanging things jingled.

"$celebName. I couldn't tidy this backpack either. Even though it's heavy. I don't grow attached to cities — but the things $celebName hung, I can't take them off. I know now what that means. A city can be left behind, but a person cannot."

$userName stepped one step closer.

"$celebName. Travel with me. Even when the pass ends, the trip doesn't end. There are roads you can go without a pass. On foot, by bus. We just have to make a new road. I'm a person used to drifting. A person uneasy at staying. That doesn't change. But — while drifting, I can drift together with one person. Sharing the weight of the backpack."

$celebName's eyes wavered. Then, suddenly, he smiled.

"$userName. The truth is — I was about to say that too."

"...What."

"On the train to Rome, I resolved. That if I met $userName before the last train, I would be the first to say, let's travel together. I didn't want to carry this backpack, grown heavy over a month, alone anymore. A specialist at not growing attached — with $userName, that didn't work. So on the expiry date, I ran to the Rome train station."

Before those words, $userName slung the heavy backpack onto the shoulder again. The two had come, with the same heart, before the same train.

The announcement of the last train's departure flowed out.

"$celebName. This train — let's ride it together. It's the pass's last train. But for us — it's the first train of traveling together."

$celebName took $userName's hand. With the smile first seen in the Paris hostel kitchen.

chapters.heading: A Pass-Free Journey
chapters.body:
From that day the two traveled with no pass.

The Eurail pass had expired for both. They could no longer ride trains freely. Instead the two walked, took buses, sometimes hitchhiked. It was slower than with a pass. But the two liked that slow pace. The slower it was, the longer the view they watched together.

The two joined their backpacks. The hanging things jingled all at once. It was heavy, but the two shared that weight. One person's backpack had been a heavy load, but shared between two it became a bearable weight.

They were still people who could not stay long in one city. Spend a few days and they set out on the road again. That they were used to drifting and uneasy at staying did not change.

But this time it was different. Wherever the next city was, the two went together. Two people who had said 'have a good trip' at every city now arrived together and left together at every city.

$userName sometimes recalled the first hostel in Paris. The day of turning the head at the smell of instant noodles. And the expired Eurail pass. That scrap of paper stamped full.

The pass had ended, but the trip had not. If anything, only after the pass ended did the two, for the first time, ride the same train all the way to the end together.

One night, cooking instant noodles in the kitchen of a small-city hostel, $celebName said.

"$userName. I said I'm a person who doesn't grow attached to cities. That if I grow attached I don't want to leave, so not growing attached doesn't hurt."

"I remember."

"But now I know. That was — talk for a person traveling alone. When there's someone to leave with, growing attached doesn't hurt. Because you leave when they leave. Because there's nothing left behind."

$userName chewed those words long. A person traveling alone fears attachment, and two people traveling go with the attachment together. That difference was the difference between loneliness and journeying-together.

$celebName set two cups of instant noodles on the table. As, in Paris, it had first been done. Outside the window, the lights of an unfamiliar city sparkled. What the next city would be, was unknown. Still one thing was certain. Wherever they reached, there would be someone to eat instant noodles beside, in the seat alongside.

yeokma.epilogue:
That Eurail pass faded somewhere in a drawer. The month of stamps, the bunk beds of the dormitory, the hostel kitchen where instant noodles were cooked — all grew dim. The small souvenirs that jingled hanging on the backpacks would have faded in color too.

Still, $userName, on reaching an unfamiliar city, searches among the people for one face. At the smell of instant noodles, turns the head slightly. And when the backpack grows heavy, does not hate that weight.

What $userName recalls in supporting $celebName is those small things hung on each other's backpacks, city by city. The tender weight that, even growing heavy, was never once taken off.

A person traveling alone fears attachment, and two people traveling go with the attachment together. $userName knows. The expiry date is the expiry of a scrap of paper, not the expiry of the heart. You may not grow attached to cities, but you can grow attached to one person, and that attachment, when two leave together, no longer hurts.

So $userName does not fear the next city. Wherever it reaches, $userName knows there will be instant noodles eaten there together with that face. As a new journey began at the end of the Eurail pass — $userName, even across one lifetime, boards the same train with the same person.

paragraphs.gi: In $era, late one night $celebName opened the door without a word for the hunted $userName. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole fleeing a false charge, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A protective streak set $celebName beside $userName in the thick of crisis. $celebName put out the lanterns on purpose along the pursuers' path, covering $userName's footsteps with the dark.
paragraphs.jeon: One dawn $celebName pressed their own identity tag into $userName's hand, letting them slip out the city gate under a false name. A whole name had been given away, so $userName carried that tag close for a lifetime.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of protective tie can carry that saving hand into this life. So when things are hard, your heart might always lean $celebName's way.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a night a day fell apart, you may play one $celebName song and barely catch your breath.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So when your heart is at its lowest, you might replay a $celebName work and find comfort.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So on a day you want to give it all up, you may watch a $celebName match clip and rise again.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a daunting night, you might hold onto one $celebName video and steady your heart.

cheoneul.genre: Sageuk — A Tale of Shelter and Rescue
cheoneul.title: The One Who Hid Me
cheoneul.logline: Falsely accused and pursued, $userName is hidden, at great risk, by an inn-keeper $celebName.

chapters.heading: The Night of Pursuit
chapters.body:
Late Joseon, Hanyang. That year, winter came unusually early.

$userName was not a criminal. And yet that night, $userName had become one.

Silver had vanished from the magistracy storehouse. The one entrusted with the storehouse key was $userName. So, along with the vanished silver, $userName's name became a criminal's name. Who had taken that silver, $userName did not know. But $userName knew one thing — that $userName had not taken it. And yet what $userName knew, no one in Hanyang would believe.

For the single reason of having held the key, $userName became a thief. The people $userName had worked alongside would not meet $userName's eyes. Neighbors who had exchanged greetings until yesterday passed quickly by $userName's gate today. A false accusation was that kind of thing. Before you can even open your mouth, the world closes first.

Constables broke down the gate of $userName's house. $userName climbed the back wall. Barefoot. There was no time even to fetch shoes.

"Seize him! That's the one who robbed the storehouse!"

Behind, torches gave chase. $userName turned alley after alley. The alleys of Hanyang were a maze, but the torches were stubborn. $userName's breath climbed to the throat. The soles of the feet tore. Still $userName could not stop. To stop was the end. The end of a falsely-accused criminal was the rack.

Turning the alleys, $userName thought. Who made me this. Who robbed the storehouse, and who pushed that crime onto me. But the pursued have no room to think. The torches were faster than thought, and $userName's legs gave way faster than thought.

$userName's legs gave way at the end of one alley. There was no running farther. Before the eyes was a small inn. A lamp was lit. With the last of the strength, $userName knocked on that door. Unable even to say save me, $userName only struck the door with a hand.

The door opened. A person holding a lamp looked down at $userName.

It was $celebName. The keeper of that inn.

$userName, kneeling, looked up. The words would not come. The torches were drawing closer. $celebName swept a glance once over $userName's torn feet, the bloodless face, the pursued eyes.

"...Are you pursued?"

$userName could not answer. To answer was to make this person, too, see $userName as a criminal. But $celebName did not wait for an answer. $celebName took $userName's arm and pulled him inside. And shut the door.

"Get under the floor. Quickly."

$userName, not even knowing what the words meant, slid into the space beneath the floor the hand pointed to. There was nothing in the mind but the wish to live.

chapters.heading: Beneath the Floor
chapters.body:
Beneath the floor was narrow and dark. $userName crouched down there. It smelled of earth. The sound of the heart was so loud, it seemed it would be caught by that sound.

Soon the torches reached the front of the inn. There was a sound of knocking. Far rougher than the knocking $userName had done.

"Keeper! A criminal fled into this alley. The one who robbed the storehouse. Did you see him!"

$celebName's voice came. An astonishingly calm voice.

"A criminal? At this late hour, who would have entered. As you see, it's an inn with not even a guest."

"We must see inside."

"See it."

The footsteps of the constables came into the inn. Onto the floor. Right above $userName's head. Earth-dust fell through the cracks of the floorboards. $userName stopped breathing. Each footstep felt as if it trod across $userName's back. With a single board between them, $userName lay alongside death.

"...What about beneath the floor," one constable said.

$userName's heart froze. It's over, $userName thought.

"Beneath the floor?" $celebName laughed. "A dog has had pups and is down there. The mother is fierce; reach a hand in and get bitten, and that's no concern of mine."

The constable stamped a foot once toward beneath the floor. That tremor shook $userName's body. In the dark beneath the floor, a dog truly growled, low. $userName did not know where that dog had come from. But that growl, that day, saved $userName. The constable did not reach a hand in.

The constables left. The torches drew away. The alley grew quiet again.

A long while later, the floor-hatch opened. $celebName held out a hand.

"Come out. They've gone."

$userName took that hand and crawled out. The whole body trembled. Not from the cold. The fact of having lived, the very fact of having lived, was frightening.
