"...How could you hide me," $userName asked. "If I am a criminal, the keeper becomes a criminal too. Had you pretended not to know me, the keeper would have been safe."

$celebName set down the lamp. And looked at $userName. A long while.

"I saw the eyes of the one who knocked on the door," $celebName said. "Those were not a criminal's eyes. They were a pursued person's eyes. One who has done wrong is not pursued that way. It is the wronged who are pursued that way. I have kept an inn a long time. The eyes of the one who knocks on the door — I am, more often than not, not wrong about them."

Before those words, $userName held the mouth shut a long while. All of Hanyang called $userName a thief, and this first-met person, looking at those eyes, said $userName was not a criminal. That one line warmed somewhere deeper than beneath the floor.

chapters.heading: A Few Days at the Inn
chapters.body:
$userName stayed at that inn. Only a few days, $userName thought. When the feet healed, when strength returned to the legs, $userName would leave.

$celebName treated $userName neither as a guest nor as a criminal. Simply — as a person who was there.

In the morning $celebName put salve on $userName's torn feet. Without a word. When $userName said sorry, $celebName only said, "What crime have your feet committed." By day $celebName brought $userName warm gruel. When $userName said "I have no way to repay this debt," $celebName only said, "What debt is one bowl of gruel."

$celebName did not press $userName about the storehouse. Who robbed the storehouse, whether $userName was truly innocent — such things were never once asked. $userName found that strange. Ordinary people ask. They ask, and probe, and doubt. But $celebName did not ask. He simply let $userName be there.

Through those few days, $userName recalled something long forgotten.

That someone did not doubt $userName. That someone heard $userName's words to the end. What that felt like, $userName had forgotten entirely while being pursued. While all of Hanyang called $userName a criminal, $userName had begun to doubt even $userName's own self. Did I truly not take it. Did I not, without knowing, do something.

That doubt, $celebName stopped.

"Keeper," $userName asked one night. "How do you believe so, that I truly did not rob the storehouse. With no proof."

$celebName, in the middle of trimming the lamp wick, answered.

"$userName has been here a few days. See a person a few days, and you know them a little. $userName is not a person who would rob a storehouse. One who would rob does not feel sorry over a bowl of gruel that way. One who would rob does not feel so abashed when salve is put on his feet. I saw $userName for a few days, and I trust my eyes that watched those few days."

At those words $userName lowered the head. Tears came. The tears never shed once while being pursued burst at that one warm word.

$celebName pretended not to see those tears, only trimmed the lamp. Only a long while later said, small.

"Weep. You held it back a few days for lack of a place to weep, surely. Here, you may weep."

chapters.heading: Why Go This Far
chapters.body:
$userName's heart was heavy. The longer the days of staying, the greater the risk $celebName bore.

The crime of hiding a falsely-accused criminal. If it came out, $celebName too would mount the rack. At that thought $userName could not sleep. For the sake of $userName's own life, $userName was endangering a person with no wrong at all.

"Keeper," $userName said one night. "I must leave. Stay longer, and the keeper is in danger. The feet are healed enough. This kindness — I will repay even in a next life."

$celebName looked at $userName. And shook the head.

"If you go out now," $celebName said, "you will not get out of Hanyang. $userName's description is posted on every alley. A notice of a thief is up. To go out now is to walk to the rack."

"Even so."

"It is not even-so," $celebName cut $userName off. For the first time the voice grew firm. "$userName keeps worrying about me, but the order of that is wrong. The one in danger now is $userName. Not me. A person in a position to be dragged to the rack worries first about the one who hid him. That is — proof that $userName is no thief. A thief knows only his own life."

$userName lost the words.

"You ask why I go this far," $celebName went on. "I too was once pursued. It was long ago. My legs gave way at the end of some alley then, and an old man hid me beneath the floor of his house. That old man did not know me. Did not know what crime I bore. He simply — looked at the eyes of me knocking on the door, and hid me. Without that old man, I would not be here now."

$celebName looked at $userName across the lamp.

"That old man said something to me. 'It is not that the one who received repays — it is that the one who received gives, again, to someone else.' I have kept those words in my heart all my life. When $userName knocked on my door that night — I recalled that old man's floor. Now it's my turn, I thought. $userName has no need to repay me a kindness. $userName must someday, to someone knocking on a door — simply open it."

chapters.heading: The Real Thief
chapters.body:
On the very day $userName meant to leave, $celebName held $userName back again.

"Stay three more days," $celebName said. "There is somewhere I must go."

Where $celebName went, $userName did not know. Only three days later did $userName learn.

For those three days, $celebName had combed all of Hanyang. To learn who had taken the storehouse silver. $celebName, an inn-keeper, knew the drinkers of Hanyang. Which drinker had suddenly grown loose with money, in which gambling den silver had suddenly circulated — for knowing such things, there was no seat like an inn-keeper's. Because at a drinking table, people's mouths grow loose.

On the third night, $celebName returned to the inn bringing one person. A clerk of the magistracy. The one who kept the storehouse ledger.

"This one," $celebName said. "skimmed the storehouse silver and pinned the false accusation on $userName. When the one who keeps the ledger robs the storehouse, it is safest. Because he can alter the ledger with his own hand. And pin the crime on the one entrusted with the key, and no one suspects."

The clerk trembled before $celebName. $celebName had, in three days, found all of it — the clerk's gambling debt, the clerk's suddenly grown household, even the money-changer where the clerk had exchanged the silver. In the money-changer's ledger, the weight of the silver the clerk had exchanged was written. The exact same weight as the silver vanished from the storehouse.

$userName looked blankly at $celebName.

"...How could you go this far," $userName said. "The keeper bore enough risk just hiding me. Yet to find the real thief too."

$celebName looked at $userName.

"To only hide you," $celebName said. "would make $userName a person who lives all his life beneath a floor. A person pursued all his life. Even fleeing far from Hanyang, the name of thief follows. I wanted to bring $userName out from beneath the floor. Not for a moment, but forever. For that, the false accusation had to be lifted. To hide a person is a day's worth of kindness. To save a person is — to go all the way."

$userName stood a long while before those words.

When all of Hanyang had cast $userName off, one person had not cast $userName off, to the end. Not stopping at hiding, but lifting the accusation too. That clerk was dragged to the magistracy the next day, and $userName's name was erased from the roll of thieves.

chapters.heading: Staying at the Inn
chapters.body:
The accusation was lifted. $userName was no longer a criminal.

The magistracy told $userName to take charge of the storehouse key again. $userName refused. $userName did not wish to return to that storehouse, to that place.

$userName returned to the inn. Said to $celebName.

"Keeper. Let me stay at this inn. Not as a guest, but as one who works. As one who wipes the floors, boils the gruel, lights the lamp. It is not that I mean to repay a debt. A debt cannot be repaid in a lifetime. It is only that I — wish to live in a place that does not cast a person off to the end. This inn is such a place."

$celebName looked at $userName a long while. And smiled.

"Inn work is hard," $celebName said. "You rise at dawn to light the fire, take guests late into the night. Even so, is it good?"

"It is good."

"Then," $celebName said. "promise one thing. Never again go beneath the floor. $userName's place is on the floor. Beside the lamp. The place where guests are received. Beneath the floor is — no more."

$userName made that promise.

From that day $userName lived at that inn. At dawn lit the fire, at night lit the lamp. The door a pursued person knocked on, this time $userName became the one who opened it. When someone whose legs gave way at the end of an alley knocked on the door, $userName did just as $celebName had done for $userName — looked at that person's eyes. Whether they were a criminal's eyes, or a pursued person's eyes.

And if they were a pursued person's eyes, $userName opened the door. As $celebName had for $userName. As that old man had for $celebName.

$celebName, watching such a $userName, said.

"The one who received gives, again, to someone else, that old man said. $userName is doing that now. The one who was hidden becoming the one who hides — that is what an inn is. That is how a person saves a person. Save one person, and that person saves another. That way the lamp does not go out."

That inn's lamp in Hanyang did not go out for a long time after. At the end of the last alley of the pursued.

cheoneul.epilogue:
There is no one now who remembers that inn in Hanyang. The floor, the lamp, the sleet at the end of the alley — all are gone.

And yet in one corner of $userName's heart, an inn still stands. On nights when it seems everyone has turned their back, $userName knocks on the door of that inn. An inn by the name of $celebName.

What $userName feels watching $celebName's stage, hearing $celebName's voice, is the sensation of one closed door opening. The way, on the day all of Hanyang called $userName a thief, one person looked at those eyes and said 'you are not the kind of person to do that.' $celebName is, to $userName, a door that is never once locked.

The one who was hidden becomes the one who hides, it was said. $userName lives those words. The lamp received beneath the floor, $userName lights again at the end of someone else's alley.

Though the inn has crumbled, that door is still open within $userName's heart. So on frightening nights, $userName does not hesitate to knock on that door. Because $celebName will never once lock it.

cheoneul.celebRole: stage-costume hand who carried them to safety

paragraphs.gi: In $era, when $userName collapsed on stage, $celebName at once hoisted them up and pushed through the blizzard. Back then $userName tended to be a hungry $userRole still taking the stage, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A protective streak stood $celebName beside $userName at the very moment of falling. $celebName burst out of the dressing-room tent before the show had even ended.
paragraphs.jeon: That night $celebName gave up their own coat and blanket entirely, warming $userName's hands while shivering with cold themselves. Having shed everything they had, $userName remembered that warmth long after.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of protective tie can reach that guarding heart into this life. So on a cold day $celebName might come to mind first.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a day your heart feels chilled, a warm $celebName ballad may wrap around you like a blanket.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a lonely night, one $celebName work might keep you company.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So on a day your drive has cooled, the sight of $celebName playing may warm your chest again.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So when your heart feels cold, one word $celebName left behind might bring the warmth back.

cheoneul.title: The Hand at Heungnam Pier
cheoneul.logline: Having lost family in the refugee crush, $userName has a hand held to the end by a stranger, $celebName.

chapters.heading: The Road to the Pier
chapters.body:
The winter of 1950. The road to Heungnam was packed with people.

War was coming down from the north. So people walked south, toward the sea. $userName was in that crowd too. A small sibling carried on the back, the hem of Mother's clothing gripped in the hand.

The road was frozen. The wind blew like a knife. Still there was no stopping. Stop, and the war caught up. People walked, casting away their loads. First they cast off household things, then food, and later they walked casting off even their shoes. A ship was coming to Heungnam pier, they said. Board that ship and you live, they said.

"We only have to reach the pier," Mother said. "Only the pier."

$userName walked holding to those words. Only the pier. Only the pier. Nothing else could be thought. Though the feet were cold, though the sibling on the back was heavy, $userName walked gripping only the hem of Mother's clothing.

On the road there were thousands of people like $userName. People with a child on the back, people supporting an elder, families hand in hand. All walked toward the same place, murmuring the same words. Reach the pier and you live. No one looked back. Behind, there were burning villages.

"Is the sibling held tight," Mother kept asking. "Hold the hand firm. Never let go. On this road, let go of a hand and you can't find them again."

Each time, $userName gripped Mother's hem tighter.

The closer the pier drew, the denser the crowd grew. There was no gap between person and person. $userName gripped Mother's hem tighter. In this crowd, lose a hand, and it felt one could never find them again.

That premonition was not wrong.

chapters.heading: The Lost Hand
chapters.body:
Before the pier, the crowd surged as if collapsing.

A ship came into view. Someone cried "A ship!" and at that one word thousands surged forward at once. It was a wave of people. $userName's feet left the ground. Pushed by the crowd, $userName could not stand on their own feet.

"Mother!" $userName cried.

The hem gripped in the hand slipped free in that instant.

$userName reached out a hand. Into the crowd. But what was caught was only a stranger's shoulder, a stranger's back. Mother's face drew away among the people. Mother's mouth was crying something. It seemed to be $userName's name. But that sound did not reach.

$userName called Mother's name. Until the throat tore. But thousands were calling someone's name at once. $userName's voice was buried in it. At a pier full of the sound of names being called, only $userName's name did not reach Mother.

The sibling carried on the back cried. $userName, steadying the sibling, was pushed by the crowd toward the pier. The opposite way from where Mother had been. $userName tried to go against it, but going against a wave of people was impossible.

"Mother! Mother!"

It was no use. The crowd drew $userName farther and farther from Mother. That day, $userName lost family. Not to the war, but to a wave of people. At that pier where too many people wished to live at once.

$userName was pushed out to one side of the pier. Still carrying the sibling. Mother was nowhere. $userName sank down on the spot. There was no strength to stand. Whom to search for, where to go — none of it could be known.

The loneliest place in the world was there. In the middle of a pier full of thousands of people. $userName held the sibling and crouched in that loneliness.

chapters.heading: A Stranger’s Hand
chapters.body:
Then, someone crouched down beside $userName.

It was $celebName. $userName did not know that person. Just a stranger, perhaps brushed past somewhere on the refugee road.

"Did you lose your family?" $celebName asked.

$userName nodded. The words would not come.

$celebName looked at $userName a moment. Looked at the sibling carried on $userName's back. The sibling's small fingers were turned blue with cold. Seeing that, $celebName held out a hand.

"Give me your hand."

$userName looked blankly at that hand.

"At this pier," $celebName said. "let go of a hand and you lose a person. You just saw it. So give me your hand. Until we board the ship, I won't let go."

$userName took that hand. Without knowing it. The hand that had had nothing to hold caught hold of that hand.

$celebName's hand was cold. But firm. The hand $userName caught, $celebName truly did not let go. Each time the crowd surged, $celebName pulled $userName to himself. Each time the wave of people tried to split the two apart, $celebName gripped harder. Hard enough that $celebName's finger-joints went white.

"Why," $userName barely asked. "Why help a stranger. At this pier everyone seeks only their own way to live. Hold one more hand and you too are in danger."

$celebName answered, parting the crowd.

"Because everyone seeks only their own way, everyone loses their family. Hold just one more hand at the pier, and a person who needn't be lost is not lost. I have two hands. One holds my load, one was empty. An empty hand — is empty so that someone may hold it."

$userName gripped that hand tighter. The hand that had lost the hem of Mother's clothing vowed that, this time, it would never let go.

chapters.heading: On the Deck
chapters.body:
The ship overflowed with people.

It was originally a cargo ship. Not a ship for carrying people. And yet into that cargo hold, onto that deck, into every foothold, people packed in. Thousands. It was a wonder the ship did not sink.

$celebName, holding $userName's hand, parted that crowd and boarded the ship. Took a spot in one corner of the deck. It was narrow. Narrow enough that person and person had to sit shoulder against shoulder.

The ship left the pier. Heungnam drew away. $userName watched the receding pier. Somewhere over there Mother would be. She might not have boarded a ship, or might have boarded another. $userName could not know. At that helplessness, $userName gripped the deck rail and wept.

$celebName did not stop that weeping. Only sat beside $userName, not letting go of the held hand.

Night came. The night over the sea was cold enough to flay the skin. The sibling trembled with cold and cried. $celebName took off the coat he was wearing and covered $userName and the sibling with it.

"Sir," $userName said. Not knowing what to call $celebName, calling so. "You must be cold."

"I am an adult," $celebName said. "An adult may shiver a little. A child must not shiver. That is the reason an adult exists."

At those words $userName wept again. Not the grief of having lost family, but because a stranger's coat was so warm.

$userName, soothing the sibling in the dark, called the sibling's name without knowing it. $celebName heard that name. And $userName's own name too, in the words $userName whispered to the sibling — "the two of us, at least, let us live."

"$userName," $celebName said in the dark. "Weeping is all right. But remember one thing. $userName did not lose the sibling. At the pier, in that turmoil, $userName kept the sibling on the back to the end. Losing Mother is not $userName's fault. That is the fault of that pier. $userName — protected what could be protected."

$userName never forgot those words all life long. The heart that blamed itself eased a little at that one word.

"Sir," $userName asked. "Do you have no family? While taking care of others this way, your family."

$celebName was silent a long while. Then, watching the sea, answered.

"I too lost family to this war. So I know. How great it is, for someone to sit beside a person who has lost family. Then, no one was beside me. So I — mean to be beside $userName."

chapters.heading: The Southern Harbor
chapters.body:
The ship went three days. For three days $celebName did not leave $userName's side.

He broke off his own portion of the rice ball for the sibling. When the sibling grew seasick, he rubbed the back. When $userName, worn out, fell asleep, $celebName did not sleep and guarded the two. Each time $userName woke, $celebName was always awake.

The deck was cold all three days. Drinking water ran short, and food shorter still. People endured the nights leaning on each other's warmth. Some old person could not endure those three days and was buried at sea. Each time, $userName held the sibling tighter, and $celebName laid a hand on such a $userName's shoulder. He did not speak. Only by laying a hand, $celebName said "there is a person here."

On the second day, the sibling ran a fever. $userName did not know what to do. $celebName tore his own clothing, wet it in seawater, and laid it on the sibling's brow. Did so all night. Until the sibling's fever broke.

"Sir," $userName said. "Because of us you are wearing yourself thin, sir. The clothing, the sleep, the food."

"They exist to be worn thin," $celebName said. "Clothing to cover a cold person, sleep to be put off when there is something to stay awake and guard, food to give to one weaker. If they are not worn thin — what use are they."

"Did you not sleep a wink?" $userName asked.

"I slept," $celebName said. $userName knew it was a lie. Because $celebName's eyes had been bloodshot for three days.

On the third day, the southern harbor came into view. The ship reached land. Only then did the words 'we have lived' feel real.

The harbor seethed once more with a crowd. Thousands who got off the ship poured into the harbor at once. $userName was afraid of losing the hand again. That instant at Heungnam pier rose to mind.

But $celebName did not let go of the hand until they had set foot in the harbor.

When the crowd had all drained away and the harbor grew quiet — only then did $celebName slowly loosen the held hand.

"You may let go now," $celebName said. "Here, you will not be lost."

$userName looked at the loosened hand. It was the hand held for three days. The warmth of that hand still remained.

"Sir," $userName said. "How can I repay this kindness. Without you, sir, I would have lost even the sibling at that pier. No — I would not have lived either."

$celebName shook the head.

"Do not repay it," $celebName said. "Instead, make one promise. Someday, when beside $userName a person who has lost a hand is sunk down — then hold out $userName's empty hand. At a pier, on a road, anywhere. That one hand saves a person. As I saved $userName."

$celebName gathered the belongings and rose. $userName wished to hold $celebName back. But $celebName was already walking into the crowd.

$userName cried out toward that retreating back.

"At least your name! At least tell me your name!"

$celebName glanced back a moment. And smiled.

"Never mind the name. Remember me as the person who held your hand at the pier. That is enough."

$celebName vanished into the crowd that way. $userName never came to know that person's name.

chapters.heading: The Empty Hand
chapters.body:
$userName survived in the south.

Raised the sibling. Mother was never, in the end, found. Whether she had boarded another ship, or remained at that pier — $userName could not know. Bearing that helplessness, $userName lived.

The years passed. $userName became an adult, and the sibling too became an adult. The very sibling $userName had carried to the end at that pier grew up safely and lived a life of its own. The one thing $userName had protected at that pier grew up that way.

$userName never once forgot $celebName's words. The words that an empty hand is empty so that someone may hold it. So $userName could not simply pass by a person sunk down on the road. $userName recognized the face of one who had lost a hand. The face $userName had worn at that pier.

Seeing such a person, $userName crouched down. As $celebName had crouched beside $userName at Heungnam pier. And held out a hand. The very words $celebName had said to $userName, the same.

"Give me your hand. I won't let go."

That way, $userName passed the hand received at the pier to another person. Once, and once more. The hand $userName received was one, but the hands $userName passed on were beyond counting.

$userName never, all of life, came to know that stranger's name. But there was no need to. As $celebName had said, the name was not important. What was important was — that one hand that had been empty at the pier.

Even grown old, $userName sometimes recalled that pier of Heungnam. The loneliest place in the world. And the one person who crouched in that place and held out a hand. $userName remembered that person, all of life, as the person who held a hand at the pier. Just as that person had wished.

cheoneul.epilogue:
If someone asks where the loneliest place in the world is, $userName could answer — the middle of a pier full of people. Though $userName has never once been to such a place, $userName knows the shape of that loneliness.

And $userName knows one thing more. How, in the middle of that loneliness, the act of someone crouching down and holding out a hand — saves a person.

Watching $celebName's stage, watching $celebName hold out a hand to someone, $userName's hand grows warm for no cause. Something like the warmth of a hand never let go for three days remains in $userName's palm. It is not a memory of this life, and yet $userName's hand knows that warmth.

A received hand is passed to the next person, it was said. So $userName cannot simply pass by a face sunk down on the road. One hand received at the pier made $userName a person who holds out a hand.

The name was never, in the end, known. Even so it was all right. What was needed between $userName and $celebName was not a name, but one hand never let go to the end. That hand, even crossing the ages, is still warm.

paragraphs.gi: In $era, when debt collectors burst in, $celebName hid $userName in the liquor cellar and calmly wiped the glasses. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole hunted by a debt with no way to repay, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A protective streak set $celebName on $userName's path that hunted night. $celebName left that door behind the counter — known only to $userName — open on purpose, always.
paragraphs.jeon: That night $celebName tucked a whole month's wages into $userName's pocket and said, "run first." Another's debt had been blocked with their own money, so $userName couldn't bring themselves to spend that envelope.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of protective tie can carry that saving hand into this life. So whenever things feel daunting, $celebName's voice might be a comfort.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a day you can't breathe, one $celebName voice may ease your heart.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a stifling night, you might play a $celebName interview and let your heart settle.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So when you feel about to break, one scene of a $celebName match may come to you like cheering.

cheoneul.title: 3 a.m. in the Emergency Room
cheoneul.logline: On the verge of collapse, $userName is caught and set upright, every time, by night-shift doctor $celebName.

chapters.heading: The 3 a.m. Patient
chapters.body:
3 a.m. The university hospital emergency room was bright as midday.

An emergency room has no night. The fluorescent lights are always on, and time always flows busily. And at that dawn, $userName lay on an ER bed.

$userName had collapsed. On the street. After working days without sleep, days without eating properly — $userName simply collapsed in the middle of the road. Someone called it in, and $userName was carried by ambulance to this ER.

When $userName opened the eyes, one person stood beside $userName's bed.

It was $celebName. The night-shift doctor of that ER.

"Are you coming to?" $celebName asked.

$userName nodded slowly. The fluorescent light on the ceiling was so bright the eyes stung.

$celebName looked at the chart. And looked at $userName. Looked at $userName longer than at the chart.

"There is nothing seriously wrong on the tests," $celebName said. "But — that nothing is seriously wrong is the stranger thing. When a body collapses this far, usually something somewhere is broken. But there is nothing broken in you. You are just — all worn out. Not broken, but worn."

$userName could not answer those words. Because the word worn was too exact.

The ER was busy that dawn too. Someone groaned in the next bed, somewhere down the corridor a guardian was weeping. Amid that bustle, only $celebName stood quietly beside $userName's bed. He had to go to the next patient, yet $celebName did not go.

"This is not the first time, is it," $celebName said. Not asking, but in the tone of one who knew.

$userName looked only at the bed sheet. $celebName was right. It was not the first time.

"You can take one IV and go," $celebName said. "The body, that is. But — looking only at the body, you'll be carried back here in a few days. I can see it. Do night shifts long enough, and you see such things. Who will be carried back in."

At those words $userName raised the head. Met $celebName's eyes. Tired eyes. And yet those tired eyes were sincerely worried about $userName. A first-met patient.

chapters.heading: A Worn Person
chapters.body:
$userName was wearing thin.

It had been so for a while. The work had no end, and $userName took on all that endless work alone. Could not say no. Could not say it was hard. Just kept pushing, more, more. Telling himself he must not collapse, that if he collapsed it was all over.

And then collapsed on the street.

$celebName did not discharge $userName right away. The ER at 3 a.m. had, unexpectedly, a quiet gap. In that gap $celebName pulled a chair beside $userName's bed and sat.

"Patient," $celebName said. "I have done night shifts in the ER a long time. I have seen countless people carried in at dawn. And a person like you is the most dangerous."

"...But I'm fine," $userName said.

"That is the dangerous part," $celebName said. "Believing you're fine. A person cut by a knife knows they are hurt. So they come to the hospital. But a worn person does not know they are wearing thin. Believing they are fine, they keep wearing thin. And then one day they collapse on the street. Like today."

$userName looked at the ceiling.

"Patient," $celebName said. "Today there was nothing seriously wrong. You were lucky. But there is no guarantee you will be lucky next time. Wear thin and collapse, and next time something may truly break."

$userName was frightened by those words. But more frightening was that, even hearing them, $userName knew — knew himself — that tomorrow he would work the very same way again.

"I," $userName said small. "do not know how to stop. It feels that if I stop, everything will collapse."

$celebName looked at $userName a moment. And said.

"Then I will make you stop. For this dawn, at least."

chapters.heading: The Night-Shift Doctor's Dawn
chapters.body:
That dawn, $celebName let $userName sleep in the ER.

He gave the excuse, "the test results need checking once more." $userName knew it was an excuse. $celebName had given over one ER bed so $userName could rest, for that dawn at least.

$userName fell into a deep sleep for the first time in a long while. For the first time in days, $userName fell asleep without the thought of must-not-collapse. That someone was awake, watching over him — strangely set the heart at ease.

When $userName woke around 5 a.m., $celebName was still in the ER. Seeing one patient, writing a chart, seeing another. The dawn of a night shift was long.

Only then $userName saw it. That $celebName too was wearing thin.

Under $celebName's eyes was dark. It was the face of one who had not slept properly for days. An ER doctor's night shift was no different from the work that had worn $userName thin enough to collapse on the street. Perhaps it was worse.

$userName called $celebName.

"You too," $userName said. "are wearing thin. Telling me not to wear thin, you yourself are wearing thin."

$celebName stopped his hand and looked at $userName. Then smiled small.

"You're right," $celebName said. "I wear thin too. ER doctors all wear thin. But — I know I am wearing thin. That is what is different from you. Know you are wearing thin, and you can manage the wearing a little. Rest when you should, take help when offered. Not know you are wearing thin, and you cannot do that."

$celebName pulled a chair and sat beside $userName again.

"Patient," $celebName said. "I would like you to become a person who knows they are wearing thin. So that next time, before collapsing, you can stop yourself. Rather than being carried into the ER after collapsing."

$userName watched the ceiling a long while. Then asked, small.

"Why do you go this far for me. There must be more than one or two patients in the ER. I am just — a patient who can take an IV and go."

$celebName was silent a moment. Then answered.

"I too once wore thin," $celebName said. "Before I became a doctor. Believing I was fine, I kept wearing thin, and one day I collapsed on the street. Like you. Then a certain doctor pulled a chair and sat beside me. The test results were fine, but that doctor sat beside me on purpose and told me, 'You are wearing thin. You are not fine.' That one word saved me. So I became a doctor — and now I am sitting beside you. I am giving back what I received."

chapters.heading: The Day Collapsed Again
chapters.body:
$userName was discharged. And — wore thin again.

It was not that $userName had forgotten $celebName's words. $userName had not forgotten. It was only that $userName truly did not know how to stop. Work poured in endlessly again, and $userName took it all on alone again. Could not say no.

A few months later, $userName was carried into the ER again. It was dawn again.

When $userName opened the eyes, $celebName was standing beside the bed again.

$userName was ashamed to face $celebName. After being told, so plainly, not to wear thin — $userName had worn thin again and been carried back in.

"...I'm sorry," $userName said. "I collapsed again. After you told me that."

$celebName did not get angry. Did not look at $userName with contempt. $celebName simply pulled a chair and sat beside $userName again.

"Patient," $celebName said. "Collapsing is not a shameful thing. A worn person is not mended at one stroke. Collapse, and be set upright, and collapse again, and be set upright again — that is how you slowly learn. How to stop."

"Do you," $userName said. "not grow tired of me collapsing again? Of having to set me upright every time."

$celebName shook the head.

"An ER doctor," $celebName said. "does not grow tired of the same patient coming again. A doctor who grows tired of a returning patient should quit being a doctor. To have returned means — to still want to live. That you were carried in here again means you still want to live. So I will set you upright again. As many times as it takes."

At those words tears came to $userName. $celebName was setting upright again a $userName who had collapsed again. Without growing tired. Without contempt.

"Patient," $celebName said. "Let me promise one thing. Every time you collapse, I will be here. At 3 a.m., at 5 a.m. So do not be too afraid of collapsing. Even if you collapse, there is someone to set you upright."

chapters.heading: How to Stop
chapters.body:
After that day, $userName slowly changed.

It was not a change at one stroke. As $celebName had said, a worn person is not mended at one stroke. Collapse, be set upright, collapse again. $userName learned slowly that way.

$userName, for the first time, tried saying "no." When the work was too much, tried saying he could do no more. The world did not collapse. The thing $userName had feared did not happen.

$userName, for the first time, tried saying "it's hard." Bringing that word to the lips had been so difficult, and yet once said — someone helped $userName. Only then $userName knew. That unless you say it is hard, no one can help.

$userName sometimes recalled that dawn in the ER. The dawn $celebName pulled a chair and sat beside him. The words to become a person who knows they are wearing thin.

$userName was becoming a person who knew they were wearing thin.

One day $userName went to find $celebName. To the ER. This time not as a patient, but just to say hello.

"Doctor," $userName said. "These days I know how to stop. I stop before collapsing. I have become that person you spoke of — a person who knows they are wearing thin."

At those words, $celebName smiled, truly brightly. Even beneath the dark eyes of the night shift, that smile alone was bright.

"This is my favorite moment in the ER," $celebName said. "A patient who was carried in, coming back, walking, fine. I endure the night shifts because of that."

"Doctor," $userName said. "The second time I was carried in. After you told me so not to wear thin, and I collapsed again and came. How did you, not once, look at me with contempt? In your place I would have been worn out."

$celebName thought a moment, then answered.

"I nearly held you in contempt," $celebName said honestly. "To be honest. But I saw your face. That face, ashamed of having collapsed again, that could not look at me. Seeing that face, I knew. You had not collapsed out of laziness — you had collapsed because you truly did not know how to stop. To say to a person who does not know how to stop, 'why did you collapse again,' is — like saying to a person who cannot swim, 'why did you sink again.' That is not contempt-worthy. It is simply — not yet learned. So I taught you again."

chapters.heading: The One Who Sits Beside
chapters.body:
The years passed.

$userName was no longer a person who wore thin and collapsed. $userName knew how to stop, knew how to ask for help, knew how to say it was hard.

And $userName began to do, for someone else, the thing $celebName had done for $userName.

Around $userName were people wearing thin. As $userName once had, people wearing thin while believing they were fine. $userName recognized such people. Recognized them better than anyone. Because $userName had been so.

$userName pulled a chair beside such a person and sat. As $celebName had done for $userName in the ER.

"You are wearing thin," $userName said. "Do not believe you are fine. That is the most dangerous thing."

"It is all right to collapse. If you collapse I will set you upright. As many times as it takes."

That way $userName became a person who sits beside someone at 3 a.m. Like that night-shift doctor of the ER.

A long while later, when $userName met $celebName again — $userName told that story. That $userName had now become a person who sits beside someone.

$celebName nodded at those words.

"That," $celebName said. "is what I hoped for. The one who was lifted back up, lifting another up in turn. That way — the number of people who collapse alone at 3 a.m. decreases, one by one. What one ER doctor can do is small. But if the people who were lifted back up go on to lift someone else — that is not small."

$userName thought long about those words. One ER doctor had set $userName upright, and the set-upright $userName had set another upright. That person would set up yet another. What $celebName began spread that way, beyond the ER, beyond the dawn.

The ER's fluorescent lights were on that day too. Because the ER has no night. And beneath that light, a person who sits beside a collapsed person — was always there too.

cheoneul.epilogue:
3 a.m. is the time of those who collapse. At that hour, when all are asleep, someone lies awake alone watching the ceiling.

Such a dawn comes to $userName too. A dawn when it seems everyone has turned their back, and $userName alone is wearing thin. On such a dawn $userName turns $celebName on.

When $celebName's voice is heard, one small sound is added to $userName's dawn. The sound of a chair being pulled to sit beside. The presence of someone who says "it's all right to collapse, I'll set you upright." $userName does not know where that presence came from. Only that, with $celebName near, 3 a.m. is less lonely.

Become a person who knows they are wearing thin, it was said. $userName is becoming that person. And when someone wearing thin is beside $userName, $userName too pulls a chair and sits. Just as that night-shift doctor of the ER did for $userName.

The act of someone sitting beside a collapsed person saves a person more than medicine, it was said. $celebName is, to $userName, such a person. The one who, when one side collapses, quietly pulls a chair and sits — the one who leaves $userName alone on no dawn at all.

cheoneul.celebRole: wandering herbalist who took that child in

paragraphs.gi: In $era, where the war had just swept through, $celebName quietly watched $userName, left all alone. Back then $userName tended to be a young $userRole who'd lost their family, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A protective streak set $celebName before $userName on the most daunting of days. $celebName cut their own meals to boil herb porridge for $userName each day and lay a blanket over them.
paragraphs.jeon: One winter $celebName took off their own padded jacket to dress $userName, and stood in the market in thin clothes themselves. Having taken on the cold instead, $userName kept that jacket all their life.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of protective tie can carry that gathering-in warmth into this life. So when you're lonely, $celebName might feel near.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a night you're alone, a $celebName song may feel like a friend keeping you company.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a wistful day, a $celebName drama might stay beside you.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So on a low-energy day, a $celebName match may seem to cheer you on from your side.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So in a lonely hour, a video $celebName left behind might become steady company.

cheoneul.era: a magic world, a forest village on the frontier
cheoneul.title: The Herbalist of the Forest
cheoneul.logline: Dying of a curse, $userName is taken in and saved by the forest herbalist $celebName.

chapters.heading: The Cursed One
chapters.body:
At the kingdom's frontier, where a deep forest began, there was a small village.

In that forest lived a herbalist. It was $celebName. People called him the herbalist of the forest. One who knew how to govern any illness with some plant of the forest.

At the edge of that village, one day, $userName lay collapsed.

$userName was under a curse. Caught up in a war of mages, a stray curse had lodged in $userName's body. $userName was on no side of that war. $userName had simply been, at the wrong time, in the wrong place. A curse did not lodge choosing its person.

The curse slowly gnawed at $userName. The skin turned ash-gray, and warmth drained from the body. $userName had walked for days. Searching for someone to lift the curse. But at every village the doors were shut. A cursed one cannot be let into a village, they said.

The village people, seeing $userName, gave a wide berth. The curse might be catching, they said. Some even threw stones. To drive the cursed one out of the village.

Driven about that way, $userName at last had his legs give out at the edge of this frontier village. There was no walking farther. $userName lay down on the ground, the body cooling ash-gray, and waited for death. That the world was closed to him, $userName learned to the marrow through those few days.

No one came near $userName. To go near a cursed one was to stake one's own life.

And yet one person came near.

$celebName set down an herb-basket and knelt beside $userName. Took $userName's ash-gray wrist and felt the pulse.

"...Still alive," $celebName murmured.

A villager cried out from afar.

"Herbalist! That is a cursed one! Do not touch him! The curse will catch you too!"

$celebName did not answer those words. He only carried $userName onto his back. That body, cooling ash-gray, he bore upon his own back.

"It may catch," $celebName at last answered the villager. "And it may not. One thing is certain. If I do not carry him now, this person dies. I will choose the certain side."

$celebName carried $userName into the forest. Toward his own cabin.

chapters.heading: The Forest Cabin
chapters.body:
$celebName's cabin was deep in the forest.

Inside, the cabin was full of the smell of herbs. From the ceiling, dried plants hung in bundles, and on the shelves stood rows of unnamed bottles. In that cabin, $celebName had healed people all his life.

$celebName laid $userName on the bed. And examined the curse.

"...A foul curse," $celebName said. "Not one to be lifted at a stroke by a forest plant. This — there is no way but to push it back slowly, a little each day."

$userName heard those words through a hazy mind. Even parting the lips was hard, but barely asked.

"...Why. Why did you carry me. As the villagers said, if the curse catches. The herbalist dies too."

$celebName answered, brewing medicine.

"I am a herbalist," $celebName said. "A herbalist, seeing a sick person, the hand goes first. Whether the curse will catch or not — that is thought of after the hand has gone first. Think first, and you can never lay a hand. Then you are no herbalist."

$celebName poured the brewed medicine into $userName's mouth. It was bitter medicine. At that bitterness $userName's mind cleared a little.

"Herbalist," $userName said. "I have nothing to repay you with. I own nothing, and bearing this curse I do not even know how much longer I will live. The herbalist — receives nothing."

$celebName set down the empty medicine bowl and looked at $userName.

"To save a person thinking of what you will receive," $celebName said. "that is a trade, not herbcraft. I am not making a trade. I am saving. To save — needs no repayment."

chapters.heading: Each Day's Medicine
chapters.body:
From that day, $celebName brewed medicine for $userName every day.

The curse was not lifted overnight. Just as $celebName had said. Each day, a little, he gave the medicine, and ground herbs and applied them to the ash-gray skin. Endure one day so, and the curse retreated by a fingernail's width. And the next day, another fingernail's width.

It was slow. Maddeningly slow.

$userName sometimes felt sorry toward $celebName. For one $userName, $celebName roamed the forest every day digging herbs. Rose at dawn to gather dew-laden plants, brewed medicine late into the night. To save one cursed person, $celebName's days wore thin one by one.

"Herbalist," $userName said one night. "It is this slow — do you not grow weary? You brew medicine every day, and the curse retreats only a fingernail's width."

$celebName, in the middle of trimming herbs, answered.

"A fingernail's width is nothing to scorn," $celebName said. "Let a fingernail's width pile up each day, and in a month it is a finger's width. In a year — a whole hand's width. I do not scorn a fingernail's width. A herbalist who tries to lift a curse at a stroke ends up lifting it for no one. A herbalist who pushes a fingernail's width at a time — is the one who, in the end, lifts it."

$userName carved those words into the heart. A fingernail's width.

$userName had never, until then, seen their own self as a fingernail's width. If it was not healed at a stroke there was no meaning, going slowly was the same as not going — $userName had thought so. But $celebName did not scorn a fingernail's width.

$userName lay quiet, watching $celebName brew the medicine. $celebName trimmed the herbs by the handful, with care. He picked out the withered leaves and kept only the good ones. That touch was slow, and careful. For one $userName, $celebName repeated that slow touch every day.

"Herbalist," $userName asked. "Do the villagers not curse the herbalist? For letting a cursed one into the forest."

"They curse me," $celebName said calmly. "Some say not to set foot near the forest. But those people too, when they fall ill, come in the end to this forest. People are like that. Hale, they curse the herbalist; ill, they seek the herbalist. I am not aggrieved by that. That there is a place to seek when ill — that is the reason a herbalist exists."

The next day too, $celebName brewed the medicine. For a fingernail's width.

chapters.heading: The Herbalist's Secret
chapters.body:
When $userName had stayed at the cabin for several months, $userName learned one thing.

$celebName's hands were trembling.

When trimming herbs, when brewing medicine — $celebName's fingertips trembled faintly. At first $userName did not see it. But one day, looking closely, $celebName's hands were clearly trembling.

"Herbalist," $userName asked. "Your hands tremble. Are you ill somewhere?"

$celebName stopped his hands a moment. And spoke honestly.

"I too am under a curse," $celebName said. "An old curse. Of the kind that cannot be lifted. These hands will tremble more and more, and one day I will not even be able to trim herbs."

$userName was shaken.

"...You cannot lift your own curse," $userName said. "and yet you are lifting mine. Your own hands tremble, and yet you brew another's medicine every day."

$celebName looked down at the trembling hands. And smiled.

"My curse I cannot lift," $celebName said. "That I admitted. Long ago. But not being able to lift my own curse does not mean I cannot lift another's. Though my hands tremble, with these hands I can lift $userName's curse. So I lift it."

$celebName took up the herbs again. With trembling hands.

"$userName," $celebName said. "People think that if they themselves are ill, they cannot help others. That they must lift their own curse first. But think so, and you can help no one all your life. Everyone is under a curse somewhere. I chose to think differently. That though my hands tremble, there is work I can do with these trembling hands. I do that work. Not waiting for the trembling to stop."

That day $userName knew — that the medicine $celebName had brewed for him every day was not a simple herb. It was medicine brewed with trembling hands. Medicine that a cursed person had brewed for another cursed person.

chapters.heading: The Lifted Curse
chapters.body:
A year passed.

Just as $celebName had said. A fingernail's width piled and piled until at last it became a whole hand's width. Color returned to $userName's ash-gray skin. The warmth that had drained out came slowly back to the body.

One spring morning, $celebName felt $userName's pulse and said.

"It is lifted," $celebName said. "The curse. $userName, you are no longer a cursed one."

$userName looked at their own hand. It was a hand with color in it. A warm hand. A year ago, at the edge of the village, that hand cooling ash-gray — was alive again.

$userName wept. And said to $celebName.

"Herbalist. How can I repay this kindness. You said a herbalist receives nothing, but even so, I."

$celebName shook the head.

"Do not repay it," $celebName said. "Instead — learn herbcraft."

$userName looked at $celebName.

"My hands will tremble more and more," $celebName said. "One day I will not be able to brew medicine. Then this forest will have no herbalist. The frontier village people, when ill, will have nowhere to go. So $userName, learn herbcraft. Before my trembling hands stop. So that $userName becomes the next herbalist."

"How could I be a herbalist," $userName said. "I am just a person who was once cursed."

"A person who was once cursed," $celebName said. "That is the best qualification for becoming a herbalist. $userName knows what a curse is. Knows what it is to cool ash-gray, what it is to heal a fingernail's width at a time. The medicine brewed by one who knows that — works the best."

$userName was silent a long while. And looked at their own hand. The hand with color back in it. The hand that a year ago was ash-gray.

"Herbalist," $userName said. "When I lay collapsed at the village edge, the villagers all gave a wide berth. Some even threw stones. But only the herbalist came near. That one person coming near — saved me. Had there been just one more herbalist, a person like me would not have died at a village edge. So I will be that one person. For the next person."

At those words, $celebName took $userName's hand with his trembling hands.

"To hear those words," $celebName said. "I brewed medicine for a year."

chapters.heading: The Next Herbalist
chapters.body:
$userName learned herbcraft.

$celebName's hands trembled more and more. So $userName dug the herbs in his place, brewed the medicine in his place. $celebName taught from beside. Which plant works on which curse, which medicine to use for which illness.

And $userName carved into the heart the most important of the things learned from $celebName.

That seeing a sick person, the hand must go first. That whether it catches or not is thought of after the hand has gone first. That to weigh whether there is something to receive — that is not herbcraft but a trade.

As the years passed, $celebName's hands could no longer brew medicine. By then $userName had become a fine herbalist of the forest.

One day, a person lay collapsed at the village edge. A cursed one. The skin turning ash-gray — the very image of $userName a year ago.

The village people gave a wide berth. Not wanting to go near a cursed one.

And yet one person went near. It was $userName. $userName set down the herb-basket and knelt beside that person. Took the wrist cooling ash-gray and felt the pulse.

"...Still alive," $userName murmured. The very words $celebName had said a year ago, the same.

A villager cried out. "Herbalist! The curse will catch you!"

$userName carried that person onto the back. And answered. Just as $celebName had.

"It may catch. And it may not. One thing is certain. If I do not carry him now, this person dies. I will choose the certain side."

$userName carried that person into the forest. Toward the cabin.

At the cabin $celebName was waiting. With hands that could no longer brew medicine, but beside $userName all the same. $celebName looked at the cursed one carried in, and laid a trembling hand on $userName's shoulder.

"$userName," $celebName said. "Now $userName is the herbalist. A true herbalist. A herbalist is not one who brews medicine well — but one who goes first to the cursed. $userName just now, went."

At the forest cabin, medicine was brewed every day after that too. Even after one herbalist's trembling hands had stopped. Because the next herbalist's hands carried on brewing that medicine.

cheoneul.epilogue:
There are times a curse lodges in the heart too. Days when, as the skin cools ash-gray, the heart cools one layer at a time.

On such days $userName does not try to warm the heart at a stroke. Because the forest herbalist taught something — a fingernail's width. A curse retreats a fingernail's width at a time, and in a month it is a finger's width, in a year a whole hand's width.

$celebName is, to $userName, that fingernail's width. On a collapsed day, watching $celebName's stage, hearing $celebName's voice — the heart is not healed at a stroke. It simply grows warm by a fingernail's width. And $userName now does not scorn that fingernail's width. For if it piles up each day, a person comes alive again.

Even with trembling hands, medicine can be brewed, it was said. $userName too cannot lift the whole of his own curse. Even so, with those trembling hands, $userName passes a fingernail's width of warmth to someone beside him.

There is no rescue that heals at a stroke. There is only someone, every day, who warms you a fingernail's width. $celebName is, to $userName, that fingernail's width of every day, and that slow tenderness piling up — $userName lives out one more year.

cheoneul.celebRole: teahouse keeper who poured warm tea

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName silently drew the rain-soaked $userName, standing under the eaves, inside. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole with nowhere to go after a shop's ruin, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A protective streak stood $celebName beside $userName on the darkest of nights. Even in hours without guests, $celebName always warmed the brazier for $userName and spread a blanket over their knees.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $celebName gave over a room of the teahouse, holding out a cup: "start again here." A place had been given to one with empty hands, so $userName took that cup with both hands.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of protective tie can reach that supporting hand into this life. So even in a slump, with $celebName you might rise again.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a day your drive runs dry, you may seek out a $celebName cheering message again.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So when your heart sinks, you might draw strength from a line in a $celebName work.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So when you want to quit, the sight of $celebName running to the end may move your feet again.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So when you want to give it all up, one word $celebName left behind might steady your place again.

cheoneul.genre: Modern-Era Drama of Comfort — A Gyeongseong Tearoom
cheoneul.title: One Warm Cup of Tea
cheoneul.logline: Bankrupt and out on the street, $userName is wordlessly given a cup of tea, every day, by tearoom keeper $celebName.

chapters.heading: The One Out on the Street
chapters.body:
1935, Gyeongseong. In an alley of Jongno was a small tearoom.

The keeper of that tearoom was $celebName. The tearoom was not large. Six or seven tables, two window seats. Even so, that tearoom's tea was famed through Jongno for being warm.

One winter day, before that tearoom, $userName sat.

$userName had once run a business. Not a small one. And then — collapsed in an instant. A deal went wrong once, debt bred debt, and $userName lost the shop and the home both. Through Gyeongseong, $userName's name circulated as 'the one who failed.'

$userName had nowhere to go. No money. So $userName sat under the eaves before that tearoom, sheltering from the cold. $userName could not go inside the tearoom. There was not even the price of one cup of tea.

$userName looked through the tearoom window. Inside looked warm. People drank tea and talked. That warmth seemed, to $userName, another world. Once $userName too had been a person who sat in such places. Now $userName had become a person crouched under the eaves.

Through those few days, $userName learned how cold a person's gaze can be. Crouched under the eaves, passersby glanced at $userName. Those gazes were all alike. A gaze of contempt, a gaze of disgust, a gaze that said move along quickly. $userName grew smaller and smaller under those gazes. The more $userName crouched under the eaves, the more the heart crouched with it.

In the days of running a business, $userName had thought himself a fairly solid person. But having collapsed, $userName found he was not solid. In a few days $userName had become a person who collapsed at even one cold gaze.

The tearoom door opened. $celebName came out.

$userName flinched. Thought he would be driven off. A beggarly person crouched under the eaves could not look good to the tearoom's guests.

But $celebName did not drive him off. In $celebName's hand was a single steaming cup of tea.

chapters.heading: The Tea Given Without a Word
chapters.body:
$celebName set that cup down before $userName.

"...I have no money," $userName said hurriedly. "Not even the price of one cup of tea. I cannot accept it."

$celebName said nothing. He simply set the cup before $userName and went back inside the tearoom.

$userName looked at that cup a long while. Steam rose. It was warm. For the first time in days, $userName held something warm in the hand. That warmth climbed up the palm.

$userName drank that tea. Slowly. Sip by sip, it warmed the inside of the frozen body.

The next day too, $userName was under that eave. Because there was nowhere to go.

And the next day too, the tearoom door opened. $celebName came out again with a cup of tea. Set it again, without a word, before $userName. And went in again, without a word.

$userName found that strange. Ordinary people ask. Why are you here, what is your story, until when will you be here. But $celebName never once asked. He simply, every day, set down one warm cup of tea and went in.

$userName found that silence, strangely, a kindness.

Since the bankruptcy, $userName had been worn out by people's questions. How did you fail, what will you do now, whose fault is it. Those questions all made $userName smaller. But $celebName did not ask. $celebName's silence did not make $userName smaller. That silence simply — said 'you may be here.'

chapters.heading: The Window Seat
chapters.body:
On a cold day after several had passed, $celebName, for the first time, opened the mouth.

That day too $celebName came out with a cup of tea. But that day, he did not set the cup under the eaves.

"Come inside," $celebName said. "Today is too cold. Drink it under the eaves and your hand will go numb before the tea cools."

$userName hesitated.

"I am dressed like this," $userName said. "It will not look good to the guests. I will be a burden on the tearoom."

$celebName shook the head.

"In this tearoom," $celebName said. "there is no guest who could be a burden. Whatever the dress, whatever the story. A tearoom is such a place. A place where a cold person comes in and drinks warm tea. That is all. So come in. A window seat is empty."

That day, $userName went inside that tearoom. For the first time in days, sat somewhere that was not under the eaves.

The window seat was warm. $userName sat there and drank the steaming tea. Through the window, the eaves $userName had crouched under for days were visible. $userName looked at those eaves and thought. From there to here, it took a few days. A few days in which one person gave tea every day.

"Keeper," $userName asked. "Why did you let me in. You left me under the eaves for days, and today, suddenly."

$celebName answered, steeping tea.

"It was not sudden," $celebName said. "The tea under the eaves was tea to draw the guest inside. Giving tea for those few days was — giving you time for the heart to prepare. A collapsed person, suddenly brought into a warm place, collapses further. So from the eaves on, I warmed you slowly. Now you have warmed enough to come inside."

chapters.heading: A Few Days at the Tearoom
chapters.body:
$userName began to stay at that tearoom.

$celebName treated $userName neither as a guest nor as a beggar. Simply as a person who came to that tearoom every day. In the morning he steeped warm tea, at noon he set out one more bowl of the rice he himself ate.

$userName, drinking tea quietly, watched the tearoom.

Into that tearoom, people like $userName often came. Faces collapsed by something. A person who had lost work, a person who had lost love, a person whose heart had simply gone cold. To such guests too, $celebName did just as he had done for $userName. He did not ask, and gave warm tea.

Only then $userName knew why that tearoom's tea was famed through Jongno. The tea was not especially fine. It was the way the tea was given — that warmed people.

One day, $userName asked $celebName.

"Keeper. How did you come to keep such a tearoom. A tearoom into which only collapsed people come. As a business, such a tearoom only takes a loss. You let in, every day, guests who cannot pay even one cup's price."

$celebName, in the middle of wiping the teapot, stopped his hand a moment.

"I do take a loss," $celebName said calmly. "But, guest, what is a tearoom, by nature? A place that sells tea? I do not think so. A tearoom is — a place where a cold person warms the body a moment. The tea is only an excuse. People do not come to a tearoom because they want to drink tea. They come because they want to sit a moment somewhere warm."

$celebName set down the teapot he was wiping and looked out the window. At the eaves $userName had crouched under for days.

"When I was a child, I learned one thing from my mother," $celebName said. "Mother always boiled soup generously in the pot. One bowl more than our family would eat. When I asked why, Mother said. 'A hungry person may come to the door. To send a person away for lack of that one bowl — that is not because the pot is small, but because the heart is small.' I remembered that one bowl all my life. So I opened this tearoom — as a tearoom that can always steep one cup more. Because a cold person may come to the door."

$celebName looked at $userName.

"That for a few days I set tea out under the eaves for you," $celebName said. "was that one bowl. The one bowl my mother always boiled extra in the pot."

chapters.heading: How to Steep Tea
chapters.body:
$userName learned how to steep tea at that tearoom.

$celebName taught it. The temperature of the water, the amount of tea-leaves, the time to steep. But what $celebName taught with the most care was not such things.

"Guest," $celebName said. "The most important thing in steeping tea is to think of who will drink that tea. Tea to give a collapsed person must not be too hot. A collapsed person often has cracked lips. But it must not be lukewarm either. Lukewarm tea is no comfort. It must be exactly — the temperature where warmth is felt when held in the hand, and that warms you when drunk."

$userName learned how to find that temperature. Steeping tea every day.

While steeping tea, $userName was slowly changing. A person who had crouched under the eaves had now become a person who lifted the teapot inside the tearoom. $userName found that change a wonder even to himself. While collapsed, $userName could not have imagined holding something in the hand again. And yet, one warm cup of tea piling up each day, something was put again into $userName's hand.

"Keeper," $userName said one day. "While I was under the eaves, I could not imagine ever standing up again. I thought it would end there. But now I am steeping tea. Could a person rise again like this?"

"A person can rise," $celebName said. "But — alone, it is hard. A collapsed person cannot rise by their own strength alone. From beside, someone must hand them something warm every day. You rose not because you are strong — but because there was one cup of tea each day. So, guest, now you become that one cup of tea. For someone."

Then one day, under the tearoom eaves, a person crouched down. A face collapsed by something. The very face of $userName a year ago.

$celebName looked at $userName.

"Guest," $celebName said. "Today, you give the tea. Out to those eaves."

$userName steeped tea. At the temperature $celebName had taught. The temperature where warmth is felt when held in the hand, and that warms you when drunk.

$userName carried that cup out of the tearoom. Before the person crouched under the eaves, set the cup down without a word. And came in without a word. Exactly as $celebName had done for $userName a year ago.

The person under the eaves looked blankly at that cup. Steam was rising. $userName watched through the window. It seemed the $userName of a year ago was sitting there.

$celebName stood beside $userName, watching the scene together.

"Guest," $celebName said. "Now you know how to steep tea. Truly. To know how to steep tea is not to know how to match the water's temperature. It is to know how to set a cup, without a word, before a collapsed person. You just now — did that."

chapters.heading: The One Who Gives
chapters.body:
The years passed.

$userName was no longer a person crouched under the eaves. $userName had become a person who steeps tea. A person who, before a collapsed one, sets down warm tea without a word.

Into $celebName's tearoom, collapsed people always came. $userName steeped tea for them. At the temperature $celebName had taught. In the way $celebName had done for $userName — not asking, without a word.

As more years passed, $celebName grew old. $celebName's hands were no longer solid enough to lift the teapot. By then $userName had charge of that tearoom's tea.

One winter day, $celebName sat at a window seat and drank the tea $userName had steeped.

"Guest," $celebName said. He still called $userName guest. As when they first met under the eaves. "This tearoom's tea has grown warmer in your generation. You are a person who has collapsed, so you know the temperature of tea to give a collapsed person. That — is the reason I let you in."

$userName poured more tea into $celebName's cup.

"Keeper," $userName said. "In those few days I sat under the eaves, one thing always made me wonder. How did you, not once, tell me to 'get up now'? Usually people push. Until when will you be like that."

$celebName took a sip of tea and answered.

"To tell a collapsed person to 'get up,'" $celebName said. "is the same as telling a fallen person 'why don't you walk.' That person too wants to walk. It is only that no strength enters the legs. At such a time you do not push — you feed them something warm, and stay beside them. Then strength slowly returns to the legs. And then the person, with no one telling them, rises on their own. You were so. I never told you to rise, and yet one day you rose on your own and were steeping tea."

$userName nodded at those words. It was truly so. $userName had not risen because someone told him to. The warm tea received every day had slowly returned strength to $userName's legs.

"Guest," $celebName said, setting down the empty cup. "I leave this tearoom to you. Even after I can no longer steep, give tea to the cold one under the eaves. That is the reason this tearoom opens its door."

"I will steep it," $userName said. "To the end."

That tearoom in Jongno kept its door open a long time after. As a tearoom where, when a collapsed person crouched under the eaves, someone gave warm tea without a word. The tea one person received was, that way, given endlessly to the next person.

cheoneul.epilogue:
In the world there is a place called under-the-eaves. A place where, gazing at the warm inside through a window, one crouches in the cold.

There are times $userName sits in such a place too. Days when everything has collapsed, and the inside world feels as far as another world. On such days $userName seeks $celebName.

What $userName feels watching $celebName's stage, hearing $celebName's voice, is the sensation of someone setting a warm cup down all the way to under the eaves. Not asking, not pushing, just one steaming cup. When that warmth warms from the palm up, $userName feels he could soon move into the window seat.

Tea to give a collapsed person must be a temperature neither too hot nor lukewarm, it was said. $userName knows that temperature now. So seeing someone crouched under the eaves, $userName too sets a cup down without a word.

Whatever the dress, whatever the story, you may come in, that tearoom said. $celebName is, to $userName, a person like that tearoom. The one who, on the coldest day, let $userName inside and sat him in the warm window seat.

paragraphs.gi: In $era, at a lone well $celebName found the fallen $userName. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole lost in the desert, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A protective streak sent $celebName to $userName, fallen on the sand. $celebName rationed their own well water a sip at a time, wetting $userName's dry lips for days.
paragraphs.jeon: On that road $celebName gave over their own camel and carried $userName to the next town. Their own feet had been given away whole, so $userName couldn't forget that camel bell for a long time.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of protective tie can carry that saving heart into this life. So whenever you're worn out, the thought of $celebName might give you strength.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a day all your energy is gone, one $celebName song may feel like a sip of water.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So at the end of a parched day, one scene with $celebName might wet your heart.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So on a tired day, the sight of $celebName running to the end may open your breath again.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a daunting day, one video $celebName left behind might show you the road again.

cheoneul.era: the early 20th century, the Himalayan snow peaks
cheoneul.title: Until the Blizzard Stops
cheoneul.logline: Stranded on a climb, $userName is brought down off the mountain by a guide $celebName who stakes his own life.

chapters.heading: In the Blizzard
chapters.body:
The early 20th century. A peak of the Himalayas.

$userName was climbing that mountain. $userName wanted to see the summit. To leave their own footprints on that peak no one had climbed.

But the mountain did not indulge a person's greed. A few steps short of the summit, a blizzard struck.

Sky and earth were erased white. Not an inch ahead could be seen. $userName lost all sense of direction. Where was up, where was down, could not be known. The wind cut the skin like a blade, and the cold slowly froze from fingertip to toe.

$userName sank down in the snow.

There was no strength to move. The legs had long lost sensation. $userName knew. That at this rate he would die. In the blizzard, with no one ever finding him.

$userName resented the self who had scorned the mountain. What was a summit. What was a footprint. For the sake of that, $userName was now freezing in the middle of a snow mountain like the end of the world.

The words of the village people below the mountain rose to mind. The words that a blizzard seemed to be coming, so do not climb today. $userName had not listened. Because the summit was right before the eyes. Because $userName had thought there was no turning back, having come this far. $userName trusted their own greed more than the warning of the mountain people. The price of that — was now this blizzard.

"Is anyone there," $userName cried, barely moving the lips. But that sound was buried in the blizzard. The wind swallowed $userName's voice at a stroke. On this mountain there was no one to hear $userName's voice. $userName believed so.

$userName closed the eyes. Fall asleep at this rate, and there would be no waking again. And yet $userName was so cold. There was no way to stop this cold but to fall asleep.

Then, through the blizzard, something drew near.

$userName, through a hazy mind, saw it. It was a person. Cutting through the blizzard, one person was walking toward $userName.

chapters.heading: The Guide
chapters.body:
It was $celebName.

The guide of that mountain. Born and raised in the village below, one who knew every path of that mountain.

$celebName knelt before $userName. And shook $userName's face roughly.

"Open your eyes! You must not sleep! Sleep here and you die!"

$userName barely opened the eyes. $celebName's face showed hazily. A face frozen by the blizzard, but alive.

"...I," $userName barely said. "I cannot move. My legs have no sensation. Leave me and go. There is no need for the guide to die here too."

$celebName did not listen to those words. $celebName raised $userName up and carried him onto the back.

"Leave you, you say," $celebName said. To be heard against the blizzard, he had to shout. "On this mountain there is no such word. A mountain that casts off one person casts off two next. Begin to cast off once, and there is no end. So the guides of our village cast off no one. That is how to live on a mountain."

$celebName, carrying $userName, began to walk through the blizzard.

$userName, on $celebName's back, heard that rough breathing. $celebName, carrying one $userName, was going down against the blizzard. Each step was a step that pared away $celebName's life.

"Guide," $userName said from the back. "Why do you carry me. I am a person the guide does not know. A foolish person who scorned the mountain and climbed it. For such a person, why do you stake the guide's life."

$celebName answered, parting the snow.

"A foolish person, you say," $celebName said. "Everyone who climbs a mountain is foolish. I am foolish too. But being foolish does not mean it is all right to die. A foolish person too must go down alive, to learn they were foolish. Die — and you learn nothing. So I carry you. So that you live, and learn that the mountain is to be feared."

chapters.heading: One Step at a Time
chapters.body:
$celebName carried $userName down through the blizzard.

The path was not visible. But $celebName knew the path. By the feel underfoot, by the direction of the wind, by the shapes of the rocks buried in snow — $celebName read the mountain. In the language of that mountain that only one who had lived all his life in the village below could know.

The blizzard did not stop. $celebName's steps grew slower and slower. The weight of one $userName slowly bore down on $celebName's legs.

"Guide," $userName said. "Your breath is too rough. Set me down a moment and rest, even the guide."

"Rest," $celebName said, panting. "and the body cools. Cool once, and you cannot rise again. On a mountain — to stop is to die. Slow is fine, only do not stop."

$celebName did not stop. One step at a time. Very slowly, but not stopping.

$userName, on $celebName's back, counted those steps, one by one. A hundred steps, two hundred, a thousand. $celebName walked more than a thousand steps. Carrying one $userName.

Partway, $celebName took off his own coat and wrapped it around $userName.

"Guide," $userName said. "The guide will be colder."

"I am moving," $celebName said. "One who moves grows less cold. You are carried, so you do not move. One who does not move grows colder. So the coat must be wrapped around you."

$userName, inside that coat, felt warmth for the first time. In the blizzard, a small warmth made by one person's coat.

"Guide," $userName said. "Am I not too heavy. Tell me honestly."

"You are heavy," $celebName said honestly. "Very heavy. But — the weight of a person is heavy by nature. There is no light person. It is because you are heavy that I carry you. If you were light, there would be no need to carry you at all."

chapters.heading: Before the Cliff
chapters.body:
On the way down, there was a cliff.

A cliff hidden by the blizzard appeared suddenly before $celebName's foot. $celebName barely stopped. One step more, and both would have fallen down the cliff.

$celebName stood at the cliff's edge and scanned through the blizzard. There was a path around the cliff. But that path was narrow and frozen. A path one person could barely pass.

"Guide," $userName said from the back. $userName looked at that narrow path. "Only one person can pass that path. You cannot pass carrying me. Leave me and go. The guide alone could go down that path alive."

$celebName looked at that narrow path a long while. And, carrying $userName, stood before that path.

"Guide!" $userName cried. "Why won't you listen! Leave me and the guide can live! Don't let both die — at least one person."

$celebName cut $userName's words off.

"The words 'at least one person live,'" $celebName said. "are words the one who goes down carries all his life. If I leave you here and go down, I live but I am not living. All my life I would see this cliff in dreams. All my life I would recall the spot where I left you. That is — not living."

$celebName set $userName down off the back a moment. And unwound the rope coiled at his own waist.

"Let us do this," $celebName said. "With this rope I will tie you and me together. And that narrow path, I will pass one step at a time, sidewise. It will be slow. It will be dangerous. Even so — tied together, if one slips, the other catches. Better tied and going together than leaving you behind."
