$celebName tied $userName and himself with the rope. Firmly. The hands that knotted it were frozen, but that knot had not an inch of slack.

"This knot," $celebName said. "is the knot the guides of our village have tied for generations. Tie it once, and as long as one person lives it does not come undone. For a guide to tie a traveler with this knot is — a promise. The promise to go together to the end."

$userName looked at that knot tied at their own waist. That knot was, to $userName, the most reliable thing in the world. In the blizzard, $userName was no longer alone.

"Now," $celebName said. "we are one body. Live together, or die together. I will bet on living together."

chapters.heading: Tied Together
chapters.body:
The two, tied by the rope, passed that narrow path.

When $celebName stepped one step sidewise, $userName followed behind. $userName's legs still had no sensation, but the rope held $userName up. $celebName's body pulled $userName's body.

Once, $userName slipped. Missing the ice of the narrow path, $userName's body lurched toward the cliff.

In that instant, the rope went taut.

$celebName braced that rope with his whole body. $celebName's feet dug into the ice, $celebName's hands gripped the rock. $userName's weight pulled $celebName toward the cliff along the rope — but $celebName held. Gritting his teeth.

"I've got you!" $celebName cried. "I won't let go! Slowly, the foot back onto the path!"

$userName, holding to that rope, climbed back onto the narrow path. One rope $celebName had held drew $userName up from the cliff.

The two at last passed that cliff.

At the end of the narrow path, $celebName sank down a moment. His breath seemed about to break. $userName sank down beside him. The two were still tied by the rope.

"Guide," $userName said. "Just now — had the guide not caught me, I would have fallen. The guide could have been pulled along too, so how did you not let go of that rope."

$celebName steadied the rough breath and answered.

"To let go of a rope," $celebName said. "is one instant. Spread the fingers once and it is done. But after that one instant, I would regret that one finger all my life. I cannot live bearing that regret. To be pulled along together is — less frightening to me. Less than living alone after dropping you."

$userName was silent a long while before those words.

One person had staked their own life on that rope, for the sake of $userName. For a stranger. For a foolish person who had scorned the mountain. $userName resolved, that day, never to forget the weight of that rope all his life.

chapters.heading: Down From the Mountain
chapters.body:
The blizzard stopped.

When the sky opened, the two had come down to where the village below the mountain was visible. $celebName, carrying $userName, having walked more than a thousand steps — had at last passed all the way through the blizzard.

They reached the village. $userName lived. Sensation returned to the frozen legs, and the warmth that had drained out came slowly back to the body.

$userName asked $celebName.

"Guide. How can I repay this kindness. The guide carried me a thousand steps, and at the cliff staked his own life to catch me."

$celebName shook the head.

"Do not repay it," $celebName said. "Instead — learn the mountain. And become a guide."

$userName looked at $celebName.

"On a mountain," $celebName said. "a foolish person always climbs up. Wanting to see the summit, wanting to leave a footprint. And one of them gets trapped in a blizzard. Like you. Then, if a guide is beside that person, that person goes down alive. With no guide — they die. So you become a guide. A person who casts off no one on the mountain."

$userName, that day, stayed in the village below the mountain.

$userName became a guide. Learned the language of the mountain. How to read the path by the feel underfoot, how to read the blizzard by the direction of the wind. And the most important of the things learned from $celebName — $userName carved into the heart.

That on a mountain you cast off no one. That to stop is to die. That carrying a heavy person is what makes a guide.

One day, after the years had passed, $userName found a person in a blizzard. Sunk down in the snow, about to fall asleep. A person who had scorned the mountain and climbed it — the very image of $userName a year ago.

$userName knelt before that person. And shook that face roughly.

"Open your eyes! You must not sleep! Sleep here and you die!"

$userName carried that person onto the back. Just as $celebName had done for $userName. And began to go down against the blizzard, one step at a time. Slowly, but not stopping.

Until the blizzard stopped, $userName walked. Carrying one person.

cheoneul.epilogue:
The guides of the Himalayas tie the traveler and themselves with a rope, it was said. With a knot that, tied once, does not come undone as long as one person lives.

Between $userName and $celebName, there is such a rope. It cannot be seen with the eyes, but $userName knows it is there.

On a day that feels like being trapped in a blizzard — a day when not an inch ahead is visible, and where is up and where is down cannot be known — $userName looks at $celebName. Then that invisible rope goes taut. That there is, at the other end of that line, a person who says live together or die together, and so never lets go — makes $userName take one more step.

To stop is to die, it was said. Slow is fine, only do not stop, and you pass through the blizzard. $userName lives those words. And seeing someone sunk down in the snow, $userName too carries that person onto the back. Becoming a guide who casts off no one.

So the heart with which $userName supports $celebName is the act of tying that invisible rope again. Live together or die together — the act of, when one side slips, tying once more, firm, the knot the other braces with the whole body.

cheoneul.celebRole: old neighbor who never skipped a visit

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName never once skipped a visit to the imprisoned $userName. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole jailed on a false charge, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A protective streak carried $celebName's steps into the darkest cell. Each visit, $celebName brought a warm rice ball and a line of encouragement without fail.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $celebName spent all their saved money and finally found a witness to clear $userName's false charge. Having emptied their own household to free another, $userName wept a long while stepping out the prison gate.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of protective tie can carry that saving hand into this life. So when you feel about to break, $celebName might become your greatest cheer.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a day you want to sink down, you may watch a $celebName cheer video and rise again.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So through every hard stretch, you might lean on a line from a $celebName work.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So when you want to give it all up, your heart may race again at a $celebName comeback scene.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a pitch-dark day, one line $celebName left behind might light your heart like a lamp.

cheoneul.genre: SF Human Drama — Adrift in Space
cheoneul.era: the near future, an orbital station
cheoneul.title: When the Oxygen Was Half Gone
cheoneul.logline: Adrift after an accident, $userName is rescued by pilot $celebName who breaks regulations to come.

chapters.heading: Adrift
chapters.body:
The near future. On a station orbiting Earth, an accident occurred.

$userName was a mechanic of that station. In the middle of outer-hull maintenance, part of the station exploded. By that shock, $userName's safety line snapped. In nothing but a spacesuit, $userName was torn away from the station.

$userName began to drift through space.

All around was black. There was no up, no down. $userName, rotating slowly, was pushed off into black space. The station drew farther and farther away. To a distance $userName's reaching hand could not touch.

On the spacesuit's display, a number appeared. Oxygen remaining.

The oxygen was decreasing. $userName watched that number. When that number reached zero, $userName would die. In black space, with no one knowing.

$userName cried into the comm device.

"This is a mechanic. Adrift. Safety line snapped. Requesting rescue. Anyone who hears, respond."

There was no response. $userName cried again. And again. And again. Into black space, only $userName's voice scattered.

$userName knew. That for one adrift mechanic, no one would launch a rescue craft. The station would be frantic with accident control, and to search space for one adrift person was — not in the regulations.

While working at the station, $userName had heard that regulation many times. That when an accident occurs, a rescue craft is not used carelessly. That protecting the whole station comes first, and one adrift person — comes after. $userName had never once objected to that regulation. Had thought it a reasonable regulation. But now, having become that 'one adrift person' himself, $userName knew how cold that regulation was.

$userName, in the rotating field of view, watched the receding station. Inside it were people who knew $userName. Colleagues he had worked with. But they too could not come out to save $userName. Because the regulation would bind their feet.

The oxygen kept decreasing. $userName, in the middle of black space, watched that number and waited for death. Black space was vast, and $userName, one point, was too small.

chapters.heading: A Response
chapters.body:
When the oxygen had decreased by about half, a sound came from the comm device.

"...Mechanic adrift. Do you read. This is a rescue craft."

At that sound $userName's mind snapped alert. Someone had responded.

"I read! This is the mechanic! Adrift! Oxygen at half!"

"Coordinates locked," the voice beyond the comm said. "I'm coming to you now. Hold on."

The owner of that voice was $celebName. The pilot of a rescue craft.

$userName clung to that voice. In black space, that one voice kept $userName alive.

A long while later, $celebName's voice came into $userName's comm device again. But this voice was a little heavier.

"Mechanic," $celebName said. "Know one thing. I am coming now in violation of regulations. To launch a rescue craft for one adrift person is not in the regulations. During accident control, a rescue craft cannot be used carelessly. The station did not grant me launch authorization."

At those words, $userName's chest sank.

"...Then," $userName said. "Turn back. Break regulations and the pilot is punished. For me alone, the pilot cannot lose even his qualification."

Beyond the comm, $celebName was silent a moment. Then answered.

"Do you know why I broke the regulation," $celebName said. "The regulation says 'do not launch a rescue craft for one adrift person.' But nowhere in that regulation does it say 'one adrift person may die.' The regulation is about sparing the rescue craft, not about casting off a person. I have not broken the regulation — I am going to do what the regulation left out."

chapters.heading: In Black Space
chapters.body:
While $celebName's rescue craft came toward $userName, $userName drifted through black space.

$celebName did not cut the comm. The whole way the rescue craft drew near, $celebName kept speaking to $userName.

"Mechanic," $celebName said. "Keep talking. As long as I hear your voice, I know you are awake. You must not lose consciousness while adrift. The breathing slows, and the oxygen burns faster. So keep talking. Anything."

$userName talked. Anything. Stories of working as a station mechanic, stories of Earth, the story that black space was frightening.

$celebName talked too. Stories of working as a rescue-craft pilot, stories of the people he had saved.

"Pilot," $userName asked. "I did not know being adrift in black space was this lonely. There is nothing all around. No up, no down, nothing to hold. In this loneliness, how should I keep my mind?"

"Hold to a voice," $celebName said. "Even with nothing in black space, my voice is there. To be adrift is to have nothing to grip. But a voice you can grip. You cannot grip it with the hand, but with the heart you can. So hold to my voice. Because along that voice — I am coming to you."

$userName did as told. Held to $celebName's voice. In black space, that one voice was the only thing $userName could grip.

"Pilot," $userName asked again. To hold the mind, $userName kept speaking. "The pilot is coming even prepared to be punished by the station. I do not even know who the pilot is. We have never met. To stake your own qualification for such a person — is it not frightening?"

Beyond the comm, $celebName steadied his breath a moment.

"It is frightening," $celebName said honestly. "Honestly, frightening. Lose the rescue-craft pilot qualification, and I can never do the work I have done all my life. That is a frightening thing. But, mechanic, there is something more frightening. To hear an adrift person's voice on the comm and not go to those coordinates. To hide behind regulations while hearing that voice grow weaker. That I find more frightening. More than losing a qualification — hearing a person and not going."

At those words, $userName gripped the comm device tighter.

The oxygen remaining kept decreasing. But $userName was no longer alone. Beyond the comm device, there was one person's voice. That voice made $userName's black space a little less black.

chapters.heading: Sharing the Oxygen
chapters.body:
At last, $celebName's rescue craft reached $userName.

$celebName opened the rescue craft's hatch and pulled $userName inside. $userName, who had drifted through black space, at last came into a place where there was something to grip.

But inside the rescue craft, a new problem waited.

"Mechanic," $celebName said, watching the console. "I will tell you honestly. This rescue craft is loaded with oxygen for one person to ride. Launched in haste during accident control, there was no time to load more oxygen. For two people to make it back to the station — the oxygen is short."

$userName's face stiffened.

"...Then," $userName said. "Then the pilot should return alone. I will stay here. The pilot came to rescue me even breaking regulations. I cannot let that pilot die for lack of oxygen."

$celebName shook the head.

"We can share the oxygen," $celebName said. "There is a way for two people to return on one person's worth of oxygen. Slow the breathing, keep movement to a minimum, use the oxygen sparingly. It is tight. By the time we reach the station, the oxygen will be near zero. But — we can reach it before it hits zero. Tightly."

"Tight means," $userName said. "that if the calculation is off even a little, both die."

"It does," $celebName said honestly. "But, mechanic, my calculations rarely go off. I have worked long as a rescue-craft pilot. And — rather than leaving a person behind and regretting it all my life, I would rather bet on a tight calculation. Let us live together. By sharing the oxygen."

$userName, inside the rescue craft, saw $celebName's face properly for the first time. A person known only by the comm voice. That face looked tired. The face of one who, amid a station overturned by accident control, had launched a rescue craft with no authorization and crossed black space.

"Pilot," $userName said. "You may still set me down and go. Now that you know the oxygen is tight. The pilot alone would surely live."

"Mechanic," $celebName said firmly. "Do you think I crossed black space to set you back down into black space? I came to bring you. There is no pilot who, having come to bring someone, casts them off again. So stop saying that. We return together."

$celebName turned the rescue craft's heading toward the station.

"Slow your breathing," $celebName said. "Match my breathing. Slowly, together. We are now returning, two people, on one person's worth of oxygen. For that — we must even breathe together."

The two headed for the station that way, breathing the same oxygen, sharing it.

chapters.heading: One Person's Worth of Oxygen
chapters.body:
The way back to the station was long.

$userName and $celebName slowed their breathing. Kept movement to a minimum. Barely spoke. Speaking burned oxygen. The two, in silence, breathed the same oxygen slowly, sharing it.

The number of oxygen remaining slowly fell toward zero.

$userName, watching that number, listened to the sound of $celebName's breathing. A slow, even breath. $userName matched their own breathing to it. The two breaths became one.

Partway, $userName said, small. Knowing it burned oxygen, but one word had to be said.

"Pilot," $userName said. "I have been unable to say thank you, afraid of burning oxygen. Still, I will say it. Thank you. Had the pilot not responded, I would have died alone in black space."

$celebName answered between the slow breaths.

"The thank-you," $celebName said. "say it when we reach the station. Said now, it sounds like a last farewell. We are not at the last. Both of us will reach it alive. So say that word — in the station's oxygen room, breathing all you want."

At those words, $userName thought he must live. If only to say the thank-you properly.

When the oxygen remaining fell to a single digit, the station came into view.

$celebName brought the rescue craft against the station's docking port. The hatch opened. The station's oxygen flowed into the rescue craft.

$userName and $celebName breathed in that oxygen all they wanted. The tight calculation — of two people returning on one person's worth of oxygen — had, in the end, come out right.

$userName looked at $celebName. And said. This time with no worry over oxygen.

"Thank you."

chapters.heading: The Next Adrift One
chapters.body:
$celebName was investigated over breaking regulations.

$userName went to that investigation and testified. That what $celebName had broken was not a regulation, but had filled what the regulation left out. That a regulation which does not rescue a person adrift in black space — must be changed.

The station accepted that testimony. The regulation was changed. After that, a new article was written into the station: 'even one adrift person shall, without fail, be rescued.' The very regulation $celebName had broken was, thanks to $celebName, rewritten.

$userName, having gone through that, quit being a mechanic. And learned to pilot a rescue craft.

"Why do you want to become a pilot," $celebName asked.

"Because I am a person who has drifted through black space," $userName answered. "I know that loneliness. That black place with nothing to grip. So I want to become a person who sends a voice to the next adrift one. A person who says 'I'm coming to you now, hold on.'"

$celebName nodded at those words.

One day, after the years had passed, a signal came into $userName's rescue-craft comm device.

"...Adrift. Safety line snapped. Requesting rescue. Anyone who hears, respond."

Somewhere in black space, one person was drifting. Watching that number of decreasing oxygen, waiting for death. The very image of $userName a year ago.

$userName turned on the comm device. And said. Just as $celebName had said to $userName.

"Person adrift. Do you read. This is a rescue craft. Coordinates locked. I'm coming to you now. Hold on."

$userName turned the rescue craft toward those coordinates. And did not cut the comm. Kept speaking to the adrift one. To become a voice that person could grip.

In black space, $userName headed toward the next adrift one. Like that voice that had once come toward $userName.

cheoneul.epilogue:
To be adrift in black space means there is nothing at all to grip. There is no up, no down, and reach out a hand and nothing is touched.

Such a drifting day comes to $userName too. A day when it seems no one will find $userName, and whatever signal is sent there will be no response. On such a day $userName recalls one voice from beyond a comm device.

"I'm coming to you now. Hold on." What $userName hears, watching $celebName's stage, hearing $celebName's voice, is that response. The voice of a person who crosses black space, who comes to rescue an adrift one even breaking regulations. $userName cannot grip that voice with the hand, but with the heart grips it.

To hear an adrift person's voice and not go is more frightening, it was said. So $userName too, hearing someone's distress signal, turns on the comm device. As that voice once came toward $userName, responding "I'm coming to you now."

However vast black space, a person who has once received a response is never again wholly alone. As long as $userName sends a signal, that response — crossing the distance, in the end reaches. As a voice by the name of $celebName.

cheoneul.celebRole: stage tuner who kept watch beside them

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName kept watch beside the $userName who'd set down the bow to illness, mending instruments in the dressing room. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who'd lost the stage, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A protective streak kept $celebName beside $userName on their most fragile days. Even on nights without a show, $celebName tuned the strings beside $userName's sickbed and tucked the blanket in.
paragraphs.jeon: One dawn $celebName pressed a newly carved bow, fit just to $userName's hand, into it and said, "it will sound again." Having made a place to rise once more, $userName used that bow all their life.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of protective tie can reach that supporting warmth into this life. So through every hard stretch, you might come to lean on $celebName's kindness.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a day your heart has weakened, you may rest leaning on a tender $celebName song.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a tired night, you might entrust your heart to the comfort within a $celebName work.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So when your knees feel about to buckle, the sight of $celebName rising again may give you strength.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a shaky day, you may listen again to one tender word $celebName left behind.

cheoneul.era: now, a city struck by a great earthquake
cheoneul.title: A Voice in the Rubble
cheoneul.logline: Trapped in a collapsed building, $userName is reached by the voice of rescuer $celebName and made to hold on to the end.

chapters.heading: The Collapsed Building
chapters.body:
It was an ordinary afternoon when the earthquake shook the city.

$userName was, at that hour, inside a building. The ground shook, the ceiling came down — and when $userName came to, $userName was trapped in the rubble.

All around was concrete. Collapsed walls and ceiling had penned $userName into a narrow gap. $userName could not move. A slab of concrete lay over the legs, and above the head there was only a hand's width of space.

It was dark. Not a single thread of light entered. In that dark, $userName heard only their own breathing.

$userName cried out.

"There is a person here! Is anyone there! There is a person here!"

There was no answer. $userName cried again. Until the throat went hoarse. But the rubble was thick, and $userName's voice seemed unable to pierce that concrete.

$userName grew more and more afraid.

No one would know $userName was here. Collapsed buildings were not one or two in the city. There was no guarantee a rescue team would dig through this pile of rubble. $userName might, in this dark, be forgotten with no one knowing.

$userName, in the dark, thought all sorts of thoughts. Was the greeting given leaving home this morning the last greeting. Did the people waiting for $userName even know $userName had been inside this building. The time within the rubble called such thoughts up endlessly. The dark was skilled at gnawing at a person's heart.

$userName felt around with the hand, touching the surroundings. Only cold concrete. Nothing to grip, nothing to lean on. The only thing $userName could touch was their own trembling hand.

$userName closed the eyes in the dark. It was frightening. It was lonely. The world had shaken and collapsed, and $userName was trapped alone in the very deepest part of that collapse.

chapters.heading: A Voice Beyond the Rubble
chapters.body:
How much time had passed. $userName had lost track. In the dark it seemed time did not flow.

Then, from beyond the rubble, a sound came.

"...Is anyone inside? If you hear me, answer!"

$userName's mind snapped alert. It was a person's voice.

"Here! There is a person here! I hear you!"

A silence fell a moment, then that voice came again. This time clearer.

"I hear you. I hear you. I am a rescuer. I am beyond the rubble now."

The owner of that voice was $celebName. A rescuer combing the collapsed buildings.

"Listen carefully," $celebName's voice said. "The way to you now is blocked by rubble. Clearing this rubble takes time. Touch it wrong and it could collapse further, so it must be cleared slowly, carefully. So — it takes time. Not short."

$userName's chest sank.

"...How long," $userName asked. "How long will it take."

"I will tell you honestly," $celebName said. "It could take several hours. Perhaps longer. But one thing I will promise. I will be here until I have cleared all this rubble — I will not move a single step away. So you too, hold on to the end. Until I get to you."

$userName, in the dark, clung to that voice.

"I am afraid," $userName said. "It is too dark here, too narrow. I am alone."

"You are not alone," $celebName said. "I am here. One layer of rubble away. I cannot hold your hand, but the voice reaches. So — as long as you can hear my voice, you are not alone."

chapters.heading: Holding to the Voice
chapters.body:
$celebName kept the promise.

The whole time he cleared the rubble, $celebName kept speaking to $userName. From beyond the rubble, ceaselessly.

"You said there is something over your legs. Does it hurt a lot? If it hurts, say it hurts. Don't bear it in silence."

"If you grow sleepy, say so. You must not fall asleep. I will keep speaking, so you keep answering me."

"What is your name? ...$userName. $userName, that is a good name. I will keep calling that name. You too, do not forget that name. In the rubble, you must not forget your own name."

$userName held to that voice and held on.

The dark was still dark. The legs still hurt. The narrow gap was still narrow. And yet — as long as $celebName's voice was heard, $userName could endure that dark. One voice did not leave $userName alone in the dark.

"Rescuer," $userName asked. "Clearing the rubble, is it not dangerous? You said touch it wrong and it could collapse further. Then the rescuer could be hurt too."

$celebName answered, clearing the rubble.

"It is dangerous," $celebName said. "But $userName. Rescue is, by nature, like that. To only watch a collapsed building from a safe place is not rescue. You have to step one foot into the dangerous place to reach the trapped person. I became a rescuer to step that one foot. So $userName, do not worry about me — just hold on. Until I reach you."

At those words, $userName resolved once more to hold on. If only for that voice beyond the rubble.

As time passed, $celebName, between clearing the rubble, told $userName all sorts of stories. Stories from before he became a rescuer, stories of the people he had pulled from rubble, stories of seeing a collapsed city rise again.

"$userName," $celebName said at some point. "I keep telling these stories partly to hold your mind together, but — also because I want to show you something. That outside the rubble there is still a world. That a world for $userName to return to is still there. Stay long in the dark, and you forget that. That there is an outside. So while you listen to my stories, do not forget the outside."

At those words, $userName pictured the outside in the dark. The world $celebName described. And then the dark grew a little more bearable.

chapters.heading: The Longest Time
chapters.body:
The hardest thing to endure was that, as time passed, $celebName's voice did not seem to grow nearer.

Several hours passed. $celebName kept clearing the rubble. But to $userName, it felt $celebName was not getting any nearer. $userName grew more and more worn out.

"Rescuer," $userName said. The voice had lost its strength. "Tell me honestly. Can you really reach me? Is the rubble not too thick? Am I just — going to end here?"

Beyond the rubble, $celebName's hand stopped a moment.

"$userName," $celebName said. "Let me tell you how far I have come. At first, between you and me there was very thick rubble. But now — if I tap the concrete my hand reaches, you will hear that sound. Listen."

$userName listened. In the dark.

From beyond the rubble, a tap, tap, tap of knocking came. Clearly — nearer than before.

"Do you hear it?" $celebName asked.

"...I hear it," $userName said. "It is near."

"Yes," $celebName said. "It has grown near. To you it felt I was not getting nearer, but in truth I was getting nearer, layer by layer. Pulling someone out of a collapse is, by nature, like that. You grow nearer with no sign of it, and at some moment a hand reaches. So $userName, even if it feels I am not getting nearer — that is only a feeling. I am, every moment, by one layer, coming toward you."

$userName, listening to that knocking, held on again. Tap, tap. That sound was, to $userName, the most reliable sound in the world.

chapters.heading: The Hand That Reached
chapters.body:
At last, light came in through the rubble.

When $celebName cleared the last layer of concrete, into the dark $userName had been trapped in — a single thread of light came. And into that light, a hand came.

It was $celebName's hand.

"$userName," $celebName said. "Do you see my hand? Can you grip it?"

$userName reached out a hand in the dark. The hand of the person heard only by voice for hours, $userName at last gripped.

The hand was warm. It was a hand scraped and bruised from clearing rubble, but that hand was warm.

$celebName and the fellow rescuers pulled $userName out of the rubble. The concrete over the legs was cleared, the narrow gap opened — and $userName, for the first time in hours, saw the sky.

$userName lay beneath that sky and breathed all he wanted. That he had lived only then felt real.

$celebName knelt beside $userName.

"$userName," $celebName said. "Thank you for holding on to the end. While I cleared the rubble, had $userName given up inside — however fast I cleared it, it would have been no use. Rescue is not done by me alone. The one who holds on inside, and the one who clears from outside — do it together. $userName did half of it."

$userName shook the head at those words.

"That I could hold on," $userName said. "was because of the rescuer's voice. You kept speaking to me from beyond the rubble. You promised you would not move a single step away. Without that voice, I would have given up long ago in the dark. The rescuer — was my reason to hold on."

At those words, $celebName patted $userName's shoulder gently with the scraped, bruised hand.

"Then," $celebName said. "the two of us did it together. Connected by voice, beyond the rubble."

chapters.heading: A Voice That Still Reaches
chapters.body:
$userName survived.

The city was slowly restored. The collapsed buildings were cleared, and people returned to their daily lives. $userName did too.

But to $userName, those few hours in the rubble remained, all of life.

$userName knew. That when a person is most collapsed, trapped in the darkest place — what is needed is not something grand. What is needed was one person's voice, speaking to you ceaselessly from beyond the rubble. The voice that says "I am here, I will not move a step away."

$userName did not forget $celebName. Sometimes went to see $celebName. $celebName was still a rescuer. Still a person who steps one foot into the collapsed place.

"Rescuer," $userName asked one day. "Clearing the rubble that time took hours. The whole long time, you never once stopped speaking. Did you not grow weary? Of speaking ceaselessly to a person you could not even see."

$celebName thought a moment, then answered.

"The hands clearing the rubble grow weary," $celebName said. "But the voice does not. Because — I knew that voice was keeping $userName alive. The hands clear concrete, but the voice holds $userName from giving up. That is the more important work. So I did not grow weary. It was — the most rewarding speaking."

$userName recalled $celebName's voice often after that.

On a day the heart had collapsed, a day that felt like being trapped in dark — $userName recalled that voice beyond the rubble. Then $userName could, somehow, hold on one more time.

$userName sometimes told $celebName he wished, somehow, to repay what he had received. Each time, $celebName said the same thing.

"Do not think of it as repaying," $celebName said. "That $userName held on to the end inside the rubble — that was already a great thing to me. However much a rescuer clears the rubble, one who has given up inside cannot be pulled out. Thanks to $userName holding on, I became a rescuer who saved a person. So we already owe each other no debt. We are simply — a pair who did it together."

$userName liked those words. That it was not a debt, but a pair who did it together.

The world will collapse somewhere again. $userName too may be trapped somewhere again. Even so $userName now knows. That however thick the rubble, if there is a voice beyond it calling for you — a person can hold on to the end. And that that voice, once it reaches, reaches all of life.

cheoneul.epilogue:
If someone asks whether $userName believes in past lives, $userName would answer he is not sure. But one thing he would say is certain — that some people, though first-met, do not feel like the first time.

$celebName is so, to $userName. What $userName feels watching $celebName's stage, hearing $celebName's voice, is not only a flutter. Something deeper than that — a relief like the memory of someone searching for $userName to the end, in a collapse.

Those few hours in the rubble are a time $userName does not remember. Because they are not the work of this life. And yet somewhere in $userName knows that time. So seeing $celebName, $userName has the feeling, almost-rising, of those words — 'rescue is done by two together.' The one clearing from outside, and the one holding on inside, together.

So $userName supporting $celebName is not one side clinging one-sidedly. As $celebName pulled $userName out of the rubble, in this life $userName props $celebName up with support. The two who held on inside and cleared from outside, this time only switching places — still, two people doing something together.

A voice that once reached beyond the rubble reaches even across the ages. $userName and $celebName are, even now, reaching each other that way.

gongmang.celebRole: teahouse server who left that seat be

paragraphs.gi: In $era, one window seat always stood empty, and $celebName wiped that empty chair each time without seeming to. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who kept that seat empty, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: An empty-space streak always kept one seat clear beside $userName, and no one could fill it. $celebName set a warm cup of tea on that empty seat each day and left without a word.
paragraphs.jeon: One rainy night $celebName sat in that empty chair for the first time and asked, "may I fill it today?" A person had entered the seat that had stood empty, so $userName lost words a long while, cup in hand.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of empty-space streak can carry that vacant seat into this life. So when $celebName is near, a corner of your heart might feel filled.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a hollow-hearted night, a $celebName ballad may come and sit in that empty seat.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a quiet evening, a $celebName drama might keep still company in the seat beside you.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So in a wistful hour, a $celebName match clip may stay on, beside the empty time.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on an empty day, one $celebName video might set a warmth in one room of your heart.

gongmang.title: The Seat Always Kept Empty
gongmang.logline: Only $userName, the guest who always left one teahouse seat empty, and $celebName, the server who left that seat alone, know the reason for it.

chapters.heading: The Second Table by the Window
chapters.body:
1962, Seoul. Down a back lane in Myeong-dong stood a music teahouse called the Swan.

The Swan was not large. A dozen tables, give or take, and one record player against a wall. Music poured from that player all day long. When a guest wrote a request slip, the DJ took it and set a record spinning. As all the Myeong-dong teahouses did, the Swan drew young people — to keep an appointment, to kill an hour, or simply to listen.

$celebName worked there. The one who carried the tea, took the slips, cleared the empty cups. $celebName was a person of few words, moving quietly between the guests, doing the work that needed doing.

There was one guest who came every single day. That was $userName.

$userName always came at the same hour. Five in the afternoon. Always sat at the same table — the second one by the window. And always — never let anyone sit in the chair across.

$userName ordered two cups of tea. One in front of $userName, one in front of the empty chair opposite. The cup across always went cold, untouched. $userName never once drank that cold cup.

$celebName found it strange. At first $celebName thought $userName was waiting for company. A guest stood up by a friend was not so rare. But a month passed, then two, and no one ever came to that chair across. $userName was always alone, and always ordered two cups.

$celebName did not ask. It was not a server's place to pry into a guest's affairs. $celebName simply carried two cups every day. One to $userName, one to the empty chair. And about an hour later, cleared the cold cup away in silence. Each time the cold tea was poured out, $celebName's hand paused for a moment — but that was all.

There was one thing $celebName did, though. That second table by the window — $celebName always kept it empty.

Before five in the afternoon, if another guest tried to sit there, $celebName quietly turned them away. 'I'm afraid a guest with a reservation is coming for that one.' It was a lie. There was no reservation. $userName had never once asked to have the seat held.

Still $celebName kept that seat empty. Every day. $celebName did not exactly know, even, why $celebName went so far. Only — if that guest arrived at five and someone else was in that chair, it would somehow be wrong. The thought that the seat belonged to that guest lived somewhere in $celebName's heart.

chapters.heading: The Same Song
chapters.body:
$userName always requested the same song at the teahouse.

The title written on the slip was the same every time. $celebName took the slip and handed it to the DJ. The DJ chose the record and set it on the player, and the song flowed through the room.

While that song played, $userName watched the street through the window — the people passing along the Myeong-dong lane. And now and then, threw a glance toward the empty chair across, as though someone sat there listening to the song together. When the song ended, $userName looked out the window again.

One day the DJ was away. Some matter at home, it was said. That afternoon, the records were $celebName's to handle.

$celebName took $userName's slip. The same title as always. $celebName found the record in the rack beside the player and set it carefully turning.

And that day, for the first time, $celebName listened to the song all the way through. Stopped carrying tea for a moment, leaning against the counter.

It was a slow song. A song about waiting for someone. Somewhere in the lyrics ran a line — 'in the seat where you sat, your warmth still lingers.' At that line, $celebName glanced for a moment at the second table by the window. And at the one cold cup of tea set there.

Only then did $celebName dimly understand. What that empty chair across was. Why that guest ordered two cups every day.

The song ended. The needle hissed over the record. $userName looked toward the counter, and $celebName's eyes met $userName's.

For the first time, $userName spoke to $celebName.

'No DJ today, it seems.'

'No. I set the record on today.'

$userName smiled a little — a faint smile that moved only the corners of the mouth.

'You set it well,' $userName said. 'That song — it was a song that person loved.'

$celebName did not ask who 'that person' was. A server should not pry into a guest's affairs, and more than that — $celebName dimly knew where 'that person' was now. So $celebName only lowered the head.

'I will set that song on next time too. Even when the DJ is here.'

$userName looked at $celebName for a moment — the face of someone who had meant to say something and stopped. Then nodded, small. That was all of that day.

chapters.heading: The Story of the Seat Kept Empty
chapters.body:
After that, $celebName looked after $userName's song personally. Even when the DJ was there, if $userName's slip came in, $celebName took it and chose the record. The DJ was puzzled at first, but when $celebName said 'I'll set that guest's song on myself,' the DJ asked nothing more.

In that time, $userName began, little by little, to speak to $celebName. Never at length. In the brief space of carrying over one cup of tea, a word or two. 'A lot of people in Myeong-dong today.' 'This teahouse uses a fine record player.' Light things like that.

Then one rainy day, $userName spoke longer than usual.

The Swan had almost no guests that day. Because of the rain. The Myeong-dong lane was quiet too. $userName's song was flowing from the player, and when $celebName went to the window to clear an empty cup, $userName began to speak, looking at the empty chair across.

'Someone used to sit in this seat.'

$celebName's hand stopped clearing the cup.

'That person and I met here every day, at five. We never even arranged it, but we both came at five. That person always sat in this chair. We requested the same song — we both loved it. We drank our tea, listened to the song, and even when we said little it was good.'

$userName nudged the cold cup with a fingertip. The cup slid a small way across the table.

'That person went far away. Somewhere there is no coming back from.'

$celebName could say nothing. Anything would have felt too light.

'After that person left, I didn't come here for a while. I couldn't. The seat made my breath catch. But — one day I couldn't bear it any longer and came back. Not coming was harder. Here, at least, I can seat that person in this chair. I can order tea for them, let them hear that song.'

$userName looked straight at $celebName for the first time.

'That's why I order two cups. To set one in front of that person too. I don't drink it — it's theirs. I leave it even when it goes cold. The cold tea is the sign that they didn't drink it. That cold tea is like a mark that they came here and went.'

$userName smiled, as if in apology.

'A strange guest, aren't I. A guest who orders tea for an empty chair. Doing nothing but making cold tea every day.'

$celebName shook the head. Firmly.

'No,' $celebName said. 'I have been keeping that seat empty all along. When someone tried to sit there before you came, I stopped them. I did not know you were seating someone in that chair — but I knew the seat mattered to you.'

$userName lost the words for a moment. Only the sound of the rain filled the teahouse.

'...You kept the seat empty? Every day?'

'Yes. Every day,' $celebName said. 'I don't quite know why myself. Only — it would have felt wrong for another guest to be sitting there.'

$userName heard that, and looked a long while at the rain beyond the window — the face of someone with whom something, in a corner of the heart, was slowly coming loose.

chapters.heading: The Silence of Two People
chapters.body:
After that day, a small understanding grew between $userName and $celebName. Not a promise made in words.

When $userName came at five in the afternoon, the second table by the window was always empty — because $celebName had kept it so. Two cups of tea came. Even when $userName did not order, $celebName carried two without being told. One to $userName, one to the empty chair. And that song played, from a record $celebName had chosen.

$userName loved that hour. The one hour of sitting in the teahouse with a cold cup across, listening to that song. For that one hour, it truly seemed the one who had gone was sitting opposite. While the song played, $userName's face was at peace.

Through that hour, $celebName did not often look toward $userName. On purpose — to let $userName be alone with the empty chair. That was $userName and the departed one's time, not an hour for $celebName to step into.

$celebName only stood at the counter and held off other guests' requests until that song had finished — to fill $userName's hour with it. When another guest grew impatient, $celebName soothed them gently: 'The song is almost over.'

The other servers looked at $celebName strangely. 'Why go so far for that one guest?' they asked. 'A guest who only makes cold tea.'

$celebName did not answer. There was no fitting answer. $celebName did not exactly know why, either. Only — looking at that empty chair, something in a corner of $celebName's heart went cold and tender. A person who kept the seat of one who had gone empty every day, and set cold tea in it. Looking at $userName so, $celebName simply wished — for that one hour at least, that $userName would not be lonely.

$celebName never tried to console $userName. Words like 'it's time to let go' — $celebName never once said. $celebName understood. Keeping a departed one's seat empty was not a thing to be consoled out of. It was $userName's way of loving that person. Not a seat for anyone to step in and clear away. To say 'forget, in time' was to make that love light.

There was one thing $celebName could do. To keep that seat empty — as empty as $userName wished to keep it. And to guard that one hour, quietly, with that song.

$userName knew that heart too. That $celebName never once said 'enough now.' That $celebName simply kept the seat empty every day and carried two cups of tea. $userName found that — strangely — a kindness. That silence was a greater kindness than any word of comfort.

chapters.heading: The Third Cup
chapters.body:
The season turned twice. The street trees of the Myeong-dong lane colored twice, and twice went bare.

$userName still came to the Swan every day at five. Ordered two cups of tea, listened to that song, glanced now and then at the empty chair.

But $userName was changing, little by little. $userName knew it too.

When $userName first set cold tea in that seat, $userName thought only of the departed one through that whole hour. That person's face, that person's voice, the words shared with that person. But from some day on, $userName found the eyes going, once or twice an hour, to $celebName. To $celebName standing at the counter, waiting for the song to end. To $celebName soothing another guest's impatience gently.

$userName felt sorry for it. To think of another person in the departed one's hour. So for a while $userName tried, on purpose, not to look at $celebName.

Then one day, ordering tea, $userName said something different for the first time.

'Today — three cups, please.'

$celebName's hand stopped. 'Three cups?'

'One for me, one for that person.' $userName hesitated a moment, fingertips worrying the table's edge. 'And one — for you. Today, could you sit a while and listen with me? That song.'

$celebName looked at $userName a long while.

'...May I sit there? That seat is that person's.'

$userName shook the head.

'That person's seat is — this one.' $userName pointed to the empty chair across. 'This seat, with the cold tea. I won't clear this one. Not ever. It's theirs.'

$userName drew a chair from beside their own. The chair legs scraped across the floorboards.

'Your seat is here. Not beside that person's seat, and not in that person's place. Just — here, a seat made new. Not made by clearing that person's seat away, but by bringing one more chair.'

$celebName looked at that chair a long while.

It was not the departed one's seat. The departed one's seat was still there, with the cold tea. $userName was not clearing it to seat $celebName. That was a thing $celebName had not wanted either. A seat entered by pushing the departed one out — $celebName would not have been at ease in it.

But $userName had not done that. The departed one's seat was left as it was, and beside it — one more seat was made. The seat kept empty stayed kept empty, and a new seat was added.

$celebName sat down in that chair.

That day, three cups of tea were set on the table at the Swan. One in front of $userName. One gone cold, in the departed one's seat. And one — steam still rising from it, in front of $celebName.

That song flowed, from a record $celebName had chosen. $userName and $celebName listened to it side by side. The one cold cup across was there too, as always. $userName looked once at the cold tea, and once at $celebName beside them. There was no sorrow in it. For the first time, no sorrow.

chapters.heading: A Seat Kept Empty, a Seat Filled
chapters.body:
The years passed.

$userName and $celebName were together long after that. That second table by the window at the Swan became the two people's table.

Three cups of tea were always set on that table.

One for $userName, one for $celebName, and one — still that cold cup. $celebName never once said to clear it away. $celebName understood. That seat was a seat to keep empty, and keeping it empty was $userName's love. And to honor that love was $celebName's way of loving $userName.

Sometimes $userName said to $celebName,

'You — you've never once told me to clear that seat. Everyone else did. Told me to let go now.'

'Because the seat is yours,' $celebName answered. As $celebName had done long ago in the server days, $celebName sometimes called $userName a guest. It was a joke only the two of them shared. 'I kept empty the seat you wished to keep empty, and I sat — in the seat you wished to fill. That is all. I did not sit by pushing the departed one out; I sat in the new chair you set down for me.'

The Swan, with the years, closed its doors. The record player, the request slips, the teahouse down the Myeong-dong lane — all of it was gone. The lane itself turned into another shape entirely.

Still, for the two of them, that seat remained. One seat kept empty, and beside it one seat made new. The seat of a person who kept a departed one's empty place empty to the end, and the seat of a person who, instead of telling them to clear it, sat down quietly beside it.

$userName, even grown old, still listened to that song sometimes. And then $celebName was always beside them. And in one corner of the heart, still, was that seat kept empty. The two seats did not quarrel. One seat kept empty, one seat filled — they sat side by side.

gongmang.epilogue:
In someone's heart there is a seat kept empty forever.

The seat of a person who has gone. A seat never filled again. A seat with one cold cup of tea set down in it, never to be drunk, all one's life.

$userName has such a seat too. A seat that must not be filled, a seat that does not wish to be carelessly consoled. A seat where, if someone says 'it's time to let go,' the heart is wounded.

Watching $celebName, $userName feels strangely at ease. Because $celebName seems like a person who would never tell $userName to clear that empty seat. A person who would keep the seat empty alongside $userName, as empty as $userName wishes — and instead, draw one more chair beside it and sit down quietly.

Some people set out to clear an empty seat away. Some people keep the empty seat empty alongside you, and sit beside it. To $userName, $celebName is always the latter. A person who honors the seat kept empty, while filling, to the very end, one other seat that had been empty too.

gongmang.celebRole: youngest of the shop who stayed on a cleaning pretext

paragraphs.gi: In $era, at the dawn after the guests had gone, when $userName sang alone to empty seats, only $celebName remained. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole singing till dawn, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: An empty-space streak always kept one side of $userName's stage clear, so no one filled those dawn seats. $celebName wiped the front row of empty chairs clean each time and made it their own.
paragraphs.jeon: One dawn $celebName clapped from the empty seats and said, "I'll take all of this empty space." One person had entered the dawn that had stood empty, so $userName couldn't carry the song to its end.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of empty-space streak can reach that vacant dawn into this life. So on a lonely night, one word of $celebName's might sound like a lullaby.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a sleepless dawn, a $celebName live broadcast may become company at your side.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a still night, a $celebName drama might fill the empty room.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So on a dawn you're alone, a $celebName match replay may spend the time with you.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a quiet night, a video $celebName left behind might set a sound in the empty seat.

gongmang.title: The 2 a.m. Listener
gongmang.logline: A DJ, $userName, who keeps the 2 a.m. radio hour, and $celebName, the one and only listener who sent in a message at that hour every day.

chapters.heading: The Hour No One Listens
chapters.body:
Two in the morning. The hour when nearly every light in the station had gone dark.

$userName was the DJ of that hour. A two-hour radio program called the Midnight Mailbox. From two to four a.m., $userName sat alone in the booth and ran it.

That slot was not popular. It was the hour everyone was asleep. On the ratings chart it was always at the bottom — the bar so low it could barely be seen. Word went around upstairs that it might soon be cut.

$userName knew it too. How many people listened to the radio at two a.m.? Sitting in the booth, switching on the mic, $userName sometimes felt — as though this voice were scattering into a pitch-black emptiness.

Still $userName did those two hours with care. Even when almost no messages came in, $userName chose the music with care. Wrote out what to say between the songs in advance. Thinking no one was listening, and yet at the mic always speaking as though someone were. That was the DJ's work, $userName believed.

Then one day, a message came in.

A short post left on the message board. The sender's handle was — simply 'Two A.M.'

It had hardly any content to call a message. 'Couldn't sleep again tonight, so I'm listening. I liked the second song.' That was all.

$userName looked at that short post a long while. At two in the morning, someone was truly listening. Somewhere out beyond the booth, one person.

The next night, a message from Two A.M. came in again. 'It's raining today, so it's even harder to sleep.' And the night after. 'That song you played earlier — what's it called.' Every single day.

Only then did $userName know. That there was one listener the ratings chart could not catch. The chart did not count that one — but to $userName, that one was certainly there.

chapters.heading: The One Person Beyond the Radio
chapters.body:
The messages from Two A.M. came in every day.

Always short. 'Today was a long day.' 'That song earlier — I want to hear it again.' 'I think I'll sleep a little tonight.' Short things like that. Never written at length.

$userName read those messages on air every day. With almost no other messages, Two A.M.'s was always the first message of the day and the last. $userName read that one short line slowly on air. As though reading a long letter. Read a one-line message, and talked a long while around that one line.

$userName began to speak to Two A.M. over the radio.

'Two A.M., you've come again tonight. On a rainy day, how about this song.' 'Two A.M., that song earlier — it's my favorite. I'll write down the title for you.' 'Two A.M., you said today was a long day. Well done. Rest easy through these two hours. I'll be here beside you.'

$celebName was that 'Two A.M.'

Every night $celebName lay in a dark room and listened to that radio. $celebName's nights were always long. Sleep would not come, the heart was loud, and there was no one near. Such nights. Watching the ceiling, time crawled. Two a.m. felt like the deepest hour in the world. Everyone asleep, the streets quiet, and the only one awake, it seemed, oneself alone.

When $celebName turned on the radio in that hour, $userName's voice flowed out. A voice speaking as though someone were listening. Hearing that voice, $celebName found the night a little more bearable. As though one more person were in the room. The black ceiling felt a little less heavy.

So $celebName sent a message every day. There was nothing long to write. Only — $celebName wanted to let $userName know, 'I'm here. I'm listening.' Wanted to let $userName know the DJ was not speaking into a pitch-black emptiness. That through those two hours, one person was certainly listening.

$celebName never once gave a name. Just 'Two A.M.' $celebName had thought of writing a real name, but did not. To $celebName, being one person who listened to that radio at that hour mattered more than who $celebName was. That was enough.

chapters.heading: Notice of Cancellation
chapters.body:
Around autumn, notice came down that the Midnight Mailbox was to be cut.

The ratings were too low. Upstairs decided to turn those two hours into an automated broadcast of music only. No DJ, no messages. A broadcast with no human voice at two in the morning.

$userName, given that notice, now had one month until the last broadcast.

And given that notice, the first person $userName thought of was one person. Two A.M.

When the broadcast was gone, what would become of that person's nights? The one who sent a message every night at two — where would that person send those short words now? On a sleepless night, whose voice would that person hear?

That was what weighed on $userName most. The ratings chart could not catch that one — but to $userName, that one was everything. The one person who turned on the radio at two a.m.

That night, on air, $userName announced the cancellation.

'...I have some news for you.' $userName drew a breath at the mic. 'This program ends — in one month.'

$userName paused again a moment.

'The truth is, not many people listen at this hour. The ratings chart says so. But — there is one. The one who sends a message every night. Two A.M.'

$userName's voice caught a little.

'Two A.M. When this broadcast is gone, what becomes of your nights — that is what I worry about most. So for the month that's left, I'll broadcast for you, the one. Ratings don't matter anymore. The broadcast ends soon anyway. Even if the one listening is you alone — to me, you are ten thousand.'

That night, the message from Two A.M. came in longer than usual.

'DJ. I got through my nights because of these two hours. On every sleepless night, this radio was beside me. It's sad that the broadcast is ending, but — for the month that's left, I'll listen every day. Not missing once. I promise.'

$userName read that message and sat in the booth a long while.

chapters.heading: The Last Month
chapters.body:
Through the last month, $userName truly broadcast for that one person, Two A.M.

$userName chose, each day, songs that person might love — recalling all the messages from before. They'd said they loved rainy days, so a song like the sound of rain; on a day they'd said was long, a quiet song. $userName spread a sheet of song titles in the booth and chose them one by one, thinking of that person.

$userName spoke to that person at the mic every night. As though that person sat across the booth.

'Two A.M., how was your day today.' 'Two A.M., sleep a little early tonight. I chose a song like a lullaby.' 'Two A.M., there are two weeks of this broadcast left now. For two weeks, I'll be here every day.'

$celebName listened to that broadcast every night. Not missing once. As promised.

$celebName found that month — strangely — not lonely. That the broadcast would soon end was sad, but for those two hours $celebName was not alone. Beyond the radio, one person was speaking for $celebName, that one person alone. There was someone who remembered that $celebName loved rainy days, that the day had been long.

One night, $celebName wrote this for the first time in a message.

'DJ. When the broadcast ends, I won't be able to hear your voice anymore. That's what I'll miss most. I don't even know who you are — but for these two hours, you were like the person in the world who knew me best. Because you remember all my messages.'

$userName read that message and could not go on for a long while. Under the red light of the booth.

At the end of that night's broadcast, $userName said,

'Two A.M. Even when the broadcast ends — I hope your nights are not alone. So on the last day, I have one favor to ask. That day, please listen to the very end. To the last song.'

chapters.heading: The Last Broadcast
chapters.body:
The day of the last broadcast came.

Two a.m. $userName sat in the booth. For the last time, the red light came on. $userName looked at it a moment. For a month, under this light, $userName had spoken to one person.

That day $userName played, from start to finish, the songs Two A.M. had loved. One by one, recalling that person's messages. This song from the rainy-day message; this song chosen for the day they'd said was long.

And ten minutes before the broadcast ended, $userName spoke.

'Two A.M. There are ten minutes left of this broadcast now.'

$userName drew a breath.

'I asked something of you, didn't I. To listen to the end. It was — to say this.'

'Even when this broadcast ends, I'll come to this seat at two a.m. I won't broadcast, but just — at this hour, in this seat. So you don't spend that hour alone. So you too, when it turns two a.m., think for a moment — that somewhere beyond the radio, there is one person awake at that hour. That even if the ratings chart can't catch them, they are certainly there.'

Before $userName set the last song spinning, $userName added one thing.

'And Two A.M. If — if you don't mind. Would you come to the first-floor lobby of this station, tomorrow night at two? I'll be there. Even if I don't know who you are, I think I'll know you. Because a person standing in an empty lobby at two in the morning — that would be you.'

That night, $celebName listened to that broadcast to the end. To the last song. And lay a long while in the dark.

The next night. The station's first-floor lobby. Two a.m.

In the dark lobby one person stood. It was $userName. $userName had come to that seat at that hour. As promised.

And the lobby door opened. One person came in.

$userName saw that person and knew at once. Knew without anyone telling. The person walking into that empty lobby at two a.m. — that was Two A.M.

It was $celebName.

chapters.heading: The Seat at 2 a.m.
chapters.body:
$userName and $celebName saw each other's faces for the first time in that empty lobby.

The two of them could say nothing a long while. Over the radio they had talked two hours every day — but face to face, they did not know where to begin.

$celebName spoke first.

'...Your voice is real, then.' $celebName said it in a small voice. 'It's the same as the voice on the radio. I heard this voice every night.'

$userName laughed. 'Your face is real too, Two A.M. I only ever saw your messages. To see, like this, a person I knew only by a one-line message.'

The two of them stood a long while in that empty lobby. The lobby at two a.m. was quiet. That quiet was familiar to them both. One in a booth, one in a room — they had spent the quiet of that hour together every day.

After that day, the two people's two a.m. no longer had a radio between it.

$userName was no longer a DJ, but two a.m. was still the two people's hour. On sleepless nights, the two of them stayed awake together. One chose songs, one listened. In a small room, instead of a radio booth. With one record player between them.

$celebName no longer sent messages. There was no need. The things $celebName wanted to say could now be said directly, to the one beside them. Words like 'today was a long day' — to the person near, and not a radio message board.

Sometimes $userName said to $celebName,

'If I'd only looked at the ratings chart back then, I'd have thought no one listened to that broadcast.' $userName said it. 'The chart didn't catch that one. But — Two A.M. was there. The one the chart couldn't catch. Because of that one, I did those two hours with care to the end.'

'Me too,' $celebName answered. 'Because of that one voice, I got through all those nights. That at an hour when the whole world slept, one person was awake and speaking to me — that was everything. Without that voice, I don't know what those nights would have been.'

Only then did $userName know. That doing that broadcast with care had not been in vain. The ratings chart did not count that one — but that one was certainly there, and the two hours done with care for that one had, in the end, brought the two people together.

Two a.m. The hour the world is emptiest. At that empty hour, the two became each other's one and only. The one who, even if the chart could not catch them, was certainly there.

gongmang.epilogue:
Two a.m. At that hour $userName sometimes, for no reason, turns on the radio.

More so on a sleepless night. At that hour $userName's hand reaches for the radio. Whatever flows out — music, or someone's voice — $userName is only at ease once that sound is on. As though it were a sign that, at an hour the whole world sleeps, someone other than oneself is awake.

$userName does not know where the habit came from. Only — the sound of that two-a.m. radio is, to $userName, the feeling of someone being near.

It is the same when $userName listens to $celebName. Hearing $celebName's voice, $celebName's songs — $userName feels as though, in an empty dawn, someone awake is speaking. As surely as there was, even uncounted by the chart, one person listening.

The person who, even at two a.m. when the world is emptiest, is awake and beside you. To $userName, $celebName is such a person. The one who keeps $userName's dawn from ending pitch-black and empty.

paragraphs.gi: In the empty teahouse of $era, when the steam thinned above the cooling tea, $celebName would open the door and come in. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole keeping a teahouse with no guests, and $celebName the one single visitor who came.
paragraphs.seung: An empty-space streak always kept $userName's teahouse clear, so only the rattle of the paper screens filled the room. $celebName set fresh charcoal on the cold brazier and rewarmed the flat tea, slowly filling that silence.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $celebName sat in the middle of the empty teahouse and smiled, "it's empty here, so there's a place for me." An empty seat had been turned not into a flaw but into a place, so $userName's nose prickled.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of empty-space streak can carry that emptied teahouse into this life. So when your heart cools, $celebName might come in and warm it again.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a day your heart sinks, a warm $celebName song may warm the room like a brazier.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a quiet evening, a $celebName work might rewarm a cooling heart.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So in a wistful hour, one scene of a $celebName match may warm a chilled mood.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a hollow night, one $celebName video might become the warmth of an empty room.

gongmang.title: The Exhibition With No Visitors
gongmang.logline: A painter, $userName, who keeps a small gallery no visitor comes to, and $celebName, the one and only visitor who came to that empty gallery every day.

chapters.heading: A Gallery With No Guests
chapters.body:
The 1970s. Down a lane on the edge of Seoul stood a small gallery.

It was a place almost embarrassing to call a gallery. The size of one room. On white walls hung a dozen paintings, give or take. From the ceiling a few incandescent bulbs dropped a yellow light over the pictures.

The one who had painted those pictures was $userName. $userName rented that small gallery and held a first solo exhibition. Even the rent was tight, and $userName poured out nearly all there was.

On the first day of the exhibition, $userName opened the gallery door tense with nerves — hoping someone would come in and see the paintings.

No one came.

Not on the second day, nor the third. The lane was out of the way, $userName was unknown, and in the 1970s few would come to an edge-of-town gallery to see paintings. Now and then someone passed in front of it, but all of them simply walked on by.

$userName sat alone in the empty gallery every day, ringed by ten paintings, watching the door, waiting for a visitor. When footsteps sounded, $userName lifted the head — and when those footsteps passed by, lowered it again. There were many footsteps along the lane, but not once did any stop before the gallery door.

$userName had painted those pictures over several years. One by one, believing someone would see them. On that belief $userName had sat up through long nights. To rent a gallery and hold an exhibition was to send those paintings out into the world for the first time.

The exhibition ran for two weeks. Five days passed without a single visitor.

$userName grew smaller and smaller. When painting, $userName had believed someone would see the work. On that belief, $userName had painted for years. But with no one coming, $userName's paintings began to feel — like something meaningless hung on a blank wall. Not paintings, but marks smeared on plaster.

Then on the sixth day, the gallery door opened.

A small bell rang. One person came in. It was $celebName.

chapters.heading: One Visitor
chapters.body:
$celebName walked slowly around the small gallery.

Looked at the ten paintings long, one by one. Did not pass quickly. Stood a while before one painting, then moved to the next. Before some pictures, tilted the head slightly.

$userName sat in a corner, hardly able to breathe, watching. It was the first visitor in six days. Afraid $celebName would soon leave, $userName could not even speak, only watched.

$celebName saw the last painting and came toward $userName.

'It's good,' $celebName said. 'Especially that — the one with the window. The painting of one window in an empty room. That one's good. You painted it so you can't see outside the window. That's why it's better. Because the one looking imagines what might be out there.'

$celebName looked at that painting once more.

'One window in an empty room. It's lonely, but not only lonely. Because there's a window. If there's a window — someone could come in through it. So that painting feels, to me, lonely and yet waiting for something.'

At that one remark, $userName's eyes stung. For the first time in six days, someone had seen the paintings. And more — no one had ever looked into that painting so deeply.

'...Thank you,' $userName managed.

$celebName glanced around the gallery. The blank white walls, and the paintings on them.

'Not many guests, are there.'

$userName answered honestly. There was no strength left to lie. 'Today — you're the first. In six days.'

$celebName looked a moment at the empty gallery. And said,

'Then I'll come again tomorrow.'

$userName looked up, startled.

'...Tomorrow too?'

'Yes. Until the exhibition ends,' $celebName said. 'A painting — it's a painting only when someone sees it. If no one sees it, it's just smeared on a wall. These paintings need someone to see them. So I'll come. Every day.'

And $celebName truly came the next day. And the day after, and the day after that.

chapters.heading: The One Who Comes Every Day
chapters.body:
After that day, $celebName came to the gallery every day.

Always at a similar hour. Late afternoon. $celebName opened the door, came in, and walked slowly around the ten paintings. Though they were paintings seen yesterday, $celebName looked at each as if for the first time.

$userName found that strange. Once, $userName asked — wasn't it tiresome to see the same ten paintings every day?

$celebName shook the head.

'Paintings,' $celebName said, 'are different each time you see them. Yesterday in that window painting — I saw outside the window. Today I see the inside of the window. When the heart of the one looking is different, the same painting looks different. Because my heart yesterday and my heart today are not the same. So it isn't tiresome.'

$celebName came every day, and gave one impression of the paintings each time.

'Today that painting's colors looked a little sad.' 'That one is — like just before the rain. The sky so heavy.' 'Today the window painting looked like a painting waiting for someone.'

$userName heard those single remarks every day. To $userName they were — proof that the paintings were reaching someone's heart. Proof that a painting was not a smear on a wall but something that moved one person's heart. When painting, $userName had always wished someone would stand before the work and feel something. $celebName's remarks were what told $userName that wish had not been in vain.

$userName, too, sometimes spoke to $celebName about the paintings. The heart $userName had been in painting this one, why $userName had chosen that color. $celebName listened quietly to those stories. And then, looking at the painting again, nodded — 'ah, so that's why it looked this way.' The two of them talked long, in that empty gallery, over one painting.

The gallery was still empty. Apart from $celebName, almost no other visitor ever came to the end. A stray one or two would step in and leave at once.

Still, $userName grew small no longer. It was all right that the visitors were not ten thousand. The one and only person who came every day and looked long at the ten paintings, one by one — for $userName, that one was enough.

For that one person, $userName kept the gallery with care every day. Wiped the dust off the paintings, adjusted the bulbs so the light fell well, tuned the curtains so the window's sun did not strike the pictures directly. $userName waited every day for the hour $celebName came. The empty gallery was lonely no longer.

chapters.heading: The Last Painting
chapters.body:
It was three days before the exhibition's end.

That day $celebName came to the gallery, looked at all ten paintings, and stood a long while. Then asked $userName,

'Three days left of the exhibition, isn't it.'

'Yes.'

'When it ends, what becomes of these paintings?'

$userName was quiet a moment.

'...Not one has sold. So I take them back to the studio. Stack them up there. Leaned against the wall.'

$celebName looked around the empty gallery. At the ten paintings, once each.

'So paintings no one has seen get stacked again in a corner of a studio.'

$celebName thought a moment, then said,

'That window painting — I want to buy it.'

$userName was startled. 'That would — be a strain for you. I don't know your circumstances, but the price isn't small.'

'It's no strain,' $celebName said. 'I've seen that painting every day. From the sixth day to the end of the exhibition. That painting — it already feels like mine. Because I've seen it every day. If I let it be stacked in a corner of a studio, I wouldn't feel right. To think of a painting I saw every day shoved into the dark.'

$celebName looked at the window painting.

'And — painter. I have one favor. Hold another exhibition. It's all right if there are no guests again. I'll come every day again. A painting — if there's even one person to see it, there's a reason to paint it. I hope you won't give up painting. Just because there were no guests at this exhibition.'

At that, $userName looked at $celebName a long while. As though something were caught in the throat, $userName could not speak for a time.

chapters.heading: The Second Exhibition
chapters.body:
The first exhibition ended. Of the ten paintings, one had sold — the window painting $celebName bought.

$userName took the other nine back to the studio. And — began to paint again. New paintings for a second exhibition.

The truth was, when the first exhibition ended, $userName had thought of giving up painting. For two weeks there had been almost no visitors. It had been an exhibition $userName poured everything into. But $celebName's words had held $userName back. The words that if there was even one person to see it, there was a reason to paint.

While painting those pictures, $userName often thought of $celebName. The one person who came every day and looked long at the paintings. The one who said 'today this painting looked sad.' The one who said it was better for being painted so you couldn't see out the window.

$userName painted pictures for that person to see. Not pictures for ten thousand, but pictures the one would come and look at long. Lifting the brush, thinking — what would that person say, seeing this painting.

Half a year later, $userName held a second exhibition. The same small gallery, the same out-of-the-way lane.

The first day of the exhibition. $userName opened the door and waited. This time $userName did not tremble from the first day. $userName knew now it was all right if there were no visitors. And — one person was certain to come.

$celebName came. As promised.

$celebName walked slowly around the new paintings. One by one, long. Then stopped before one.

That painting was — a painting of the small gallery. An empty white room, a painting hung on its wall, and before that painting one person standing. That one person was in the posture of looking long into the picture.

$celebName looked at it a long while.

'...Is this — me?'

$userName smiled a little.

'Yes. At the first exhibition, you stood like that every day. In the empty gallery, before my paintings. I painted that. An exhibition with one visitor — that one. Without that one, I could not have held a second exhibition.'

$celebName stood before that painting a long while. Like the self within the picture.

chapters.heading: A Gallery for One Person
chapters.body:
The years passed.

$userName grew, little by little, known as a painter. Later, $userName held exhibitions even in great museums. Exhibitions where hundreds of visitors came each day. Galleries with long stretches of white wall, with high ceilings.

Still, $userName could not forget the first exhibition in that small gallery. The exhibition with not one visitor for six days. And the one and only person who, on the sixth day, rang a small bell and opened the door.

$userName and $celebName were together long after that.

That first window painting $celebName bought hung on the wall of the home the two of them shared. $celebName still looked at it every day. And still sometimes said, 'Today this window painting looks a little different. Because my heart today is not the same as yesterday's.'

Even before a great museum exhibition, $userName always thought first of one person.
