'Even with hundreds of visitors,' $userName said, 'in the end I paint for one person. Believing that one person will come and stand long before the painting. Because it was so at the first exhibition. Because of that one person who came every day to the empty gallery, I could keep painting. When I think of that one person, I don't tremble even before hundreds.'

$celebName answered.

'I didn't go to see your paintings, back then,' $celebName said. 'The first time I did — but after that, I went because it weighed on me that you sat alone in that empty gallery. Sitting watching only the door. Lifting your head at footsteps, lowering it again when they passed. I didn't go to see paintings. I went so you wouldn't be alone in that gallery. A painting you see once and you're done — but I went every day. That wasn't for the paintings. It was for the person.'

$userName heard that and looked a long while at $celebName. Only then did $userName understand. That $celebName had looked long at the paintings each day as if for the first time — not because the paintings were new each time, but to keep $userName from being alone in that gallery.

The exhibition with no visitors. To that empty gallery, one and only person came every day. And that one person filled the empty seat to the end. Because of that one person, the exhibition did not, in the end, become a meaningless one. Because of that one person, $userName did not set down the brush.

gongmang.epilogue:
And now, each time $userName makes something and sends it into the world — $userName recalls the empty gallery.

Writing or painting or work, once something made with care is sent out, $userName always feels as though sitting alone before that white wall. Footsteps passing, no one stopping, growing small with the thought — was what I did meaningless after all.

At such times $userName looks at $celebName. At $celebName unfolding something on a stage, on a screen. And then $userName feels, strangely, as though someone has rung a bell and opened the door of an empty gallery. Like that one person who stood long before a painting and said 'today this looked different to me.'

$userName knows it is all right if the visitors are not ten thousand. That the one and only person who comes every day and looks long at each piece — that is enough to keep an empty exhibition from being empty.

To $userName, $celebName is like that 'one and only visitor.' The one who, even on a day it feels no one is watching, will come and stand long before you. The one who keeps what $userName has made from hanging in vain on a blank wall.

gongmang.celebRole: person who stayed on as a cleaner and listened

paragraphs.gi: In $era, while $userName drew the bow in an audienceless hall, $celebName swept the empty seats and listened to that sound. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole practicing in an empty hall, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: An empty-space streak always kept $userName's hall clear, so not one round of applause reached it. $celebName hung their own coat on an empty chair, marking it: "one person is here."
paragraphs.jeon: One night $celebName listened to the last note from the middle of the empty seats and, for the first time, clapped. A sound had returned for the first time to the empty hall, so $userName set the bow down and stood a long while.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of empty-space streak can reach that vacant audience into this life. So when you're wistful, $celebName's applause might come to mind first.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a day it feels like no one's there, a $celebName cheering message may sound like applause.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a lonely night, a $celebName work might fill the seats alongside you.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So in an hour you're alone, the roar of a $celebName match may fill the empty space.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a quiet night, one word $celebName left behind might reach your heart like applause.

gongmang.era: a navigation-marker station on a far-future shipping route
gongmang.title: All That Remained Was the Last Signal
gongmang.logline: A signal operator, $userName, who keeps a space navigation-marker station alone, and $celebName, who reached $userName only by signal across a distance the speed of light itself divided.

chapters.heading: A Station With Only Signals Left
chapters.body:
The far future. Space had its shipping routes, and along those routes marker stations were raised. They were something like the lighthouses of old.

$userName was the signal operator of one such station. The work was simple. Relay the signals of ships crossing the route, log the starlight. It was a small station, and the one who did that work was $userName alone.

The place where the station stood was, even in space, out of the way. To the nearest inhabited planet, light took several days. That meant — whoever $userName spoke to, the words took several days to reach them. And several days more for an answer to return. A distance where one question and its answer took a fortnight.

$userName was alone inside that distance. If someone asked what the loneliest work in space was, $userName could answer. To live as a signal operator at a distance where even light took days. A human voice always arrived several days late, and so $userName's day was always — a day of speaking with someone from several days ago. There was no one within this distance to share this very moment with.

$userName relayed signals every day. Took the words ships traded and passed them on, logged them. Those were other people's words. $userName's own words reached no one, in this distance, in time.

$userName sometimes watched the stars beyond the control-room window. That starlight too was — light from days ago, years ago. In this universe everything arrived late. Light, voices, and the heart.

chapters.heading: A Voice That Comes Days Late
chapters.body:
Then one day, a signal caught in the station's receiver.

It was from a ship. A signal sent from a distance of several days, so by the time $userName received it — it was already a voice from several days ago.

'...Marker station, do you read. This is a cargo ship. This route is my first, and I'd like to receive some starlight coordinates.'

It was $celebName's voice.

$userName arranged the coordinates and sent a reply. That reply would take several days more to reach $celebName. $celebName would receive it and send an answer, and that would take several days more to come to $userName. That was the law of this distance in space.

So the two people's signals began. A slow signaling, where one word and its answer took a fortnight.

At first it was only talk of coordinates. But $celebName's ship sailed that route a long while, and in that time the two of them — came to trade other words besides coordinates.

$celebName sent long signals. They were signals that reached after a fortnight anyway; there was no reason to send them short. $celebName wrote at length about the stars seen that day, the lonely voyage inside the cargo ship, the planet where $celebName once lived. Into one signal, $celebName put all the words that had piled up over several days.

$userName answered at length too. The station's day, the starlight arriving days late, the loneliness of life as a signal operator. Writing that reply, $userName felt, after long, that there was someone to hear $userName's words. Though words that would reach only a fortnight later.

The two of them had never seen each other's faces. The voice was always a voice days late. They had never once shared words at the same time. When $userName asked 'are you well,' that question flew days to reach $celebName, and $celebName's answer flew days back. The two people's talk was always so, slipped past each other across time.

Still $userName sat before the receiver every day. To hear $celebName's voice from days ago, and to send $userName's voice to $celebName, days hence. Even a slipped conversation was, to $userName, the best hour of the day.

chapters.heading: A Conversation That Takes a Fortnight
chapters.body:
The two people's signaling was, being slow, all the deeper for it.

Send one word and the answer came a fortnight later. So the two of them wrote each word with long thought. There were no words flung in haste. $userName turned a signal to send $celebName over in the heart for days before writing it. $celebName did the same.

Once $celebName sent a signal like this.

'$userName. Isn't it strange. We trade words only once a fortnight. We've never even seen each other's faces. And yet I feel — I know you better than anyone on a near planet. Because a signal received after waiting a fortnight, I read again and again. The words you write, I memorize down to the letter.'

It was so for $userName too. When $celebName's signal came, $userName heard it again and again. A voice days late, but to hear that voice once a fortnight was — $userName's best hour. Hearing that signal over and over, $userName imagined what kind of person $celebName might be. The face was unknown, but the manner of speaking was known. What $celebName loved, what grieved $celebName. Inside the long signal that came once a fortnight, the person $celebName was slowly took shape.

Only then did $userName understand. That the reason $userName had been lonely was not that people were far. As long as $userName lived as a signal operator in space, people would always be far. That could not be helped. The true reason $userName had been lonely was — that $userName's words reached no one in time, that there was no one waiting for $userName's words. Not because of distance, but because there was no one waiting.

And now, there was one person. The one who waited a fortnight for $userName's signal, and memorized that one word down to the letter. The signal was still days late — but $userName was lonely no longer. Days late or not, there was certainly someone there receiving $userName's words.

But the two of them knew. When $celebName's cargo ship had crossed the whole of that route, the distance between them would grow. As the distance grew, the signals would slow — not a fortnight but a month, two months. And past some distance — the signal would never reach.

$celebName's ship was moving, a little each day, toward that distance.

chapters.heading: A Distance Beyond Reach
chapters.body:
$celebName's ship went to the end of the route.

The distance between them grew. The signals slowed and slowed. What had been a fortnight became a month, the month became two.

One day, the last signal $celebName sent reached $userName. A voice two whole months old.

'$userName. My ship will soon cross a distance signals cannot reach. This will — likely be the last signal I send. By the time this reaches you, I will already be beyond that distance.'

$celebName's signal was long. As though, knowing it was the last, $celebName had written down everything there was to say.

'We never saw each other's faces in the end. We never shared words at the same time. My voice always reached you days late, and yours did the same. But $userName. I think it is — not only sad. We were never once in the same place, but I memorize every word you wrote to me. Those words are inside me. Distance may divide us, but it cannot divide the words too.'

$userName heard that signal and sat before the receiver a long while. Hearing that two-month-old voice over and over.

$userName sent a reply. Whether it would reach $celebName, $userName could not know. A signal that might, beyond that distance, never arrive.

'$celebName. I know this reply may not reach. Still I send it. Fortnight or two months, I always met you in a late voice. That late voice was the nearest thing I had in this universe. You said it — that distance cannot divide the words we shared. I will believe that. So you too — wherever you go, carry the words I sent. Because inside those words, I will be there.'

$userName sent that reply. To a distance two months hence, that might, perhaps, never arrive.

chapters.heading: A Voice Left as a Record
chapters.body:
$celebName's ship crossed that distance.

After that, $celebName's signals did not come. Beyond a distance signals could not reach, $celebName's ship had gone.

$userName was alone again. Even sitting before the receiver, $celebName's days-late voice no longer came.

But $userName had one thing left.

All the signals $celebName had sent. $userName had logged every one. Those voices received once a fortnight, once a month. From the first coordinate signal to the last farewell.

$userName played the record again. $celebName's voice flowed once more through the station's control room. A voice days late, a voice a month late. And now — a voice forever late.

$userName listened from the first signal. At first, the businesslike voice asking coordinates. Then the signals growing longer in time. The voice talking of stars, the voice missing the planet where $celebName once lived, and the last voice of farewell. Listening to those voices in order, $userName could know that the time the two of them had spent together — though always slipped by several days — had certainly been there.

$userName listened to the record, recalling what $celebName had said. That distance cannot divide the words we shared. It was truly so. $celebName's ship had gone beyond a distance past reach, but the words $celebName sent — were here, just as they were, inside $userName's record. Those words had not worn thin in the distance, had not blurred.

$userName recalled, too, the reply $userName had sent $celebName. The reply that perhaps never reached. But $userName believed. That the reply, whether it took two months or many years — would one day reach $celebName. Because light does not stop. A signal once sent goes on forever through some part of the universe.

$userName played that record once a day. And sometimes sent again a signal that might never reach. Toward the direction $celebName had gone. It was not a signal hoping for an answer. It was only — to go on sending $userName's voice to $celebName, somewhere out there.

chapters.heading: To the End Where Light Reaches
chapters.body:
The years passed.

$userName kept that station long. As a signal operator, relaying voices that came days late.

In that time, $userName never stopped one thing. Sending, now and then, a signal toward the direction $celebName had gone.

That signal got no answer. $celebName's ship was beyond a distance signals could not reach. Still $userName sent it. Believing that $userName's voice was going on forever, at the speed of light, through some part of the universe.

Other signal operators looked at $userName strangely. Why keep sending signals that get no answer.

$userName answered. 'I send them to one person. Even if they don't reach now — my voice does not stop. Because light does not stop. Someday, at some distance in the universe, that person may receive my signal. Even years late. For that one chance, I keep sending.'

$userName knew. That perhaps the signal would never reach. Still it was all right. The words $celebName had left were inside $userName's record, and the words $userName had sent were going on through some part of the universe. The two of them had never once been in the same place — but the words the two of them traded, divided by no distance, remained in the universe.

$userName played that record sometimes. $celebName's days-late voice flowed through the control room. Hearing that voice, $userName was not lonely. Even not beside, even not at the same time — one person's voice was certainly there, remaining inside the record.

gongmang.epilogue:
$celebName is always beyond some distance.

Beyond a screen, on a stage, in a far place a hand cannot possibly reach. Even when $userName sends a heart, that heart does not reach $celebName in time. A distance that can never be in the same time together, always far behind.

But $userName has learned not to grieve that distance only. $userName hears $celebName's voice again and again, and again. $celebName's one word, one verse, memorized down to the letter — as one memorizes a signal that reached days late.

Distance divides two people. But distance cannot divide the words two people shared, either. The voice $celebName leaves remains inside $userName's heart down to the letter, and the heart $userName sends — goes on like light, not stopping, somewhere. Believing it will reach, someday, however late.

To $userName, $celebName is like that 'voice beyond the distance.' The one whose voice, though they cannot be in the same place, certainly reaches $userName and remains, clear down to the letter, inside the heart. The one who keeps any distance, however far, from dividing the two in the end.

gongmang.celebRole: lighthouse keeper who always stayed at the dock

paragraphs.gi: In $era, at a dock-end where no one came to meet the docking ship, $celebName always kept a light lit for $userName. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole no one came to greet, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: An empty-space streak always kept $userName's place clear, so even on returning there was no one to welcome them. At the hour $userName's ship came in, $celebName always raised the lantern one notch brighter.
paragraphs.jeon: One night $celebName came all the way to the dock-end and waved, "it's not an empty dock anymore." One person had stood in the greeting place that had been empty, so $userName set down the load and smiled.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of empty-space streak can carry that vacant dock into this life. So when you feel alone, $celebName might be at your side.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a night your heart is dark, a $celebName song may light the road like a lantern.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a wistful evening, a $celebName work might welcome you as if come out to meet you.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So at the end of a tired day, a $celebName match clip may switch on as if it had waited for you.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a lonely night, a video $celebName left behind might feel like a lit window.

gongmang.era: a lighthouse on a remote island, the early 1900s
gongmang.title: The One Who Never Lets the Light Go Out
gongmang.logline: A lighthouse keeper, $userName, who keeps the light of a remote island alone, and $celebName, a fisherman who reached the island once each year guided by that light.

chapters.heading: The Lighthouse on the Island
chapters.body:
The early 1900s. In the middle of a sea, there was one small island.

It was an island hard for people to live on. All rock, and water scarce. Hardly a few trees grew. The only one who lived there was a single person — the lighthouse keeper, $userName.

$userName's work was to light the lighthouse beam.

Every day when the sun set, $userName climbed to the top of the lighthouse and lit the light. Filled the oil, trimmed the wick, set the flame, wiped the glass of the lamp. That light shone over the sea all night. Passing ships saw it and steered clear of the island's rocks.

$userName was alone on that island. For months on end. Once a year, only when the supply ship came carrying food and oil, did $userName see another person. When the supply ship had gone, $userName was alone again.

It was lonely work. A lighthouse keeper was always lonely. Lighting the beam every night, the ships that saw it and passed — did not see $userName. The ships saw only the light and went on, never thinking there was a person on the island who lit it. They saw the light, but not the one who lit it.

$userName sometimes stood at the top of the lighthouse and watched the lights of distant passing ships. There would be people on that ship. They would eat supper together, share talk. Such thoughts made $userName's island feel lonelier still. An island where one person lived, in the middle of a sea.

Still $userName had never once missed the light. Every night, at the exact hour, $userName lit it. Whether anyone saw it or not. That was the lighthouse keeper's work, $userName believed.

chapters.heading: The Ship That Comes Once a Year
chapters.body:
In that sea, there was one fisherman who passed near $userName's island.

It was $celebName.

$celebName went out to far waters to catch fish. Once a year, when a certain season came, $celebName passed that sea. Then $celebName's small boat brushed past $userName's island.

The sea of that season was rough. Fog frequent, currents savage. Rocks hid below the water. $celebName crossed that rough sea relying on one thing — the lighthouse beam.

$userName's beam.

$celebName saw that light and steered clear of the island's rocks. Without it, $celebName's boat would have struck the rocks many times. More so on foggy nights. When the light glimmered faintly through the fog, only then did $celebName find the way.

One year, $celebName brought the boat close to the island. $celebName wanted to thank the keeper. Having crossed this sea for years relying on that light, $celebName wanted, just once, to meet the one who lit it.

$celebName climbed onto the island, and $userName came down from the lighthouse. So the two of them met for the first time. On the jetty of the rocky island.

'You're the lighthouse keeper,' $celebName said. 'I'm a fisherman who passes this sea once a year. Thanks to your beam, I've crossed safely every time. I wanted to thank you, so I brought the boat in.'

At that, $userName looked a long while at $celebName.

Since becoming a lighthouse keeper, it was the first time anyone had thanked $userName for the light. The ships always passed seeing only the light. $celebName was the first to come to thank the one who lit it.

'...You came having seen the light, then.'

'Yes. I see it every year,' $celebName said. 'Without that light, I can't cross this sea. You are — the one who lights the way of one year of mine, every year. To me, that one light matters more than this whole sea.'

chapters.heading: A Guest for One Night
chapters.body:
That night, $celebName stayed one night on the island.

The sea was rough, and it was safer to leave the next morning. $userName gave $celebName one room of the keeper's quarters. It was a small room, but it had been long since $userName had a guest.

That night, $userName climbed to the top of the lighthouse as always and lit the beam. $celebName followed up.

The two of them stood side by side at the top, watching the light stretch out over the sea. The lamp's beam parted the fog and reached, long, into the dark.

'Aren't you lonely,' $celebName asked. 'Being alone on this island.'

$userName was quiet a moment. Thought of lying, then let it go.

'I'm lonely,' $userName said honestly. 'I light the beam every day — but the ships that see it don't see me. They pass seeing only the light. I sometimes feel this light I light — has nowhere to land. Whom it reaches, I'll never know my whole life.'

$celebName shook the head.

'It reaches,' $celebName said. 'It reaches me. Once a year, certainly. You wouldn't have known — but every time I crossed this sea, I saw this light and thought of you. That in the middle of this rough sea, on an island, someone is lighting a beam. That because of that someone, I live. More so on foggy nights.'

$celebName looked at the beam — that light stretching long into the dark.

'Your light is — not a light with nowhere to land. There are only few ships that receive it. But few does not mean none. To one ship — that light is everything. It saves my life every year. A light that saves one ship's life is not — a lesser light than one ten thousand ships see.'

$userName held those words long in the heart. In the wind at the top of the lighthouse. Until then $userName had thought the light meant little, the watchers being few. But $celebName's words were different. Even if the watcher was only one, if that light was everything to that one — it was no small light at all.

chapters.heading: Beside the Beam
chapters.body:
The two of them stood long at the top of the lighthouse that night.

$celebName told $userName of the seas $celebName sailed. One sea's currents savage, another's fog frequent. Crossing those rough seas, how it eased the heart to see, far off, a lighthouse beam.

$userName told a story too. The years lived as a keeper, the days of not seeing a soul except the once-a-year supply ship, how lighting the beam at the same hour every day was lonely and yet, being the work, could not be set down.

$celebName listened quietly. Then said,

'Keeper. Coming to this island, I learned one thing. That a lighthouse beam is lonely — because the one who lights it is lonely. Ships see only the light and not the one who lights it. But today, I saw that one. The one who stands beside the beam.'

At that, $userName lost the words a moment.

That night, while the beam shone over the sea, the two of them were beside that light, side by side. A night with someone near who saw the one lighting the light. For $userName, it was the first.

That night $userName spent, for the first time, a night with someone other than oneself in the keeper's quarters. At the top of the lighthouse the flame burned, and in the quarters below there was one guest. $userName's island, for one night, was not empty.

chapters.heading: Next Year's Promise
chapters.body:
The next morning, $celebName made ready to leave.

The sea had calmed. Overnight the fog had lifted too. $celebName's small boat waited by the island to set out.

$userName came down to the jetty to see $celebName off.

'...Will you come again,' $userName asked.

'I will,' $celebName said. 'This time next year, I pass this sea again. Then again — I'll come having seen your light.'

Boarding the boat, $celebName asked one thing.

'Keeper. Just one thing. That light — please don't let it go out. When I cross this sea next year, let it be lit there. I see that light and — I know you are still on this island. The light is a sign that you are there. When the light is lit, I am at ease.'

At that request, $userName slowly nodded.

$celebName's boat left. Became a small dot and vanished over the sea.

$userName was alone again.

But from that day, $userName lit the beam differently.

Until then $userName had lit it 'for any ship at all.' A light lit by regulation, a light lit by duty. But now $userName began to light it for one person.

Lighting the beam each night, $userName thought. $celebName will see this light next year. Crossing this rough sea, will rely on this one light. So this light must — be clear. Through a whole year, never once go out. On a foggy night, reach far.

For that one person, $userName lit the beam all year. Wiping the lamp glass with more care, trimming the wick more closely.

chapters.heading: A Light That Shines a Year
chapters.body:
A year passed.

Through that year, $userName never missed the light a single day. On a stormy night, on a night the body ached, $userName climbed to the top and lit it. One night a fever burned, and $userName, gripping the rail, barely made it up to set the flame.

$userName was not lonely. To say it exactly — $userName was still lonely, but inside that loneliness there was a waiting.

$celebName comes once a year. For that one time, $userName bore the rest of the days. Lighting the beam each day was meaningless work no longer. Each of those lights was the work of guarding the sea $celebName would return across. Today's light might, in some fog, save $celebName's boat.

That season came again.

The sea began to roughen. Fog fell, currents turned savage. It was the time $celebName crossed this sea.

$userName watched the sea each night from the top of the lighthouse. Waiting for the light of a small boat to appear, far off. Searching long beyond the fog.

Then one night, beyond the fog, a small light showed.

A boat was crossing the rough sea. Seeing $userName's beam, steering clear of the island's rocks. Slowly, but in a clear direction.

$userName drew the beam brighter. Raised the wick a hand's width, wiped the glass once more. So $celebName's boat would cross safely.

$celebName's boat reached the island.

$celebName climbed onto the island. $userName came down from the lighthouse. The two of them stood face to face again, after a year. On that rocky jetty.

'That light,' $celebName said. 'You didn't let it go out. I saw it from far off. Even through the fog.'

'I didn't,' $userName said. 'Not one day.'

chapters.heading: The One Who Stayed on the Island
chapters.body:
That year $celebName stayed on the island a few more days.

Through those days, the two of them climbed the lighthouse together. $userName's work of lighting the beam, $celebName watched at $userName's side. How to fill the oil, trim the wick, wipe the glass.

And a few days later, leaving, $celebName said to $userName,

'Keeper. Now I — find it harder and harder to go out to far waters fishing. I'm getting older. To cross this sea once a year, even that may be the last. My body is not what it was.'

At that, $userName's heart sank. It meant that even that once-a-year meeting would soon be gone.

'So — I want to ask you one thing,' $celebName said. 'On this island, may there be one more lighthouse keeper? I know the sea well. I can learn to light the beam too. Watching at your side these few days, I think I could. I — want to stay on this island. Not to come once a year to see this light, but as one who lights it together.'

$userName looked at $celebName a long while.

Until then $userName had kept that empty island alone. Lighting the beam every day, bearing the loneliness. That loneliness, $userName had thought, was the keeper's fate.

But now, there was to be one more person on that island. The one who came once a year was to stay outright.

'...There may be,' $userName said. 'This island — has always been short of people. With one more, it's good. Very good.'

$celebName smiled slowly.

From that day, two people lived at the lighthouse on the remote island.

$userName and $celebName climbed to the top together each evening and lit the beam. One filled the oil, one trimmed the wick. One set the flame, one watched it stretch out over the sea.

That island was empty no longer. The beam was no longer a light with nowhere to land.

Sometimes $userName said to $celebName,

'I thought this light went unseen by anyone my whole life. But — one person was seeing it. The one who, once a year, crossed this rough sea relying on that one light.'

'Thanks to that light, I lived every year,' $celebName answered. 'And now — I light it, together.'

The beam of the remote island was lit every day after that. By the hands of two people.

gongmang.epilogue:
When it grows dark, $userName lights the lamps of the house one by one.

$userName tends to light them earlier, and brighter, than others. $userName does not quite know why. Only — when one light is lit in the dark, $userName is at ease. As though that light is showing the way to someone, somewhere.

$userName sometimes spends a night when it seems the heart $userName lights reaches no one. As a keeper lights a light no one sees. On such a night $userName looks at $celebName. At $celebName shining on a stage, within a screen. And then $userName feels, strangely, as though one boat crossing a rough sea has recognized that light.

There are only few ships that receive the light — not none. To the one and only ship that crosses that sea once a year — that light was everything.

To $userName, $celebName is like that 'one ship that comes having seen the light.' The one who, when all the other ships simply pass by — recognizes the light and reaches the island to give thanks. The one who keeps the light $userName lit from burning, in the end, in vain.

gongmang.celebRole: neighbor who looked in on the empty studio each day

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName looked in each day at a studio where the paintings had all sold and only bare walls remained. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole left in an empty room, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: An empty-space streak always kept $userName's studio walls clear, so only nail marks remained and no paintings. $celebName tucked one wildflower at a time into that bare wall, filling the space.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $celebName stood before the bare wall and said, "it's empty here, so there's room for the next painting to come." An empty wall had been turned into a place of waiting, so $userName lifted the brush again.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of empty-space streak can carry that vacant wall into this life. So when your heart feels hollow, $celebName might fill that empty space.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a day your heart feels empty, a new $celebName song may come into that space and stay.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a hollow evening, a $celebName work might be at your side like a painting on a bare wall.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So in an empty hour, a $celebName match clip may set a warmth into the empty day.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a hollow night, one $celebName video might quietly take a place in the empty heart.

gongmang.title: The Two Left in a Deserted Neighborhood
gongmang.logline: In a redevelopment neighborhood where everyone has left and only empty houses remain, two people, $userName and $celebName, stay to the end.

chapters.heading: A Lane With One Light Left
chapters.body:
The 2020s. A neighborhood of some city went into redevelopment.

It was an old neighborhood. Narrow lanes, aged single-story houses. Flowerpots set along the walls, a small corner shop at the end of the lane — that kind of neighborhood. The whole of it was to be torn down, and apartments raised in its place.

People left the neighborhood one by one. Took the compensation, found new homes. When one house left, that house became an empty house. Plywood nailed over the windows, a slip reading 'scheduled for demolition' stuck on the door. The flowerpots in the yards stayed and dried out.

After about half a year, the neighborhood was nearly all empty. Every lane an empty house. The corner shop had closed too. When night came, the whole neighborhood was pitch dark. There were almost no lit windows. Half the streetlamps were out.

But in that neighborhood, there were two houses that stayed to the end.

One house at this end of the lane, one at that end.

In the house at this end lived $userName. In the house at that end lived $celebName.

Each of them had reasons they could not leave. Hadn't yet found somewhere to go, or had been slow to ready themselves, or the compensation paperwork had tangled. In any case, the two of them — stayed, to the end, in that empty neighborhood everyone had left.

When night came, the lit windows in that neighborhood were exactly two. One window at this end of the lane, one at that end.

$userName watched out the window every night. At the far end of the dark neighborhood, one lit window showed. Seeing that light, $userName thought. There — one more person is staying. In this dark neighborhood, I'm not alone. That one light was, to $userName, the only thing that bore up the empty neighborhood's nights. As long as that light was lit, $userName could endure that dark lane.

chapters.heading: From One End of the Lane to the Other
chapters.body:
At first the two of them did not know each other.

Even in the days the neighborhood teemed with people, the two had lived not knowing each other. This end of the lane and that end were, even in one neighborhood, far. They had only, each night, seen each other's light from the two ends of the lane.

$userName was afraid of the empty neighborhood. When night came it was pitch dark, the empty houses eerie, not one shadow of a person in the lane. When the wind blew, something rattled in an empty house somewhere. What let $userName endure such nights was — that one light at the far end. There is someone there. In this neighborhood I am not alone. That light told $userName so.

One night, that light was out.

$userName's heart dropped. Has that house left too. Am I now the only one left in this neighborhood. $userName watched the dark far end of the lane a long while. The light never came on.

$userName barely slept that night. To think only $userName's window was lit in the dark neighborhood was hard to bear.

The next day, $userName walked to the far end of the lane. $userName wanted to check on that house. Past the empty houses, to the end of the lane.

At that house, one person was out in the yard. It was $celebName.

$celebName had not left either. Last night, $celebName had simply fallen asleep early and turned off the light.

$userName was relieved. So relieved the legs nearly gave way.

That night $userName had not slept a wink, at the thought that only $userName's light might be on in the dark neighborhood. To be truly left alone in that empty neighborhood was — the most frightening thing $userName could imagine. So the moment it was light, $userName had walked to the far end of the lane.

$userName spoke honestly.

'Last night — your light was out, so. I thought you'd left, and worried, and came to check. I didn't sleep all night. I thought I was the only one left in this neighborhood.'

$celebName looked at $userName. With a startled face for a moment.

'...You were watching my light?'

'Yes. Every night,' $userName said. 'There are two lit windows in this neighborhood. Mine, and yours. I endure looking at that second light. That in this neighborhood I'm not alone.'

$celebName was quiet a moment.

'Me too,' $celebName said. 'I watch the light at the far end of the lane every night too. Watching that light, I thought the same thing. There is one more person there. Even turning off my light early last night, I thought of it for a moment. What if someone is watching my light.'

chapters.heading: The Suppers of Two Houses
chapters.body:
After that day, the two of them sometimes ate supper together.

They took turns. Some days at $userName's house, some days at $celebName's. With one small table between them.

The suppers of the empty neighborhood were quiet. In the lane there were no people, no cars, no shop lights. The two of them ate supper in that quiet neighborhood, a small table between them. The sound of eating, of setting down spoons, rang especially clear.

One day $celebName said,

'It's strange, isn't it. When this neighborhood teemed with people — I didn't even know who lived next door. I hardly greeted anyone. Everyone was busy. There were so many people in the lane, and yet I knew not one of them. But everyone left, and the two of us alone are left, and — here we are eating together.'

$celebName looked a moment at the small table.

'What you couldn't see when there were many people, you see when there are none. That one person lives at the far end of the lane. That that person too fears this empty neighborhood. That that person too endures the nights watching a lit window. The neighborhood emptied, and so — one person came into view.'

$userName nodded.

'The neighborhood emptied, and — so there's something you see,' $userName said. 'When there are many people, there are dozens of lit windows. Then each window isn't visible. It's just the neighborhood's lights. But when only two windows are left — those two are clearly visible. Who's in that window over there, whether their light was on tonight or off.'

Every night, the two of them checked each other's light.

Before $userName went to bed, $userName looked out the window. If $celebName's light at the far end was on, $userName turned off the light at ease. That person is all right today too. Only after confirming it was not just $userName's light alone in the dark neighborhood did $userName let the heart rest.

$celebName did the same. Only after seeing $userName's light on at this end of the lane did $celebName turn off the light. On a night $userName's light stayed on late, $celebName too kept the light on and waited. Is that person sleepless tonight, has something happened.

It became a kind of small promise between the two of them. Not a promise in words. Only — to check each other's light once before sleeping. And to turn off one's own light only after seeing the other's lit. So the two of them watched over each other's nights, from the two ends of the lane.

chapters.heading: The Demolition Date
chapters.body:
Around autumn, the demolition date for that neighborhood was set.

Notices went up here and there in the neighborhood. The two houses left also had to leave before that date.

$userName and $celebName each began to ready themselves to leave. Packed their things, looked into where to go.

But the two of them knew. When they left the neighborhood — the two of them would part. The places to go were different. $userName to this city, $celebName to that. The empty neighborhood had bound the two of them to one lane, and when that neighborhood was gone — what bound them would be gone too.

A few nights before the demolition, the two of them ate a last supper at $userName's house.

'When the neighborhood is gone,' $celebName said, the table between them. 'We'll part too, then.'

$userName could not answer a long while. Spoon still in hand.

Only then did $userName clearly know. That what had let $userName endure was not the empty neighborhood itself. The light at the far end of the lane — that one person's light. Without that light, the empty neighborhood's nights could not have been borne.

'$celebName,' $userName said. 'I — found this neighborhood's emptiness bearable. Because your light was there. But when the neighborhood is gone, and your light too — then wherever I go, it'll all feel like an empty neighborhood. Even a neighborhood teeming with people, because your light won't be there.'

$userName looked at $celebName.

'So — I have a favor. Let's not part. Wherever we go, couldn't we go together. Then whatever lane I live in, there'll be — two lit windows. The light I check before sleeping will be there.'

$celebName looked at $userName a long while.

And slowly, nodded.

chapters.heading: Two Lights on the Last Night
chapters.body:
The night before the demolition came.

It was the neighborhood's last night. The next day, the neighborhood would be gone. The lanes, the empty houses, the houses the two of them lived in, the dried flowerpots on the walls.

$userName and $celebName decided to spend that last night each at their own house. They would leave together the next day, but that last night at least — each at their own house, for the last time. In the very way the two of them had watched each other's light from the two ends of the lane.

$userName stood at the window late that night.

The dark neighborhood. The lit windows in it were still exactly two. $userName's window at this end of the lane, $celebName's at that end.

$userName watched that second light a long while.

For half a year, $userName had watched that light every night. When the empty neighborhood was frightening, that one light had let $userName endure. There is someone there. In this neighborhood I am not alone. Even when the wind rattled an empty house, with that light it was bearable.

That night, $userName kept the house light on late on purpose. $userName knew $celebName would be watching that light too. Because it was the last night, $userName wanted to keep it lit longer.

And the light at the far end of the lane stayed lit late that night too. Far later than usual.

That last night, the two of them — with each other's lights kept on — said a farewell from afar. With the two lights at the ends of the lane. There was not a word, but the two of them knew it was a farewell. They had greeted each other so every night for half a year. Checking each other's light before sleeping had been the two people's greeting.

$userName stood long at the window that last night. The next day the neighborhood would be gone, and the two lights at the ends of the lane gone too. But the two of them had decided not to part. That was the answer $userName had reached at the end of those dark nights.

The next day, the neighborhood was torn down.

The two of them left the neighborhood together.

chapters.heading: Two Lights
chapters.body:
The two of them, leaving the demolished neighborhood behind, settled in a new neighborhood.

This time it was one house. Not the two ends of a lane, but under one roof.

That new neighborhood teemed with people. At night, dozens of lit windows showed. In the lane there were people, cars, shop lights.

But $userName sometimes recalled that empty neighborhood. The neighborhood with exactly two lit windows.

One night $userName said to $celebName,

'Because that neighborhood was empty — I recognized your light,' $userName said. 'In a neighborhood full of people, your window would have been one among dozens of lights. I wouldn't have seen it. I'd have lived my whole life not knowing you lived there. But that neighborhood was empty — so your one light was clearly visible.'

$celebName nodded.

'Me too,' $celebName said. 'Because the neighborhood was empty — I knew. That one person was left at the far end of the lane. That that person's one light let me endure the empty neighborhood. If the neighborhood had teemed, I might never have known it. I couldn't have picked your light from dozens.'

$userName looked out the window. At the bustling new neighborhood, dozens of lit windows showing.

'There's one thing that empty neighborhood gave us,' $userName said. 'The whole neighborhood emptied, and so, we saw each other. If only one person had been left, that person would have been alone to the end — but two were left, and we became each other's light. Without that, we'd have lived our whole lives not knowing.'

Even in that big neighborhood, $userName and $celebName lit the lights under one roof together every night. The two lights they had lit from the two ends of an empty lane, now gathered into one window.

Before sleeping, the old habit still came out in $userName, once each time. The habit of checking whether $celebName was near. The habit of checking the light at the far end of the lane had become the habit of checking the one person beside them.

The two who stayed to the end in a neighborhood everyone left. In that empty lane, the two of them became each other's light. And even after the neighborhood was gone, the two of them did not part. The empty neighborhood had brought the two of them together, and the two of them kept that meeting — to the end, even after the neighborhood was gone.

gongmang.epilogue:
And now, on a pitch-dark night, $userName sometimes looks out the window.

$userName does not quite know why. Only — if even one lit window shows somewhere, $userName is at ease. Someone is awake there; on this night I am not alone.

A day when it feels everyone is leaving comes to $userName too. A night when the people near empty one by one, and it seems only $userName's one light is left at the end of the lane. On such a night $userName looks at $celebName. At $celebName on a stage, within a screen. And then $userName feels, strangely, as though one light is coming on at the far end of a dark neighborhood.

The neighborhood must empty for that one light to be clearly visible. There is one person left there. In this empty neighborhood I am not alone.

To $userName, $celebName is like that 'light at the end of the lane.' The one who, even when everyone leaves — stays there, in that place, with the light kept on, to the end. The one who keeps $userName's empty neighborhood from being pitch dark in the end.

gongmang.celebRole: traveler who sought the same room each day

paragraphs.gi: In $era, in an inn full of nothing but empty rooms, $celebName always sought the same room of that inn. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole keeping an inn, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: An empty-space streak always kept $userName's inn clear, so even with the lanterns lit no guest came. $celebName opened a window in each empty room and said, "let at least the wind fill it."
paragraphs.jeon: One night $celebName took one empty room for a long stay and promised, "this empty space — I'll keep it." Someone had come to stay in the room that had stood empty, so for the first time $userName lit the lanterns bright.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of empty-space streak can reach that vacant room into this life. So even lonely at dawn, you might feel $celebName filling the space.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a sleepless dawn, a $celebName song may stay on in the empty room.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a quiet night, a $celebName drama might keep watch over the empty seat.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So in an hour you're alone, a $celebName match clip may stay on like a room next door.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a hollow dawn, a video $celebName left behind might become the empty room's light.

gongmang.title: The Traveler Who Always Sought the Same Room
gongmang.logline: An innkeeper, $userName, who keeps a remote inn no guests come to, and $celebName, the one and only traveler who always sought the same room.

chapters.heading: An Inn With No Guests
chapters.body:
Late Joseon. At a junction of the road into the mountains, there stood a small inn.

It was a place small even to call an inn. Three rooms, one wood-floored hall, one kitchen. A well in a corner of the yard, and a lantern hung from the eaves. That was all of the small inn.

$userName was the keeper of that inn.

Once, that inn had no end of guests. People going into the mountains, people coming out, peddlers, travelers — all passed that junction. On market days the hall filled with guests.

But one year, a new road was cut over the mountain. A wider, faster road. People all traveled that new road. The old road where $userName's inn stood — emptied.

Guests stopped abruptly. Barely one in several days. On some days, not a soul came through the yard the whole day.

$userName kept that empty inn alone. Swept the yard each day, wiped the rooms clean, kept a fire in the kitchen. Even with no guests coming. The lantern at the eaves $userName lit each evening too — in case someone passing the dim old road might see it and stop.

Others said to $userName. Why keep an inn with no guests so. Better to close it and move to the new junction. At the new junction guests would overflow.

$userName did not. That inn was an inn $userName's parents, and their parents, had kept. $userName could not leave it. That hall, that well, that lantern at the eaves — to $userName, all of it was a thing that could not be left.

So $userName kept the empty inn clean every day. Believing someone might, perhaps, step onto that old road.

chapters.heading: The Guest of the Third Room
chapters.body:
Then one day, onto that old road came a traveler.

It was $celebName.

$celebName came into $userName's inn yard with a bundle on the back. Came having seen the lantern at the eaves, $celebName said.

$userName, glad of a guest after days, went out at once to greet them.

'Welcome. I'll set out a room for you. Any room you like is empty.'

$celebName glanced once over the three rooms, then chose the third. The one at the end of the yard, the innermost room.

That night, $celebName stayed in that room. $userName set out a clean supper table. Setting a guest's table after long, $userName's hands were busier than usual.

The next morning, $celebName left.

$userName was alone again.

But a few days later, $celebName came again.

$celebName was one who came and went from those mountains. For some matter, $celebName traveled often between beyond the mountain and the mountain village — but unlike the others, $celebName did not take the new road. Always the old road, past $userName's inn.

And $celebName always sought the same room. The third room. The inner room at the end of the yard.

Once $userName asked. 'The other rooms are empty too, yet you always seek that one. It isn't that the others are smaller.'

$celebName answered.

'I like that room,' $celebName said. 'It's at the end of the yard, so it's quiet — and the window looks onto the mountain. To open the eyes in the morning and see the mountain is good. And more than anything, that room is always clean. With no guests, I can feel that the keeper wipes it every day. The floor has a sheen, and the bedding smells of sunlight. To step into such a room, the weariness of wandering the road — eases a little.'

$celebName glanced a moment at the yard.

'To one who wanders the road, keeper, the room to stay in is not all the same. Some rooms are just a room to sleep one night and leave. Some rooms are — rooms where, stepping in, the heart settles. This third room is the latter. So I always seek this room. The new road has many fast inns, but a room where the heart settles like this — there is none.'

chapters.heading: The Room Kept Empty
chapters.body:
After that day, $userName kept the third room — always empty.

If another traveler came and sought that room, $userName quietly guided them to a different one. 'The third room is a regular guest's room,' $userName said.

The truth was, $celebName had never once asked to have the room held. Still $userName kept it empty. $celebName might come at any time, so it had to be always kept empty.

$userName wiped that room every day. With more care than the other rooms. Opened the window to let in the mountain wind, dried the bedding in the sun, wiped the floor to a sheen. Wiped even the dust off the window frame.

When $celebName would come, $userName did not know. Sometimes after a few days, sometimes after a fortnight. Still $userName kept that room ready — so $celebName could stay at once, whenever $celebName came.

$userName loved that work.

Keeping an empty inn was lonely. Sweeping the yard, wiping the rooms each day, with no guest to fill them. $userName sometimes felt these rooms $userName wiped were — like care with nowhere to land. Wiping every day a room no one would stay in.

But the third room was different. Wiping that room, $userName thought of one person — of $celebName, who would stay in it. The one person who would step into this clean room, open the window onto the mountain, set down the bundle, and rest easy.

And then wiping that room was lonely no longer. It was care for one person. Care that certainly had somewhere to land.

Others looked at $userName strangely. To keep one room always empty and wipe it every day, in an inn where barely one guest in days came through. 'Just take another guest into that room,' they said.

$userName did not answer. $userName knew. That room was not a room for taking other guests. That room was one person's room, a room kept empty so that person could step into it at once whenever they came. Wiping that room was not trade. It was the work of waiting for one person.

chapters.heading: The Window Onto the Mountain
chapters.body:
When $celebName stayed at that inn, the two of them sometimes spoke briefly.

After the supper table was cleared, when $celebName sat a while on the wood-floored hall with the third room's window left open — $userName brought out a cup of tea. The two of them sat on that hall and watched the mountain go dim.

$celebName told $userName the things seen on the road. The look of the village beyond the mountain, the people met on the way, how the mountain path changed with the seasons. $userName listened quietly. $userName had hardly ever left that inn, so $celebName's stories were the outside world itself.

$userName, too, sometimes told the inn's old stories. The days guests had no end, the days the hall filled on market days. The story of $userName's parents, and their parents, keeping that inn.

Once $celebName said,

'It must be hard for you to leave this inn, keeper. It's an inn your parents kept.'

'It is hard,' $userName said. 'Even with the guests gone, I cannot leave this inn. This hall, that well, that lantern at the eaves — all of it is a thing that cannot be left.'

$celebName heard that, and glanced a moment around the inn. The dim yard, and the lantern at the eaves. And said nothing. Only — looked at that inn a little longer.

chapters.heading: Footsteps Grown Late
chapters.body:
So a little over a year passed.

$celebName kept traveling that old road, and always sought the third room. $userName kept that room always empty, and wiped it clean. The two of them, with that room between them, became those who traded a small greeting.

But one season, $celebName's footsteps grew rare.

A fortnight passed and $celebName did not come. A month passed and $celebName did not come.

$userName still wiped the third room every day. But wiping it, the heart grew, little by little, heavier. Has something happened to $celebName. Was $celebName waylaid on the mountain path. Has $celebName begun traveling another road. Will $celebName never come again.

$userName wiped that room with more care. As though, keeping the room clean, $celebName might come back. Opened the window, dried the bedding in the sun. Every day.

In the second month, one day, $celebName stepped into the yard.

It was a much-thinned face. The bundle was light too. $celebName had been gravely ill in that time, $celebName said. Had lain sick two months, at the mountain-village home.

$celebName stood in the yard and looked at the third room.

'...Keeper,' $celebName said. 'Did you keep that room empty all that time. Even though I did not come for two months.'

'I kept it empty,' $userName said. 'I wiped it every day.'

$celebName looked at that room a long while. Not once stayed in for two months, and yet the room was still clean. The window wiped, the bedding smelling of sunlight. As though a guest had stayed only yesterday.

'Keeper,' $celebName said. 'Through the two months I lay sick, there was one thing I kept thinking of. This inn's third room. That room — it felt as though it would be waiting for me. That window onto the mountain, it felt, would always be wiped. Thinking that, I wanted to rise from the sickbed. Because I wanted to step into that room again. That thought raised me up.'

chapters.heading: Staying at the Inn
chapters.body:
$celebName rested deeply in the third room that day.

Stayed a few days, mending the thinned body. $userName set out clean tables, and brewed medicine too from herbs dug in the mountain. Through those few days, $celebName sat before the window onto the mountain and slowly regained strength.

A few days later, making ready to leave, $celebName stopped and said to $userName,

'Keeper. I'd like to ask you one thing.'

'Yes. Please.'

'I do the work of coming and going from the mountains. But — that work grows harder and harder for me now. After two months of illness, more so. I've come to want to stop the wandering and stay in one place. I want to set the bundle down.'

$celebName looked around the empty inn. The yard, the hall, the lantern at the eaves.

'Keeper. At this inn — may there be one more person? I work well. I can sweep the yard, lend a hand in the kitchen. I can draw water from the well too. It's all right if there are no guests. I only — wish to be at this inn, not as the traveler of the third room, but as one who stays.'

$userName looked at $celebName a long while.

Until then $userName had kept that empty inn alone. Wiping a guestless inn every day, bearing the loneliness. That loneliness, $userName had thought, was the fate of one who kept an old junction.

But now, there was to be one more person at that inn. The traveler who always sought only the third room was to stay outright.

'...There may be,' $userName said. 'This inn — has always been short of people. With one more, it's good.'

$celebName smiled slowly.

From that day, $celebName stayed in the third room. No longer a traveler, but as one who lived at that inn. $celebName swept the yard each day, drew water from the well, lent a hand in the kitchen. And in the evening, lit the lantern at the eaves.

chapters.heading: The Two People's Inn
chapters.body:
The years passed.

At that remote inn, two people lived after that. $userName and $celebName.

The inn still had guests rarely. Those who traveled the new road still did not come to the old.

Still, that inn was empty no longer. The two of them swept the yard together, wiped the rooms together, kindled the kitchen fire together. The lantern at the eaves the two of them lit by turns.

The third room was still clean. Now that room was $celebName's room. $userName still wiped it with care, and $celebName did not leave it. The window onto the mountain, too, was still wiped every day.

Sometimes $userName said to $celebName,

'I thought I would keep this inn lonely all my life,' $userName said. 'Wiping a guestless inn every day. Pouring out care with nowhere to land. But — one person always came by that old road. Past the new road. And always sought the same room. Because of that one person, I kept this inn without loneliness.'

'I liked that old road,' $celebName answered. 'The new road is fast — but on that road, there was never a clean third room. No window onto the mountain either. I came back to the old road, always, for that one room. Because a room that waits for me was better than a fast road. And now — I stay in that room.'

The remote inn. That empty inn with its guests gone, the two of them kept together. In the room one person always kept empty, one person, in the end, stayed.

gongmang.epilogue:
When $userName is waiting for someone, $userName, without meaning to, tidies the place.

Sets the cushion straight, wipes the dust once, smooths things so the light falls well. Makes ready, in advance, a clean place for that person to come in and sit at ease. $userName does not know where the habit came from. Only — keeping a place empty and wiped for one person is, to $userName, strangely, a thing that settles the heart.

$userName sometimes spends a day when it feels $userName's care has nowhere to land. Like a keeper wiping a room with no guest to stay in. On such a day $userName looks at $celebName. At $celebName on a stage, within a screen. And then $userName feels, strangely, as though one traveler is walking onto an empty old road.

Even when everyone else takes the fast new road, one person always came by that old road. And always sought the same room. Because of that one person, the room kept empty was not, in the end, empty in vain.

To $userName, $celebName is like that 'traveler who always sought the same room.' The one who, even when there is a fast road — turns back onto the old road on purpose, to fill the place $userName kept empty. The one who keeps $userName's care from scattering, in the end, with nowhere to land.

gongmang.celebRole: lighting hand who kept the last light on

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $userName stood alone on a stage the audience had left, and $celebName kept one last light from going out. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who stayed on the empty stage, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: An empty-space streak always kept $userName's stage clear, so neither applause nor cheers reached it. $celebName turned the light toward the empty seats and said, "one person here is watching."
paragraphs.jeon: One night $celebName kept the empty seats through the last song and clapped alone to the end. A sound had returned for the first time to the empty stage, so $userName stood still a long while up there.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of empty-space streak can reach that vacant stage into this life. So when your heart feels empty, $celebName might fill it from your side.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So on a night that feels hollow, a $celebName song may light your heart like a stage lamp.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So on a wistful evening, a $celebName work might sit beside you in the next seat.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So in an empty hour, a $celebName match clip may keep you quiet company in that time.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So on a still night, one $celebName video might keep watch at your side.

gongmang.era: a small halt about to be taken off the line, the 1980s
gongmang.title: The Station Where the Last Train Stopped
gongmang.logline: A stationmaster, $userName, who keeps a small halt about to be closed, and $celebName, the one and only passenger who got off at that station to the end.

chapters.heading: A Station Where Trains Stop Twice a Day
chapters.body:
The 1980s. In a mountain valley, there was one small halt.

It was a place small even to call a station. One station house built of wood, one short platform. At the end of the platform, a small flowerbed. In the ticket office there was one stationmaster. That was $userName.

At that halt, trains stopped twice a day. Once in the morning, once in the evening. Slow trains. The fast trains did not stop there; they passed on by.

Once, a fair number of people had got off at that station. The people going into the mountain village all got off there. But as the people of the mountain village left, one by one, for the city, the people getting off there grew fewer and fewer. The village itself shrank.

$userName met the trains each day at the empty platform. When the morning train stopped, on many days no one got off. The evening train was the same. The train would stop a moment and leave again, having taken no one and let no one off.

Still $userName went out and stood on the platform each time. Raised the flag, met the train, sent it off. Even with no one getting off. That was the stationmaster's work, $userName believed. Whether anyone watched or not, $userName raised the flag in that place each time.

$userName kept that station clean. Wiped the station-house floor, swept the platform's fallen leaves, planted flowers in the bed. That small station almost no one saw. There was hardly anyone to see the flowerbed's flowers either, but $userName planted them all the same.

Word went round upstairs that the line would soon be removed. With almost no one boarding or alighting, it would be closed.

$userName, even hearing that news, kept the station clean every day. A station soon to be gone, $userName did not want to leave neglected. $userName wanted to keep it, to the last, a clean station.

chapters.heading: The One Person on the Evening Train
chapters.body:
At that empty station, there was one person who got off every day.

It was $celebName.

$celebName always took the evening train and got off at that station. $celebName lived in the mountain village. $celebName commuted to the city for work — took the train from that station to the city each morning, and came home each evening on that slow train.

When the evening train stopped at that small halt, $celebName got off. Mostly $celebName alone. On many days there were no other passengers. Onto the empty platform, one person, $celebName, stepped down.

$userName met $celebName on the platform every evening.

At first they were only stationmaster and passenger. But meeting just the two of them, evening after evening, on an empty platform — the two naturally came to exchange greetings.

'Hard work today,' $userName said, lowering the flag, and
'You too, stationmaster,' $celebName answered.

Then the two began to talk briefly. In the short space of $celebName getting off the train and leaving the station house.

'It rained in the city today.' 'Flowers have bloomed in this station's bed. Did you plant them, stationmaster.' 'The evening train was a little late. You must have waited.'

$userName waited for that short talk every day. As the hour of the evening train drew near, $userName went out to the platform and watched the far track. Waiting for the train that would bring that one person.

Keeping the empty station was lonely. $userName saw almost no one the whole day. Through the long midday between the morning train and the evening, $userName was always alone. But when evening came, the train brought one person. $userName filled the end of a lonely day with the short greeting traded with that one person.

And $userName tended the flowerbed with more care, for that one person. After $celebName said 'flowers have bloomed,' the bed was no longer a bed with no one to see it.

chapters.heading: The Flowers of the Bed
chapters.body:
The two people's short greeting grew, season by season, a little longer.

When spring came, $userName planted new flowers in the bed at the platform's end. $celebName saw them, getting off the evening train.

'You've planted new flowers in the bed.'

'Yes. Because it's spring,' $userName said.

'At this station — there aren't many to see those flowers,' $celebName said. Not a rebuke, only a thing asked out of curiosity.

$userName looked a moment at the bed.

'There aren't many,' $userName said. 'But there is one. The one person who gets off at this station every evening. If that one person sees them, I have a reason to plant flowers.'

At that, $celebName lost the words a moment. And then, from that day, watched the flowerbed more closely. Which flower had bloomed, which had faded. Each evening $celebName looked at that bed and said a word to $userName. 'That yellow flower has bloomed.' 'It rained, so the bed is damp.'

$userName heard those single words every day. The flowerbed of the empty station, thanks to that one person's eyes — was no longer a bed with no one to see it.

Keeping the empty station was lonely. But inside that loneliness was that short meeting of each evening. $userName filled the end of the day with it, and bore another day.

chapters.heading: News of the Line's Closure
chapters.body:
Around autumn, the closure of that line was confirmed.

A date was set. On that day the last train would stop at the station, and after that the line would close forever. The station would close its doors with it.

$userName, hearing that news, thought first of one person.

It was $celebName.

When the line was gone, $celebName could not come home on that train. Then $celebName might — have to leave the mountain village. That train was $celebName's only way of commuting to the city. From the mountain village to the city, any other way was too far.

That weighed on $userName. More than the station being gone, that $celebName would lose somewhere to go weighed on $userName.

That evening, $celebName got off the train. $userName, lowering the flag, told $celebName the news of the closure.

'...This line is — soon to be removed,' $userName said. 'A date has been set.'

$celebName was quiet a moment. $celebName looked around the empty platform. The short platform, and the bed at its end.

'So this station is — gone too.'

'Yes. Once the last train has stopped.'

$celebName looked a moment at the flowers in the bed. The flowers $userName had planted.

'Stationmaster,' $celebName said. 'I got off at this station every evening. It was an empty platform — but the stationmaster always stood there with the flag raised. I — liked that, every day. Worn down all day in the city, I'd get off at this station, and someone met me with a raised flag. In the city no one meets me so.'

$celebName looked at $userName.

'When this station is gone, I won't see that flag. That — is what I'll miss most.'

chapters.heading: The Last Train
chapters.body:
The day of the closure drew near.

$userName kept that station clean every day until then. Wiped the floor, swept the fallen leaves, tended the bed's flowers. Even a station soon to be gone. Even a bed soon to be torn out.

Others said to $userName. Why wipe a station soon to be gone so. Why water a bed soon to be dug up.

$userName answered. 'I want to send it off clean, to the last. Because there is one person who gets off at this station to the end. I want the platform that person steps onto to be clean until the last day.'

At last the day of the last train came.

That evening, the last slow train came into that small halt. A whistle sounded far off, and the train drew slowly onto the platform.

$userName stood on the platform. With the flag raised for the last time.

The train stopped. The doors opened.

$celebName got off. The last passenger of the last train, of that station.

$celebName stepped onto the platform and stood a long while. Taking in the small station, the flowerbed, and $userName with the flag raised. Slowly, as if to keep them in the eyes.

The train left again. The line's last train vanished beyond the empty track. The whistle grew distant.

Now, at that station, a train would never stop again.

$userName lowered the flag. For the last time.

'...This is the last, then,' $userName said.

$celebName came beside $userName. Watching the empty track together.

'Stationmaster,' $celebName said. 'I'd like to ask one thing. When this station is gone — where will you go.'

$userName was quiet a moment. 'I — don't know yet. Move to another station, or. Nothing is decided.'

$celebName took up $userName's words.

'Then — would you not come with me.'

chapters.heading: The Night of the Closed Station
chapters.body:
At $celebName's words, $userName looked at $celebName.

'I,' $celebName said. 'When this line is gone, I mean to leave the mountain village. The way to the city is gone now. I'll go somewhere near the city and make a new start.'

$celebName looked at the empty track. The place the last train had vanished.

'But stationmaster. Getting off at this station each evening was — the best moment of my day. Seeing you standing on the empty platform with the flag raised. When I saw that flag, I felt I had come all the way home. The city's weariness ended there. So that flag was — to me, the sign that I had come home.'

$celebName looked straight at $userName.

'When this station is gone, I lose that sign. So — would you not come with me. Then wherever I go to live, each evening — I won't lose that sign of coming home. Because at whatever station I get off, you'll be there.'

$userName stood on the empty platform of that closed station and looked at $celebName a long while.

Until then $userName had kept that station lonely. Raising the flag every day on an empty platform. Meeting and sending off trains no one got off. That loneliness, $userName had thought, was the fate of one who kept a small halt.

But what had let $userName bear that empty time was — the one and only person who got off at that station each evening. The short greeting traded with that one person had filled the end of a lonely day.

That one person was saying, come with me.

'...I'll go,' $userName said. 'I'll go with you.'

$celebName smiled slowly.

That night, the two of them sat long on the platform of that closed station. Watching the empty track the last train had left. Watching the bed at the platform's end. Spending the last night at that station, together.

chapters.heading: The Flag of the New Station
chapters.body:
The two of them, leaving the closed station behind, moved to a neighborhood near the city.

$userName worked again as a stationmaster, at another station of that neighborhood. It was a large station. Trains stopped dozens of times a day, and many people got off. The platform was long, and there was a wide plaza instead of a flowerbed.

That large station's platform always teemed with people. Each time a train stopped, dozens poured off at once.

But $userName sometimes recalled that small halt. The station where trains stopped twice a day, where almost no one got off.

One evening $userName said to $celebName,

'Because that halt was empty,' $userName said. 'I recognized you every evening. At a large station, you'd have been one among dozens. I wouldn't have seen you. I'd have spent my whole life never noticing you. But that station was empty — so you, the one who got off each evening, were clearly visible.'

$celebName nodded.

'Me too,' $celebName said. 'On the empty platform of that small station, you, the one with the flag raised, were clearly visible. At a large station, I might never have noticed that flag my whole life. The station being empty — that's how the two of us saw each other.'

Even at that large station, $userName raised the flag every evening.

On the platform where dozens got off, $userName always searched for one person: $celebName, coming home on the evening train. And when $celebName got off, $userName waved the flag slightly toward that one person. Out of the dozens, to that one alone. It was a greeting only the two of them knew. The greeting begun on the empty platform of a small halt.

$userName no longer thought the flag $userName raised was in vain. Raised on an empty platform or a teeming one — that flag was, in the end, a flag for one person. If that one person saw the flag and felt they had come all the way home, the flag had not been waved in vain.

The halt about to be taken off the line. On that empty platform, the two of them became each other's one and only. And at the new station too, that flag was always waved for one person.

gongmang.epilogue:
On an evening when $userName is waiting for someone, $userName, without meaning to, raises a hand.

When that person shows far off, $userName's hand waves a little. Though no one bid it. Like a person who, long ago, raised a flag on an empty platform to meet one person.

There comes to $userName too a day when it feels the place $userName keeps faces an emptiness. An evening when the wave of $userName's hand, $userName's heart — seems to scatter toward where no one is.

At such times $userName looks at $celebName. At $celebName shining on a stage, within a screen. And then $userName feels, strangely, as though an evening train is bringing one person to an empty platform. Like that one person who recognized, from far off, the flag-raising self.

The station must empty for that one person to be clearly visible. The one and only passenger who gets off each evening. Because of that one person, the empty station's flag was not, in the end, waved in vain.

To $userName, $celebName is like that 'one person who got off the last train.' The one who, even when everyone else leaves — gets off at that station each evening and recognizes the flag-raising self. The one who keeps the place $userName keeps from being empty in the end.

hap.celebRole: regular who picked only the same book

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName always picked the same book of poems from $userName's shop. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole copying books to sell through the night, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak of accord can tie two tastes onto one line, so $celebName loved the very lines $userName loved. Each time $celebName bought a book, they dotted a small mark by a favorite line and returned it.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $userName read along those dots and found them placed exactly where their own heart had been. Tastes had overlapped though no word was spoken, so $userName couldn't bring themselves to sell that book.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of meshing streak can carry that feeling into this life. So whatever $celebName likes, you might seem to know it in advance.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So listening to a playlist $celebName shared, the songs you already loved may stand lined up.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So a work $celebName recommends, you might know is your taste before even watching.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So a match style $celebName is said to like turns out, oddly, to be the one you enjoy watching.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So a video $celebName makes, even the edit rhythm, may be the pace you've always liked.

hap.era: 8th-century Chang'an, a lane thick with the scent of books
hap.title: The Two Who Chose the Same Book
hap.logline: In a used-bookshop lane of Chang'an, a friendship for life begins from a hand laid on the last copy together.

chapters.heading: The Last Copy
chapters.body:
