The first time $userName met that person was at the shop at the end of the used-bookshop lane in Chang'an.

It was an afternoon when summer had just begun. The lane was thick with the smell of aged paper. Book spines bleached by the sun packed every stall, and the owners dozed, fanning themselves. Cicadas cried. That cry pressed the hot air of the lane heavier still.

$userName walked that lane every day. There was no money to buy. It was simply good to look. To walk slowly between the stalls, reading the letters on the spines one by one — to $userName, that was the best hour of the day. A cost-free luxury allowed to the child of a poor house.

That day, $userName was searching for one book. A Journey Through Cloud Mountains. The writing of some poet who had wandered the mountains of the frontier. The teacher always said: only after reading that book does one's writing open. A person who has never seen a mountain cannot write a mountain, and a person who cannot write a mountain cannot write people either.

$userName ransacked every shop in the lane. It was nowhere. At one shop $userName heard a snort. 'You think a book like that is so common.' The last shop, the dimmest corner. There $userName saw it.

Exactly one copy was left. The spine worn, the corners frayed — but unmistakably, that book.

$userName reached out a hand. Breath held, slowly. And then onto that book, another hand touched down with it.

Two hands gripped the same spine and stopped. $userName's hand, and a stranger's hand. For a long while neither moved.

$userName lifted the head. A person of about the same age stood there. Sleeves stained with ink. Tangled hair. A forehead damp with sweat. The look of them was no less shabby than $userName's. But the eyes alone were clear. Those eyes looked at $userName, looked at the book, and looked at $userName again.

'...You don't mean to let go.'

$userName spoke first. The person broke into a small grin. Grinning, and yet the hand did not come off the book.

'I don't. You don't look like you do either.'

'...I don't.'

Neither let go of the book. The shop owner woke from dozing and looked from one to the other. Fanned once, and said, as if it were a bother, 'There's only the one copy. Settle it yourselves. Fight, or play odds and evens.' Then dozed off again.

$userName and that person stood facing each other, the book between them. Neither let go. A cicada in the lane cried again. Through that cry, $userName calculated in the head. Take it by force. Or plead. Or —

Then the person said,

'How about this. Half a day you read, half a day I read. Until it's finished. There's one book, but there are two of us. We can't tear a book in half — so we halve the time instead.'

$userName stood blank a moment, hearing that. It was the first time $userName had seen a person who would not fight over one book. More exactly — the first time $userName had seen a person not bent on winning. Every person $userName had met on the streets of Chang'an was always bent on winning something.

$userName slowly nodded. That was $celebName. The one who, in the used-bookshop lane of Chang'an, had laid a hand on the same book.

That day the two sat side by side on the bench before the shop, one book opened between them. When $userName read a page, $celebName read the next. When a good passage came, without either being first, they read it aloud. Until the sun slanted low. When the book was finished, the two knew each other's names. And they knew, too, at which passage the other had held a breath.

chapters.heading: Words Written in the Margins
chapters.body:
$userName thought it would end with that one copy. It did not.

The next day $celebName came to the lane again. A book in hand. 'This — I thought you'd like it.' It was a worn collection of poems. $userName took it and opened it. In every margin of the pages, fine letters were written. Thoughts that had come to $celebName while reading. Beside one passage, 'this is sad'; beside another, 'this poet is lying.'

$userName found reading the margins better than the body of the text. The body was the words of a dead poet, but the margins were the living words of $celebName.

From that day the two traded books. $userName read and wrote in the margins. $celebName took it and wrote answers in those margins. Within one book, the two conversed across paper. Even on days they did not meet, when a page was turned, the other was there. When $userName wrote 'this passage is good,' a few days later $celebName's hand had come below it. 'I like the next passage better. Why did you stop there.'

'$userName.' One day $celebName said it on the bench, the book on the knee. 'Your writing — it's a little strange.'

$userName flared up. 'What's strange.'

'Too careful. Every single letter is writing afraid of being wrong. You're clearly thinking of something rougher. I saw it in the margins. The words you wrote and then crossed out. That's your real writing.'

$userName fell silent. No one had ever said that. Not even the teacher. The teacher praised $userName's writing as 'orderly.' $userName had liked that praise. But $celebName saw what $userName had hidden behind the orderliness. The self $userName had pretended, even to oneself, not to know.

'$celebName's writing is strange too,' $userName shot back after a long while. 'Too rough. Pours it all out and never tidies it. You clearly know something more orderly. The sentences you scrawled in the margins and then rewrote, neat and careful. That's your real writing.'

$celebName looked at $userName a long while. Sunlight came aslant over the bench. Then $celebName laughed. For the first time, aloud and large. Loud enough to wake the dozing shopkeeper of the lane.

'We're a matched pair.'

$userName laughed too. The words were right. $userName's caution and $celebName's roughness, within one book, meshed strangely well. Where one fell short, the other filled it exactly. When $celebName's hand touched $userName's writing, it grew bold; when $userName's hand touched $celebName's writing, it grew orderly.

That day the two made a promise. To write together. The writing of two people, one writing and one revising. They did not hook little fingers on the bench. They only nodded, one book between them. That was enough.

chapters.heading: The Civil Examination
chapters.body:
Autumn came. The cicadas of the lane stopped, and instead the sound of wind turning pages was heard. And in Chang'an, the notice for the civil examination went up.

$userName had no thought of sitting it. What could the child of a poor house do with a single brush — there was a line $userName had drawn for oneself. If $userName failed, $userName had no confidence to bear that failing. So $userName stopped before the line entirely. If you do not try, there is no failure.

But $celebName caught $userName's sleeve before that notice.

'Sit it together.'

'...I can't.'

'Why.'

$userName could not answer. $userName could give a hundred reasons why not, but $userName knew, too, that the hundred were really one. $userName was afraid. That was all of it.

$celebName looked straight into $userName's eyes. 'I remember everything you wrote in the margins. Even what you crossed out. That is not writing that fails. You just don't see it because you don't trust yourself. Before you even sit at the desk, you already act like a person who has failed.'

$userName's chest grew hot. It was not anger. It was that no one had ever believed in $userName that far, and the belief was so unfamiliar that the chest grew hot. Unfamiliar warmth, sometimes, could not be told apart from pain.

From that day the two prepared the examination together. In a back room of $celebName's house, one oil lamp between them, they wrote every night. The room was narrow. When the two sat facing each other, their knees touched. When $userName wrote a piece, $celebName revised it in red ink. 'Here, you shrank too much. Open it.' When $celebName wrote a piece, $userName trimmed it. 'Here, you spread too wide. Gather it.'

The two people's writing began, little by little, to look like one person's. Writing rough and orderly, bold and careful. As the back-room nights deepened, $userName felt the rough thing inside, little by little, come out. It was not frightening. $celebName was beside. Even if $userName was wrong, $celebName would fix it in red ink.

The night before the examination, the lamp oil had nearly run out. $celebName, watching the guttering flame, said,

'$userName. If only one of us passes, that one writes for the other's share too. Understand. The one who passes does not feel ashamed of the one who fails. And the one who fails does not hate the one who passes.'

$userName nodded. 'If only one passes.'

'If both pass.' $celebName laughed. 'Then we become the two loudest people in Chang'an. The two of us going around together, making the world a little louder with our writing.'

The lamp flared up one last time. The two people's shadows on the narrow wall overlapped into one and parted, overlapped and parted. $userName never forgot that night for the rest of life. The poorest, and the richest, of nights. With nothing but two brushes and one person, and yet it felt like having the whole world.

chapters.heading: The Day the List Went Up
chapters.body:
On the day of the examination, the two sat side by side in the same hall.

When $userName lifted the brush, the hand trembled. The air of the hall was heavy. From every direction came the breathing of other candidates, the sound of brushes moving. $userName's mind began to go white. The moment $userName had feared all life long had come.

Then $userName glanced at the next seat. $celebName was there. $celebName did not tremble. Spine straight, slowly wetting the brush. Seeing that firm profile, the trembling left $userName's hand.

$userName wrote exactly as in the back room. As though $celebName were watching from beside. Each time $userName wrote a sentence and faltered, $userName heard $celebName's voice inside. *Here, you shrank too much. Open it.* $userName opened. For the first time in life, $userName opened oneself fully, on the examination paper.

The day the list went up, the lane filled with people. The list of those who passed was written on a great sheet and posted on a wall. $userName rose on tiptoe in the crowd. Searched for the name. From top to bottom, with trembling eyes.

$celebName's name was there. And quite near the top.

$userName laughed. Laughed as if it were $userName's own. Truly, it was a greater joy than passing oneself. And kept searching for the name. $userName's own name — was not there. Not at the top, not in the middle. Look as $userName might, it was not there.

$userName's laughter slowly settled. Still it's all right, $userName thought. We promised. If only one passes, that one writes for both. $celebName passed, so it's enough. The one who fails would not hate the one who passes.

$userName, trying not to hate, bit a lip and turned away.

Then someone gripped $userName's shoulder roughly and turned it back. It was $celebName.

'Why do you stop there. Look to the end.'

$celebName took $userName's arm and pulled $userName back before the list. Pushing through people. And pointed a finger at the bottom of the list, the very last line. There, $userName's name was. Dead last. The final place, barely held. But unmistakably, there.

$userName nearly sank down on the spot. The legs gave way. $celebName caught $userName.

'You saw it.' $celebName's voice trembled. Laughing, and yet there was water in the eyes. 'You meant not to look to the end. You'd decided from the start your name couldn't be there. Because you don't trust yourself, you nearly went home not knowing you'd passed.'

$celebName pointed at the list again. 'We both passed, $userName. We've become the two loudest people in Chang'an.'

$userName pulled $celebName into an embrace. In the middle of the crowd. The two stayed that way a long while. Let anyone watch. Somewhere in the used-bookshop lane, a cicada out of season cried that day too.

chapters.heading: The Two People's Writing
chapters.body:
The years passed.

$userName and $celebName made their names in Chang'an with writing. People called the two a pair. When one's writing seemed too rough, the other's hand touched it and it grew orderly; when too careful, the other's hand touched it and it grew bold. The two people's writing was good read apart, but only read together was it complete. People could not tell the two apart. Where $userName ended and $celebName began.

The path of office was not smooth. Both spoke straight words, and straight words always earned someone's hatred. When $userName was demoted to the frontier, $celebName said the same fate would be followed.

'There's no need for you to fall too,' $userName tried to dissuade. 'You stay here, and walk your own road.'

$celebName snorted. 'Who said anything about falling. I just mean to be beside you. Our writing is complete only with the two of us. If you go to the frontier alone, your writing will shrink again. That I can't watch.'

And so the two went together, all the way to the mountains of the frontier. The mountain of that very book that had first bound the two — A Journey Through Cloud Mountains. The frontier mountain where that poet had wandered. $userName and $celebName walked that mountain together. The road the poet walked, a hundred years on, two people side by side. When one recited a verse, the other took up the next. Just as they had on the bench of the lane, dividing one book.

At the mountain's summit $celebName drew a book from a bundle. A new book. An empty book. Nothing written on the cover, every page blank.

'This is the book we fill,' $celebName said. 'One page you, one page me. Until it's full. The day we first met, I said it, didn't I. Half a day you read, half a day I read. This time it isn't reading but writing. One page you write, one page I write.'

$userName laughed. It was exactly the words that person had said in the used-bookshop lane that first day. There is one book but two of us, so we halve the time.

$userName lifted the brush and wrote on the first page. *We chose the same book.* And passed the brush to $celebName. $celebName wrote below it. *And so for all our lives, we walked the same road.*

The mountain wind turned the pages. The blank leaves fluttered, awaiting the next words. One page, then another. The two stayed long on that mountain. One book between them, just as the day they first met. Only this time, the book was one the two were filling themselves.

hap.epilogue:
And now, $userName is standing before one shelf in a bookshop.

$userName reaches a hand toward one book. In that moment, $userName always feels a strange déjà vu. As though another hand will touch the same book from beside. Lift the head, and there, the face $userName loves — $celebName is within a screen, on a stage. Though they have never once met, $userName's fingertips always choose the same thing.

Each time $celebName puts out a song, a film, a piece of writing, $userName reads it the way one reads margins. Where a rough expression meets an orderly one, the lamplight of a back room a hundred years ago flickers. $userName feels as though even the words $celebName scrawled and then crossed out are visible. With that old habit of loving the margins more than the body of the text.

Now $userName cannot revise $celebName's writing in red ink. Cannot press knees together in a back room. But $userName takes in and reads every single thing $celebName puts out, missing not one. As they did on the bench a hundred years ago — half a day $celebName, half a day $userName. There is one book but two of us, so we halve the time.

And so the two still choose the same book. As the kind of pair where one writes, and one reads to the very end.

hap.celebRole: poet who used the same cafe corner

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName wrote poems in the cafe corner, watching $userName. Back then $userName tended to be a poor $userRole painting by the window, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak of accord can tie two together, so when $celebName put $userName's painted scenes into verse, they matched, oddly well. Each time $celebName finished a poem, they wrote it on the back of $userName's painting like a letter.
paragraphs.jeon: One day the cafe owner hung the two works side by side, and customers thought one person had made them. Made apart, the touch had overlapped, so $userName and $celebName looked at each other and laughed.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of meshing streak can reach that rhythm into this life. So with $celebName the everyday might come together naturally.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So a stage concept $celebName likes may always be the very stage you'd treasured most.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So a work $celebName names as a lifetime favorite turns out, oddly, the same as yours.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So a match $celebName calls a classic was the one you'd been rewatching.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So the works $celebName says they draw inspiration from may almost overlap your own taste list.

hap.era: Hongdae 2003, the summer of a basement practice room
hap.title: The Start of Our Band
hap.logline: Two people who could not keep time, in a Hongdae basement practice room, end up on the same stage in the end.

chapters.heading: The Noise From Next Door
chapters.body:
The first time $userName met that person was in the corridor of a Hongdae basement practice room.

It was the summer of 2003. The basement of the practice-room building was always damp. One fluorescent light flickered as if about to die, and the corridor mixed cigarette smoke, amp heat, and someone's instant-noodle smell. On the walls, torn show posters of broken-up bands were layered thick. That basement was a place where people just starting and people already finished lingered together.

$userName rented the room at the end of that corridor, alone. Five thousand won an hour. An hour scraped together from a convenience-store night shift. $userName so grudged that one hour that, once inside, $userName did not even go to the toilet.

$userName played guitar. Was writing a song. The melody was clearly there in the head. Vivid enough to surface even from sleep. But the hand kept losing it. What was in the head and what came out of the hand were different. That was so frustrating that $userName played the same bar a hundred times. A hundred times, and the song in the head still did not come down to the fingertips.

Then from the room next door came the sound of drums.

It was precise. So precise it grated. That faltering rhythm of $userName's, the drum next door pounded out without an inch of error. When $userName stopped, next door stopped. When $userName started again, next door started again. As though someone beyond the wall were mimicking $userName's clumsy hand in mockery.

$userName set down the guitar and went out to the corridor. Angry. Threw open the next door.

'Hey. Are you doing that on purpose?'

One person sat at the drums. A sweat-soaked T-shirt. A stick in one hand, that person looked at $userName. A face not even very startled.

'Doing what.'

'You're copying my rhythm. Mocking me?'

The person broke into a grin. 'I wasn't copying. You keep getting it wrong, so I was showing you where the right beat is. Each time you dropped a beat, I left a beat in that spot. So you could find your way back.'

$userName was dumbfounded. Was about to get angrier. But the person tapped the rim of the drum lightly with a stick and said,

'Your melody's good. I heard all of it through the wall. Really good. But the rhythm can't keep up with it. The song's losing out. That good melody keeps tripping over your rhythm.'

$userName fell silent. The words were precise enough to make $userName angry. A problem $userName had failed to solve for months, a person who had only heard it through one wall pinned in a single sentence.

That was $celebName. The one who, in the next room of a Hongdae basement practice room, did not drop a single beat of $userName's rhythm.

chapters.heading: Just One Hour
chapters.body:
'Just one hour. Let's play together.'

When $celebName said that, $userName meant to refuse. It was a song $userName worked on alone. Not finished enough to show anyone. $userName did not want a clumsy thing found out. $userName was always so. Until something was finished, it was shown to no one. And because of that, nothing ever got finished.

But $celebName had already shifted the grip on the sticks.

'Let's go to your room. From the top.'

$userName's room was narrow. With a guitar amp and drums both in, the two had to sit almost pressed together. Close enough for knees to touch. $userName slung on the guitar. The hands sweated.

'Three, four.' $userName did the count. But from the very first bar the rhythm slipped. $userName's hand could not follow the count.

$celebName stopped the sticks. 'Don't count.'

'If I don't count, how am I supposed to—'

'Just play. Exactly as it is in your head. The count isn't the song you really want to play; it's a fence you built so you wouldn't get it wrong. Inside that fence, your song doesn't come out.'

'...Then the rhythm will fall apart completely.'

'I'll hold the rhythm. You, just the melody. Wherever you go, I'll follow.'

$userName played the guitar again. Without counting. Followed the melody in the head, whether the hand lost it or not. And then a strange thing happened. When $userName faltered, $celebName's drum waited. When $userName suddenly sped up, the drum sped up too. $celebName did not fit $userName into a set rhythm — $celebName moved the rhythm itself to wherever $userName went.

The song, for the first time, flowed all the way through. Never once breaking.

The last note rang and the room went quiet. $userName stood blank, the guitar still slung on. The song that had been only in the head for months had just now truly existed in the air and vanished. The hands trembled.

'...Just now.' $userName barely managed to speak. 'Did you hear that just now?'

'I heard it.' $celebName set the sticks on a knee. Laughed with a sweat-soaked face. 'I said your song was good. To leave it alone is a waste. You need someone to hold the rhythm. You're the one who makes the melody, and the rhythm — someone else can hold it. It didn't work because you tried to do it all alone.'

$userName looked at $celebName. '...You want to do it? The rhythm.'

$celebName spun a stick between the fingers. 'I meant to do just one hour. Let's rent two more. I'll pay. I can just skip supper tonight.'

chapters.heading: A Band Without a Name
chapters.body:
That whole summer the two lived in that basement room.

When $userName brought a song, $celebName clothed it in rhythm. When $celebName made a rhythm, $userName laid melody over it. The two fought often. $userName wanted the song to stay exactly as in the head. Did not want to yield a single note. $celebName wanted a structure that would not collapse when an audience heard it. The song in the head and the song on stage are different things, $celebName said. There were days they fought an hour over one bar. The voices grew loud enough that the room next door pounded the wall.

But after a fight, the song was always better. Where $userName's stubbornness and $celebName's stubbornness collided, something neither had thought of came out.

'What should we name our band.' One day $celebName asked it, eating a cup of instant noodles. Sitting on the floor.

'It's not a band. What band, with two people.'

'Two people isn't a band?' $celebName lifted the chopsticks. 'There's a drum, a guitar, a song. It's a band. The number of people doesn't make a band.'

$userName did not answer. The truth was, $userName was afraid. The moment you put the name 'band' on it, this becomes real. And once it's real, it can fail. An unnamed jam was just a hobby. There was nothing to fail. $userName was always a person who stopped before that line. Just before the naming.

$celebName caught it. Having finished the noodles, $celebName set down the empty cup and said,

'$userName. If you'd been alone, these songs would never have come out of that room. They'd have rotted in your head. You're afraid of starting. You try not to show until it's finished, but then it never gets finished. It's by showing that it gets finished.'

$celebName pushed the empty cup aside.

'I'm the opposite. I'm good at starting but can't see the end. Every band I made alone broke up within two months. Started all excited, then ran when it got hard. If it's the two of us—' $celebName looked straight at $userName. 'I start, and you hold us so we go to the end. The start you're afraid of, I do; the end I can't bear, you keep. That's why we're a matched pair.'

$userName was quiet a long while. Under the flickering fluorescent light. Then said, in a small voice,

'...For the name. Let's go with Next Door.'

$celebName burst out laughing. 'Next Door?'

'At first you were next door, weren't you. Holding my rhythm beyond the wall. You said each time I dropped a beat, you left a beat in that spot. So I could find my way back.'

$celebName looked at $userName a long while, then slowly nodded. With a serious face, the laughter gone. 'All right. Next Door.' That day the two people's band got a name. It became real.

chapters.heading: The First Stage
chapters.body:
In autumn, a first stage was set at a small Hongdae club.

The audience was about twenty. Most had come to see other bands. The people gathered before the stage looked as if they had no interest in who Next Door was. Backstage, $userName's hands trembled. $userName wanted to run. It was the moment in twenty years of life $userName most wanted to run from. $userName seriously considered vanishing on the excuse of going to the toilet.

$celebName saw that hand. Saw that $userName was trembling. Without a word, $celebName pressed one drumstick into $userName's trembling hand.

'Hold this. If it's heavy, you don't tremble.'

It made no sense. How heavy could one stick be. But strangely, gripping the stick tight, the trembling eased a little. With something to hold in the hand, it was as though the heart too had something to hold.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. In the dark backstage. 'It's all right to be wrong on stage. If you drop a beat, I'll leave a beat in that spot. Like the first day we met. You just find your way back to that beat. The audience won't know. You just play like the two of us play in that basement room. Don't look at the audience, look at me. As if it's only the two of us in that room.'

They went up on stage. The lights were hot. The eyes of twenty felt heavy. When $userName took the first chord, $userName really did drop a beat. The heart fell to under the feet. $userName thought it was over.

But in that moment, $celebName's drum filled that empty spot exactly. As though that beat had been there from the start. So natural that no one knew $userName had been wrong. The one beat $userName dropped became part of the song.

$userName looked at $celebName. $celebName tapped a stick and nodded. It's all right. You've found your way back. Let's go.

$userName forgot the audience. Forgot the lights. Played exactly as the two played in the basement room. $celebName held $userName up with rhythm, and $userName filled $celebName with melody. One song ended. The next. And the next. $userName heard the song from the head truly flowing, on stage.

When the last song ended, the twenty in the audience clapped. Not a loud applause. The fair applause of people who had come to see other bands. But to $userName, it was the loudest sound in the world.

Coming down from the stage, $userName said to $celebName, '...I didn't run.'

$celebName wiped sweat and laughed. 'I know. I saw. You didn't run, you went to the end. Even after dropping a beat, you found your way back.'

chapters.heading: The Beat Beyond the Wall
chapters.body:
Next Door went on a few more years after that.

It was not a band that made it big. It went around small clubs, pressed one album itself and sold it at venue doors. There were days not a single copy sold. $userName and $celebName still fought often, and after a fight the song was better. They were poor, but $userName later thought those few years were the best of life.

The last stage was that small club where they had first stood. Both knew. That this was the last. $celebName had to go far, very far, for a family matter. $userName could not hold $celebName back. $userName knew, too, that not holding back was for $celebName's sake. So $userName, for the first time, practiced not holding on to something.

Before the last song, $celebName took the mic. $celebName almost never took the mic. $celebName was always the one who just held the rhythm from behind.

'This person.' $celebName pointed at $userName. 'When I first met them, they dropped the beat a hundred times in the next room. I heard all of it through the wall. But the melody was good. So good that I wanted to hold the rhythm for them. That's why our band is called Next Door. A band that started across one wall.'

$celebName looked at $userName. Before an audience of about twenty. Like the first stage.

'$userName. Even if I go far, your rhythm keeps going. Even with a wall, the rhythm crosses over. We matched our rhythm across one wall at first, didn't we. Distance growing doesn't mean it can't be heard. Even when I'm not in the next room, you can go to the end alone now. I've seen it. You've become a person who doesn't run.'

They played the last song. $userName played guitar crying, and $celebName played drums laughing. The two people's rhythm meshed as one body for the last time. When $userName dropped a beat $celebName filled it, when $celebName left a space $userName filled it. Exactly as it had been in that first basement room.

When the song ended, $celebName threw one stick to $userName. $userName caught it.

'If it's heavy, you don't tremble.' $celebName said that last, on the stage. 'If you ever stand on a stage again, hold that. Then I'm in the next room. Beyond one wall, holding the rhythm for you.'

$userName kept that stick for life. Each time something was frightening, $userName gripped that stick. And the trembling eased. As though someone were holding the rhythm beyond a wall.

hap.epilogue:
Somewhere in a drawer of $userName's desk, there is one worn drumstick.

$userName does not know why it is there. Only — when something is frightening, $userName's hand reaches for that stick on its own. That holding something heavy eases the trembling — $userName does not find that reasonless habit strange.

Put in the earphones, and $celebName's voice, the rhythm $celebName made, flows out. $userName is a person who hears rhythm unusually well. At which bar of a song $celebName left a beat empty, at which spot $celebName waits for the next person — like a person who once heard that rhythm beyond one wall, $userName's ear knows it first.

They cannot stand on the same stage. $userName cannot back $celebName with drums from beside. Still, each time $celebName puts out a new song, $userName fits the heart to that rhythm. Breathes a beat in the space $celebName left, speeds up when $celebName speeds up. And if $celebName should ever drop a beat, $userName quietly leaves the heart's beat in that spot. To find the way back. As $celebName did for $userName the first day they met.

Even with a wall, the rhythm crosses over. Distance growing doesn't mean it can't be heard. And so the two still keep time, next room to next room.

hap.celebRole: drummer who kept the beat alongside

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName beat the drum beside $userName, keeping the rhythm. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole drawing the strings and singing on the street, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak of accord can tie two rhythms into one, so even a piece never rehearsed together fell right into place. $celebName left the drum a beat empty just before $userName changed a note, each time.
paragraphs.jeon: One market day the two improvised a new piece, and the market crowd thought it rehearsed and clapped. Hands had matched though nothing was promised, so $userName remembered that drumbeat long after.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of meshing streak can carry that tune into this life. So with $celebName the timing might match even without words.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So when you imagine the song $celebName will sing next, it may always be the one you wanted to hear.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So when you picture the role $celebName takes next, it might overlap your wish, oddly well.

hap.era: the voyage of the deep-space survey ship Yeobaek
hap.title: The Two Who Watched the Same Star
hap.logline: In the cramped observation room of a survey ship, two people who looked up at the same star become lifelong companions.

chapters.heading: The Intruder in the Observation Room
chapters.body:
The first time $userName met that person was in the observation room of the survey ship Yeobaek.

The Yeobaek was a small ship. A ship where twelve of an outer-planet survey crew had to live three years. The corridors were narrow enough for one person to barely pass, and a private cabin was a cell that ended once a bed went in. Inside the ship there was always the low sound of machinery turning. That sound grated for the first month, and after that was no longer heard. People grew used to anything. To narrowness, to distance.

Still the ship had one place that was wide. The observation room. The whole ceiling was a transparent dome, so lying there, you saw the universe whole. Stars on every side. No up, no down, just countless lights set in a black ground.

Every night — the ship had no real night, but the lights-out hour was called that — $userName went to the observation room. Alone. $userName watched one star. A bluish star afloat at the far end of the route. The survey crew's destination. Still too far to be more than a dot, but unmistakably unlike the other stars. Watching that star settled $userName's heart. It was a comfort that three years of voyage were narrowing toward that one dot.

That day, someone was in the observation room first.

One person lay under the dome. In $userName's spot. In the same posture. Arm for a pillow, facing the ceiling. And — $userName knew only on coming close — watching the same star. That blue dot at the route's end.

$userName halted. The observation room was a place like $userName's secret. On a ship where twelve lived together, that hour and that spot alone were $userName's. $userName had never imagined anyone would be there.

That person felt the presence and turned the head. Looked at $userName. For a moment neither spoke. Then that person shifted the spot where they lay a hand's width to the side. Silently. As if to say, lie down too.

'...Watching the same star?' the person asked.

$userName hesitated, then answered. 'The one at the end of the route.'

'The blue one.' The person looked at the ceiling again. 'I watch it too. Every day. At the hour everyone else sleeps.'

$userName hesitated a moment. Then lay down in the hand's width that had been cleared. The two lay side by side and watched the same star. Said nothing. The silence of the universe filled beyond the dome. Still it was not awkward. When you are watching the same thing, even without speaking it is not awkward.

That was $celebName. The one who, in the observation room of the Yeobaek, watched the same star as $userName.

chapters.heading: The Watch Handover
chapters.body:
The Yeobaek's voyage was long and monotonous. The survey crew stood watch in pairs. When the roster was redrawn, $userName's pair became $celebName.

A watch was twelve hours. The two shut in a cramped control room, looking and looking at the instrument panel. In space almost nothing happened. So the watch was long. At first it was awkward. Neither was a person of many words. But $userName soon came to know. With $celebName, those long twelve hours were strangely short.

$userName was a meticulous person. Checked every figure three times, looked even below the decimal. Did not let a small anomaly pass. $celebName was the opposite. $celebName had no interest in figures one by one. Instead $celebName watched the whole ship. When a small vibration somewhere differed from usual, $celebName said, before even looking at the instruments, 'somewhere in the left thruster.' And the words were always right.

'$userName looks at the trees, and I look at the forest.' $celebName said it on one watch. The panel lights faintly lit the two faces. 'That's why our pair has fewer accidents than the others. With only one of us, there's something we can't see, but with two, it's all visible. While you count each leaf, I watch whether the forest is on fire.'

$userName thought long about those words. They were right. The large flow $userName missed, $celebName saw, and the small signal $celebName let slip, $userName caught. The two looked at different places. So joined, there was no place unseen.

Even in off-watch hours the two met in the observation room. Watched the same star and talked. $userName told of family left on Earth. A house with a small yard, the tree planted there. $celebName told of things imagined about that blue star never once seen. Would there be sea, mountains, a sky for someone like us to look up at.

'When we reach that star.' $celebName said it. '$userName, what will you do first.'

'Measure the air,' $userName answered. Without an instant's hesitation.

$celebName laughed. 'Of course. Looking at the trees. You'd measure first whether the ground for your feet is safe.'

'And you?'

'I'll look at the sky.' $celebName looked at the stars on the ceiling. 'From that star, how our star — Earth — looks. Whether Earth is a small dot there too. That's what I'm curious about. I'm always a person more curious about what's overhead than underfoot.'

chapters.heading: Communications Cut
chapters.body:
In the voyage's second year, an accident happened.

A cluster of micro-meteoroids scraped past the Yeobaek's outer hull. It was not great damage. The ship continued the voyage soundly. But of all places, the communications device with Earth was in that spot. One meteoroid smashed that device exactly. Communications cut. The Yeobaek became, in the middle of the universe, completely alone.

Unease spread through the crew. Through three years of voyage, Earth's voice was the only thread. Each day at a set hour, the short signal Earth control sent. With that, everyone endured. With that sense — we are connected. And it was cut.

$userName threw everything at repairing the device. Traced the damaged circuits, one strand at a time. Stayed up days at it. But the figures gave no answer. $userName found which part of the circuit had died, but the means to revive it was not on the ship. $userName began, little by little, to crumble. It felt like $userName's fault. The guilt that, when the meteoroid cluster approached, if $userName had watched the figures more closely it might have been avoided. $userName could not shake that guilt.

$celebName took $userName, in that state, to the observation room. Almost dragging.

'$userName. Stop looking at the circuits and lie down here.'

'I have to revive the comms—'

'Lie down.'

$userName lay down. On the ceiling was that blue star. Far larger than two years before. Still, at the route's end, waiting for the two.

'$userName.' $celebName lay beside and spoke. 'Communications being cut doesn't mean we've lost our way. Think it through. Earth didn't show us the way. Earth only told us, you're going well. The way was, from the start, shown by that star at the end. Because we aren't going toward Earth — we're going toward that star.'

$userName looked at the star. $celebName's words seeped slowly in. It was true. The destination was not the Earth at our backs, but that star before our eyes.

'$userName, you watch the circuits. That's what you do best. Watch them to the end. But when the circuits give no answer, lift your head. Then I'll watch the forest. That's why we became a pair, isn't it. When one is blocked, the other sees.'

That night — the lights-out hour — the two drew up a new navigation in the observation room. $celebName took the large direction from the positions of the stars. On the line joining which star and which star did the blue star lie. $userName moved that direction into precise figures. Into angle and speed and time. Forest and trees met. The Yeobaek went on toward the star, even without Earth's voice.

chapters.heading: Arrival
chapters.body:
In the voyage's third year, the Yeobaek entered the orbit of that star.

The blue star was a dot no longer. It rose, filling the window, a true planet. Whorls of cloud were visible. Deep markings that looked like continents were visible. The twelve of the crew gathered in the observation room and watched it in silence. Someone wept. They were people who had run three years toward one dot.

The landing craft held only two. The survey leader named $userName and $celebName. 'The pair with the fewest accidents. The pair that watches the trees and the forest both. Let those two go.'

The landing craft descended into the star's atmosphere. It shook. Roughly. The atmosphere's resistance struck the hull. $userName watched the panel. Descent angle, speed, hull temperature. Every figure. $celebName watched out the window. The flow of cloud, the color of the air, the bending of light. $userName held the attitude with figures, and $celebName, by instinct, dodged dangerous currents. 'More to the right.' When $celebName said it, $userName turned the figures. The landing craft set down on the ground of the blue star. Lightly. As if it had always set down there.

The door opened. Strange air pushed in. The two stepped together onto the ground of that star. Ground a human foot touched for the first time. The soil underfoot was like Earth's, and yet not.

$userName measured the air, as promised. Spread the equipment, read the figures with trembling hands. Once. Twice. Three times. Breathable. $userName's hand stopped. Unable to believe it, measured once more. Still, breathable.

And $celebName — as promised — lifted the head and looked at the sky.

That star's sky was a paler blue than Earth's. And in one corner of that sky, one small blue dot floated. It was Earth. Beyond the distance run for three years, the star the two had left.

'$userName.' $celebName pointed at that dot. The voice trembled. 'There. The place where we watched the same star has become a star itself. As we watched a blue dot in the observation room, now Earth is the blue dot.'

$userName lifted the head past the helmet and looked at that dot. For three years $userName had watched a blue star on the observation-room ceiling, and now $userName stood on that star and watched Earth. The direction of watching had flipped whole. But the one watching together was still beside.

The two stood side by side on the ground of a strange star. As they had in the observation room at first, watching the same sky without a word. One confirming the ground underfoot was safe, one finding the home overhead.

chapters.heading: The Route Home
chapters.body:
The crew stayed a year on that star, then boarded the Yeobaek again and turned for Earth.

The route home was three years too. $userName and $celebName were still a pair, still watched stars in the observation room. Only this time the star they watched was the blue dot at the route's end — Earth. A dot as blue and as small as the star they had watched on the way out.

On one watch $celebName said it. Under the panel lights.

'$userName. When we reach Earth, we'll part, won't we. The crew disbands, and each goes back to their own place.'

$userName spoke without taking the eyes off the panel. '...Probably.'

'Still.' $celebName turned the chair to look at $userName. 'We watched the same star, didn't we. Two of them. One blue star at the route's end, and one Earth seen from there. People who have watched the same star — wherever they are, end up watching the same thing. Even apart, the night sky is one.'

Only then did $userName lift the head. $celebName was smiling.

'Even if $userName is watching a tree in some yard on Earth, I'll be somewhere watching the forest that yard belongs to. Even apart, we'd still be watching one scene. One up close, one from far. A matched pair, even parted, watches the same place.'

$userName chewed long on those words. From the time they first watched the same star in the observation room, the two had always been so. $userName measured a star's brightness, $celebName watched the place the star sat in. The way of watching differed, but the star watched was the same. Parting could not change that.

'$celebName.' $userName said it. 'Let's make one promise. Even after we land on Earth and go back to our own places — when night comes, let's look up at the sky once. At the same hour. Whatever city you're in, whatever village I'm in.'

$celebName nodded. 'Then every night, we'll be watching the same star again. Without an observation room.'

'Without an observation room.' $userName repeated it. 'Even if the ceiling isn't a dome, the sky is everyone's ceiling.'

The day the Yeobaek entered Earth's orbit, the two lay in the observation room one last time. Earth rose, filling the ceiling. A blue, round, living star. No longer a dot, but grown large enough to hold the two whole.

$userName said, '$celebName. Of all the stars we've watched, this one is the prettiest.'

'I know.' $celebName, beside $userName, watched the same star. 'Because $userName is watching it together beside me. Watched alone, it's just a star; watched together, it becomes a scene. That's why it's the prettiest.'

The Yeobaek descended slowly into Earth's embrace. The two who had watched the same star, the same star welcomed back. The two parted on that star, but until the moment of parting they were looking up at the same sky.

hap.epilogue:
When night comes, $userName sometimes, for no reason, looks up at the sky.

A few stars show between the city lights. Watching those stars, $userName strangely feels not alone. The feeling that, at the same hour, somewhere, someone is watching the same star. $userName has never made that promise. And yet somewhere in the heart, a promise remains — to look up at the sky once every night.

To $userName, that someone is $celebName. Within a screen, on a stage, the person $userName always looks up at. When $celebName draws a large picture and goes far, $userName reckons even the small figures of each step. When $celebName watches the forest, $userName counts the trees of that forest — like the two on watch, dividing the forest and the tree.

They cannot board the same survey ship. They cannot lie side by side in an observation room. Still, toward whatever star $celebName turns, $userName lifts the head in the same direction. Even if the ceiling isn't a dome, the sky is everyone's ceiling. The two, even apart, still watch one scene.

People who have watched the same star, wherever they are, end up watching the same thing. The night sky was, from the start, one.

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName served tea to guests beside $userName. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole cooking in a teahouse, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak of accord can tie two together, so the tea $celebName chose always suited the dish $userName made. When a new tea arrived, $celebName wrote down a snack to pair with it and handed it to $userName first.
paragraphs.jeon: One day a guest asked, "who sets this place's menu?" — and both pointed to the other. Chosen apart, it had matched like one person's skill, so $userName felt quietly pleased.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of suiting streak can carry that touch into this life. So together with $celebName the everyday might run well.

modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So a next project $celebName picks might match exactly the genre you wanted to watch.

hap.era: a Song-era jianghu, an old roadside inn
hap.title: The Two Swords of One Tune
hap.logline: Two swordsmen who hummed the same tune at a roadside inn become friends walking the same road.

chapters.heading: The Old Inn
chapters.body:
The first time $userName met that person was at an old inn standing at a junction of the jianghu.

It was an evening of falling rain. The inn was half collapsed. Water leaked from the eaves, the lamp swayed in the wind. The floorboards creaked at every step, and the smell of liquor mixed with the smell of mold. Still, it was the only shelter from the rain. $userName wiped a wet sword with a cloth and sat in the darkest corner.

$userName was a wandering swordsman. Ten years roaming the jianghu, but not one place to rest the heart. Those who used the sword were mostly one of two kinds. They coveted name, or they coveted money. The famous grew hard guarding the worth of their names; those who chased money used the sword only as a tool of killing. $userName hated both. So $userName was always alone. Being alone is comfortable, $userName had told oneself for ten years.

Wiping the sword, $userName, without meaning to, hummed a tune. A song the mother used to sing in childhood. A song from before the sword was taken up, when $userName was still only someone's child. A slow, gentle tune ill-suited to the jianghu. $userName had never sung that song before people. Only when alone, wiping the sword, let it slip out between the lips.

Then, from the opposite corner of the inn, the same tune was heard.

The song $userName was humming, someone took up and continued. When $userName sang a verse, the other side sang the next. As though they had sung it together long. Not a beat off.

$userName stopped humming. The hand wiping the sword stopped too. Looked across the leaking inn. One person sat there. Another wanderer, a sword laid on the knee. The look of them no different from $userName's. Wet clothes, a weary face.

That person stopped the song too and looked at $userName. For a moment neither spoke. Only the rain beat the eaves. Then that person lifted a cup.

'That song.' The person said it. 'Not a song to sing in the jianghu.'

'You know it too.'

'...My mother sang it to me.' The person drank a sip. The lamp wavered over the side of that face. 'Ten years in the jianghu, and I've met the first person who knows that song. Because the people who sing it have all died before taking up the sword, or taken up the sword and forgotten it.'

$userName moved the sword-wiping hand again. But the heart would not wipe clean. That was $celebName. In a leaking inn, the only one who knew the same tune as $userName.

chapters.heading: Walking the Road Together
chapters.body:
That night the two sat facing each other at one table and drank.

When two jianghu wanderers meet, they usually size each other up. How fast the sword, who is stronger. They contend first with the eyes, probe with words. But $userName and $celebName did not. The two did not talk of the jianghu. Who they had cut and who had beaten them, what this sect and that master were like — they spoke not a word of such things.

Instead the two talked of childhood. The mother's song, the mountain of home, themselves before the sword. $userName was speaking, after long, as a person and not a swordsman. For the first time in ten years.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'Why did you take up the sword.'

$userName watched the cup a long while. '...There was something I wanted to protect. But once the sword was in hand, all there was to protect had vanished and only the sword was left. With nothing to protect, the sword grew heavy. I roamed ten years carrying a heavy sword.'

$celebName nodded. Deeply. 'Same for me. A sword with nothing to protect is only heavy. Cut anyone and it rings hollow, beat anyone and it rings hollow.'

The two were quiet a long while. The sound of rain filled that silence. Then $celebName lifted a cup.

'$userName. If the road runs the same way, let's go together. The jianghu is too long to walk alone. Ten years walking alone, I've come to know it. With a person who knows the same song, the road might grow a little shorter.'

$userName met the cup. The clear sound of cups striking rang a moment in the leaking inn. From that day the two walked the road together.

The two's swordwork differed. $userName's sword was solid. A sword that, once it took a stance, did not give way — a blocking sword. A sword like rock. $celebName's sword was fast. A flowing, never-stopping, piercing sword. A sword like water. Used apart, both had gaps. $userName was too slow, so against many the back lay open. $celebName was too light, so once the flow broke, the center was lost.

But when the two stood back to back, those gaps vanished.

While $userName blocked, $celebName pierced; while $celebName flowed, $userName held. $userName's rock became a dyke that held $celebName's water-channel, and $celebName's water wrapped and flowed around $userName's rock. Meeting a band of mountain bandits, meeting a crowd of the heterodox sects, the two, back to back, did not collapse. The jianghu folk whispered, watching the two. Those two — their swords are like one person. Two blades, but it looks like one person wielding them.

chapters.heading: The Harmony of One Tune
chapters.body:
The two had a signal.

When a fight began, one of the two hummed that song. The slow tune the mother used to sing. When $userName sang a verse, $celebName took it up, and to the rhythm of that tune the two swords were matched. When the song sped up the swords sped up, when the song slowed the swords slowed. The enemies did not know what signal it was. They merely thought the two swordsmen, before death, were idly humming a song.

One day a band surrounded the two. Famed sword-hands of the jianghu's heterodox sects. They were many. More than ten. $userName and $celebName stood back to back. In the midst of blades that ringed them like rain.

'$userName.' $celebName said it small. Through the back, the tremor of that voice carried. 'There are rather a lot today.'

'...I know.'

'Let's have a song.'

$userName hummed the first verse. $celebName took it up. The heterodox sword-hands sneered. Singing before death. Madmen. But that song was not a thing to sneer at.

To the rhythm of the tune, $userName's sword took its stance. To the same rhythm, $celebName's sword flowed. In the one beat $userName blocked one man's blade, $celebName grazed three. In the one beat $celebName pierced into the enemy's gap, $userName guarded the open back. The rhythm of the song bound the two swords into one breath. As one sang and one took up, so one blocked and one cut.

When the song had turned through one verse, the band that had ringed them was halved.

The leader of the heterodox sect cried out, falling back. 'What technique do those two use! What sorcery is in the song!'

$celebName laughed, sword in hand. Rain ran down that face. 'No technique. No sorcery. We just know the same song. Two people who know the same song breathe to the same rhythm. That's all.'

The rest of the band scattered in the end. That day the two survived. Back at the inn, drying wet clothes, $userName poured liquor.

'$celebName. Without you I'd have died today. The back was open.'

'It's the same for both of us.' $celebName lifted a cup. 'If $userName hadn't blocked, I'd have flowed and flowed and been cut somewhere. We can't do with only one. Rock alone, or water alone. With two, it becomes a river.'

chapters.heading: Something to Protect
chapters.body:
In the third year of walking the road together, the two passed a village.

That village was tormented by bandits. It was a poor village, with little to take, but the bandits took even that little. There was no light in the villagers' eyes. Being poor, the village had no money to give a jianghu swordsman. So the jianghu swordsmen simply passed such villages by. It was work that paid nothing.

$userName too, in the old days, would have passed by. A sword with nothing to protect was only heavy. Guarding someone else's village was not $userName's affair.

But $celebName stopped at the village entrance.

'$userName. That village — its song is like ours. The one the children sing.'

$userName listened. It was true. The tune of the song some children were singing at the village entrance was like the song the mother used to sing. The words differed and the melody a little, but it had clearly branched from one source. A song of another region, but a song with the same root.

$celebName looked at $userName. $userName looked at $celebName. The two knew without speaking. People who know the same song, without speaking, thought the same thing.

That day the two stayed in the village. The night the bandits came down, $userName and $celebName stood back to back at the village entrance. Humming the song. $userName blocking, $celebName cutting. To the rhythm of the tune. The bandit band fell back that night, and never came down to that village again.

The villagers asked the two to stay. They had nothing to give, but warm rice and a dry bed, those they had. $userName felt, after long — truly after long — that the sword was not heavy. There was something to protect now. A sword with something to protect, only then forgot its own weight.

That night, in a village house and no inn, under a lamp, $celebName said,

'$userName. We roamed ten years alone. Because we had nothing to protect. But now I know. What we lacked was not a strong sword. It was something to protect.'

'...It was.'

'And one more thing.' $celebName laughed. The laugh wavered in the lamplight. 'Someone to sing with. That's needed for the sword to grow light. Someone to take up a verse when you sing one. That's what was missing for ten years.'

chapters.heading: The Same Road to the End
chapters.body:
The years passed.

$userName and $celebName gained a name in the jianghu. Only, that name was not gained by a strong sword. The two chose poor villages to guard. They took no money, coveted no fame. Having driven off one village's bandits, they went to another poor village. People called the two 'the two swordsmen who sing.' The two who sing a song when they fight.

$celebName aged first. The sword grew heavy, and could no longer flow fast. The water-like sword began, slowly, to pool. One spring of some village, the day the plum blossoms bloomed, $celebName sat beneath an old tree and held the sword out to $userName.

'$userName. Take my sword now. It's giving a flowing sword to a solid sword. You, of anyone, won't wield this sword carelessly.'

$userName took that sword. The hand trembled. The hand that had not trembled even roaming alone for ten years.

'$celebName. Without you how do I stand back to back. With the back open, I'm only a slow rock again.'

$celebName laughed. Aged, but the same laugh as that first one seen at the inn.

'Even if we can't stand back to back, we can still sing together. $userName, whatever road you walk, hum that song. One verse. Then somewhere I'll be taking up the next. Because we're a pair who know the same tune. People who know the same tune take up the next verse, even far apart.'

$celebName hummed one tune for the last time. In an aged voice, slowly. The first verse of the song the mother used to sing. $userName took up the next verse. Beneath the plum tree, the two sang one song through, for the last time. As one sang and one took up. Exactly as at the inn at first.

'$userName.' When the song ended $celebName said it. 'This song brought us together. At a leaking inn. So sing this song, and we meet again. In whatever life.'

$celebName closed the eyes that spring, beneath the plum tree. $userName, two swords on the back, walked the jianghu road again. Choosing poor villages to guard. From one village to another. Having driven off one village's bandits, seeking another poor village. And each time $userName walked the road, $userName hummed that song. The song $celebName used to take up.

Strangely, when $userName sang one verse — whether the sound of wind, or of a bird, or truly someone's voice — the next verse seemed to be heard from somewhere. As it had been at the inn at first. Not a beat off.

$userName walked alone the rest of life after that, but never once felt alone. A person who knew the same tune was always taking up the next verse. Even with the back open, the song was not empty.

hap.epilogue:
$userName sometimes, without meaning to, hums a tune.

More so when listening to $celebName's song. When $celebName sings a verse, $userName's mouth takes up the next. Though they have never once sung together, like people who have sung together long. As they did the first time at a leaking inn, not a beat off.

$userName watches what $celebName flows toward. When $celebName's sword pierces fast like water, $userName wants to hold the place like rock. Even though they cannot stand back to back, cannot back the sword, cannot strike cups at the same inn. $userName only takes in and listens to every single thing $celebName puts out, and guards that side, solidly.

Rock alone will not do, and water alone will not do. With two, it becomes a river. So $userName is always ready to sing the next verse. To take it up whenever $celebName throws a verse out.

A pair who know the same tune know each other's next verse, however far apart. That slow song the mother used to sing carries the two on, across one more life.

hap.celebRole: set painter who loved the same passages

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName painted the set, listening to $userName's voice. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole reading scripts aloud to the actors, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak of accord can tie two together, so $celebName always painted the scene $userName loved most with the greatest care. $celebName slipped a small token into a corner of that scene's set each time.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $userName gathered those tokens and found them the same as the lines they'd underlined. Hearts had overlapped though not a word was matched, so $userName looked at that set painting a long while.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of overlapping streak can reach that taste into this life. So if $celebName chose it, it might always be to your liking.

hap.title: The Same Café Corner
hap.logline: In the same corner seat of a Montparnasse café, a hand that draws and a hand that writes sit facing each other.

chapters.heading: The Second Seat by the Window
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that person was in a corner seat of a café in Montparnasse.

It was the winter of 1925, Paris. The café was hazy with cigarette smoke. Painters, poets, musicians got through a day on one cup of coffee. You could order one cup and sit all day, and no one said anything. So poor artists gathered in that café. The café was warmer than a cold garret.

$userName was one of them. $userName wrote. A poor writer who had not yet published a single line. A year since leaving home for Paris, but $userName's notebook was still nearly empty.

$userName had a set seat. The second one by the window. A seat where the light fell just right, where you saw people's backs. It was good to see people's backs and not their faces. Backs did not interfere with writing. $userName sat in that seat every day and opened the notebook.

That day, someone was in that seat.

One person was drawing in $userName's seat. A small drawing board on the knee, sketching the people in the café with quick movements of the hand. The sound of pencil scratching paper was clear through the café's murmur. $userName halted a moment. The seat was taken, in effect, and strangely $userName was not angry. Because the way that person drew was so absorbed. Like a person for whom only that one board was left in the world.

$userName, with no choice, sat across. Opened the notebook. Meant to write, but not a line came. Only the next person's pencil sound was heard. Scratch, scratch. That sound strangely touched the heart. Someone was making something so freely, while $userName's notebook stood empty.

After a long while, the person lifted the head. Eyes met $userName's.

'I took your seat,' the person said. 'Sorry. This seat — the light is the best.'

'...I know. That's why it's mine.'

The person laughed. Apologetic, and yet with no intention of giving up the seat. Then turned the board and showed it to $userName. A sketch just drawn. Some writer in a café corner, a notebook open, unable to write a line — the profile of such a writer.

It was $userName.

'Your writing isn't coming, is it,' the person said. 'It's all over your face. Lifting the pen and setting it down, looking at the notebook and looking out the window. Drawing that profile, I thought — this is the profile of a person who's stuck.'

$userName looked at the sketch. Truly, there was a stuck person in it. And the person who had recognized that stuckness at a glance sat across, drawing board in hand. That was $celebName. In a Paris café corner, the one who recognized at a glance that $userName was stuck.

chapters.heading: Two Hands Facing Each Other
chapters.body:
The next day too, and the day after, $celebName was in that seat.

At first $userName tried to win the seat back. Tried going early. But $celebName was always there first. And before long the two were sitting facing each other in that corner seat. $celebName by the window, $userName across. One drew, one wrote. The two who had fought over the seat became the two who shared it.

Each time the writing stuck, $userName looked at $celebName's sketch. $celebName was fast. Did not hesitate. Drew one line, and if it seemed wrong, drew another over it. Did not erase. Left the wrong line too as part of the drawing. So $celebName's sketches always had several layers of line. Drawings where the traces of hesitation showed plainly.

'$celebName.' One day $userName asked. 'The wrong line, why don't you erase it. It would be clean if you erased it with a rubber.'

$celebName stopped the pencil. 'Where is there a wrong line. It's all part of drawing. Only when the first line is wrong do you know where the second should go. The first line shows the second line the way.' $celebName pointed at $userName's notebook. The nearly empty notebook. 'You're the opposite. Write one sentence, and if you don't like it, erase it all. So not a line remains. Because you erase the first sentence, the second sentence loses its way.'

$userName looked at the notebook. It was true. Every page was black with marks of writing and erasing. Not writing, but a notebook full only of traces of erasing.

'$userName, just write. Leave the wrong sentence too. Later, you'll see it's a bridge to the next sentence. Like the wrong lines in my sketch.'

That day, for the first time, $userName left a sentence not liked, undeleted. Forcing back the hand that reached for the rubber, $userName wrote the next sentence below it. And strangely, the next sentence came. Because the first sentence was not liked, the second came out to fix it. And the next. For the first time, one page of $userName's notebook was filled. With no marks of erasing.

$userName lifted the head. $celebName was drawing $userName. The face of a writer who had filled one page. $celebName's pencil was drawing a profile different from the day they first met.

'$celebName.' $userName said it. 'How did you know I was stuck. On the first day. At a glance like that.'

$celebName laughed. Pencil in hand. 'Because I've been stuck the same way. Once I too erased it all when the first line wasn't liked. So I spent months with an empty board. A stuck person recognizes a stuck person. Because I've been stuck in the same place.'

chapters.heading: Each Other's Work
chapters.body:
While winter turned to spring, the two became each other's work in that corner seat.

When $userName wrote, $userName read it to $celebName. When $celebName drew, $celebName showed it to $userName. $celebName knew nothing of writing. Not grammar, not meter, not which writer was great. And yet $celebName pinned exactly where $userName's sentence was blurred. Pointing a finger at one paragraph, $celebName said, 'This part, I can't see your picture. I get what it means, but it doesn't draw before my eyes.'

$userName knew nothing of drawing. Not light and shade, not composition, not which painter was great. And yet $userName pinned where $celebName's sketch was empty. 'This person — I can't hear what they're thinking. There's a face, but no heart.'

The two did not know each other's field. So they saw it better. What you cannot see from inside, the person outside saw. $userName was too deep inside the writing to see the blur of $userName's writing, and $celebName was too deep inside the drawing to see the empty spot of $celebName's drawing. The two became, for each other, an eye standing one step back.

'$userName.' One spring day $celebName said it. A day spring light came long through the window. 'Let's make a book together. My drawings in your writing.'

$userName stopped the pen. '...A book like that, no one would buy. The writing unknown, the drawing unknown. Who would buy it.'

'There you go again.' $celebName set down the pencil. 'Afraid it won't sell, you try to erase it before you even start. Like erasing a wrong sentence. Before even making the book, in your head you've already erased that book.'

$userName fell silent. $celebName held up the board. There was a new sketch on it. A café corner seat, two people facing each other. One writing, one drawing — it was $userName and $celebName. In several layers of line, the traces of hesitation left as they were.

'This is the cover of our book. I've already drawn the first page. See, it's started. $userName, now you just need to write the first sentence. Don't erase it. It's all right for the first sentence to be wrong. It'll show the second sentence the way.'

$userName looked long at that sketch. Two people facing each other. Then opened the notebook. Lifted the pen. And wrote the first sentence — without erasing. The hand reaching for the rubber, this time $userName did not need to force back.

chapters.heading: One Small Book
chapters.body:
That spring and summer, the two made a book in that corner seat.

When $userName wrote a page, $celebName drew on it. When $celebName drew a page, $userName put writing to it. Neither was first. Writing called for drawing, drawing called for writing. One sentence of $userName's brought a drawing to $celebName's hand, and one scene of $celebName's brought a sentence to $userName's hand. The two made one book in one breath.

The two were poor. They had no money to put the book to a printing house. So they made it themselves. $celebName bound the paper by hand, and $userName wrote the title on the cover with a pen. The Same Café Corner. Exactly one copy. A book one of a kind in the world.

The day they finished that book, the two sat facing each other in that corner seat, the book set between them. A small, clumsy book, bound by hand with uneven corners.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'This book — it's all right even if it doesn't sell, isn't it.'

'...It's all right.' $userName said so for the first time. Sincerely. 'Because we made it. It could have ended an empty notebook, but it became a book. That's enough.'

$celebName smiled brightly. 'See. You've changed. At first you couldn't even start, afraid it wouldn't sell. Now you say it's all right even if it doesn't.'

But that book — sold.

A publisher who frequented the café saw the book. While the two were away from the seat, the publisher opened that hand-bound book left on the table. The small book where writing and drawing flowed in one breath. The publisher waited for the two to return. And proposed to put the book out properly.

The Same Café Corner became, that year in Paris, small, but clearly, known. It was not a bestseller. But the people who read it talked of it long. People said, seeing the book, that the writing and drawing seemed to come from one person's hand. That they could not tell where the writing ended and the drawing began.

Each time $userName and $celebName heard that, they looked at each other and laughed. It was not one person's hand. It was two hands facing each other. Only, two hands grown in the same corner under the same light.

chapters.heading: A Seat Kept Empty
chapters.body:
The years passed.

$userName and $celebName made several books together. Writing and drawing were always one breath. As the books became known, the two could have had a better studio. A wide, warm, real studio. But the two worked, to the end, in that café corner seat. In the second seat by the window, where they had first sat facing each other. That seat was the two people's studio.

$celebName aged first. The hand trembled, and could no longer draw a fine line. The fast hand grew slow. One winter, $celebName drew a last sketch in that corner seat. With a trembling hand, very slowly. Drawing one line took long.

It was a drawing of that café corner seat. The second seat by the window. Two chairs. But a person sat in only one chair. $userName, writing. The chair across was empty.

'$celebName.' $userName said it, seeing that drawing. The throat tightened. 'Why did you leave one seat empty. You should have drawn yourself there.'

$celebName set the trembling hands on the knees. Setting down the pencil.

'Even without me, you'll come to this seat. Every day. When the seat across is empty then, you can think of me sitting there. A seat kept empty is not an empty seat. It is a seat where someone was. An empty chair and a chair where someone sat are different things. $userName, you'll know that difference.'

$celebName closed the eyes that winter. Like the first hand-bound book, quiet, and not clumsy.

$userName, after that, went every day to that café corner seat. The second seat by the window. $userName always took two seats. Sat in one, and kept the seat across empty. Ordered two coffees too. Drank one, and let one go cold.

$userName wrote, watching that seat kept empty. When $userName wrote one sentence, it seemed someone across moved it into a drawing. $userName no longer erased sentences. A wrong sentence too is a bridge to the next sentence. Because $celebName had taught it so. That the first line shows the second line the way.

On the cover of $userName's last book, that corner-seat drawing was set. Two chairs. One seat filled, one seat empty. The drawing with the trembling hand, the last $celebName drew. The book's title was The Same Café Corner. The same title as that book the two had first bound by hand.

hap.epilogue:
Stepping into a café, $userName always searches for the same seat.

By the window, a corner where the light falls just right. $userName sits there and keeps the chair across empty. If anyone asked, $userName could not explain the reason either. Only that empty chair simply does not feel like an empty chair. Like a chair where someone was.

The one $userName faces is $celebName. Within a screen, on a stage. When $celebName puts out something — a song or a drawing or writing or acting — $userName watches it flow in one breath, like a small book made by writing and drawing facing each other. And $userName sees, in $celebName's work, the blurred place, the empty place. What you cannot see from inside, from a seat one step back.

They cannot sit facing each other and make a book. $userName cannot put writing to a drawing. So the one thing $userName can do is this. To keep one seat empty. To make, in one corner of the heart, a seat for $celebName, and sit there to watch together what $celebName puts out.

Like that chair, one side left empty, in the last drawing $celebName drew. A seat kept empty is not an empty seat. It is a seat where someone was.

hap.celebRole: taffy seller who circled the same road

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName met $userName always at the same hour, in the same alley. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole circling the market, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak of accord can tie two paths onto one line, so in any alley the two met at the same hour. Each time they met, $celebName slipped a stick of taffy onto $userName's load.
paragraphs.jeon: One rainy day the two ran under the same eaves at the same moment, with no plan at all. Paths had overlapped though nothing was arranged, so $userName laughed amid the sound of the rain.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of meshing streak can carry those steps into this life. So with $celebName the road you take might always be the same.
