hap.era: the days of the magic academy, a hill where the Starlight Tower stands
hap.title: A Matched Magic
hap.logline: Two magics, each incomplete alone, become one for the first time in the academy pair examination.

chapters.heading: An Incomplete Magic
chapters.body:
The first time $userName met that person was beneath the Starlight Tower of the magic academy.

The Starlight Tower was an old tower standing at the top of the academy hill. When night came, a light like starlight leaked from the tower's top. Students went beneath that tower when their magic was blocked. There was a story that if you chanted a spell alone there, the tower would lend a little of its magic. Whether it was true, no one knew.

$userName was a somewhat special student at the academy. Special in a bad way. $userName's magic was always incomplete. When $userName chanted a spell, the magic circle was drawn perfectly. Not a line wrong. The light gathered too. But at the last moment, that light did not take a form and scattered. As though the vessel was made but there was nothing to hold in it. $userName's magic circle always shone, empty.

The teachers said $userName had no talent. Drawing the magic circle well does not make a mage. $userName believed those words. So $userName always, alone, beneath the Starlight Tower, drew only the magic circle. Even with nothing to hold, only practiced making the vessel. Vaguely believing that if even an empty vessel were made perfectly, someday it would be filled.

That day, someone was beneath the tower first.

One student was using magic. $userName saw that magic and stopped. That student's magic was — the exact opposite of $userName's. The light gathered enormously. Dazzlingly. The form too was intense. But there was no magic circle. That student was pouring out only the contents, with no vessel. With no vessel to hold it, that strong light lost where to go and burst in every direction. The grass beneath the tower was scorched by that light.

That student sank onto the grass, breathing hard. Each time the magic burst, it seemed all the energy was spent. Then that student looked at $userName.

'...What are you looking at.'

'There's no magic circle,' $userName said, without meaning to. 'There's that much light, but no vessel to hold it. That's why it all bursts.'

That student glared at $userName. And then, the glaring eyes wavered a little.

'...I know. The teachers have said it a hundred times. I can't draw a magic circle. My hand can't follow it. The magic circle is in my head, but moving it to my hand, it all goes wrong. So I just gather light. With no vessel.'

$userName stood still on the spot. Looked at $userName's own magic circle. A perfect vessel. A vessel with nothing to hold. And before the eyes, a person with overflowing contents and no vessel sat on the grass. That was $celebName. Beneath the Starlight Tower, the one incomplete in exactly the opposite way to $userName.

chapters.heading: The Vessel and the Light
chapters.body:
That day $userName hesitated a long while. Then spoke.

'...Just once. Let's try together.'

$celebName furrowed the brow. Still sitting on the grass. 'Try what.'

'I'll draw the magic circle. You gather the light.'

$celebName wore a look of disbelief. Magic was used alone. The academy taught it so. One person draws the magic circle, that person gathers the light, that person completes the magic. Two people using one magic was a thing in no book of the academy. $celebName had never even heard of it.

Still $celebName slowly rose. There was nothing to lose. Both were already the most hopeless students at the academy. A person at the very bottom can try anything.

$userName drew a magic circle on the ground. On the grass beneath the Starlight Tower. A perfect vessel, with not an inch of error. The magic circle $userName had practiced all life, shining empty. And nodded to $celebName.

$celebName chanted a spell. The light gathered — as always, enormously, with nowhere to go. But this time, there was a place to go.

$celebName's light flowed into $userName's magic circle. The vessel met its contents. The magic circle filled with light, and the light gained the form of the magic circle. $userName's empty vessel was, for the first time, filled. $celebName's light with nowhere to go, for the first time, found a place to go.

Beneath the Starlight Tower, a great bird of light rose. It spread wings and slowly circled the tower once. Each beat of the wings was as precise as $userName's magic circle, and the whole bird was as dazzling as $celebName's light. Neither $userName nor $celebName had ever in life seen such magic. Though it was their own magic, the two held a breath, watching it.

The bird of light soared to the top of the Starlight Tower and scattered. The two were quiet a long while. The afterglow of that light lingered on the grass.

$celebName spoke first. In a trembling voice.

'...Just now. That. Did I do it?'

'We did it,' $userName said. The voice trembled with it. 'What neither you alone nor I alone can do. Your light met my vessel, and both of us were completed for the first time.'

$celebName looked at $userName. For the first time, not with glaring eyes.

'$userName. We're a matched pair.'

chapters.heading: The Pair Examination
chapters.body:
That year a 'pair examination' was introduced to the academy for the first time.

It was an examination where two students completed one magic together. A new attempt by the academy. It was something one old mage had long argued for. That mage's thought — that magic is not used alone — became, for the first time, an examination. Most students paired with close friends, or with students of similar talent.

When $userName and $celebName came forward as a pair, the hall stirred. The two most hopeless students of the academy. The child who only made vessels, and the child who only spilled light. To anyone's eye, a pairing where both would fail.

One student sneered. Loud, as if for the other students to hear too. 'Two incompletes joined, and what do you get. A bigger incomplete. Pour light with nowhere to go into an empty vessel, and it'll just burst bigger.'

$celebName flared up and started forward. To say a word to that student. $userName caught the sleeve.

'$celebName. We just show them. Don't say it. What we did beneath the tower that day — do it once more in the hall.'

The examination began. Each pair unfolded a magic. That magic rose in the middle of the hall. One pair made a small flame, one pair a ring of water. They were skillful pairs. But somewhere, it looked like two people's separately made magics forced together.

$userName and $celebName's turn came. $userName knelt on the hall floor and began to draw a magic circle. Fast, precise. The hand did not tremble. $celebName was beside. $celebName steadied the breath, watching that magic circle. Waiting for the moment $userName completed the vessel.

$userName drew the last line. Exactly to that moment, $celebName poured all the light.

A tree of light, reaching to the hall's ceiling, rose. On every branch, leaves like starlight came out. Each leaf was as precise as $userName's magic circle, and the whole tree as intense as $celebName's light. The tree of light lit the whole hall.

The hall fell quiet. The sneering student fell silent too.

The old mage in charge of judging looked up long at that tree of light. The very mage who had long argued for the pair examination. Then the mage said,

'...I have taught forty years. I have seen many perfect vessels, many strong lights. But the two of them meshing in one breath like this, I see for the first time. You two were not incomplete. You had only not yet met your pair. It was not that you had no talent — only that you were alone.'

chapters.heading: How to Use It Alone
chapters.body:
After passing the pair examination, $userName and $celebName became famous at the academy.

The two were always together. $userName made the vessel, $celebName filled the light. The two's magic was more beautiful than any magic of the academy. The students who had once sneered at the two now came beneath the Starlight Tower to watch the two's magic.

But one day, the old mage called the two separately. The mage who had made the pair examination.

'When you graduate, you will walk different roads. The world of mages is wide, and you must each be of use in different places. You cannot always be beside each other. For that time, you must learn one thing.'

The mage had $userName learn how to gather light, and $celebName learn how to draw a magic circle. Each other's weakness. The two resisted at first. We're a matched pair, with two of us it's perfect, why must we learn to be apart.

'To be a matched pair,' the mage said, 'is not to lean on each other. It is to learn from each other. If you only lean, when one is gone, both collapse. But if you learn from each other, even apart, both can stand. $userName, learn light from $celebName. Then you can hold even a small light of your own in your vessel. $celebName, learn the vessel from $userName. Then your light will not lose where to go.'

From that day the two taught each other. $userName learned from $celebName how to gather light. It was slow and clumsy. Unlike the magic circle the hand knew, gathering light was a thing done with the heart. Still, after some months, a small light was held, for the first time, in $userName's magic circle. Faint, but it was $userName's own light.

$celebName learned the magic circle from $userName. The hand kept going wrong. Moving the magic circle in the head to the hand was the hardest thing for $celebName. Still, after some months, $celebName drew, alone, one clear if crooked magic circle. Holding $celebName's own light in that vessel, $celebName's light gained, for the first time, a small form.

One night beneath the Starlight Tower, $celebName said,

'$userName. It's strange. Learning the vessel from you, I come to know what shape your heart is. Drawing the vessel was drawing the heart.'

$userName laughed. 'Me too. Learning light from you, I come to know why you pour out so much. Gathering light was pouring out the heart.'

The two were no longer people incomplete without each other. They were a matched pair growing to resemble each other — so that even apart, each could stand.

chapters.heading: The Promise of the Starlight Tower
chapters.body:
On graduation day, $userName and $celebName stood for the last time beneath the Starlight Tower.

The two were truly to walk different roads. $userName to a village far to the north, $celebName to a city far to the south. The world of mages was wide, and the two had to be of use, each in that place. It was as the old mage had said.

$celebName drew a small magic circle on the ground. The magic circle $celebName had not even known how to draw on first coming to the academy, $celebName now drew alone. Crooked, but a clear vessel. The vessel learned from $userName.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'This magic, only the two of us know. The Starlight Bird. Wherever you are, wherever I am — once a year, on the same night, let's use this magic. Each of us. Apart.'

$celebName held a small light in that magic circle. Alone. With that small light learned from $userName. One small bird of light rose. Beside the great bird the two had made together, it was a meager thing. The wings small, the light faint. But unmistakably, a bird. A bird $celebName made alone.

'If, on the same night, the two of us each send up a Starlight Bird,' $celebName said. 'Somewhere in the sky, those two birds will meet. Even if we can't meet, our magics do. The bird sent up from the north and the bird sent up from the south, in the middle of the sky.'

$userName drew $userName's own magic circle. On it held $userName's own light — that small light learned from $celebName. Another small bird rose. A bird $userName made alone. Small and faint too, but unmistakably a bird.

The two small birds of light circled the Starlight Tower once. Then, at the tower's top, brushed past each other a moment. Like a pair who had known each other long. As one bird made way for the other.

'$userName.' $celebName said it, looking up at the birds. 'The day we first met, you told me there was no magic circle. I remember. At the time it felt like a jab at my weakness, but now I think — that day you saw what I couldn't see. You saw the empty place.'

'You did too,' $userName said. 'That my vessel was empty, you saw it. We were both the first to see each other's empty place.'

$celebName laughed. 'That's why we're a matched pair. You can't see your own empty place. That always has to be seen by the person standing beside.'

$userName and $celebName parted that day. To north and south. But for life after that, once a year when the same night came, each sent up a small bird of light. Drawing the vessel alone, holding the light alone. And those birds always met, somewhere in the sky. Even if the two could not.

hap.epilogue:
$userName keeps being drawn to something. Without knowing the reason.

What $celebName makes — a song or a stage or a work. Each time $userName sees it, $userName feels that one-instant sensation of vessel and light meshing exactly. The sensation of something incomplete alone being, at last, completed. $userName does not know where that sensation came from. Neither the Starlight Tower nor a magic circle is in this life of $userName's.

Still, when $celebName pours out light, $userName's heart draws a vessel on its own. So $celebName's strong light does not lose where to go. When $celebName goes intensely toward something, $userName quietly backs that form. With that habit of the hand that made a tree of light rise together in the academy hall.

In this life the two, many times a year, each send up a small light. $celebName on a stage, $userName within the heart. Those two lights always meet somewhere. As the Starlight Birds sent up from north and south met in the middle of the sky.

Two people incomplete alone. But two people who showed each other their own empty place. So the two, even apart, each stand — and yet, in the end, become each other's pair.

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName ordered the same tea every time at $userName's teahouse. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole choosing the tea to suit a guest, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak of accord can tie two together, so $celebName would order ahead of time the very tea $userName meant to recommend that day. When the cup was empty, $celebName always set it back in the same place, the same way.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $userName brought out a new tea and asked, as a test, and $celebName described the taste in the exact words $userName had jotted down. Tastes had overlapped though never taught, so $userName was quietly surprised.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of meshing streak can reach that taste into this life. So together with $celebName your heart might settle into calm accord.

hap.era: the kitchen of a small restaurant at the end of an alley, 2012
hap.title: The Pair Who Set One Table
hap.logline: In a small restaurant at the end of an alley, a hand that cannot season and a hand that cannot handle fire stand in one kitchen.

chapters.heading: The Restaurant at the End of the Alley
chapters.body:
The first time $userName met that person was in the kitchen of a small restaurant at the end of an alley.

It was 2012. To anyone's eye that restaurant was on the verge of closing. The end of an alley, the sign faded, the guests a few a day at most. Even at lunchtime there were more empty tables than not. The restaurant's paint was peeling, the door creaked each time it opened and shut.

$userName had inherited that restaurant from the mother. Inherited, or rather, suddenly had it thrust on. Because the mother had fallen ill. One day, all at once, $userName was standing alone in the kitchen.

The trouble was, $userName could not cook.

$userName handled fire well. Growing up beside the mother, $userName had learned to read fire. Which ingredient needs which strength of fire, when to raise the fire and when to draw it back — that, $userName's hand knew. The very moment an ingredient reached its best state, $userName could know with the eyes shut. But the seasoning, $userName could not match. The mother's touch, however $userName mimicked it, $userName could not bring out. Same ingredients, same fire, and $userName's food was always bland or salty. Between the mother's taste and $userName's taste lay a distance that could not be closed.

That day, $userName was ruining the seasoning again in the kitchen. A pot of soup. Had added and regretted salt several times. Then the restaurant door opened with a creak.

One person came in. $userName thought a guest. But that person did not even look at the menu and came straight toward the kitchen. Stood at the kitchen doorway and looked inside.

'You hiring here?' the person asked.

$userName was flustered. '...I'm in no position to hire anyone. As you can see.'

The person looked around the kitchen. Looked at the pot $userName had ruined. Came close without leave and put the nose to the pot. Drew one deep breath. Then said,

'This — the fire's perfect, but the seasoning is dead. The ingredients aren't dead. They took the fire properly, so the ingredients are alive. But the seasoning can't bring them out. Living ingredients, dead seasoning has covered over.'

$userName halted. That one remark pinned exactly the problem $userName had failed to solve for months. The problem of the fire being right but the seasoning wrong.

'...You can taste seasoning?'

'I can't read fire,' the person said honestly. 'I'm afraid of fire, so I always burn things or undercook them. Stand before fire and my hands freeze. But seasoning — the tongue knows. Add even one pinch and it knows at once whether it's right or wrong. The tongue has never once been wrong about that.'

$userName looked at that person. A person who, in place of reading fire, knew seasoning. That was $celebName.

chapters.heading: One Pot, Two People
chapters.body:
$userName took $celebName in, in the end. It was a restaurant that could not even pay a proper wage, but $celebName said it was all right.

'Because I have nowhere to go.' $celebName said only that. $userName did not ask further. $userName had nowhere to go either. Two people with nowhere to go stood together in the kitchen of a restaurant on the verge of closing.

At first it was a mess. When $userName watched the fire, $celebName fidgeted beside, anxious. The moment the fire grew a little stronger, $celebName flinched. When $celebName checked the seasoning, $userName, distrustful, kept peering in. Each time $celebName added a pinch of salt, $userName's eyes followed. Two people watching one pot was harder than it seemed. They had to trust each other's hands, and at first neither could.

Then one day, the two found the way.

$userName took the fire. The very moment an ingredient reached its best state, $userName's hand knew it. When that moment came, $userName gave a signal. Short. 'Now.' Then $celebName put in the seasoning. At that one instant the ingredient was most alive. $celebName's tongue knew the amount exact to that moment.

$userName watched only the fire. Did not watch the seasoning. $celebName watched only the seasoning. Did not watch the fire. Not crossing each other's domain, only then did it become one pot.

That day $userName tasted a pot of soup the two had made.

$userName's hand stopped. It was the mother's taste. No — a little different from the mother's taste. More exactly — it was a taste $userName had never once brought out. A taste where fire and seasoning met at exactly one point. Beyond that taste $userName had tried all life to mimic and could not, there was another taste.

'$celebName.' $userName said it. 'This. Did we make this?'

$celebName tasted a mouthful too. Then opened the eyes wide. $celebName wore a startled face too.

'...Alone I couldn't have brought this out. Because I can't read fire. However well I season, no seasoning works on a dead ingredient. And you alone couldn't either. Because you can't taste seasoning. A living ingredient, if the seasoning is dead, is finished.'

$celebName looked at $userName. For the first time, smiled brightly. Not the face that had frozen before the fire.

'$userName. We could set one table together. You watch the fire, I watch the seasoning.'

chapters.heading: The Sign Lit Again
chapters.body:
From that day the restaurant at the end of the alley changed.

$userName took the fire, $celebName watched the seasoning. The two moved in one breath before one pot. When $userName said 'now,' $celebName's hand went in; when $celebName said 'one breath more,' $userName fed the fire. It was a breathing no one had taught. Just, standing every day in the same kitchen, the two hands came to know each other. $userName came to trust $celebName's tongue, and $celebName came to trust $userName's fire.

As the food grew good, guests increased. First the people of the alley came back. Then people from beyond the alley sought it out. The empty tables decreased. At lunchtime there was even a line. $userName painted the faded sign with new paint. Oiled the creaking door.

One day a guest finished the meal and looked into the kitchen.

'The food here is strange,' the guest said. 'It doesn't seem made by one person, and yet it fits exactly as if one person made it. Usually when two make it, somewhere it goes off. But here it doesn't. What's the secret?'

$userName and $celebName looked at each other. $userName said, smiling,

'There's no secret. We're just a matched pair. I watch only the fire, this person watches only the seasoning. We're each a half, so the two have to gather for it to become one table. Alone, I couldn't have made this food.'

$celebName added from beside. 'When two halves gather, sometimes it's better than one whole. A whole person does it all alone, so they stay within their own limit, but two halves know exactly what each lacks. And the lacking spot, the other fills.'

That night, the restaurant closed, the two sat on the kitchen floor and shared the leftover soup. Only then did $celebName tell why, at first, there had been nowhere to go. $celebName had worked at some large restaurant, and had always been alone there. They only told $celebName to do everything well alone; no one became a pair. $celebName was scolded always for not being able to read fire. No one watched $celebName's seasoning.

'$userName.' $celebName said it, holding the cold soup bowl. 'Coming here, I knew for the first time. I wasn't a person who's good alone, I was a person who needs a pair to be good. At that large restaurant I didn't know that. I only thought myself a poor sort.'

$userName lifted a bowl. 'Same for me. I thought myself a poor child all life, for not bringing out the mother's taste. We're both that kind of person. Poor alone, but all right with two.'

chapters.heading: The Mother's Taste
chapters.body:
One spring, $userName's mother stopped by the restaurant.

The mother, recovered a little from the long illness. The mother walked slowly once around the restaurant the child had inherited. The newly painted sign, the oiled door, the lined-up guests. And in the kitchen, $userName and $celebName standing side by side before one pot. The mother's eyes rested long on those two.

The mother sat and ordered a bowl of soup. The soup the two had made went out. While the mother tasted that soup, $userName's hands trembled in the kitchen. $userName was a child who had blamed oneself all life for not bringing out the mother's taste. Now the mother would taste that poor taste directly.

The mother lifted a mouthful. And was still a long while. Spoon in hand.

The mother rose and came to the kitchen. $userName lowered the head. Made excuses first. 'Mother, I'm sorry. I can't bring out your taste. However I try, it doesn't work—'

'$userName.' The mother cut in. 'This is not my taste.'

$userName's shoulders sank. Just as I thought.

'It's better than my taste.'

$userName lifted the head. The mother was smiling.

'I stood in this kitchen alone all my life. I watched the fire, I watched the seasoning. I set the guests' tables too. So my taste was always within my limit. As far as I could reach. But this — this is two people's taste. The one who knows fire watched the fire, the one who knows seasoning watched the seasoning. To a place one person can never reach all life, two people reached. The line I couldn't cross in forty years, the two of you crossed together.'

The mother looked at $celebName. 'Thank you for being beside $userName. This child, left alone, keeps blaming oneself.'

$celebName lowered the head. There was water in the eyes. As if it had been long since someone said thank you.

That night, $userName said to $celebName. '$celebName. I felt sorry all my life for not bringing out my mother's taste. Today I knew. I didn't need to bring out my mother's taste. Instead of spending a life mimicking my mother's taste, I just had to bring out our taste. Together with you.'

chapters.heading: A Seat Never Left Empty
chapters.body:
The years passed.

The restaurant at the end of the alley became the oldest restaurant in the alley. While the other shops of the alley changed several times over, that restaurant alone stayed in its place. $userName and $celebName stood in one kitchen into old age. $userName watched the fire, $celebName watched the seasoning. That breathing was the same forty years on. 'Now.' 'One breath more.'

$celebName aged first. The sense of the tongue dulled, and the seasoning could no longer be matched exactly. The difference of one pinch, $celebName's tongue could no longer catch. One day $celebName, untying the apron in the kitchen, said,

'$userName. My tongue won't work now. I can't taste the seasoning. I'm of no use in the kitchen anymore.'

$userName was quiet a long while. Then handed $celebName one small spoon.

'$celebName. For forty years, when you tasted the seasoning, I was beside you. Through those forty years, I learned a little too. Over your shoulder. Each time you put in a pinch, I watched it. For forty years. Now I'll watch the seasoning.'

$celebName looked at $userName, startled.

'Instead.' $userName said it. 'Stay in the kitchen. You don't have to taste the seasoning. When you're beside me, my hand doesn't tremble. From the time you first walked into this kitchen, it's been so. When you're beside me, I become a person who does better.'

From that day $userName watched both the fire and the seasoning. It was clumsy. Nothing to compare to $celebName's tongue. But $celebName was beside. When $userName hesitated tasting the seasoning, $celebName said 'one pinch more.' Not with the tongue, but with forty years of memory. Those forty years stood in for $celebName's tongue.

$userName tasted that soup. Fire and seasoning still met at one point. Even without $celebName tasting the seasoning directly, the two still set one table. With one person's hand, and one person's memory.

One night, the restaurant closed, the two sat again on the kitchen floor and shared the leftover soup. In that first spot. $celebName, holding the soup bowl, said,

'$userName. At first I said it. That I came because I had nowhere to go. But looking now, it wasn't that I had nowhere to go — I was looking for somewhere to come. Here, this kitchen, was where I was meant to come.'

$userName laughed. 'Me too. I thought the restaurant was suddenly thrust on me by my mother. But I was standing here to meet you.'

The two finished the cold soup. In the kitchen of the oldest restaurant at the end of the alley. That soup was still a taste where fire and seasoning met at one point.

Until that restaurant's last day, two people always stood in the kitchen. One watching fire and seasoning, and one beside them saying 'one pinch more' — a matched pair. $userName never once left $celebName's seat empty.

hap.epilogue:
When $userName watches a person making something, the heart goes out to them unusually.

It is $celebName. Within a screen, on a stage, $celebName always makes something and sets it before people. A song, acting, a table of something. When $userName watches it, $userName knows that sensation of fire and seasoning meeting at one point. The moment $celebName raises the fire strong, the moment of adding exactly one pinch of seasoning — $userName's hand notices it first. Like a person who stood forty years together in one kitchen.

They cannot stand in one kitchen. $userName cannot give the signal 'now' from beside. $userName cannot relearn, over the shoulder, how to taste seasoning. So the one thing $userName does is this. To finish, not leaving a mouthful, that one table $celebName sets and serves. That is $userName's share, as a guest.

$userName keeps a seat for $celebName in one corner of the heart. Never once leaves that seat beside empty. As $userName filled that seat when an aged $celebName could no longer taste the seasoning — this time $userName keeps that seat.

When two halves gather, sometimes it's better than one whole. The two people, poor alone but all right with two, still set one table together.

hap.celebRole: stage hand who watched the stars from the same rooftop

paragraphs.gi: In $era, after the show $celebName always climbed to the rooftop before $userName, watching the stars. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who climbed the rooftop for the stars, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak of accord can tie two together, so $celebName pointed to the very constellation $userName loved. $celebName always laid out two cushions ahead of time where the stars showed best.
paragraphs.jeon: One night both raised a hand toward the same star at the same moment, and looked at each other and laughed. Their gazes had overlapped with no promise, so $userName remembered that night sky long after.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of overlapping streak can carry that feeling into this life. So with $celebName the direction you look might always be the same.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So a point $celebName emphasizes on stage may always be the part you loved most.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So a scene $celebName cherishes in a work might be the very one you'd captured.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So a moment $celebName names a highlight may be the one you'd been replaying.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So a part $celebName puts care into in a video might always catch your heart first.

hap.era: 1969, a launch control center in the middle of a desert
hap.title: The Same Countdown
hap.logline: At a desert launch control center, a person who watched only numbers and a person who watched only the sky count one countdown.

chapters.heading: Two Newcomers at the Control Center
chapters.body:
The first time $userName met that person was at a launch control center standing in the middle of a desert.

It was 1969. In those days, people were mad about the work of shooting something beyond the sky. A vast control center stood in the heart of the desert, and hundreds gathered there to work. By day the desert's heat, by night the desert's cold, ringed the control center. Inside it, people thought only of the sky.

$userName was a newcomer who had joined that year. $userName did calculation. Orbital calculation. At what angle, at what speed a launch vehicle had to rise — $userName solved it on paper. $userName loved numbers. Numbers did not lie, and gave an answer exact to how exactly you solved them. $userName loved that honesty of giving an answer only as much as you worked. $userName shut oneself in the control center's small calculation room and watched only numbers all day. Did not much look at the window either. There was no answer outside the window.

That day, the calculation room door opened and someone came in.

Another newcomer. The person, on coming in, went straight to the window. Looked up long at the sky beyond the desert. The sky beyond the window $userName had never once properly seen. Then glanced at the calculation sheets on $userName's desk. The numbers packed dense.

'These numbers.' The person said it. 'These all go to the sky.'

$userName stopped the pen. '...The numbers don't go to the sky. The launch vehicle goes.'

'Same thing.' The person laughed. 'If even one digit of these numbers is wrong, the launch vehicle won't reach the sky. One digit. One digit below the decimal. So what you're writing isn't just numbers, it's the road to the sky. You're drawing a road. On paper.'

$userName stood blank a moment, hearing that. $userName had watched only numbers ten years — counting the student days, it was ten years exactly — and it was the first time someone had spoken of those numbers that way. A person who called numbers a road.

'What do you do,' $userName asked.

'I watch the sky.' The person pointed out the window. 'Weather. Wind, cloud, atmosphere. Watching whether it's a sky to launch into is my work.' The person looked at $userName. 'But I can't watch numbers. Watch numbers and my head goes white. Those calculation sheets on your desk, to me they're just black dots.'

$userName looked at that person. A person who, in place of watching numbers, read the sky. That was $celebName.

chapters.heading: Numbers and Sky
chapters.body:
The control center's work could not be done by $userName alone.

$userName calculated a perfect orbit. On paper. Not one digit wrong. But that orbit always assumed a 'clear sky.' A sky with no wind, no current, no cloud. But the real sky was not clear. Wind blew, upper currents flowed, cloud gathered. $userName's perfect numbers kept missing before the real sky. Perfect on paper, off in the true sky.

$celebName came often to that calculation room. When $userName was calculating an orbit, $celebName brought that day's sky. 'Today the upper current flows this way. Your numbers, you have to correct them by this much.' $celebName could not speak the figures. Only showed, with a gesture, this much.

$userName was distrustful at first. To fix numbers with what is not numbers. To turn a precise orbit by one gesture. But when $userName put in the correction $celebName had pinned, the orbit fitted. In the real sky too. To $userName's perfect calculation, when $celebName's sky was added — only then did that orbit work even off the paper.

'$celebName.' One day $userName asked. 'How do you read the sky so exactly. Isn't that, in the end, numbers too? The speed of wind, the angle of a current.'

'I don't read it with numbers,' $celebName looked out the window. 'Just, watch the sky long and you know. What the sky is saying. The shape of cloud flowing, the angle of light bending. But that can't be written on paper. Try to write it and it vanishes. So I need you. The sky I read, you have to move into numbers, or the launch vehicle won't understand it. The launch vehicle can't read the sky, only numbers.'

$userName thought long about those words. $celebName read the sky but could not move it into numbers, and $userName handled numbers but could not read the sky. With only one of them, the launch vehicle could not reach the sky. With $celebName's sky alone the vehicle would not understand, and with $userName's numbers alone the true sky was not held.

From that day the two became a team. When $celebName read the sky and brought it, $userName moved it into numbers. $celebName's gesture became a figure at the tip of $userName's pen. At the control center, the two's orbital calculation was the most exact. On paper and in the true sky. People called the two 'sky and numbers.'

chapters.heading: The Night Before the Launch
chapters.body:
That autumn, the largest launch in the control center's history was set.

A launch vehicle carrying a person. If it failed, the person would not return. The whole control center threw itself at it for months. Hundreds, toward one launch. $userName recalculated the orbit a hundred times, and $celebName read the launch day's sky from weeks before. Watching out the window at the same hour every day.

The night before the launch, the two stayed in the calculation room. A last check. The other people of the control center had all gone home, and only the calculation room's light was lit in the desert dark.

$celebName looked out the window a long while, then turned with a hardened face.

'$userName. At the launch hour tomorrow, a current comes into the upper sky. It isn't in the forecast. The forecast is clear. But I know. The sky is saying so. From days ago the cloud has been flowing that way.'

$userName looked at the calculation sheets. Every number was perfect. Not one digit wrong. The forecast was clear too. On $celebName's one word, $userName could not propose to delay this enormous launch hundreds had thrown months at.

'$celebName. There's no proof. There's nothing to write on paper. With only your words, no one will listen.'

'I know.' $celebName's voice trembled. 'That's why I came to you. $userName, you move my sky into numbers. If a current really does come, how the launch vehicle misses. Where the orbit goes wrong. Looking at those numbers, people will listen. People won't believe my gesture, but they'll believe your numbers.'

$userName stayed up that night. $celebName sat beside, drawing the sky with gestures. The current this much from this side. $userName moved those gestures into numbers. If that current came — where the launch vehicle would miss, where the vehicle carrying a person would fall. $userName's pen drew that answer on paper. In cold, exact numbers.

At dawn, the two went to the control center director with that calculation sheet. A single warning made by numbers and sky together. $celebName's sky, moved into $userName's numbers.

The director looked long at that paper. The dense numbers. Then said, slowly. '...Delay the launch by one hour.'

chapters.heading: The Same Countdown
chapters.body:
On the launch day, the control center held its breath.

At the originally scheduled launch hour, a current truly did come into the upper sky. A current not in the forecast. Exactly as $celebName had read. The current the cloud had told of days before. Had they launched at that hour, the vehicle carrying a person would have missed to the very spot $userName had calculated. The spot written coldly on paper.

An hour later, the current passed out. The sky opened. $celebName looked out the window and nodded. It's all right now.

The whole control center entered the countdown. $userName had to be in the calculation room, $celebName in the weather room, each at their own post. But in that moment, the calculation room door opened. $celebName came in.

'$userName. Count with me.'

$celebName stood beside $userName. The two counted together the countdown flowing from the control center's speaker.

'...Ten. Nine. Eight.'

$userName counted. Numbers. The numbers $userName had loved all life. The simplest numbers, narrowing from ten to one.

'...Seven. Six. Five.'

$celebName counted. And while counting, watched the sky out the window. The sky $celebName had read all life. The sky opened at last.

'...Four. Three. Two. One.'

The launch vehicle rose. Shaking the desert sand, dragging a pillar of fire, into the sky. Exactly along the orbit $userName had calculated, exactly along the sky $celebName had read. Following that road numbers and sky had made, the launch vehicle climbed. Growing smaller and smaller, until at last it vanished into the blue sky.

The two faces were reflected in the calculation room window. $userName looked beside. $celebName was crying. Smiling.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'We sent that person to the sky. Your numbers, and my sky. With only one of them, it couldn't have been sent up.'

$userName looked out the window too. The spot where the launch vehicle had vanished, the empty sky. It was a sky properly seen for the first time in ten years. The sky $celebName had watched all life, $userName too saw straight at last. There, one faint trail of smoke the launch vehicle had drawn going up remained. A trace the launch vehicle had truly followed, of the road the numbers drew.

'$celebName. We counted the same countdown. You watching the sky, I watching numbers. What we watched differed, but the counting was the same. And the number it ended on was the same too. One.'

$celebName wiped tears and laughed. '$userName. A countdown is like that. While it goes from ten to one, if two people count the same numbers — those two are living the same moment. Watching the sky or watching numbers. We've lived the same moments, all our lives.'

chapters.heading: The Orbit Left in the Sky
chapters.body:
The years passed.

$userName and $celebName worked a lifetime at that control center. Sent off countless launches together. $userName calculated the orbit, $celebName read the sky. When $celebName drew the sky with gestures, $userName moved it into numbers. The two's countdowns always ended on the same number. On one.

$celebName aged first. The eyes dimmed, and the sky could no longer be read clearly. The shape of cloud, the angle of bending light, grew blurred to $celebName's eyes. The day before a last launch, $celebName called $userName to the control center roof.

The desert night sky filled it. Stars seemed about to pour down. A desert sky you could not see in a city.

'$userName.' $celebName said it, watching the sky. 'My eyes can't read the sky well now. Cloud just looks like cloud. I can't hear what it's saying. But it's all right. The orbits you calculated will all be somewhere in that sky. The things we sent up together. Those orbits don't vanish.'

$celebName looked at $userName.

'You'll keep solving numbers even without me. That's what you do best. But sometimes, when the calculation is blocked, lift your head. Look out the window. Look at the sky. Then the sky I read all my life will be there. If you move it into numbers, we're still a team. Even far apart.'

$celebName closed the eyes that winter. $userName calculated orbits at the control center after that. And each time the calculation was blocked, $userName looked at the window outside, unwatched for ten years. Lifted the head, to the desert sky.

Strangely, looking at the sky, the answer showed. The sky $celebName had read all life remained in $userName's head. The shape of cloud flowing now said something to $userName's eyes too. $userName was no longer a person who could not read the sky. Because $userName had taken up the sky $celebName had moved over.

$userName sometimes climbed to the roof and watched the desert night sky. The roof where $userName had last stood with $celebName. There the stars filled it. $userName thought that, between those stars, the orbits of the launch vehicles the two had sent up together passed. Roads unseen, but unmistakably there. Roads numbers and sky had drawn together.

'$celebName.' $userName sometimes said it to that empty sky. 'Today's sky — what would you have said, seeing it. What is this sky trying to tell us.' And strangely, somewhere in the heart an answer surfaced. Because the forty years $celebName had been beside had taught $userName how to read the sky.

On the day of $userName's last launch, the control center counted the countdown. $userName counted those numbers in the calculation room. And lifted the head to look at the sky out the window. The seat beside was empty, but $userName did not count alone. One person numbers, one person the sky. *...Three. Two. One.* The number it ended on was the same, as it always had been.

hap.epilogue:
When $userName watches something rise, $userName, without meaning to, counts numbers.

Three, two, one. Though no one bid it, $userName's mouth always finishes that countdown. More so when $celebName rises toward a new song, a new work, a new challenge.

When $userName watches it, $userName knows that sensation of the launch control center. That only when someone reads the sky and someone moves it into numbers does something at last reach the sky. That with only one of them, it will not do. So when $celebName soars toward the sky, $userName, within the heart, calculates that orbit together. So $celebName does not miss by even one digit.

They cannot be at the same control center. $userName cannot pin the orbit from beside. They cannot stay up a night together in one calculation room. Still, each time $celebName enters a countdown toward something, $userName counts the same numbers. Watching together, missing not one instant, the moment $celebName rises.

A countdown is such a thing. While it goes from ten to one, if two people count the same numbers, those two are living the same moment. Even if one watches the sky and one watches numbers — the number it ends on is always the same, one.

chung.celebRole: exacting blade seller from the shop across the way

paragraphs.gi: In $era, the $celebName from the shop across the way nitpicked $userName's blades as dull, every time. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who believed only their own way right, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A friction streak can set two against each other, so $userName spent each day with $celebName in argument. Even while nitpicking, $celebName quietly swapped $userName's hammer for better iron and left.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $userName was startled to find the blade forged with that new iron cut better than ever. The one always clashing had in truth refined the blade, so $userName, about to lash out, fell silent.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of friction streak — refining each other through the clash — can carry into this life. So even bickering with $celebName, your heart might end up firmer.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So at $celebName's concept change you may tilt your head at first, yet end up cheering louder than anyone.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So at $celebName's bold transformation you might feel it strange, yet come to treasure that work most.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So at $celebName's new position you may doubt, yet end up believing and cheering most firmly.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So at $celebName's new attempt you might be puzzled, yet end up drawn in deeper.

chung.era: a Ming-dynasty jianghu, the season of the tournament
chung.title: The Two Swords That Crossed
chung.logline: Two head disciples raised under one master cross blades all their lives. The sword swung to break the other, in the end, makes each the strongest.

chapters.heading: The Day of Becoming Second
chapters.body:
$userName took up the sword in the spring of being seven.

The Cheongun Sword School. One of the foremost sword schools of the Ming jianghu. The master took in exactly two disciples. One was $userName, one was $celebName.

The two grew up in the same courtyard. Drank from the same well, rose at the same dawn and swung the same thousand strokes of the sword. They ate together, slept in the same room. A bond you could call brotherhood. Save one thing — until the sword was in hand.

With the sword in hand, the two were not brothers.

The first time the two crossed wooden swords was that spring of being seven, in a corner of the courtyard. In the first bout $userName lost. Sat down hard in the dirt, and to $userName looking up, $celebName said, wooden sword slung on a shoulder, 'Don't cry. Fight again next time. So until then — don't break, stay alive.' Words too grown for a seven-year-old. $userName was so galled by them that, that night, $userName vowed to win for certain next time. That was the start of the two.

'$userName.' At twelve, $celebName grinned, leveling a wooden sword. That grin always lifted only one corner of the mouth. 'Today I win. Because I lost yesterday.'

'Yesterday was luck,' $userName said, raising the wooden sword. 'Today there won't be luck either.'

The two dueled every day. Dueled without the master bidding it. In the courtyard, on the back hill, on a flat rock by the stream. The winner gloated and the loser ground their teeth. And the next day they dueled again.

The match was always by a hair. If $userName won three days, $celebName won four. Neither could overwhelm the other. Unable to overwhelm, they came to hate; hating, they wanted to be stronger.

The year they turned fifteen, the master stood the two in the courtyard. In his hand was one volume of the school's secret sword manual.

'This is passed to one person only. To the next head of the Cheongun Sword School.' The master looked from one to the other. 'In three years, the great tournament of the realm. The one who goes farther there receives this.'

That night, $userName looked long at $celebName's sleeping profile. Same room, the spot beside on the same bedding. They slept a full pace apart, and still the breathing was heard. A person to cross swords with again tomorrow. A person of whom only one, in three years, would become head of the school.

'I will win, without fail.' $userName closed the eyes thinking so. Whether it was hatred or what, $userName did not yet know the name.

chapters.heading: Three Years of Dawns
chapters.body:
From that day the two people's dawns changed.

$userName rose an hour earlier than others. Swung the sword when there was no one in the courtyard. A thousand strokes became two thousand. When the palms split, $userName bound them with cloth and swung again.

But one dawn, there was someone in the courtyard already. It was $celebName.

The two, without a word, turned their backs and swung their own swords. Said nothing. But both knew. That the other, the same way, in secret, had been rising earlier.

After that day the two people's dawns came earlier and earlier. When $userName was there first, the next day $celebName was there first. Later both stood in the courtyard before even the first cock crowed. In the pitch-dark courtyard, backs turned as if back to back, hearing only the other's sword.

$userName measured $celebName's skill by the sound of his sword. If it was faster than yesterday, $userName grew faster too; if heavier than yesterday, $userName's sword grew heavier. With $celebName beside, $userName could not stop.

The daytime duels grew fiercer. At seventeen the two now set aside wooden swords and took up live blades. Each exchange was perilous. When a sword tip grazed a sleeve, the cloth was cut away. One day from $userName's forearm, one day from $celebName's cheek, a line of blood ran.

Then the two would lower the swords. Not the one cut — the one who had cut, first.

'...Too deep,' $celebName said, looking at $userName's forearm. The corner of the mouth that always alone lifted did not lift that day.

'Fuss,' $userName said, binding the arm with cloth. 'This much won't kill me. Come again tomorrow.'

'...Yes. Again tomorrow.'

The two believed they hated each other. And yet when one was cut the heart fretted, and when the other did not come out with a sword the day stood empty. To call it hatred — they checked too often on the other's well-being.

chapters.heading: The Broken Sword
chapters.body:
In the winter half a year before the tournament, something happened.

$celebName slipped on ice while swinging the sword on the back hill. The right arm broke. The arm that gripped the sword.

The physician shook the head. 'The bone will set. But the tournament in half a year… it is hard to recover enough to grip a sword by then.'

$celebName lay in the room and watched only the ceiling. The broken arm laid on the chest. For the first time in life, the corner of the mouth that alone lifted vanished entirely.

That night $userName could not sleep.

It was a strange thing. If $celebName could not enter the tournament — the head's seat was $userName's. The seat ground at for three years, rolling in even by standing still. $userName ought to have been glad. And yet was not glad at all.

$userName could not bear it. Imagining a tournament without $celebName, imagining oneself winning there, the inside went empty. Winning had no meaning at all. With no one to contend against, what use was winning.

The next dawn, $userName went not to the pitch-dark courtyard but to $celebName's room.

Carried the sword manual. The school's secret manual the master said was passed only to the head. $userName set it down before $celebName.

'...What are you doing,' $celebName said, lying still, only the eyes raised.

'The left arm,' $userName said. 'If the right arm is broken, grip the sword with the left. Inside this manual there are forms that use both hands. In half a year a left arm alone can be made.'

'...Have you gone mad? This is for the one who becomes head only—'

'Shut up and get up.' $userName's voice trembled. For the first time. 'Winning at a tournament without you, I won't do it. That head's seat — even received, I'd not be glad. Get up, and lose to me properly. Or — win properly.'

$celebName looked at $userName a long while. And after long, one corner of the mouth lifted again. This time there was water in it.

'...Don't regret it.'

chapters.heading: One Pot of Rice
chapters.body:
For the days $celebName began to grip the left-hand sword, $userName cooked the rice for two.

Because $celebName's right arm was broken. The school's kitchen work, the courtyard sweeping, $userName did for two. $celebName seemed, at first, ill at ease with it.

'Why do you do my work too.'

'Your hand has to heal fast for me to win properly,' $userName said, lifting the pot lid. 'If I beat you with a broken hand, the jianghu sneers. It sneers at my sword along with it.'

It was an excuse. Both knew. $userName simply — wished $celebName's hand to heal fast. Though it was the hand of a hated person, $userName brewed herbs into the meal. Though it was the bedding of a hated person, woken at dawn $userName drew it up over them.

One night, $celebName, pretending sleep, said. '$userName. Why do you go this far.'

$userName did not answer a long while. Then said. '...If you set down the sword, half my reason to take up the sword is gone. For ten years I swung the sword every dawn to beat you. If that suddenly vanishes — I don't know what I'd rise for.'

The room was quiet. After long, $celebName said. '...I thought the same. If you'd broken, I'd have carried the manual to you. Just the same.'

That night, the two for the first time admitted what had been beneath the label of hatred. It was the other name for a bond of ten years of checking on each other's well-being every day.

The next dawn, $celebName's left-hand sword drew one clean straight stroke for the first time. $userName clapped. Clapping for a hated person, $userName knew that $userName was smiling.

'You saw,' $celebName grinned crookedly. 'I beat you with this hand, I told you.'

'Dream on,' $userName laughed too. 'Still — it was straight. Well done.'

Spring light came into the school courtyard.

chapters.heading: The Left-Hand Sword
chapters.body:
From that day the two people's dawns changed again.

$celebName gripped the sword with the left arm. At first could not even lift it. The sword kept slipping from the hand, and swinging it the body turned with it. $userName held that hand from beside. From how to grip the sword, again.

'Don't twist the wrist. You'll injure it again.'

'...Since when did you worry about me.'

'It isn't worry. To beat a sound you is a boast; to beat a broken you — that isn't a boast, it's a blemish.'

$celebName broke into a grin. 'Listen to you.'

Both knew. That it was worry. Only, to peel off the label of hatred, the two had dueled too long.

$userName spread open the secret manual and taught $celebName form by form. The manual said to be passed to the head only, to a rival. If the master knew, it was a thing to bring out the switch for. Still $userName did not stop.

By day they dueled again. $celebName's left-arm sword was clumsy. $userName could end it within three exchanges if so minded. But $userName did not end it. Drew the sword only as far as $celebName's left arm could receive. When $celebName gained a hand's width, $userName made the sword a hand's width heavier.

One day $celebName said, lowering the sword. '$userName. Don't go easy on me on purpose. That is an insult.'

'I'm not going easy,' $userName said. 'I'm waiting for you to catch up. That is different from going easy. We have to stand on the tournament platform together — only then is it a true match.'

Half a year passed so. $celebName's left arm became a right arm. The broken place scarred over, but over that scar a new sword grew. Perhaps a fiercer sword than before.

The night before the tournament, the two sat side by side on the flat rock of the courtyard. As in childhood.

'$userName.' $celebName said it, watching the moon. 'Tomorrow, let's both climb to the final. So that the last is — you and me. Don't lose to anyone else.'

'Of course,' $userName said. 'And in the final, I win.'

'Dream on.'

The two laughed. A pair to cross swords tomorrow, and yet that night alone, they laughed like brothers.

chapters.heading: The Road to the Platform
chapters.body:
On the morning of the tournament, the two walked one road together toward the royal-city platform.

It was half a day's road from the school to the platform. $userName with a straight steel sword, $celebName with a sword at the scarred left arm. On the road the two said little. But the silence was not awkward.

At the pass $celebName stopped. Far off the royal city's platform was visible. Banners fluttered in the wind.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'If we meet in the final today, I won't go easy. I won't go soft for a scarred arm. You don't go easy either. Understood?'

'I've no thought of going easy,' $userName said. 'But — I won't aim at the trembling arm. That isn't going easy; only so is it a true match. Sound swords against sound swords, then whoever wins, it's a boast.'

$celebName lifted one corner of the mouth.

'...Those words remind me of the night we received the manual. The master stood the two of us in the courtyard and said the one who went farther at the tournament in three years would be head. That night you, pretending sleep, glared at me. I knew it all.'

'...I didn't glare. I looked. At your profile.'

'That's glaring,' $celebName said. 'I looked at you that night too. Vowing to win for certain. That's already years ago now.'

The two walked the road again. The platform grew nearer. $userName suddenly recalled the two children crossing wooden swords in the same courtyard at seven. The two children who drank from the same well and slept beside on the same bedding, who became not-brothers when the sword was in hand. That the hatred had really been the force that dragged $userName all the way here, $userName now knew.

At the platform's entrance, the two parted. Each to a waiting room. Before parting $celebName said.

'$userName. Both climb to the final. Lose to someone else along the way — and I won't go easy on you for life.'

'You, rather,' $userName said. 'Don't fall along the way for a scarred arm. See you in the final.'

The two turned their backs. The drum of the platform began to sound.

chapters.heading: The Last Exchange
chapters.body:
The great tournament of the realm. Sword schools of every part of the Ming sent their young swordsmen.

$userName climbed. One bout, two, three. With a straight, gapless sword.

$celebName climbed too. With the sword of the scarred left arm. The sword broken and grown again came in from angles no one foresaw. The opposing swordsmen could not read that sword.

And the final. As promised, the two stood at the two ends of the platform.

The two head disciples of the Cheongun Sword School. Two who lived the same master, the same courtyard, the same dawns. The stands stirred. For one school to take both seats of the final was a thing unheard of in the jianghu.

The master sat on the dais and watched the two. The manual on his knees.

'Begin.'

Two swords met.

$userName was a straight sword. $celebName was a curved sword. Straightness tried to press down curvedness, and curvedness slipped past straightness. One exchange, two, ten, twenty. The match did not break. It could not. The two had watched each other's swords all their lives. Where $userName meant to cut, $celebName's sword was there first; the gap $celebName meant to slip into, $userName's sword filled first.

Then $userName saw it. $celebName's left arm. That scarred arm was trembling faintly. A sword made in half a year was not deep enough to bear a long match. $celebName's sword fell a beat behind.

That gap showed. If $userName's sword went in, it could be ended.

$userName — did not put the sword through that gap.

Instead stepped back one pace. Gave time to steady the breath. $celebName's eyes raised. '...$userName!'

'To win with a scarred arm,' $userName said, regripping the sword, 'is your boast. To win aiming at a trembling arm becomes my blemish. Come properly. The last exchange, properly.'

$celebName clenched the teeth. Gripped the trembling left arm once with the other hand. And put out the last sword. The fiercest exchange, honed all life, grown new over a scar.

$userName put out the last sword too. The most solid exchange, swung straight all life long.

Two swords met in the middle.

The ring of steel sounded over the platform — and one sword sprang up into the air. Turned once in the sunlight, and stuck into the dirt.

It was $userName's sword.

$celebName's sword tip stopped one inch before $userName's throat. As in that first childhood duel. This time too, it did not cut.

The crowd roared. But the two did not look at the crowd. They looked only at each other.

$celebName lowered the sword. With the trembling left hand. And held that hand out to $userName. To raise $userName, kneeling in the dirt.

'...Get up,' $celebName's voice cracked. 'Without you I couldn't have come here. If you hadn't given me the manual the day I broke. If I hadn't heard your sword every dawn. I'd have set down the sword long ago.'

$userName took that hand and rose. With a dirt-stained hand.

'Be head,' $userName said. 'Because you won. And — at the next tournament I win. So until then, don't break, stay alive.'

Those were the words $celebName had said the day the two first met. $celebName understood it, and lifted one corner of the mouth. This time both lifted.

The master on the dais came down with the manual. But did not press it into $celebName's hand. Laid it on both their hands together.

'The next head of the Cheongun Sword School is two,' the master said. 'One person is only half a school. You two were each other's whetstones all your lives. Set apart, you both go dull.'

The two, holding the manual together, looked at each other. The person they had crossed swords with all their lives. The person who had made them the strongest, all their lives.

chung.epilogue:
If someone asked $userName — who is the person you most want to resemble. $userName would hesitate a long while, and in the end give one name. $celebName.

It is a strange thing. Wanting to resemble someone usually comes from a heart of liking. But what $userName feels for $celebName, before liking — is the heart of not wanting to lose. When $celebName goes a step ahead, $userName's feet quicken on their own. When $celebName hones one more sword, $userName draws out one's own sword. The mere sight of that person stirs a will to contend. $celebName on a stage, $celebName within a screen, will not leave $userName be.

$userName did not know why for a long while. Then knew. With a whetstone beside, a blade cannot go dull. $celebName is $userName's whetstone. From those times a hundred years ago in the Cheongun Sword School courtyard, swinging the sword, backs turned, every pitch-dark dawn.

So the days $celebName wavers are hardest for $userName to bear. On a day $celebName's sword looks a beat behind, the wish to win that person leaves $userName entirely. Instead — $userName wants to give time to steady the breath. That to win must be that person's boast won with a trembling arm, $userName learned already on that scarred-left-arm platform.

Two swords split from one root, set apart, both go dull. So $userName watches $celebName today too. Looking up at that sword climbing one step, with a heart to hone one's own sword one more time, straight.

chung.celebRole: another actor who always quarreled over the lines

paragraphs.gi: In $era, in the same scene $celebName always clashed with $userName over the lines. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who wouldn't bend on their lines, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A friction streak can knock two together, so loud words flew over the script at every rehearsal. After every clash, $celebName wrote down, separately, a move to support $userName's weakest passage.
paragraphs.jeon: One night on stage $userName forgot a line, and $celebName, the quarrel forgotten, naturally filled that gap. The one always clashing had in truth been supporting, so $userName held out a hand as the curtain fell.
paragraphs.gyeol: A friction streak can reach into this life the rhythm refined by clashing. So even clashing with $celebName, your hands might end up matching.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So even when your heart wavers over $celebName's new album direction, you might end up catching every single track.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you may quibble at $celebName's project choice, yet end up loving that work most.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you might feel frustrated at $celebName's match management, yet end up cheering more firmly.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So even when opinions split over $celebName's content shift, you might end up drawn in deeper.

chung.era: the modern era, the season of a debut survival show
chung.title: For the No. 1 Seat
chung.logline: Two trainees clash for one debut-group seat. The back stared at to drag down becomes, before long, the most dependable back.

chapters.heading: No. 101 and No. 1
chapters.body:
The day $userName first stepped into that practice room, there was already one person before the mirror.

A hundred and one trainees gathered from across the country. A debut survival show. For eleven weeks a rank was given each week, and each week someone packed their bags and went home. Only the seven who survived to the end became the debut group.

$userName's number was 101. The grade given in the first evaluation became the number outright. The hundred-and-first of a hundred and one. The very end.

The number of the person standing before the mirror was 1. A perfect score in the first evaluation. The first of a hundred and one.

That was $celebName.

$celebName looked at $userName once in the mirror. Up and down. And went back to the moves of self in the mirror. That was all. No greeting, no word.

That indifference made $userName angrier still. Even a sneer $userName could have struck back at. To $celebName, $userName was — a 101 not even worth striking back at.

The first group-dance evaluation was that afternoon. A hundred and one danced the same choreography. The camera held only the front row, the No. 1 spot, all the while. $userName danced the same moves in the back row, the spot the camera never once held. Though the fingertips were not even caught on screen, $userName matched even the angle of the fingertips razor-sharp. Though no one watched. Perhaps, all the more because no one watched.

The evaluation over, leaving the practice room, $userName was stopped a moment by $celebName.

'The back row,' $celebName said. 'In a spot the camera doesn't even catch, you matched it down to the fingertips. The score wouldn't show a trace even without that.'

'...So what,' $userName shot back.

'Nothing,' $celebName shrugged. 'Just. First time seeing a kid wearing the hundred-and-first number dance like that where no one watches.'

And went off. $userName, unable to tell if it was praise or a sneer, was the more galled.

That night, $userName lay on the dorm bed and watched the ceiling. Same room, on the next bed a pace away $celebName slept. The rooms had been assigned by evaluation grade, and by what trick of fate, No. 1 and No. 101 were in the same room, beds beside.

'I will drag that down, without fail.' $userName closed the eyes thinking so. No. 1's number. That indifferent back. Whether it was hatred or what, $userName did not yet know the name.

chapters.heading: The Back in the Mirror
chapters.body:
$userName lived in the practice room.

At a dawn when everyone slept, $userName went down to the empty practice room. Before the mirror, repeated the same move a hundred, a thousand times. If a fingertip angle was wrong, again. If a beat was half-late, again. To peel off the 101.

But one dawn, there was someone in the practice room already. It was $celebName.

The two, without a word, stood at the two ends of the mirror. Did their own moves. Without a greeting. But both knew. That the other, the same way, in secret, cutting sleep, stood before the mirror.

After that day the two people's dawns came earlier and earlier. When $userName was there first, the next day $celebName was there first. At the two ends of the mirror, stealing sidelong glances at the other's moves.

$userName refined the moves watching $celebName's back in the mirror. When $celebName's fingertips grew sharper $userName sharpened the fingertips too, when $celebName's expression grew deeper $userName glared at one's own face in the mirror. With $celebName in the same room, $userName could not be idle a single moment.

There was an evaluation each week. A rank was given. $userName climbed from 101 to 88, to 70, to the 50s. One step a week.

$celebName was always No. 1. An unshaken No. 1.

On one evaluation day, $userName's stage ended and the score came. The 30s for the first time. As $userName came down from the stage gasping, someone held out a bottle of water from beside the stage.

It was $celebName.

'...The fingertips,' $celebName said, short. 'The last move, the fingertips were half a beat fast. Fix just that and you'd have gone to the 20s.'

And went off. $userName stood a long while, water bottle in hand. That No. 1 had watched the stage of a one-time 101 — down to the fingertips — was a strange thing.

chapters.heading: The Sprained Ankle
chapters.body:
In the eighth week, something happened.

$celebName sprained an ankle during group-dance practice. Misstepped on a jump landing. Not just sprained — a ligament stretched. The medical team said. 'Five days to the next evaluation… to stand on a stage by then is too much.'

$celebName sat in a corner of the practice room icing the ankle. The person who had been No. 1 all their life sat, for the first time, not before the mirror but beside it.

$userName could not sleep that night.

It was a strange thing. If $celebName could not stand on the next evaluation's stage, the score would be cut. The No. 1 seat would shake. A gap would open for $userName to step up a rung. Seven debut-group seats. If No. 1 staggered, $userName's seat came that much nearer.

And yet there was no gladness at all. $userName could not bear it. An evaluation without No. 1, oneself stepping up over a staggering No. 1 — none of it was glad.

$userName had wanted to stand on a stage, not to sit No. 1 down. Only then did $userName know. That it was not to drag down — but to catch up.

The next dawn, $userName sat not before the mirror but beside $celebName.

'The ankle,' $userName said. 'Cut the jumps. This stage's choreography has two jumps, doesn't it. Drop those — I drew up a path that fills it with upper body and expression alone.'

$userName held out a sheet of paper. A path drawn through the night. $celebName's choreography, rebuilt to spare the ankle.

'...Why are you like this,' $celebName asked, looking at the paper. 'It's by my wavering that you climb, isn't it?'

'Shut up and look.' $userName's voice trembled. 'Climbing a rung on a stage without you, I won't do it. A seat climbed to that way — even received, I'd not be glad. To beat a sound-ankled No. 1 — that's a boast. To climb beside a limping No. 1 is a blemish. Get up. Even without jumps, stand on the stage.'

$celebName looked at $userName a long while. For the first time since becoming No. 1, those eyes wavered.

'...You really are a strange kid.'

chapters.heading: The Night in the Same Room
chapters.body:
For the days $celebName began to learn the jump-less choreography, the two hardly slept in the same room.

$celebName, wearing an ankle brace, tossed with pain each night. $userName, on the next bed, woke with the sound of $celebName tossing. Then rose and changed the ice pack. On the ankle of a person said to be hated.

'...No. 101.' One night $celebName said it, watching the ceiling. Called $userName by the first day's number. 'Tell me honestly. Do you want my ankle to heal or not. It's by my wavering that you get into the debut group.'

$userName held the ice pack a long while.

'...I want it to heal,' $userName said. 'Strange, isn't it. At first I hated you for being No. 1. An indifferent No. 1. But watching your moves every day from the two ends of the mirror — before I knew it, I grew watching you. When you climbed a rung I climbed after, when you stopped I felt I would stop. Your ankle has to heal for me to have a reason to keep climbing.'

$celebName raised the body from the bed. Wearing the ankle brace.

'...I grew watching you too,' $celebName said. 'Being No. 1 was lonely, honestly. There was no back to catch up to. Then one day a hundred-and-first kid climbed to the 50s. To the 80s, the 30s. That speed was frightening. So I too couldn't sleep at dawn and stood before the mirror. Because of you.'

That night, the two for the first time admitted what had been beneath the label of hatred. It was the other name for a bond of two who had made each other the only back to catch up to.

The next dawn, $celebName danced the jump-less choreography all the way through for the first time. Filling every empty spot with fingertips and gaze alone. $userName clapped.

'You saw,' $celebName said, gasping, laughing. 'Even without jumps I beat you.'

'Dream on,' $userName laughed too. 'Still — not one spot was empty. Well done.'

The two were reflected side by side in the practice-room mirror.

chapters.heading: Practice in the Next Seat
chapters.body:
Through the five days $celebName mended the ankle, the two spent day and night in the practice room together.

$celebName, wearing the ankle brace, learned the jump-less choreography. The path $userName had drawn up. A path that filled the place the jump left empty with fingertips and gaze and expression. At first it was awkward. $celebName's body was used to jumps.

$userName danced beside, watching the mirror together.

'Don't drop the gaze there. Lift it with the eyes instead of a jump. Pull the audience's eyes upward.'

'...You're good at choreographing. I didn't know.'

'A 101 has to do all sorts of things to survive,' $userName laughed. 'You don't know because you've been No. 1 all your life.'

$celebName laughed aloud for the first time. Wearing the ankle brace.

Both knew. That this was not hatred. Only, the heart of wanting to catch up was so large that they had put the wrong name, hatred, on it.

By day each practiced their own evaluation stage. $userName the own stage, $celebName the jump-less one. When stuck, they watched for each other. $celebName fixed $userName's expression-acting, and $userName memorized $celebName's new path together.

On the third day, $celebName, dancing the jump-less choreography to the end, stopped on the last move. Glared a long while at self in the mirror, then sank to the floor.

'...It's no good,' $celebName said. 'Without the jumps this isn't my stage. What's the meaning of it. A No. 1 carrying out a stage that can't even jump.'

$userName sat beside.

'There is meaning,' $userName said. 'A kid wearing the hundred-and-first number matching it down to the fingertips in a back row the camera doesn't catch — did you say that was meaningless? You didn't. You said it was the first time you'd seen it. A No. 1 who can't jump still standing on a stage — that too is something someone is seeing for the first time. That's the meaning.'

$celebName looked at $userName. As if dumbfounded that the first day's words had come back unchanged. Then broke into a grin, and rose again.

'...You really do talk well.'

'A 101 has to at least talk well to survive.'

The night before the evaluation, the two sat side by side on the dorm rooftop. Looking down at the city lights.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'Tomorrow, let's both get into the debut group. Seven seats. You and me, together.'

'Of course,' $userName said. 'And I take center.'

'Dream on.'

The two laughed. A pair to contend ranks on the same stage tomorrow, and yet that night alone, they laughed like old friends.

chapters.heading: Before Going Up on Stage
chapters.body:
On the day of the last evaluation, the two stood side by side before the waiting-room mirror.

The makeup was done, the costume put on, the last button of the stage outfit fastened. $celebName hid the ankle brace inside the costume. So the brace would not show, $userName smoothed the hem.

'...No. 101.' $celebName looked at $userName in the mirror. 'When today's stage ends, the ranks come out. Seven debut-group seats.'

'I know,' $userName said.

'If,' $celebName said. 'If only one of you and me gets into the seven. Are you sure you won't resent me even so?'

$userName looked long at $celebName in the mirror.

'I won't resent you,' $userName said. 'If you get in and I fall, that means you did better. I didn't practice solo. I grew every day watching you from the two ends of the mirror. If you get into the seven, my having watched your dawns goes in there. Then it's enough.'

$celebName took the eyes off the mirror and looked at $userName directly.

'...Don't say things like that,' $celebName said. 'We both get in. Let's leave no spot empty on the stage. You drew up a path so my stage wouldn't be empty even without jumps. So you too — don't leave one spot empty up there. Both of us survive.'

The stage director opened the waiting-room door. 'Last stage, standby.'

The two rose from before the mirror. The two trainees who had been No. 1 and No. 101. $celebName tapped $userName's shoulder once. $userName held that hand a moment, then let go.

'Let's go,' $userName said.

'I take center,' $celebName said.

'Dream on.'

Laughing, the two stepped into the dark corridor toward the stage. From the corridor's end came the roar of the crowd.

chapters.heading: The Last Rank Announcement
chapters.body:
The last evaluation. The twenty who had survived of a hundred and one stood on stage.

$userName went up on stage. The one who had been 101. With fingertips practiced to wearing-down, with an expression honed through the night. The crowd stirred. A trainee who had been the very last number did a stage no one could ignore.

$celebName went up on stage. Wearing the ankle brace. With the jump-less choreography. And yet the place the jumps left did not look empty. The path $userName had drawn up filled that place full, with fingertips and gaze. No. 1 was No. 1 even limping.

And the final rank announcement.

Seven chairs were set on the stage. Seven debut-group seats. Only the one named could sit in that chair.

The host opened an envelope. They were named from seventh. One, two, three sat in the chairs. $userName stood at the stage's edge, gripping a hand tight. $celebName stood beside.

Fourth. Third. $userName's name was not yet. $celebName's name was not yet either.

Two chairs left. Four not named. $userName, $celebName, and two more.

'...Second,' the host said.

$userName's name was called.
