The legs went weak. A 101 was second. $userName walked staggering toward the chair. But before sitting in the chair, $userName looked back. $celebName stood there. One chair left. Three not named.

$userName did not sit in the chair, and waited. Until $celebName's name was called.

'First.'

$celebName's name was called.

No. 1, wearing the ankle brace, walked toward the last chair. Only then did $userName sit. $celebName sat in the next chair. The hundred-and-first and the first, side by side in debut-group chairs.

$celebName held a hand out toward $userName. Under the stage lights.

'...Thank you,' $celebName said. Though the cameras saw it all, $celebName did not lower the voice. 'If you hadn't drawn that path that dawn, I couldn't have sat in this chair. If you hadn't sat beside me when I sprained the ankle. If your moves hadn't been at the two ends of the mirror — I'd have gone idle in the No. 1 seat long ago.'

$userName took that hand.

'I take center,' $userName said. 'And at the next stage I take first too. So until then, look after that ankle. To beat a sound you is a boast.'

$celebName laughed. Waving the ankle brace.

'Dream on.'

The stage lights lit the two at once. Two trainees who had been No. 1 and No. 101. Two whose back, stared at to drag down, had become the most dependable back.

chung.epilogue:
$userName sometimes, without meaning to, checks the moves before a mirror.

More so on a day of having watched $celebName's stage. When $celebName uses one fingertip newly, $userName's hand tries that fingertip that night. Though they have never once stood on the same stage, like people who watched the same mirror long.

To $userName, $celebName is not simply a person liked. $celebName is the person who keeps $userName from being able to stop. When $celebName climbs one rung, $userName cannot stay still in one's own place. The wish stirs to beat yesterday's self by even a hand's width. That habit of matching down to the fingertips in a back row the camera doesn't catch, even wearing the hundred-and-first number, stays inside $userName a hundred years on.

There are days $celebName limps too. Days the expression collapses. On such days the thought of wanting to win does not come to $userName. Instead — $userName wants to draw, in the head, a path-sheet that fills the jump-less place with fingertips and gaze. That to step up over a wavering No. 1 becomes a blemish for life, $userName learned in that practice room.

When $celebName gets into the seven seats, $userName's having watched that person's dawns all goes in there. So each time $celebName climbs a rung, $userName quietly reworks one's own stage. As an unfinished contest of two who watched the same mirror.

chung.celebRole: partner who quarreled over how to run it

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName opposed $userName on every little thing, down to the menu. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole wanting to run the shop their own way, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak that pushes and refines can knock two together, so opinions split right up to closing time. Even while opposing, $celebName carefully wrote out the guest list $userName had missed and handed it over.
paragraphs.jeon: One month, thanks to that list, the shop turned its first profit, and $userName saw the quarreling words had all been for the shop. The one clashing had in truth been pulling alongside, so $userName laughed sheepishly.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of streak can carry into this life the flow that settled through the wavering. So even when opinions split with $celebName, things might end up better.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So at $celebName's comeback concept you may oppose it at first, then completely change your mind once you see the stage.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might tilt your head at $celebName's casting news, then fall deep in once you watch.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may grow anxious at $celebName's tactical shift, then come to admit it once you see the result.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you might be skeptical of $celebName's new plan, then rethink it once you see the outcome.

chung.era: Joseon, a season at the Royal Painting Bureau
chung.title: Two Painters of One Royal Portrait
chung.logline: Two court painters who matched brushes for the seat of royal portraitist. The eye that searched for flaws in the rival's painting becomes, in the end, the eye that knows the rival best.

chapters.heading: Two Hands Grinding Ink
chapters.body:
$userName entered the Royal Painting Bureau at sixteen.

The Painting Bureau. The office that painted the nation's pictures. The palace's records, the maps, and the royal portrait of the king — all came from the brushes of the Bureau's painters. Young apprentices hoping to become painters gathered in that courtyard.

In the apprentice days, beside $userName was one person. A person whose hand grinding ink was unusually straight. That was $celebName.

The two grew up in the same studio. Ground ink on the same inkstone, spread paper in the same light from the same window. But with the brush in hand, the two were not companions.

$userName's painting was delicate. Did not miss a single hair, a single fold of cloth. A painting that made the viewer marvel.

$celebName's painting was different. The hairs were not as fine as $userName's, but the gaze of the person was alive. A painting that made the viewer halt.

The master painter always stood the two side by side. '$userName's brush is precise, and $celebName's brush holds the spirit. Both are halves. One paints the likeness, one paints the person.'

$userName hated those words. That one's painting was a half.

One day $celebName said, looking at $userName's painting. 'Well done. Down to a single hair. But — is this person alive? Look at the eyes. It's a likeness, all right, but it's empty.'

$userName put strength into the brush-gripping hand. 'And your painting. Not even a likeness, and you speak of spirit.'

From that day the two contended every day. Without anyone bidding it. They painted the same model seated, and when done, glared at each other's paintings. To find flaws. Whether it was hatred or what, $userName did not yet know the name.

chapters.heading: The Eye That Searches for Flaws
chapters.body:
$userName was absorbed in finding flaws in $celebName's painting.

When $celebName finished a painting, $userName pressed the nose close and looked. A fold of cloth gone wrong in one place, one finger joint too few, the ink-shading splashed there — finding a flaw, $userName felt a clear satisfaction.

But the more $userName peered into $celebName's painting to find flaws, the stranger the thing $userName learned.

How $celebName put light into a pupil. How, with a hair's turn of a lip corner, $celebName painted a person's heart. From the painting glared at to find flaws, $userName was, one by one, stealing and learning $celebName's secrets.

It was the same for $celebName. Peering into $userName's painting in the name of finding flaws, $celebName learned $userName's precise lines. How to raise hairs one strand at a time. How to hold cloth folds without a single disorder.

The two, finding each other's flaws, were learning from each other. Without knowing it themselves.

One rainy day, only the two were left in the studio. $celebName spread out the painting drawn that day and left the seat. $userName, as always, pressed the nose close to find a flaw. But that day no flaw was visible. Peer as $userName might. What was visible instead was — the fine wrinkles caught at the eyes of the old man in the painting. $userName did not know how to paint those wrinkles. The person in $userName's painting was always smooth, without a single wrinkle. A likeness, all right, but a face that had never lived.

That day, instead of finding a flaw, $userName copied those fine wrinkles. On the own practice paper. One strand, one strand.

$celebName returned and saw it. Said nothing. Only the next day, peering into $userName's painting — was copying $userName's precise cloth folds onto the own practice paper.

The two looked at each other's papers. Caught at it, and yet neither called it a flaw.

Spirit gradually settled into $userName's painting. $celebName's painting gradually grew precise. The two brushes grew to resemble each other. Resembled, while hating.

The master painter one day set the two paintings side by side and looked long. '...You two, your paintings have grown good lately. Both.' Then smiled faintly. 'You'll come to know it someday — that you should be grateful to each other for the hatred.'

$userName let those words pass. For then $userName did not know.

chapters.heading: The Royal Portraitist
chapters.body:
The year $userName turned twenty-five, the Painting Bureau stirred.

The royal portrait. A command came down to paint anew the portrait of the king. The painter who painted that portrait was called the royal portraitist. The highest seat a painter might receive perhaps once in a life, or not at all.

The Bureau opened a contest. The painters painted the same model, and the single most outstanding became royal portraitist. There was one seat only.

$userName and $celebName remained as the last two.

Three days before the contest, something happened. $celebName injured the right hand. The hand was caught in the studio door. The hand that gripped the brush. Two fingers swelled, and the brush could not be gripped.

$celebName sat in a corner of the studio with the swollen hand bound. That hand, straight all life.

$userName could not sleep that night.

It was a strange thing. If $celebName could not enter the contest, the royal portraitist was $userName's. That seat, perhaps once in a life, rolling in even by standing still. $userName ought to have been glad.

And yet was not glad at all. $userName could not bear it. A contest without $celebName, the royal portraitist seized by stepping over a swollen hand — none of it was a thing to boast of. What $userName had wished to paint all life was a royal portrait, not a royal portrait that had sat $celebName down.

Only then did $userName know. That the painting whose flaws $userName had searched for was, in truth, the painting $userName most wished to resemble.

The next dawn, $userName went to $celebName's studio. Carrying one of the own brushes.

'The hand,' $userName said. 'If the right hand won't do, grip with the left. A brushwork drawn with the strength of the wrist alone — I'll teach you. In three days you can paint one royal portrait.'

'...Have you gone mad?' $celebName said, hiding the swollen hand. 'It's by my not entering the contest that you become royal portraitist.'

'Shut up and take the brush.' $userName's voice trembled. 'Becoming royal portraitist in a contest without you, I won't do it. A seat received that way stays a blemish for life. To beat a sound you — that's a boast. Get up, and match brushes with me properly.'

chapters.heading: Under One Lamp
chapters.body:
For the days $celebName first gripped the brush in the left hand, the two stayed up nearly all night under one lamp.

$celebName's right hand was swollen. $userName ground the ink. The ink for two. So $celebName had only to grip the brush, $userName spread the paper, drew the water, trimmed the lamp wick — did it all. In the studio of a person said to be hated.

'...Why do you wait on me,' $celebName asked, looking at the swollen hand. 'If I can't become royal portraitist, that seat is yours.'

$userName stopped the hand grinding ink.

'Ten years,' $userName said. 'Ten years I peered into your painting in the name of finding flaws. And then I knew. It wasn't finding flaws — I was learning from you. Without your painting — whose painting do I look at to correct my brush. If you set down the brush, my brush stops there too.'

$celebName watched the lamp flame a long while.

'...Me too,' $celebName said. 'Finding flaws in your painting, I copied and learned all your lines. The likeness that came into my painting — all stolen from you. For ten years. Stolen, while hating.'

That night, the two for the first time admitted what had been beneath the label of hatred. It was the other name for a bond of two who had copied each other's brushes most deeply for ten years.

As the lamp oil ran low, $userName said. '$celebName. Your left-hand brushwork, the wrist angle still floats. Give it here.' $userName took $celebName's left hand and drew one stroke together with own hand. Not the swollen hand but the sound one, two people's hands gripping one brush together.

One straight line was drawn on the paper. $celebName looked at it a long while.

'...Whose line is this. Yours, or mine.'

'Both, I suppose,' $userName said. 'Having hated ten years, it's about time we drew one line together.'

$celebName smiled even with the swollen hand. Dawn light came into the studio window.

chapters.heading: The Left-Hand Brush
chapters.body:
Through the three days $celebName mended the hand, the two spent day and night in one studio together.

$celebName gripped the brush with the left hand. At first could not draw one line straight. The brush trembled, the ink ran. $userName held that hand from beside. From the angle of gripping the brush, again.

'Draw with the wrist. Not the fingers. Two fingers are swollen, so make the path with the rest.'

'...Since when did you worry about my hand.'

'It isn't worry. To beat a sound you is a boast, that's why.'

$celebName broke into a grin even with the swollen hand. Both knew. That it was worry. That the eye that searched for flaws had become, before long, the eye that watched the other most tenderly.

$userName taught $celebName all the secrets of own brushwork. The way of using the wrist known only to oneself all life, to a rival. And in doing so $userName learned too. That even drawn with $celebName's left hand, the spirit of a person still settled into that clumsy line. That even with an injured hand, that person's eye watched the person.

On the third night, $celebName's left-hand line kept trembling. Throwing away one more sheet, $celebName set down the brush.

'...Let's stop,' $celebName said. 'A royal portrait drawn with the left hand can't be enshrined. That's a discourtesy to the king. Better I withdraw from the contest.'

$userName picked up that thrown-away sheet. A sheet with a trembling line. But at the end of that trembling line, there was the gaze of an old man. The hand trembled, but the eyes were watching a person.

'Look at this,' $userName held the sheet out. 'The line trembles. But these eyes, alive. The discourtesy to the king is — not a lack of likeness, but enshrining a face that has never lived. If your trembling line paints a living face, that is a true royal portrait. Take it up again.'

$celebName looked at that sheet a long while. And with the trembling left hand, gripped the brush again.

The night before the contest, the two sat side by side on the studio veranda. Moonlight lay over the courtyard like paper.

'$userName.' $celebName said it, watching the moon. 'Tomorrow, let's both paint to the end. Whoever becomes royal portraitist — let neither stop the hand to the end.'

'Of course,' $userName said. 'And the royal portraitist will be me.'

'Dream on. Even with the left hand I beat you.'

The two laughed. A pair to match brushes tomorrow, and yet that night alone, they laughed like old companions.

chapters.heading: The Morning of the Royal Portrait Contest
chapters.body:
On the morning of the royal portrait contest, the two readied their brushes in the Bureau studio together.

$userName five of own brushes, $celebName three brushes pared to fit the left hand. $celebName's swollen right hand lay bound in cloth on the chest. $userName wrapped $celebName's brushes into the bundle together. Because a swollen hand cannot tie a knot.

'...$userName.' $celebName said it. 'Today only one royal portraitist is chosen. One seat only.'

'I know,' $userName said, tying the knot.

'If you are chosen,' $celebName said. 'I will congratulate you sincerely. But — if I am chosen, can you bear it? It's a seat you've wished for all your life.'

$userName stopped the hand tying the knot.

'I'll bear it,' $userName said. 'If you're chosen, my lines go into that royal portrait too. For ten years you stole the likeness from my painting. Half that royal portrait is mine. So — it isn't bearing it; half my painting is simply being enshrined. In the highest seat of the Painting Bureau.'

$celebName looked at $userName a long while. The swollen hand on the chest.

'...How do you say things like that,' $celebName said. 'Saying we hated each other ten years.'

'Seems it wasn't hatred,' $userName slung the bundle on a shoulder. 'Peering only into your painting for ten years — that would have been something other than hatred. Looking at it now.'

$celebName smiled even with the swollen hand.

'...Let's go. We'll be late.'

The two went out to the Bureau courtyard. Two sheets of paper for the royal portrait were being spread in the courtyard. One sheet for a straight line, and one for a trembling line.

$userName stood before own paper, $celebName before own. The examiner commanded the ink ground. The two painters began grinding ink at once. As they had ten years ago, in the same studio.

chapters.heading: Two Royal Portraits
chapters.body:
The day of the contest. Two sheets of paper for the king's portrait were spread in the Bureau courtyard.

$userName lifted the brush. With straight, precise lines. Did not miss one dragon-pattern of the king's robe, one strand of beard. But $userName's brush was not as before. The years of peering into $celebName's painting to find flaws had breathed spirit into $userName's brush. In the king's pupils, light was alive.

$celebName lifted the brush. With the left hand. The swollen right hand laid on the knee. The line was not as straight as $userName's. A tremble showed. But within that trembling line, the king's majesty and weariness were held together. Even with the left hand, $celebName's eyes watched the person.

The Bureau officials set the two sheets side by side and looked. A long while, without a word.

And the verdict came down.

The one chosen as royal portraitist was — $celebName. The painting drawn with the left hand.

$userName stood in the courtyard and heard that verdict. The chest dropped once. It was a seat wished for all life.

But the next moment, $userName was smiling. Without meaning to.

$celebName walked toward $userName. Hiding the swollen right hand, held out the sound left hand.

'...$userName,' $celebName said. 'This royal portrait, half of it is yours. If you hadn't come to the studio that dawn, hadn't taught me the brushwork — I couldn't even have gripped the brush. And.' $celebName paused a moment. 'The light alive in my pupils — I learned that from your painting. Peering into your painting for ten years in the name of finding flaws.'

$userName took that hand.

'...Me too,' $userName said. 'The spirit settled into my royal portrait's pupils. Finding flaws in your painting for ten years — I learned it from you.'

The two looked at each other. The person they had thought they hated for ten years. The person from whom each had copied and learned most deeply for ten years.

An official lifted the two royal portraits together.

'Both of this portrait will be enshrined,' the official said. 'One sheet painted the likeness, one the spirit. Only with the two side by side — is it at last the king's whole face. You two were each other's mirror all your lives. The kind where, without one, the other becomes a half.'

$userName and $celebName looked at the two royal portraits together. A straight line and a trembling line met within one person's face.

'The next royal portrait, I'll be portraitist,' $userName said. 'So until then, look after that hand. To beat a sound-handed you — that's a boast.'

$celebName laughed. Holding up the swollen hand.

'Dream on.'

chung.epilogue:
$userName has one habit that hardly mends. The habit of searching for flaws first when looking at something.

That habit rises hardest when $userName looks at what $celebName has made. A hair's-breadth gone wrong there, a beat empty there — $userName's eye moves so automatically. Having watched that way for ten years, the eye was made that way. From those days at the Painting Bureau studio, searching for the flaws of the rival painter's picture.

But $userName knows. The more $userName peers in to find flaws, the more $userName learns something from $celebName. How $celebName holds a person's heart with a hair's-breadth, how $celebName fills an empty place with spirit. The eye that searched for flaws has become, before long, the eye that steals and learns most from that person. The ten years $userName believed to be hatred were, in truth, the ten years of copying and learning most deeply.

So on a day $celebName's painting looks to tremble, $userName stops the hand that searches for flaws. That to win aiming at a trembling line becomes a blemish for life, $userName learned in that royal portrait contest. On such a day $userName sees, instead of a flaw, the gaze alive at the end of the trembling line. Those eyes that watch a person though the hand trembles.

One person paints the likeness, one paints the spirit. Only with the two side by side does it at last become one whole sheet. So $userName today too searches for flaws in $celebName's painting — and in the end hones one's own brush finer. With the heart of that Painting Bureau, where two sheets were enshrined together.

chung.celebRole: wandering swordsman who never stopped provoking

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName picked a fight every time they met $userName. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who wouldn't lose to anyone, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A friction streak can set two against each other, so each meeting flared into a duel of wooden swords. Toward the end of the duel, $celebName would toss out one line — "your left foot was late today" — and vanish.
paragraphs.jeon: One day, in the last exchange of a great bout, $userName recalled that left foot $celebName had pointed out in passing and turned the match around. "The win is yours, but I watched your form," — that line proved sincere at last, so $userName looked at the empty space a long while.
paragraphs.gyeol: A friction streak — sharpening each other through the clash — can carry into this life. So the more you clash with $celebName, the more one weak point may be filled.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So $celebName's live remark, once like a harsh word, might end up growing you a notch.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So one prickly line from a $celebName interview may, the more you chew it, become deep advice.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So $celebName's sharp match commentary might, the more you hear it, make you firmer.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So $celebName's blunt remark may sting at first, yet end up raising you.

chung.era: the 1980s, the season of the circuit
chung.title: The Two at the Last Corner
chung.logline: Two racers who ran the same track 0.3 seconds apart. The face in the rearview mirror, stared at to overtake, becomes in the end the most trusted face.

chapters.heading: 0.3 Seconds
chapters.body:
The first time $userName came to know that person was in the rearview mirror.

The 1980s. A circuit in Europe. Machines turning with a roar. $userName was a rookie racer. A driver with no name, about the hundred-and-first, who had only just begun a season.

At the first grand prix, $userName was running mid-pack. Then on the last lap, a red machine filled the rearview mirror. Fast, fearsome.

At the last corner that machine dove into $userName's inside line. Razor-close. Tires almost touching. And then crossed the finish line ahead of $userName by 0.3 seconds.

The driver who took off the helmet was $celebName. The most watched rookie of the year. A name on everyone's lips.

$celebName looked at $userName once in the pit. Up and down. And said. 'The last corner. You brake half a beat early there. That's where the 0.3 seconds came from.'

And went off. Neither praise nor sneer, only the fact thrown out.

$userName was galled by that 0.3 seconds. Galled by the words half a beat. To $celebName, $userName was — just a rookie who brakes early at the last corner.

That evening there was a team meeting. The director introduced the season's two rookies. The top prospect $celebName, and — the director recalled $userName's name a beat late. 'And, um… $userName. Brought on for the potential.' No one in the meeting room looked long $userName's way. Everyone's eyes were on $celebName.

$userName was galled by that beat-late naming. More than the 0.3 seconds in the mirror.

The meeting over, as $userName walked toward the garage, $celebName fell in beside.

'The director named you late,' $celebName said. 'Don't mind it. The director memorizes names only by finish order. Next race, cut in front of me, and he'll name you first.'

'...Are you consoling me?'

'It isn't consolation,' $celebName said. 'It's the fact. Cut in front of me fast. An empty track in my mirror is no fun for me either.'

And went off. $userName, unable to tell if it was provocation or what, was the more galled.

That night, $userName stayed in the garage and sat by own machine. In the next bay over, a pace away, was $celebName's red machine. Same team, same garage. By what trick of fate, the season's two rookies were assigned to the same team.

'I will overtake, without fail.' $userName glared at the red machine and thought so. That car that filled the mirror. Whether it was hatred or what, $userName did not yet know the name.

chapters.heading: The Car in the Mirror
chapters.body:
From that day $userName's season changed.

$userName went out to the circuit at dawn. On the track with no one, turned the same corner a hundred, a thousand times. Pushed the brake point of the last corner a meter later each time. To shave the 0.3 seconds.

But one dawn, there was a machine on the track already. A red machine. It was $celebName.

The two, without a word, turned their own tracks. Without a greeting. But both knew. That the other, the same way, in secret, cutting sleep, was turning the same corner.

After that day the two people's dawns came earlier and earlier. When $userName was there first, the next day $celebName was there first. On the empty circuit, glancing sidelong at the other's lap times.

The races went on. $userName and $celebName always ran close. When one was ahead, one filled the mirror. The two met at the last corner every grand prix. The 0.3 seconds became 0.1.

$userName measured own speed by the red machine in the mirror. When $celebName grew faster $userName grew faster, when $celebName dove inside $userName held the inside line firmer. With $celebName in the mirror, $userName could not lift the foot off the accelerator a single moment.

After one race, as $userName came into the pit, $celebName approached. A stopwatch in hand.

'Turn 3,' $celebName said. 'Change your line there. A line that enters a beat late and exits fast. Then you'd shave 0.1 seconds more.'

And went off. $userName stood a long while, stopwatch in hand. That $celebName had watched own lap — down to each corner — was a strange thing.

chapters.heading: The Crumpled Machine
chapters.body:
At the end of the season, something happened.

A race in the rain. $celebName's machine slid on a wet corner. Struck the wall. It was not a great crash, but the machine's front crumpled, and $celebName's wrist sprained. The hand that gripped the wheel.

Five days to the next race. The crew shook their heads. 'The machine can be repaired, but the wrist… to grip the wheel at full grip within five days is too much.'

$celebName sat in a corner of the garage with the wrist bound. That hand, the fastest all life.

$userName could not sleep that night.

It was a strange thing. If $celebName could not race, the season's rookie title was $userName's. That title the two had contended for at first and second, rolling in even by standing still. $userName ought to have been glad.

And yet was not glad at all. $userName could not bear it. A grid without $celebName, a race with an empty mirror, a rookie title received that way — none of it was a thing to boast of. What $userName had wished to beat was a fast $celebName, not a sprained-wrist $celebName.

Only then did $userName know. That it was not to overtake — but to run side by side.

The next dawn, $userName went not to the track but to $celebName's garage. Carrying own data notebook.

'The wrist,' $userName said. 'If full grip won't do, change the grip. A line that turns using the wrist less — I drew it up new, for each corner. In five days you can memorize that line.'

$userName held out the notebook. Corner-by-corner lines drawn through the night. $celebName's driving, rebuilt to spare the sprained wrist.

'...Why are you like this,' $celebName asked, looking at the notebook. 'It's by my not racing that you're rookie champion.'

'Shut up and look.' $userName's voice trembled. 'A rookie title received in a race with an empty mirror, I won't do it. Even received, I'd not be glad. To beat a sound you — that's a boast. To win aiming at a sprained-wrist you is a blemish for life. Get up. In five days, stand on the grid.'

chapters.heading: Five Days in the Garage
chapters.body:
For the days $celebName began to learn the new line, the two stayed up nearly all night in the garage.

$celebName's wrist was swollen inside the brace. $userName did the work of two. Straightened $celebName's crumpled machine together, worked on own machine too. Oiled the machine of a person said to be hated.

'...You really are strange,' $celebName said, sitting on the garage floor. 'If I can't race with a sprained wrist, the rookie title is yours. So why are you straightening my machine.'

$userName set down the wrench.

'The mirror,' $userName said. 'All season your red machine was in my rearview mirror. Because it was there, I couldn't lift my foot off the accelerator. Not for a moment. If you vanish from the grid — my mirror goes empty. Then what do I push the accelerator to the end for.'

$celebName watched the ceiling a long while.

'...Same for me,' $celebName said. 'Your car was always in my mirror too. A car chasing fearsomely though just a rookie. Because it was there, I couldn't stop either. We lived a whole season as each other's rearview mirror.'

That night, the two for the first time admitted what had been beneath the label of hatred. It was the other name for a bond of two who had run a whole season as each other's rearview mirror.

As dawn drew near, $userName sat by the simulator. '$celebName. Turn 3, run it once more. The wrist-twisting still shows.' $celebName took the simulator wheel. $userName laid a hand over that wrist from beside. So it would not twist.

'...Like this?' $celebName turned the corner.

'Yes. That's it,' $userName said. 'You lost only 0.1 seconds turning without twisting the wrist. Done.'

A lap time showed on the simulator screen. $celebName saw it and laughed. Wearing the wrist brace.

'...I won't say thank you,' $celebName said. 'I'll say it after the race. After beating you with a sound hand.'

'Dream on,' $userName laughed. Dawn light came in through the garage shutter.

chapters.heading: The Newly Drawn Line
chapters.body:
Through the five days $celebName mended the wrist, the two spent day and night in the garage together.

$celebName, wearing the wrist brace, sat at the simulator and learned the new line. The line $userName had drawn up. A line that uses the wrist less. At first it was awkward. $celebName's hand was used to an aggressive line that dove inside.

$userName watched the data together from beside.

'Turn 3, don't twist the wrist. Enter a beat late and use the line wide. You barely lose any time.'

'...You're good at drawing lines. I didn't know.'

'A rookie has to watch all sorts of things to survive,' $userName laughed. 'You don't know because you've always been ahead in the mirror.'

$celebName laughed aloud for the first time. Wearing the wrist brace.

Both knew. That this was not hatred. Only, the heart of wanting to run side by side was so large that they had put the wrong name, hatred, on it.

By day each tuned their own machine. When stuck, they watched each other's data. $celebName fixed $userName's last-corner brake point, and $userName memorized $celebName's new line together.

The night before the race, the two sat side by side on the garage rooftop. Looking down at the circuit lights.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'Tomorrow, let's both run to the end. Whoever is ahead — don't lift off the accelerator until the last corner.'

'Of course,' $userName said. 'And the rookie title, I take it.'

'Dream on. Even with a sprained wrist I beat you.'

The two laughed. A pair to contend a second over the same track tomorrow, and yet that night alone, they laughed like old friends.

chapters.heading: The Morning of the Last Grand Prix
chapters.body:
On the morning of the last grand prix, the two inspected their machines in the pit garage together.

$userName own machine, $celebName own machine with the front repaired. $celebName's wrist was still inside the brace. $userName worked once more on $celebName's machine wheel grip, easing it off the wrist.

'...$userName.' $celebName said it, helmet in hand. 'When today ends, the season's rookie champion is decided. You and me, one of us.'

'I know,' $userName said, tightening the grip.

'If I take it,' $celebName said. 'Will you be all right? I know you ran the whole season watching that title.'

$userName set down the wrench and looked at $celebName.

'I'll be all right,' $userName said. 'If you become rookie champion, my rearview mirror goes in there. All season your car was in my mirror, so you couldn't stop either. Half your title is — me chasing you in the mirror. Then it's enough.'

$celebName tucked the helmet under an arm and looked at $userName a long while.

'...Don't say things like that,' $celebName said. 'We both run to the end. Whoever becomes rookie champion, don't lift off the accelerator until the last corner. That's our match.'

The pit crew called out. 'Grid lineup, ten minutes.'

The two put on their helmets. Climbed into their machines. In $userName's mirror, the red machine of the next grid spot came in. The car that had been in that spot all season.

The radio came on. $celebName's voice was heard. '$userName. See you at the last corner. Whoever's ahead there — let's end it by 0.1 seconds.'

'Of course,' $userName answered the radio. 'And that 0.1 seconds, I'm ahead.'

'Dream on.'

The engines roared all at once. The lights came on in turn. And — went out.

chapters.heading: The Last Corner
chapters.body:
The season's last grand prix. A race with the rookie title on it.

$userName stood on the grid. With the red machine in the mirror. To the last lap, the two ran within a second. When one was ahead, one filled the mirror. The stands rose. Two rookies were dragging the season's last race to a one-second gap.

And the last corner.

$userName was ahead. $celebName filled the mirror full. $celebName's machine dove into the inside line. With that wrist-sparing line $userName had drawn new.

$userName could close the inside. Closing the line, $userName could block the overtake and cross the finish first. The rookie title.

But $userName — did not close the inside line.

Half a beat, $userName opened the line. As far as $celebName could turn the wheel with the wrist-braced hand.

The two machines came out of the last corner side by side. Tires almost touching. And the finish line.

0.05 seconds.

$celebName's red machine crossed the finish 0.05 seconds first.

The crowd roared. The season's rookie champion was $celebName. $userName came in second.

In the pit, $celebName took off the helmet. Walked to $userName with the wrist-braced hand.

'...$userName,' $celebName said. 'The last corner. You opened the inside line, didn't you. You could have closed it.'

'I didn't open it,' $userName said, taking off the helmet. 'A line your wrist couldn't enter isn't a true match. To fail to block a sound-grip you — that's my defeat, and a boast. To win blocking a sprained-wrist you stays a blemish for life.'

$celebName looked at $userName a long while. And held out the wrist-braced hand.

'This rookie title, half of it is yours,' $celebName said. 'If you hadn't brought the line notebook that dawn, I couldn't have stood on the grid. If you hadn't sat beside me when I sprained the wrist. And — if I hadn't heard your machine on the track every dawn, I'd have lifted my foot off the accelerator long ago.'

$userName took that hand.

'Next season I take the championship,' $userName said. 'So until then, look after that wrist. To beat a sound you is a boast.'

$celebName laughed. Waving the wrist brace.

'Dream on.'

The circuit lights lit the two machines at once. Two rookies who had known each other only by the rearview mirror, become each other's most dependable mirror.

chung.epilogue:
When an engine sound comes from far off, $userName, without meaning to, listens.

Within that sound, what $userName searches for is one person's car. $celebName. The red machine that filled $userName's rearview mirror all season. A hundred years on, $userName's ear still searches for that car.

Each time $userName watches $celebName go ahead, $userName cannot lift the foot off the accelerator. When $celebName turns one corner faster, $userName redraws the corner of own track. When $celebName finds a better line, $userName reworks own line. It is not from hating to fall behind. With that car in the mirror, $userName at last feels that $userName is running.

There are days $celebName's machine looks to slide. Days it looks to waver. On such days the wish to overtake does not come to $userName. Instead — at the last corner, $userName wants to open the inside line half a beat. That to win blocking a wavering car becomes a blemish for life, $userName learned at that last corner.

When $celebName crosses the finish first, inside that 0.05 seconds is the whole season $userName chased in the rearview mirror. So $userName searches for that engine sound today too. Waiting for that car to come into the mirror, with the heart to draw one's own line one more time.

chung.celebRole: navigator who quarreled over the course

paragraphs.gi: In $era, on deck $celebName always argued a course different from $userName's. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who believed only their own course right, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A friction streak can knock two together, so voices ran high over the course all through the voyage. Even while quarreling, $celebName secretly tucked a note of the reefs $userName had missed into a corner of the chart.
paragraphs.jeon: One night, in a storm, the ship dodged a reef thanks to that mark, and $userName saw the quarreling words had all been to save the ship. The one clashing had in truth held the helm alongside, so $userName bowed their head.
paragraphs.gyeol: A friction streak can reach into this life the course that settled through the wavering. So even clashing with $celebName, you might end up going the same way.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So you may tilt your head over $celebName's activity direction, yet end up looking the same way.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might differ on $celebName's project choice, yet end up cheering together.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So even when opinions split on $celebName's match strategy, you might wish the same result.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you may quibble at $celebName's content path, yet end up on the same side.

chung.era: a kingdom of swords and magic, the night of the martial tournament
chung.title: The Night to Decide the Realm's Strongest
chung.logline: Two swordsmen who clashed for the title of the realm's strongest. The sword leveled to drag the other down becomes, in the end, the sword that blocks the dark together.

chapters.heading: Two Swords of Two Hues
chapters.body:
$userName took up the sword as a child, from a wandering swordsman.

A kingdom of swords and magic. Once every four years, a martial tournament was held in the royal city. Swordsmen from across the kingdom gathered to single out one person. To that one were given the title 'the realm's strongest' and the royal treasure-sword.

$userName was from the kingdom's frontier. A swordsman with no house, no patron. A nobody, about the hundred-and-first of the tournament.

In the first tournament's preliminary, a swordsman stood across from $userName. A person with a sword that held a blue light in the blade. That was $celebName. The head of the royal city's sword academy. A name everyone counted as a champion candidate.

$userName's sword was plain steel. No light turned in it. A merely solid sword, hammered in a frontier smithy.

The first exchange. $celebName's blue sword lightly flicked away $userName's steel sword. The next exchange, the next. $userName, in the end, could not properly receive a single exchange. Eliminated in the preliminary in ten exchanges.

$celebName lowered the sword and looked at $userName. 'Frontier swordwork. Rough. Still.' Paused a moment. 'One exchange, you read my sword exactly. That last one. Polished — by the next tournament you'd climb to the main round.'

And went off. Neither praise nor sneer, only the fact thrown out.

$userName was galled by those words 'next tournament.' That oneself was eliminated in the first tournament's preliminary, while to $celebName that was a natural starting line.

That night, $userName stood outside the wall of the royal sword academy and looked in. At the courtyard where blue swords trained. 'I will get inside there, without fail. And I will break that blue sword.' Whether it was hatred or what, $userName did not yet know the name.

chapters.heading: Inside the Wall and Out
chapters.body:
$userName honed for four years.

Did not return to the frontier. Stayed in the royal city, and swung the sword outside the academy wall. To the count heard from within, alone outside. There was no money for the academy's tuition, but the teaching that leaked over the wall was free.

One dawn, a person stood before $userName swinging the sword outside the wall. It was $celebName.

'...You're here every day,' $celebName said. 'Outside the wall, listening to our count.'

$userName lowered the sword. Thought, caught, $userName must leave. But $celebName said an unexpected thing.

'It's cold outside the wall. Come in. If I, the head, bring you in, no one stops it.'

$userName's pride was stung by that kindness. 'I don't need charity.'

'It isn't charity,' $celebName slung the blue sword on a shoulder. 'If you've grown four years outside the wall by our count, you've become a fairly troublesome opponent by now. A troublesome opponent — I have to keep close and duel every day, or I go dull. That's why I bring you in.'

From that day $userName was inside the academy wall. And dueled $celebName every day. Blue sword and steel sword clashed in the courtyard.

$userName's steel sword grew fiercer. Receiving $celebName's blue sword every day. $celebName's sword grew sharper too. Facing a troublesome steel sword every day. The two swords were each other's whetstone. Hating, they honed each other.

$userName, once eliminated in a preliminary, now lost to no one in the academy. Save one person, $celebName alone. The match with $celebName was always by a hair. If $userName won three days, $celebName won four.

chapters.heading: The Cracked Sword
chapters.body:
A month before the martial tournament, something happened.

It was not a sword, but the dark.

From the kingdom's north, monsters pushed down. There was an old tale that once every four years, in the season the tournament was held, the northern seal weakened. It was true. A horde of monsters bound for the royal city blocked the path of the academy's swordsmen.

In that battle a crack ran through $celebName's blue sword. Cutting the hard scales of a monster. The blue light that split the blade dimmed. And $celebName himself injured a shoulder. The shoulder that swung the sword.

The academy smith shook the head. 'This sword can't be mended. The blue light won't come back. A month to the tournament… too little time for a hand to learn a new sword, and for a shoulder to heal.'

$celebName sat in a corner of the academy with the cracked sword on the knee. That sword, blue all life.

$userName could not sleep that night.

It was a strange thing. If $celebName could not enter the tournament, the one nearest the title 'realm's strongest' was $userName. That seat honed for four years, coming nearer even by standing still. $userName ought to have been glad.

And yet was not glad at all. $userName could not bear it. A tournament without $celebName, a final with no blue sword, a title received that way — none of it was a thing to boast of. What $userName had wished to break was a shining blue sword, not a cracked one.

Only then did $userName know. That $userName had endured four years outside the wall not to drag $celebName down — but to stand on the same platform as that blue sword.

The next dawn, $userName went to $celebName with own steel sword.

'The sword,' $userName said, setting down the steel sword. 'If your blue sword is ruined, use this. It's hammered in a frontier smithy, so no light turns in it, but it's solid. And if the shoulder won't do — frontier swordwork that cuts with the wrist, not the shoulder. I'll teach you. In a month it sits in the hand.'

'...Have you gone mad?' $celebName asked. 'It's by my not entering that you become the strongest.'

'Shut up and take the sword.' $userName's voice trembled. 'A strongest received in a tournament without you, I won't do it. Even received, I'd not be glad. To break a shining blue sword — that's a boast. To win aiming at a cracked sword is a blemish for life. Get up, and match swords with me properly.'

chapters.heading: A Month in the Courtyard
chapters.body:
For the days $celebName began to set a hand to the steel sword, the two stayed up nearly all night in the academy courtyard.

$celebName's shoulder was swollen inside the brace. $userName did the work of two. Swept the courtyard, tended the swords, stopped by the smithy to ask them to see to the steel sword's edge. The sword of a person said to be hated.

'...Why do you wait on me,' $celebName asked, touching the swollen shoulder. 'If I can't enter the tournament, the realm's strongest is yours.'

$userName stopped the hand tending the sword.

'Four years,' $userName said. 'For four years I grew outside the wall, on nothing but your academy's count. Because your blue sword was inside the wall, I couldn't set down the sword outside it. Having a sword to catch up to — that's what made me endure four years. If that sword vanishes, what do I catch up to.'

$celebName watched the stars a long while.

'...Me too,' $celebName said. 'Outside the wall, a troublesome sword was swung every day. Afraid of it, even I, the head, couldn't go idle. We were each other's whetstone, across one wall. For four years.'

That night, the two for the first time admitted what had been beneath the label of hatred. It was the other name for a bond of two who had honed each other across one wall.

As dawn drew near, $userName lifted $celebName's steel sword and put it into that hand. 'Don't use the shoulder. The wrist and the waist.' $userName stood behind $celebName's back, laid a hand over that wrist, and drew one stroke together.

The steel sword cut the air. And — a faint light turned in the blade. Not a blue light, but a solid light near gray.

$celebName looked at that light a long while. '...Why does light turn in this sword. It's a frontier steel sword.'

'Because two people's hearts went in together, I suppose,' $userName said. 'They say a sword resembles its owner's heart. Having hated four years, it's about time we lit one sword together.'

$celebName smiled even with the swollen shoulder. Dawn light came into the courtyard.

chapters.heading: The Light in the Steel Sword
chapters.body:
Through that month $celebName mended the shoulder, the two spent day and night in the academy courtyard together.

$celebName gripped $userName's steel sword. At first it did not fit the hand. Heavier than the blue sword, and with no light. Swinging it, the shoulder ached. $userName taught the frontier swordwork from beside. From how to cut with the wrist instead of the shoulder.

'Don't use the shoulder. Make the path with the wrist and the waist. In the frontier, swordsmen with injured shoulders were common. So there is a way to cut without the shoulder.'

'...You're good at teaching. I didn't know.'

'I learned alone outside the wall for four years,' $userName laughed. 'You don't know because you've always been inside the wall.'

$celebName laughed aloud for the first time. Wearing the shoulder brace.

Both knew. That this was not hatred. Only, the heart of wanting to stand on the same platform was so large that they had put the wrong name, hatred, on it.

A strange thing happened. The more $celebName swung the steel sword, the more a faint light began to turn in that lightless sword. Not a blue light. A solid light, near gray, of steel as it is. The smith saw it and said. 'A sword resembles its owner's heart. The light turning in this sword is — because two people's hearts went into it together.'

The night before the tournament, the two sat side by side in the middle of the courtyard. The stars shone cold like a blade.

'$userName.' $celebName said it, watching the stars. 'Tomorrow, let's both climb to the final. So that the last is you and me — and no one else.'

'Of course,' $userName said. 'And the realm's strongest will be me.'

'Dream on. Even borrowing your sword I beat you.'

The two laughed. A pair to cross swords tomorrow, and yet that night alone, they laughed like brothers.

chapters.heading: The Dawn of the Tournament
chapters.body:
On the dawn of the tournament, the two walked the road to the royal city together.

$userName with a steel sword, $celebName with another steel sword $userName had lent. $celebName's shoulder was still inside the brace. The two steel swords shone gray in the dawn light.

On the hill road $celebName stopped. Far off the royal city arena was visible. Banners shook in the dawn wind.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'If we meet in the final today, don't go easy for an injured shoulder.'

'I won't go easy,' $userName said. 'But I won't aim at the injured-shoulder side. That isn't going easy — only so, whoever wins, is it the true realm's strongest.'

$celebName slung the gray sword aslant on a shoulder. In that posture of four years ago, eliminating $userName in the preliminary. But this time there was no sneer.

'Four years ago,' $celebName said. 'When I brought you inside the wall, everyone said it was strange. Why bring in a rival. Do you know what I said then? That a troublesome enemy has to be kept close, or I go dull. That was sincere. But.' $celebName paused a moment. 'Looking now, that was only half sincere. The other half was — I just wanted to watch your sword up close.'

$userName could not answer a long while. Because $userName was the same. That enduring four years outside the wall had been half a heart to break $celebName, and half a heart to be beside that blue sword, $userName now knew.

'Let's go,' $userName said. 'We'll be late.'

At the arena entrance the two parted. Each to a waiting room. Turning away, $celebName said.

'$userName. Both climb to the final. Lose to another swordsman — and I'll sneer at you for life.'

'You, rather,' $userName said. 'Don't fall along the way for an injured shoulder. See you in the final.'

Within the arena, a bell rang the opening.

chapters.heading: The Final, and Beyond
chapters.body:
The martial tournament. Swordsmen from across the kingdom gathered in the royal city arena.

$userName climbed. With the steel sword. One bout, two, to the main round. A frontier swordsman once eliminated in a preliminary now swung a sword no one could ignore.

$celebName climbed too. With the steel sword $userName had lent. The sword turning a gray light. Wearing the shoulder brace. A head who had lost the blue sword climbed to the final even with a sword of a different hue.

And the final. As promised, the two stood at the two ends of the arena. Holding identical frontier steel swords. One in $userName's hand, one lent by $userName.

The king watched the two from the dais. The royal treasure-sword on his knees.

'Begin.'

Two swords met. Steel and steel. One exchange, ten, twenty. The match did not break. The two had watched each other's swords for four years. Where $userName meant to cut, $celebName's sword was there first; the gap $celebName meant to slip into, $userName's sword filled first.

That was when the arena's sky darkened.

The northern seal collapsed at last. A horde of monsters covered the royal city's sky and poured down into the arena. The seal that, once every four years, was said to weaken in the tournament's season.

The crowd screamed. The swordsmen scattered.

$userName and $celebName — did not scatter.

In the middle of the final, the two turned the swords that faced each other, just as they were. The two swords that leveled at each other, at once faced outward. Back to back. Like people who had fought that way all their lives.

'$userName.' $celebName said it past the back. 'Take the left. My shoulder can't block the right.'

'I know,' $userName regripped the steel sword. 'Your weak side, I've watched it four years. Press your back. I block it.'

The two steel swords cut the horde of monsters. Two swords turning a gray light, not blue. Where $userName meant to cut, $celebName emptied first; the place $celebName's shoulder could not reach, $userName's sword filled. Every sword-path memorized leveling at each other all their lives now unfolded for the same side.

As dawn drew near, the last monster fell. In the arena only two swordsmen stood, back to back. Gasping for breath.

The king came down from the dais. With the royal treasure-sword. But did not press it into one person's hand. Laid it on both their hands together.

'The realm's strongest is not one person,' the king said. 'What guarded the kingdom today was not one sword, but two swords back to back. You two, leveling at each other all your lives — were, in truth, making the swords that could best block each other.'

$celebName looked at $userName. Smiling, wearing the shoulder brace.

'...The champion wasn't decided. The final ended because of the monsters.'

'Let's decide it at the next tournament,' $userName said. 'So until then, look after that shoulder. To beat a sound you is a boast.'

$celebName laughed. Shrugging the shoulder.

'Dream on.'

Dawn light lit the two steel swords at once. Two swords that had meant all their lives to break each other, become in the end two swords that blocked the same dark.

chung.epilogue:
$celebName is, to $userName, a person of two faces.

One is the face of the most troublesome enemy when faced head-on. When $celebName hones the sword a hand's width sharper, $userName cannot stay still. Knowing that to stop is to go dull, $userName, at the sight of that person, draws out one's own sword. From those times a hundred years ago in the academy courtyard, each other's whetstone across one wall.

The other is the face of the most dependable ally when back to back. $userName knows. That when a true dark falls, $celebName and $userName will turn the swords leveled at each other, just as they are, to face outward. That every sword-path memorized all their lives to break the other becomes, then, the path to guard the same side. On that night the monsters poured down in the middle of the final, the two already proved it.

So the days $celebName wavers weigh most on $userName. On a day $celebName's sword looks cracked, the shoulder looks heavy, before the wish to break — $userName wants to lend one's own sword. That to win aiming at a cracked sword becomes a blemish for life, $userName learned at that tournament.

An enemy when faced, an ally when back to back. To $userName, $celebName is the one and only person who does both. So $userName watches that sword today too. Some days to catch up, some days to guard.

chung.celebRole: fellow painter who always panned that work

paragraphs.gi: In $era, when they met at the studio $celebName always pointed out flaws before $userName's painting. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who believed only their own color right, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A streak that pushes and refines can knock two together, so they quarreled even over a single color. Even while criticizing, $celebName wordlessly filled in the colors missing from $userName's palette and left.
paragraphs.jeon: One day $userName's painting sold for the first time, and the colors that carried it were all the ones $celebName had filled in. The one clashing had in truth supported the painting, so $userName set the brush down and laughed.
paragraphs.gyeol: That kind of streak — deepening through the clash — can carry into this life. So even quarreling with $celebName, something better might end up coming out.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So you may argue with other fans over a $celebName song review, yet end up treasuring that song most.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might bicker over $celebName's acting reading, yet end up drawn deeper into that work.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may debate over $celebName's play, yet end up cheering more deeply.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So even when opinions split over $celebName's work, you might end up liking it more.

chung.era: 1936 New York, a season at the gym
chung.title: Two Fists in the Ring
chung.logline: Two boxers raised in the same gym clash for the champion's belt. The fist swung to knock the other down becomes, in the end, the fist that lifts him back up.

chapters.heading: The Basement Gym
chapters.body:
$userName started boxing at fourteen, in a basement gym in a New York back alley.

1936, New York. A place where the children of a poor neighborhood gathered to climb upward on one fist. A low-ceilinged basement, the smell of sweat, a worn heavy bag. That was $userName's world.

The day $userName first pushed open the gym door, there was a boy skipping rope in the ring. Light footwork. A fast jab. A boy everyone called 'a champion in the making.' That was $celebName.

The two grew up in the same gym. Hit the same heavy bag, learned from the same coach, breathed the same basement air. But with the gloves on, the two were not companions.

$userName's boxing was heavy. Each blow was weighty. A fist that made you stagger when it hit.

$celebName's boxing was fast. The feet light, the jab nimble. $userName's heavy fist could hardly catch $celebName's fast feet.

In the first sparring, $userName could not land a single proper hit on $celebName. Hit only the air through three rounds. Only $celebName's jab kept tapping into $userName's face.

The sparring over, $celebName said, unlacing the gloves. 'The fist is heavy. Really heavy. But — if that heavy thing doesn't land, what use is it? The feet are too slow. The feet.'

$userName was galled by those words. To $celebName, $userName was — a slow-footed newcomer who couldn't land a single hit.

That night, $userName stayed in the empty basement gym. A pace over, the next locker was $celebName's. Same gym, lockers side by side. 'I will catch those fast feet, without fail. And knock him down.' Whether it was hatred or what, $userName did not yet know the name.

chapters.heading: The Sound of Skipping Rope
chapters.body:
From that day $userName's dawn changed.

$userName went down to the basement gym before sunrise. Skipped rope. To make the feet fast. The heavy fist was already there; what was needed was the feet to make that fist land.

But one dawn, there was someone in the basement already. It was $celebName. Hitting the heavy bag. Weightily.

The two, without a word, did their own training. $userName the skipping rope, $celebName the heavy bag. Without a greeting. But both knew. That as $userName skipped rope to make the feet, $celebName hit the bag to make a heavy blow. That they were learning to mend each other's weakness — from each other.

After that day the two people's dawns came earlier and earlier. When $userName was there first, the next day $celebName was there first. At the two ends of the basement, mixing the sound of skipping rope and the sound of the heavy bag.

The sparrings went on. $userName's feet grew faster. Not as fast as $celebName's, but now $userName did not hit only the air. $celebName's fist grew heavier. Not as heavy as $userName's one blow, but now $celebName did not only throw jabs.

The two, copying each other's weakness, grew to resemble each other. Resembled, while hating.

After one sparring, as $userName came down from the ring, $celebName threw a towel.

'The right hand,' $celebName said. 'Your right hook is good. When it comes in — the left foot is half a beat late. Fix just that and you land on me.'

And went off. $userName stood a long while, towel in hand. That $celebName had watched own fist — down to the feet — was a strange thing.

chapters.heading: The Cut Eye
chapters.body:
Before a fight with a title shot on it, something happened.

The neighborhood champion's belt. A fight to single out one person to challenge for it was set. The two candidates risen from the gym were $userName and $celebName. One seat only.

But five days before the fight, $celebName was hurt. In a rough sparring with a fighter from another gym, the brow was cut. Deep. A wound where one more hit to the same place could cost the sight.

The coach shook the head. 'It won't heal in five days. Put him in the ring like this, and the moment he takes a hit there, it's over. He could lose boxing.'

$celebName sat in a corner of the basement with the brow bound. Those feet, light all life.

$userName could not sleep that night.

It was a strange thing. If $celebName could not fight, the title shot was $userName's. That seat wished for all life, rolling in even by standing still. $userName ought to have been glad.

And yet was not glad at all. $userName could not bear it. A ring without $celebName, an opponent with no fast feet, a title shot received that way — none of it was a thing to boast of. What $userName had wished to knock down was a fast $celebName, not a cut-eyed $celebName.

Only then did $userName know. That skipping rope every dawn had been not to knock $celebName down — but to catch up to those fast feet and stand in the same ring.

The next dawn, $userName sat before $celebName instead of skipping rope.

'The eye,' $userName said. 'If you can't block above that brow, make a guard that doesn't give that spot up. A stance with the left guard a hand's width higher and the chin tucked. In five days it sits in the body. Then you don't take a hit there.'

'...Why are you like this,' $celebName asked. 'It's by my not fighting that you get the title shot.'

'Shut up and put on the gloves.' $userName's voice trembled. 'A title shot received in a ring without you, I won't do it. Even received, I'd not be glad. To beat a sound-footed you — that's a boast. To win aiming at a cut-eyed you is a blemish for life. Get up. In five days, climb into the ring.'

chapters.heading: Five Days in the Basement
chapters.body:
For the days $celebName began to learn the new guard, the two stayed up nearly all night in the basement gym.

$celebName's brow wound throbbed under the bandage. $userName did the work of two. Tended the heavy bag, worked on the glove laces, put salve on $celebName's wound. On the face of a person said to be hated.

'...You really are a strange kid,' $celebName said, sitting on the worn mat. 'If I can't fight with a cut eye, the title shot is yours. So why are you putting salve on me.'

$userName set down the salve bottle.

'The skipping rope,' $userName said. 'Do you know why I skipped rope every dawn? To catch up to your fast feet. Because your feet were there, I had a reason to rise at dawn. If you lose boxing — whose feet do I watch and skip rope to.'

$celebName watched the old bulb on the ceiling a long while.

'...Me too,' $celebName said. 'Do you know why I hit the heavy bag every dawn? To catch up to your heavy blow. Because your fist was there, even I, a champion candidate, couldn't go idle. We grew catching up to each other, in the same basement.'

That night, the two for the first time admitted what had been beneath the label of hatred. It was the other name for a bond of two who had copied and mended each other's weakness in the same basement.

As dawn drew near, $userName put on the mitts and stood before $celebName. 'Raise the guard a hand's width and throw a jab.' $celebName threw a jab. Keeping the left guard at that height. The fist tapped into $userName's mitt.

'...Done,' $userName said. 'That spot isn't open. This way you don't lose the sight.'

$celebName lowered the gloves and gasped. Smiling with the bandaged brow.

'...I won't say thank you,' $celebName said. 'I'll say it after the fight. After beating you with sound feet.'

'Dream on,' $userName laughed, taking off the mitts. A line of dawn light came in through the basement vent.

chapters.heading: The Guard Raised a Hand's Width
chapters.body:
Through the five days $celebName mended the eye, the two spent day and night in the basement gym together.

$celebName learned the new guard. A stance with the left a hand's width higher and the chin tucked. The guard $userName had drawn up. At first it was awkward. $celebName's boxing was fast feet and a jab, not holding with a guard.

$userName stood beside with the mitts.

'Don't lower the guard. Keep the left at that height even when you jab. Don't give that spot up, and you don't lose the sight.'

'...You're good at holding mitts. I didn't know.'

'A newcomer has to watch all sorts of things to survive,' $userName laughed. 'You don't know because you've always been a champion candidate.'

$celebName laughed aloud for the first time. With a bandage on the brow.

Both knew. That this was not hatred. Only, the heart of wanting to stand in the same ring was so large that they had put the wrong name, hatred, on it.

By day each prepared their own fight. When stuck, they watched for each other. $celebName fixed $userName's late left foot, and $userName checked $celebName's new guard together.

The night before the fight, the two sat side by side on the gym rooftop. New York's lights lay under their feet.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'Tomorrow, let's both stay standing to the end. Whoever takes the title shot — both of us in the ring until the final bell.'

'Of course,' $userName said. 'And the title shot, I take it.'

'Dream on. Even with a cut eye I beat you.'

The two laughed. A pair to hit each other tomorrow, and yet that night alone, they laughed like brothers.

chapters.heading: The Morning of the Fight
chapters.body:
On the morning of the fight, the two tightened their glove laces in the basement gym together.

$userName own gloves, $celebName own gloves. $celebName's brow wound had nearly healed, with only a small bandage left. $userName pulled $celebName's glove laces tight one more time. Firmly.

'...$userName.' $celebName said it. 'When today ends, one title-shot seat is decided. You and me, one of us.'

'I know,' $userName said, tying the laces.

'If I take it,' $celebName said. 'Will you be all right? In this neighborhood, the only road up is that one title shot.'

$userName stopped the hand tying the laces.

'I'll be all right,' $userName said. 'If you take the title shot, my skipping rope goes in there. Every dawn I made the sound of skipping rope, so you couldn't stop the heavy bag either. Half your title shot is — that sound I made every dawn. Then it's enough.'

$celebName tapped $userName's shoulder with a gloved hand.

'...Don't say things like that,' $celebName said. 'We both stay in the ring to the end. Whoever takes the title shot, neither of us goes down until the final bell. That's our fight.'

The coach opened the basement door and called out. 'Ring call. Both of you, up.'

The two rose from the mat. Two boxers raised in the same basement. $celebName held a glove out toward $userName. $userName tapped own glove lightly against it.

'Let's go,' $userName said.

'The title shot, I take it,' $celebName said.

'Dream on.'

The two climbed the narrow stairs toward the ring. From the stairs' end came the murmur of the neighborhood crowd.

chapters.heading: The Last Round
chapters.body:
The day of the fight. The two candidates of the basement gym climbed into the ring before the neighborhood crowd.

The bell rang.

$userName's fist was heavy. And now the feet followed. The skipping rope of every dawn made that heavy fist land on $celebName.

$celebName's feet were fast. Fast even with a bandage on the brow. And the left guard was always raised a hand's width. The guard $userName had drawn up blocked the cut brow firmly.

Round one, two, three. The two did not give an inch. A weighty fist and fast feet clashed. The stands rose. Two boxers raised in the same gym were making a neighborhood fight into a championship bout.

And the last round.

$userName saw it. $celebName's left guard was wavering. A guard learned in five days was not solid enough to bear a long fight. The guard dropped half a beat. Above the cut brow — was open.

There it was. If $userName's right hook went in, $celebName went down. The title shot was $userName's.

$userName — did not hit that spot.

Turned the right hook's direction. Not above the cut brow, but over $celebName's guard. Into the center of the solid guard. Not a fist that knocks down, but a fist that says 'raise the guard.'

$celebName flinched back to wakefulness. Raised the wavering left guard a hand's width again. To that height $userName had taught.

And the final bell rang.

The two were standing in the ring to the end. Neither down. The verdict came. The title shot went — to $celebName. To that boxing of fast feet that guarded the guard to the end.

$userName stood in the middle of the ring and heard that verdict. The chest dropped once. It was a seat wished for all life.

But the next moment, $userName was smiling.

$celebName came toward $userName. Held out a gloved hand.

'...$userName,' $celebName said, gasping for breath. 'The last round. You didn't hit my brow. It was open there — you saw it too.'

'I didn't hit it,' $userName tapped the gloved hand. 'A title shot received aiming at a cut eye is not a title shot but a blemish. To have failed to beat a sound you — that's my loss, and a boast. And.' $userName paused a moment. 'If you lose boxing, who do I train with at dawn for the rest of my life.'

$celebName tapped $userName's shoulder with the gloved hand.

'This title shot, half of it is yours. If you hadn't drawn up the guard, I'd have lost the eye. And — if I hadn't heard your skipping rope every dawn, I'd have gone idle long ago.'

'When the championship fight is over,' $userName said. 'Then face me again. When those fast feet are fully sound. To beat a sound you is a boast.'

$celebName laughed. Winking the bandaged brow.

'Dream on.'

The basement-gym people ringed the two boxers together. Two whose fist, swung to knock the other down, had become in the end the fist that lifts each other up.

chung.epilogue:
It is dawn. $userName tightens the glove laces. As in that basement gym a hundred years ago.

There is a reason $userName moves the body every dawn. It is one person. $celebName. The person who keeps $userName from being able to stop skipping rope.

When $userName sees $celebName's feet grow faster, $userName takes up own skipping rope again. When $userName sees $celebName's one blow grow heavier, $userName stands before own heavy bag. Two boxers grown in the same basement, copying and mending each other's weakness. The dawns of those days stay inside $userName a hundred years on. When $celebName climbs one rung, $userName's body looks up that rung on its own.

But what $userName remembers most clearly is that one fist of the last round. The moment $celebName's guard dropped and above the cut brow lay open — $userName did not hit that spot. Instead hit the center of the solid guard. With a fist that says 'raise the guard.' That to win aiming at a wavering guard becomes a blemish for life, $userName learned in that ring.

So even now, on a day $celebName looks to waver, $userName cries it inwardly. Raise the guard. And when $celebName raises the guard, $userName tightens own glove laces again. Knowing that, with that person's heavy-bag sound in the dawn gym, $userName too does not stop.

paragraphs.gi: In $era, $celebName clashed with $userName for first place on every stage. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who wouldn't yield the spot, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A friction streak can set two against each other, so meeting in the practice room they tried to cow each other with harder pieces. Even so, $celebName jotted the fingering for the bar $userName got stuck on into the corner of the score.
paragraphs.jeon: On one great stage $userName cleared the hardest bar thanks to that fingering. The rival always quarreling had in truth opened the way, so $userName looked their direction first at the curtain call.
paragraphs.gyeol: A friction streak — growing together through the clash — can carry into this life. So even competing with $celebName, you might end up growing together.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So you may fret over $celebName's chart ranking, yet end up streaming all the harder.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So you might be on edge over $celebName's awards rivalry, yet end up cheering more deeply.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So you may grip your heart over $celebName's ranking fight, yet end up cheering more firmly.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So you might mind $celebName's view-count rivalry, yet end up following it all the more gladly.

chung.era: the near future, a season in the flight wing
chung.title: For the Ace Seat
chung.logline: Two pilots who contended for the flight wing's ace seat. The eye that glared at the kill score becomes, in the end, the eye that guards the other's blind spot.

chapters.heading: Two Newcomers
chapters.body:
$userName was assigned to the flight wing at twenty-one.

The near future. An age when humankind crossed between orbit and the planets. There were flight wings that guarded the routes. Units of pilots flying single-seat space fighters. To the most outstanding one of each wing was given the title 'ace.'

$userName was a newcomer. Two newcomers were assigned that day. One was $userName, one was $celebName.

The first simulated flight. The instructor scrambled the two at once. $userName's maneuvering was precise. Read the instrument panel without a gap, did not stray an inch from the route.

$celebName's maneuvering was different. Flew by feel rather than the panel. Twisted the craft at angles not in the manual. Rough, but flight no one could foresee.

In the simulated combat, $celebName's craft caught the tail of $userName's craft. With that twisted maneuvering not in the manual. $userName, in the end, could not shake off that tail.

Taking off the helmet in the hangar, $celebName said. 'Clean maneuvering. Really clean. But — too much by the manual. The next move is all read. Step one foot outside the manual once.'

And went off. Neither praise nor sneer, only the fact.

$userName was galled by those words 'by the manual.' To $celebName, $userName was — just a newcomer whose next move was all read.

That night, $userName stayed in the hangar and stood by own fighter. In the next bay over was $celebName's craft. Same squadron, hangars side by side. 'I will read that twisted maneuvering, without fail. And I will take the ace seat.' Whether it was hatred or what, $userName did not yet know the name.

chapters.heading: The Control Record
chapters.body:
From that day $userName's night changed.

At a night when everyone slept, $userName went down to the control room. Played back $celebName's simulated-flight records. The trajectory of that twisted maneuvering, frame by frame. To read that move outside the manual.

But one night, there was someone in the control room already. It was $celebName. Sitting before a screen.

The two, without a word, watched their own screens. Without a greeting. But $userName saw it. That what $celebName was playing back was — $userName's flight record. As $userName tried to learn $celebName's rough maneuvering, $celebName was learning $userName's precise routes.

After that day the two people's nights grew longer and longer. When $userName was there first, the next night $celebName was there first. Before screens at the two ends of the control room, playing back each other's flights.

The simulated combats went on. Irregular moves began to mix into $userName's maneuvering. One foot outside the manual at a time. $celebName's maneuvering grew precise. On the rough feel, the precision of the panel was laid.

The two, copying each other's flight, grew to resemble each other. Resembled, while hating.

After one simulated combat, $celebName threw $userName a data chip.

'Your six o'clock,' $celebName said. 'That's your blind spot. Each time you bank to starboard, six o'clock goes empty half a beat. I've recorded it all on that chip. Mend just that and you don't get caught.'

And went off. $userName stood a long while, data chip in hand. That $celebName had watched the blind spot of own flight — that far — was a strange thing.

chapters.heading: The Broken Thruster
chapters.body:
Before the ace-selection flight, something happened.

The wing singled out the ace by simulated combat. A last flight where the two best candidates fought one to one. Those two candidates were $userName and $celebName.

But three days before the selection flight, $celebName's craft had trouble. Passing through a micro-meteoroid field on a routine patrol, the starboard thruster was damaged. The maintenance crew shook their heads. 'The part won't come within three days. Flying without the starboard thruster, he can barely do a right turn. $celebName's twisted maneuvering — half of it is right turns…'

$celebName sat in a corner of the hangar by the damaged craft. That flight, fearless all life.

$userName could not sleep that night.

It was a strange thing. If $celebName could not enter the selection flight, the ace was $userName's. That seat contended for since the newcomer days, rolling in even by standing still. $userName ought to have been glad.

And yet was not glad at all. $userName could not bear it. A simulated combat without $celebName, an opponent with no twisted maneuvering, an ace received that way — none of it was a thing to boast of. What $userName had wished to beat was a fearless $celebName, not a broken-thruster $celebName.

Only then did $userName know. That playing back $celebName's flight in the control room every night had been not to drag down — but to catch up to that flight and stand in the same sky.

The next dawn, $userName went not to the control room but to $celebName's hangar. Carrying own data chip.
