'The thruster,' $userName held out the chip. 'If the starboard thruster won't do, a flight pattern that makes the right turn with the port thruster and attitude control instead — I drew it up. It isn't in the manual, so it takes a few days in the hand — but three days is enough.'

'...Why are you like this,' $celebName asked. 'It's by my not flying that you're ace.'

'Shut up and sit in the cockpit.' $userName's voice trembled. 'An ace received in a simulated combat without you, I won't do it. Even received, I'd not be glad. To beat a sound-craft you — that's a boast. To win aiming at a broken-thruster you is a blemish for life. Get up. In three days, scramble.'

chapters.heading: Three Days in the Hangar
chapters.body:
For the days $celebName began to learn the new flight pattern, the two stayed up nearly all night in the hangar.

$celebName's hand, with the damaged craft data up, always trembled. $userName did the maintenance for two. Inspected $celebName's damaged starboard thruster together, worked on own craft too. Set a hand to the fighter of a person said to be hated.

'...Why are you like this,' $celebName asked, sitting on the hangar floor. 'If I can't fly with a broken thruster, the ace is yours.'

$userName set down the inspection terminal.

'Six o'clock,' $userName said. 'My flight always had a six-o'clock blind spot. You pinned it for me. Because an enemy craft like you was at my six, I couldn't doze in the cockpit a single moment. If you vanish from the sky — who watches my six o'clock. Who do I stay sharp for.'

$celebName watched the hangar ceiling a long while.

'...Same for me,' $celebName said. 'My starboard five-o'clock blind spot, you wrote it all on a chip. Because an enemy craft like you was there, I couldn't leave a gap in my flight either. We spent a whole season filling each other's blind spots.'

That night, the two for the first time admitted what had been beneath the label of hatred. It was the other name for a bond of two who knew each other's blind spots best.

As dawn drew near, $userName sat by the simulator. '$celebName. Going into a right turn, throw the nose first. Once more.' $celebName took the cockpit control stick. $userName laid a hand over that hand from beside. As if setting the order.

The craft on the simulator screen drew a smooth right turn with only one thruster.

'...Done,' $userName said. 'It turns even with one thruster. This way you can fly.'

$celebName took the hand off the stick and removed the helmet. Swept back the hair with the trembling hand and laughed.

'...I won't say thank you,' $celebName said. 'I'll say it after the selection flight. After beating you with a sound craft.'

'Dream on,' $userName laughed. A line of planetary-ring light came in through the hangar vent.

chapters.heading: Flight With One Thruster
chapters.body:
Through the three days $celebName steadied the trembling hand, the two spent day and night in the hangar and simulator together.

$celebName sat at the simulator and learned flight without the starboard thruster. The pattern $userName had drawn up. A flight that makes a right turn with only the port thruster and attitude-control jets. At first it was awkward. $celebName's hand was used to using both thrusters fearlessly.

$userName watched the data together from beside.

'Going into a right turn, throw the nose first and the thrust after. Switch the order and it turns even with one thruster.'

'...You're good at drawing flight patterns. I didn't know.'

'A newcomer has to watch all sorts of things to survive,' $userName laughed. 'You don't know because you've always been an ace candidate.'

$celebName laughed aloud for the first time. With the damaged craft data up.

Both knew. That this was not hatred. Only, the heart of wanting to stand in the same sky was so large that they had put the wrong name, hatred, on it.

By day each prepared their own selection flight. When stuck, they watched each other's data. $celebName pinned $userName's six-o'clock blind spot again, and $userName checked $celebName's new flight pattern together.

The night before the selection flight, the two lay side by side on the hangar roof. Overhead the planet's ring flowed.

'$userName.' $celebName said it, watching the ring. 'Tomorrow, let's both fly to the end. Whoever becomes ace — both in the cockpit until the crash signal shows.'

'Of course,' $userName said. 'And the ace, I take it.'

'Dream on. Even with one thruster I beat you.'

The two laughed. A pair to catch each other's tail in the same sky tomorrow, and yet that night alone, they laughed like old comrades.

chapters.heading: The Dawn of the Selection Flight
chapters.body:
On the dawn of the selection flight, the two inspected their craft in the hangar together.

$userName own fighter, $celebName own craft with the starboard thruster patched by a spare part. $celebName's hand still trembled at times. $userName checked $celebName's cockpit panel together once more.

'...$userName.' $celebName said it, helmet in hand. 'When today ends, the ace is decided. You and me, one of us.'

'I know,' $userName said, inspecting the panel.

'If I become ace,' $celebName said. 'Will you be all right? You flew watching that title since the newcomer days.'

$userName set down the inspection terminal and looked at $celebName.

'I'll be all right,' $userName said. 'If you become ace, my flight goes in there. Because I was stuck to your six as an enemy craft, you couldn't leave a gap either. Half your ace title is — that flight where I chased your blind spot. Then it's enough.'

$celebName tucked the helmet under an arm and looked at $userName a long while.

'...How do you say things like that every time,' $celebName said.

'Chase an enemy craft long enough,' $userName said. 'and that craft becomes part of you. If you become ace, part of me becomes ace together. Then there's nothing to be bitter about.'

The control-tower radio sounded. 'Both candidate craft, scramble prep. Five minutes.'

The two put on their helmets. Climbed into their cockpits. Before the canopy closed, $celebName's radio came in.

'$userName. See you on the simulated battlefield. Whoever catches whose tail — neither leaves the cockpit to the end.'

'Of course,' $userName answered the radio. 'And the ace, I take it.'

'Dream on.'

The canopies closed. The two fighters slid out of the hangar in turn. At the runway's end, a sky full of stars waited for the two.

chapters.heading: The Blind Spot
chapters.body:
The ace-selection flight. The whole wing gathered before the control-room screens.

$userName and $celebName flew their two fighters up onto the simulated battlefield. One to one.

$userName's maneuvering was precise. And now irregular moves were mixed in. The years of playing back $celebName's flight every night had sent $userName one foot outside the manual.

$celebName's maneuvering was fearless. Even with one starboard thruster dead. The new flight pattern $userName had drawn up made a right turn with only one thruster.

The two craft chased each other's tails. When one caught six o'clock, the other twisted free. The control room stirred. Two newcomers were dragging the longest simulated combat in the wing's history.

Then $userName saw it. $celebName's craft had a blind spot toward the damaged starboard side. Making a right turn with one thruster, the field of view there went empty half a beat.

There it was. If $userName's craft dove into that blind spot, $celebName's tail was fully caught. A simulated kill. The ace was $userName's.

$userName — did not dive into that blind spot.

Instead switched on the radio. To switch on the radio to an enemy craft was not in the rules of simulated combat.

'$celebName. Your starboard five o'clock, the blind spot's open. The dead-thruster side. Cover it. Or in a real battlefield you die there.'

Past the radio $celebName was silent a moment. And the craft moved. Into a maneuver that closed the blind spot.

The control room murmured. $userName had thrown away a kill chance and told an enemy craft its blind spot.

The simulated combat ended, in the end, with no kill, at time expiry. The verdict came down. The one named ace was — $celebName. To that flight that, even with one thruster, covered the blind spot and held to the end.

$userName heard that naming in the cockpit. The chest dropped once. It was a seat wished for since the newcomer days.

But the next moment, $userName was smiling.

In the hangar, $celebName took off the helmet and walked to $userName.

'...$userName,' $celebName said. 'At the end. You switched on the radio. Told me my blind spot. If you'd dived in there you'd have been ace.'

'I didn't dive in,' $userName took off the helmet. 'An ace received aiming at the blind spot of a broken thruster isn't an ace but a blemish. To have failed to catch a sound-craft you — that's my loss, and a boast. And.' $userName paused a moment. 'If you die in that blind spot on a real battlefield, who do I sit in the control room with every night for the rest of my life.'

$celebName gripped $userName's shoulder.

'This ace, half of it is yours. If you hadn't drawn up the flight pattern, I couldn't even have scrambled. If you hadn't made the blind-spot chip, I'd have died at that five o'clock. And — if you hadn't been in the control room every night, I'd have gone idle long ago.'

'At the next selection I take the ace,' $userName said. 'So until then, get that thruster fixed sound. To beat a sound you is a boast.'

$celebName laughed. Pointing at the damaged craft.

'Dream on.'

The hangar lights lit the two fighters at once. Two newcomers who had glared at the kill score, become two pilots who guarded each other's blind spots best.

chung.epilogue:
Flight has a blind spot. For anyone, one unseen direction.

$userName's blind spot was six o'clock. A hundred years ago, on the simulated battlefield of a space flight wing. The person who pinned that blind spot is $celebName. The one who wrote all of $userName's gaps onto a data chip and, enemy craft though, handed it over.

After that day, $celebName became, to $userName, the person who fills the blind spot. When $celebName shows a new maneuver, $userName sends own flight one foot outside the manual. That to stop is to have the next move all read, $userName learned in the long nights of the control room. With that person's craft at $userName's six o'clock, $userName cannot doze in the cockpit.

A blind spot opens in $celebName's flight too. On a day the craft looks to tilt to one side, before the wish to catch up, $userName wants to switch on the radio. To say 'a blind spot's open there.' That to kill aiming at a blind spot becomes a blemish for life, $userName learned in that selection flight. Because on a real battlefield, a friend could be lost in that blind spot.

An enemy chasing each other's tails when faced, a wingman guarding each other's blind spots when flying the same side. To $userName, $celebName is the one and only pilot who does both. So $userName keeps own six o'clock open today too. Waiting for that craft to come into it.

chung.celebRole: loud merchant from the shop across the way

paragraphs.gi: In $era, the $celebName from the shop across the way spread word on the street each day that $userName's prices were steep. Back then $userName tended to be a $userRole who wouldn't bend on their prices, and $celebName a $celebRole.
paragraphs.seung: A friction streak can knock two together, so the sound of the two quarreling always rang down the canal street. Even while spreading those rumors, $celebName sent every customer seeking silk straight to $userName's shop.
paragraphs.jeon: One month $userName's shop did best on the street, and half those customers were people $celebName had sent. That nasty rumor had in truth pushed the business along, so $userName, gone to have it out, ended up laughing.
paragraphs.gyeol: A friction streak — turning firm through the wavering — can carry into this life. So even clashing with $celebName, you might end up raising each other.

modernPunchlineByKind.idol: So even with a fan whose views clash with yours over $celebName, you might end up waving lightsticks together.
modernPunchlineByKind.actor: So a person you quarreled with over a $celebName reading might end up the same kind of fan.
modernPunchlineByKind.athlete: So even someone who argued with you over a $celebName match, you might end up cheering beside.
modernPunchlineByKind.icon: So a person you bickered with over $celebName, you might end up following the content with.

chung.era: the 2020s, the season of the finals
chung.title: Met Again in the Final
chung.logline: Two players who met in the final every year on the pro-gaming stage. The rival glared at across a keyboard becomes, in the end, the one who reaches a hand out first.

chapters.heading: Solo-Rank No. 1 and No. 2
chapters.body:
The first time $userName saw that handle was on the solo-rank leaderboard.

The 2020s. A time when esports became an era's stage. Thousands played the same game, and those who rose highest among them became pro players.

$userName was solo-rank No. 2. Played until dawn, ground up one rung at a time with the fingers.

At the very top of the leaderboard, the No. 1 seat, was always the same handle. One that had never once come down. That was $celebName.

The two were scouted to the same pro team. On one team, $userName had to compete for a starter slot. The opponent was $celebName.

The first team scrim. $userName's play was stable. Never once overreached. By the book, not a single timing off.

$celebName's play was different. Plunged a blade at a timing not in the textbook. Rough, unpredictable, and sometimes died absurdly. But that one irregular move flipped the game.

In the scrim, $userName's character fell to $celebName's irregular play. It was a timing the by-the-book could not block.

The game over, $celebName said, taking off the headset. 'Clean play. Really clean. But — too much by the book. The next move is all read. Step outside the book once.'

And went off. Neither praise nor sneer, only the fact.

$userName was galled by those words 'by the book.' To $celebName, $userName was — just a No. 2 whose next move was all read.

That night, $userName stayed in the dorm practice room. A pace over, the next seat was $celebName's. Same team, seats side by side. 'I will drag that No. 1 down, without fail.' Whether it was hatred or what, $userName did not yet know the name.

chapters.heading: The Replay
chapters.body:
From that day $userName's dawn changed.

After everyone slept, $userName stayed alone in the practice room. Played back $celebName's match replays. That irregular move, frame by frame. To read that timing outside the book.

But one dawn, there was someone in the practice room already. It was $celebName. Sitting before a monitor.

The two, without a word, watched their own screens. Without a greeting. But $userName saw it. That what $celebName was playing back was — $userName's replay. As $userName tried to learn $celebName's irregular play, $celebName was learning $userName's stable management.

After that day the two people's dawns came earlier and earlier. When $userName was there first, the next day $celebName was there first. Before monitors at the two ends of the practice room, playing back each other's replays.

The scrims went on. Irregular moves began to mix into $userName's management. One step outside the book at a time. $celebName's play grew stable. On the rough irregularity, $userName's composure was laid.

The two, copying each other's play, grew to resemble each other. Resembled, while hating.

After one scrim, $celebName handed $userName a memo.

'Your seven-minute timing,' $celebName said. 'You never take vision there. That's your blind spot. I've written it all on that memo. Mend just that and you don't fall to that irregular play.'

And went off. $userName stood a long while, memo in hand. That $celebName had watched own play — that far — was a strange thing.

chapters.heading: The Wrist
chapters.body:
Before the league final, something happened.

That year the two became starters together. With irregularity and stability mixed on one team, the team climbed to the final. But the final opponent's team had a strong lane too, and the team could put only one of the two against that lane. In effect, the two again competed for the core seat.

But five days before the final, $celebName injured a wrist. The hand that had gripped a mouse until dawn for years finally broke down. Wrist tendinitis. The doctor said. 'It won't heal in five days. Playing a full final like this, the click accuracy collapses in the late game. $celebName's irregular play — precise clicks are its lifeblood…'

$celebName sat in a corner of the practice room with the wrist bound. That hand, the fastest all life.

$userName could not sleep that night.

It was a strange thing. If $celebName dropped from the final's core seat, that seat was $userName's. That seat contended for since the solo-rank days, rolling in even by standing still. $userName ought to have been glad.

And yet was not glad at all. $userName could not bear it. A final with $celebName wavering, one hand with no irregular play, a core seat seized that way — none of it was a thing to boast of. What $userName had wished to beat was a sound $celebName, not a wrist-injured $celebName.

Only then did $userName know. That playing back $celebName's replays every dawn had been not to drag down — but to catch up to that play and stand on the same final stage.

The next dawn, $userName sat beside $celebName instead of by the replays.

'The wrist,' $userName held out a memo. 'If a full-time click won't do, play to cut down the clicks. A path that lands the irregular play with less of the wrist — I drew it up. Even halving the click count, that irregular play goes in just the same. In five days it sits in the hand.'

'...Why are you like this,' $celebName asked. 'It's by my dropping out that you get the core seat.'

'Shut up and grip the mouse.' $userName's voice trembled. 'A seat seized in a final with you wavering, I won't do it. Even received, I'd not be glad. To beat a sound-handed you — that's a boast. To win aiming at a wrist-injured you is a blemish for life. Get up. In five days, stand on the final stage.'

chapters.heading: Five Days in the Practice Room
chapters.body:
For the days $celebName began to learn the click-reduced path, the two stayed up nearly all night in the practice room.

$celebName's wrist was swollen inside the brace. $userName did the work of two. Changed $celebName's keyboard key-pressure to ease the wrist, set the mouse sensitivity again, ordered the late-night meal in two bowls. On the keyboard of a person said to be hated.

'...You really are strange,' $celebName said, leaning back in the gaming chair. 'If I drop from the core seat with an injured wrist, that seat is yours. So why are you setting my mouse sensitivity.'

$userName set down the mouse.

'The replays,' $userName said. 'Do you know why I played back your replays every dawn? To catch up to the irregular play of a solo-rank No. 1. Because your play was there, I couldn't stop at the No. 2 seat. If you lose gaming — whose replays do I play back. Who do I step outside the book for.'

$celebName watched the monitor's black screen a long while.

'...Me too,' $celebName said. 'Why do you think I watched your replays every dawn. Because No. 2 was chasing fearsomely. Because a back like you was there, even I, No. 1, couldn't go idle. We lived a whole season playing back each other's replays.'

That night, the two for the first time admitted what had been beneath the label of hatred. It was the other name for a bond of two who had made each other the only back to catch up to.

As dawn drew near, $userName sat in the seat beside $celebName. '$celebName. This irregular play, a path that cuts three clicks to one. Run it once more.' $celebName gripped the mouse. $userName laid a hand over that wrist from beside. So it would not twist.

On the practice-room monitor, that irregular play landed just the same with one click.

'...Done,' $userName said. 'Clicks halved and the irregular play unchanged. This way you hold out a full set.'

$celebName took off the headset and leaned back in the chair. Smiling, wearing the wrist brace.

'...I won't say thank you,' $celebName said. 'I'll say it after the final. After beating you with a sound hand.'

'Dream on,' $userName laughed. A line of dawn light came in through the practice-room blind.

chapters.heading: The Click-Reduced Path
chapters.body:
Through the five days $celebName mended the wrist, the two spent day and night in the practice room together.

$celebName, wearing the wrist brace, learned the new path. Management that lands the irregular play even with reduced clicks. The path $userName had drawn up. At first it was awkward. $celebName's hand was used to fast, many clicks.

$userName watched the screen together from beside.

'Here, what would be three clicks, cut to one. Draw the path in advance, and the same irregular play goes in without twisting the wrist.'

'...You're good at drawing paths. I didn't know.'

'A No. 2 has to watch all sorts of things to catch No. 1,' $userName laughed. 'You don't know because you've always been No. 1.'

$celebName laughed aloud for the first time. Wearing the wrist brace.

Both knew. That this was not hatred. Only, the heart of wanting to stand on the same final stage was so large that they had put the wrong name, hatred, on it.

By day each prepared the final. When stuck, they watched for each other. $celebName pinned $userName's seven-minute blind spot again, and $userName checked $celebName's new path together.

The night before the final, the two sat side by side on the dorm rooftop. The city lights lay under their feet.

'$userName.' $celebName said it. 'Tomorrow, let's both stay on the stage to the end. Whoever takes the core role — both of us at the keyboard until the last game ends.'

'Of course,' $userName said. 'And MVP, I take it.'

'Dream on. Even wearing a wrist brace I beat you.'

The two laughed. A pair to compete again on the same stage tomorrow, and yet that night alone, they laughed like old friends.

chapters.heading: Behind the Final Stage
chapters.body:
On the day of the league final, the two checked their gear in the backstage waiting room together.

$userName own keyboard and mouse, $celebName own gear set up to ease the wrist. $celebName's wrist brace was hidden inside the uniform sleeve. $userName arranged $celebName's mouse cable once more, at an angle that did not twist the wrist.

'...$userName.' $celebName said it, headset in hand. 'In today's final, the core irregular-play role goes, in the end, to one person. You or me.'

'I know,' $userName said, arranging the cable.

'If I take it,' $celebName said. 'Will you be all right? We've contended for that seat since the solo-rank days.'

$userName took the hand off the cable and looked at $celebName.

'I'll be all right,' $userName said. 'If you land that irregular play on the stage, my replays go in there. I played back your play every dawn, and you played back mine. Half that irregular play is — those dawns we played back for each other. Then it's enough.'

$celebName hung the headset on the neck and looked at $userName a long while.

'...Don't say things like that,' $celebName said. 'We both stay on the stage to the end. Whoever takes the core role, both at the keyboard until the last game ends. That's our final.'

The stage director opened the waiting-room door. 'Both teams enter, one minute.'

The two rose from their chairs. The two players who had been solo-rank No. 1 and No. 2. $celebName held a fist out toward $userName. $userName tapped own fist lightly against it.

'Let's go,' $userName said.

'MVP, I take it,' $celebName said.

'Dream on.'

The two stepped into the corridor toward the stage. At the corridor's end, the lights of the final stage and the roar of the crowd waited for the two.

chapters.heading: The Final Stage
chapters.body:
The league final. Two team booths were set on either side of the stage. The stands were full.

$userName sat at the keyboard. And $celebName, wearing the wrist brace, sat in the next seat. The two were one team. And yet within the team, for the final's one core seat, they were still competitors.

The game began.

$userName's play was stable. And now irregular moves were mixed in. The years of playing back $celebName's replays every dawn had sent $userName one step outside the book.

$celebName's play was irregular. Even wearing the wrist brace. The new path $userName had drawn up landed that irregular play just the same with the clicks halved.

The match went to a full set. The last game, the last one timing.

Then $userName saw it. $celebName's hand was trembling. Even with the wrist brace, it was not enough to bear the long full-set match. $celebName's click fell a beat behind.

In that moment the team voice sounded. The core irregular-play — whether $userName would go in, or $celebName. The coach had to call one of the two. That one play decided the win.

The coach was about to call $userName. Seeing $celebName's hand tremble.

$userName spoke into the voice first.

'Go to $celebName. The core irregular play, $celebName goes in.'

The voice was silent a moment. The coach asked. 'The wrist's trembling?'

'I'll lay all the setup,' $userName said. 'So one click finishes it. $celebName only has to make that one click. That irregular play is — $celebName's. Since the solo-rank No. 1 days.'

$userName laid the board with own character. So that $celebName making one click would end the win, all the setup with own hand.

And $celebName, with the trembling hand, made that one click.

The screen turned to a victory screen. The stands exploded.

On the stage, $celebName took off the headset and turned to $userName. With the wrist-braced hand.

'...$userName,' $celebName said. 'The last core play. You could have gone in. The coach was about to call you. That was MVP.'

'I didn't go in,' $userName took off the headset. 'That irregular play has been yours since the solo-rank days. If I snatched what you couldn't do with a trembling wrist, that isn't MVP but a blemish. To compete to the end with a sound-handed you and win — that's a boast. And.' $userName paused a moment. 'If you lose that irregular play, who do I play back replays of every dawn for the rest of my life.'

$celebName held out the wrist-braced hand. Under the stage lights, though the cameras saw it all.

'This win, half of it is yours. If you hadn't drawn up the path, I couldn't have stood on the stage. If you hadn't laid the setup, I couldn't have made that one click. And — if you hadn't been in the next seat every dawn, I'd have gone idle in the No. 1 seat long ago.'

$userName took that hand.

'Next season I take MVP,' $userName said. 'So until then, look after that wrist. To beat a sound you is a boast.'

$celebName laughed. Waving the wrist brace.

'Dream on.'

The stage lights lit the two players at once. Two rivals who had glared at each other across a keyboard, become two who reach a hand out first to each other.

chung.epilogue:
When $userName sees one person's name, $userName still recalls a leaderboard.

$celebName. A hundred years ago, the handle that never once came down from the top of the solo-rank leaderboard. $userName was one rung below, No. 2. The No. 2 who ground up a step at a time, gaming until dawn.

Now $userName watches $celebName within a screen, on a stage. And each time $celebName does something well, the No. 2 of those days wakes inside $userName. When $celebName shows a new move, $userName wants to step one foot outside the book in own place. That to stop is to have the next move all read, $userName learned in the dawns of the practice room. With that person's keyboard sound in the next seat, $userName too does not stop the fingers.

There are days $celebName's hand looks to tremble. Days the click looks a beat behind. On such days the thought of wanting to snatch does not come to $userName. Instead — $userName wants to lay the setup. So one click finishes it for $celebName. That to snatch aiming at a trembling hand becomes a blemish for life, $userName learned on that final stage. Because that irregular play has been $celebName's since the solo-rank days.

When $celebName lands one move well, inside it is the whole season $userName chased on the leaderboard. So $userName watches that handle today too. With the heart to step one foot outside the book one more time.
